sermons.title: Rewiring My Worldview: How We Make Sense of Life | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎯 Central Theme
chunks.text: Like a pair of glasses, your worldview is the lens through which you view life. It forms your values, determines your decisions, and reveals your priorities. But so many don't even realize what their worldview is—they unconsciously adopt it from cultural or family expectations. As followers of Christ, we are called not to be conformed to the world's patterns, but to be transformed—rewired—by renewing our minds according to God's design. 🌍 Origin Where did I come from? 🎯 Meaning Why am I here? ⚖️ Morality Is there right & wrong? 💔 The Fall What went wrong? ✨ Destiny Where am I going?

chunks.heading: 📌 Key Takeaways / 💡 Key Insight
chunks.text: - Everyone has a worldview — a lens or map that helps them navigate life, whether they realize it or not - Worldviews drive decisions — how you spend time, money, who you spend time with, your career choices - Christianity is not a Sunday hobby — it's an all-encompassing understanding of how life works - Traditional worldviews are grouped by belief in God: Theism, Polytheism, Pantheism, Atheism - Modern worldviews are grouped by view of reality: Naturalism, Transcendentalism, Theism, Postmodernism - Biblical worldview addresses five key questions: Origin, Meaning, Morality, The Fall, and Destiny - Rewiring is ongoing — we continuously adjust our thinking to align with God's design "A worldview is your underlying assumptions that help you discern your reality. So many times people just unconsciously create their own sense of meaning and value without having ever thought about it."

chunks.heading: What Is a Worldview? / Traditional Worldview Classifications
chunks.text: Pastor Brett opens by explaining that while most people don't consciously identify their worldview, everyone has one. It's the lens through which we see life, affecting every decision we make. 📚 Oxford Dictionary "A particular philosophy of life or a conception of the world." 📖 Charles Colson "A worldview is a comprehensive life system that answers all of humanity's age-old questions: Where did I come from? Why am I here? Where am I going? Does life have any meaning and purpose?" "Your worldview is your underlying assumptions that help you discern your reality." The key insight: many people unconsciously adopt their worldview. They get tossed by waves of cultural or family expectations, jumping on trajectories of proving themselves and seeking importance—often leading to emptiness, resentment, and regrets. Traditionally, worldviews were grouped around beliefs about God: ☝️ Theism One God exists (Islam, Judaism, Christianity) 🔮 Polytheism Multiple gods exist (Buddhism, Hinduism) 🌿 Pantheism Everything is God; God is everywhere ❌ Atheism No God exists (includes Agnosticism, Secularism)

chunks.heading: Modern Worldview Classifications
chunks.text: In modern society, worldviews are better understood by their view of reality: 🔬 Naturalism Reality is physical—what you see is real. Includes materialism, secularism, hedonism. 🧘 Transcendentalism Reality is spiritual—what you see is not true reality. Includes New Age, Taoism, Buddhism. ✝️ Theism Both physical and spiritual reality exist. A personal God can be known and speaks to both dimensions. 🌊 Postmodernism Reality is unknowable and socially constructed. Includes relativism, deconstructionism, progressive Christianity. 📱 Postmodernism in Practice: This is where we see "I identify as..." statements, the pronoun movement, and the belief that marriage is merely a "social construct." Reality is seen as fluid and self-defined.

chunks.heading: Worldviews Are Everywhere
chunks.text: Pastor Brett illustrates how worldviews permeate culture—often without us noticing: 🦁 The Lion King Mufasa teaches Simba about the "Circle of Life"—that when we die, our bodies become grass, the antelope eat the grass, and so we are all connected. This is pantheism: the belief that God is in everything and everything is God. "It's a good movie, but I don't really like the theology." 🎵 Audrey Assad A popular Christian singer who deconstructed her faith. She now says: "I think there's something in the universe that happens in patterns, whether you call that mathematics or quantum physics or God is up to you." This is classic deconstructionism—moving from specific belief to vague spirituality. 📱 Instagram Influencer A woman who greets trees and moss, believing "all living things have a life force and a spirit." This is a form of animism/pantheism. "I love nature... but I don't say, 'Hello, lemon. Hi, moss.' Nature reminds me of a Creator."

chunks.heading: 1. Origin: Where Did I Come From?
chunks.text: Genesis 1:1 (NIV) "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth." Theologians describe this as ex nihilo—"from nothing." God exists outside the need to be created; He always was and always will be. Genesis 1:26 (NIV) "Then God said, 'Let us make mankind in our image, in our likeness...'" Key observations: - The Hebrew word "Elohim" (God) is plural, hinting at the Trinity - "Let us make mankind in our image" — God exists in community - We are made in the imago Dei — the image of God - Because God exists in relationship (Trinity), we were made to thrive in community

chunks.heading: 2. Meaning: Why Am I Here?
chunks.text: Pastor Brett challenges the "work hard, play hard" cycle that leads many to exhaustion and emptiness: "Your life is not about just running, meaningless, just doing it, just making the money, just obeying... and then going on vacation. That is your life? To work hard, play hard, work hard, play hard, and just repeat it for 80 years? Oh, makes me want to die now." Biblical answers to "Why am I here?": Matthew 22:37-39 (NIV) "'Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.' This is the first and greatest commandment. And the second is like it: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'" - Reflect the image of God — become more cohesive with God's purpose - Love God and love people — even those you don't like - Know God and make Him known — to the 90% in Hong Kong who don't know Christ - Live in community — encourage one another, forgive one another, spur one another on - Exercise dominion — steward your gifts, career, and influence for God's glory

chunks.heading: 3. Morality: Is There Right and Wrong?
chunks.text: We live in a world that increasingly says there is no right or wrong—"You do you. What is your truth?" But God has established boundaries: Genesis 2:16-17 (NIV) "And the Lord God commanded the man, 'You are free to eat from any tree in the garden; but you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for when you eat from it you will certainly die.'" 🤔 Why Not Murder? God could have said "Do not murder" or "Do not lie." Instead, He prohibited eating—a normal, neutral, necessary thing. Why? Because God was showing that even normal things, when made into idols or obsessions, become sin. It reveals the disease of sin within us.

chunks.heading: 4. The Fall: What Is Wrong With This World?
chunks.text: Why is there evil? Why do godly people get cancer and die young? Why do natural disasters kill innocent people? Genesis 3:21 (NIV) "The Lord God made garments of skin for Adam and his wife and clothed them." This is the first picture of a blood sacrifice—a covering for sin that previews what Christ would do. The Roman Road: - Romans 3:23 — "All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" - Romans 6:23 — "The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life" - Romans 5:12 — "Sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin" - Romans 5:8 — "While we were still sinners, Christ died for us" - Romans 10:9 — "If you confess with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved" 📝 Note on Chinese Translation: In Chinese Bibles, "sin" is often translated as "crime" (罪), which is unfortunate. Sin is not just criminal behavior—it's a condition of the heart. You can have never committed a crime and still be deep in sin.

chunks.heading: 5. Destiny: Where Am I Going? / The Series Ahead
chunks.text: John 14:2-3 (NIV) "I am going there to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am." 1 Peter 2:24 (NIV) "He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross, so that we might die to sins and live for righteousness; by his wounds you have been healed." "This life is not the end of the story. There is an eternity awaiting us. And knowledge of that life helps us navigate this one." Pastor Brett previews upcoming topics in the Rewiring My Worldview series: - Faith and Wisdom — How do we apply ancient Scripture to modern decisions? - Marriage, Singleness, and Family - Aging and Death - Money and Possessions - Sexuality, Politics, Gender, Career

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Bible Verses / 💡 Practical Application / Identify Your Current Lens / Stay Nimble & Teachable / See People as God Sees Them / Embrace Community / Discover Your Design / Watch for Idols / 🌟 The Freedom of a Biblical Worldview
chunks.text: Genesis 1:1 (NIV) "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth." Genesis 1:26 (NIV) "Then God said, 'Let us make mankind in our image, in our likeness, so that they may rule over the fish in the sea and the birds in the sky, over the livestock and all the wild animals, and over all the creatures that move along the ground.'" Romans 12:2 (NIV) "Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind." Matthew 22:37-39 (NIV) "Jesus replied: 'Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.' This is the first and greatest commandment. And the second is like it: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'" Genesis 2:16-17 (NIV) "And the Lord God commanded the man, 'You are free to eat from any tree in the garden; but you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for when you eat from it you will certainly die.'" Romans 3:23 (NIV) "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." Romans 6:23 (NIV) "For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." Romans 5:12 (NIV) "Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned." Romans 5:8 (NIV) "But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us." Romans 10:9 (NIV) "If you declare with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." John 14:2-3 (NIV) "My Father's house has many rooms; if that were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you? And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am." 1 Peter 2:24 (NIV) "He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross, so that we might die to sins and live for righteousness; by his wounds you have been healed." 👓 What worldview have you unconsciously adopted? Are you living for career success, approval from others, or material comfort? Recognize the "lens" you've been looking through—it may not be biblical. 🔄 Rewiring is an ongoing process throughout your entire life. Be willing to make adjustments where your previous views conflict with God's design. Stay teachable in the hands of God. 🚇 On the MTR, find 10 people. Only 1 of them is likely a Christian. Pray for all 10. Start to see people as "distressed, like sheep without a shepherd"—not just colleagues, but souls who need Christ. 🤝 God exists in community (Trinity); you were made to thrive in community. Be intentional about "one anothers"—encourage one another, forgive one another, spur one another on. 🎯 To rule over the world is to find how God made you and live accordingly. What are your unique gifts, passions, and aptitudes? Steward them for God's glory, not your own approval. ⛔ God prohibited eating from one tree—a normal thing—to show that even good things become sin when we obsess over them. What "normal things" have you made into idols? "God, we thank you that life in all of its meaning means we are freed from the need to impress others, to manage our reputations. We are freed because of what you have said about us. You've made us in the imago Dei, the image of God."

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 1. How has your worldview changed in recent years? Have biblical convictions changed your previous views? ▼ Consider: - What did you believe about success, relationships, or purpose 5-10 years ago? - How has Scripture challenged or transformed those beliefs? - In what areas have you experienced the "renewing of your mind" that Romans 12:2 describes? - Are there beliefs you hold that you've never examined through a biblical lens? 2. Which opposing worldview is most difficult for you to counter with your biblical understanding? ▼ In other words, what non-biblical influences are you around the most? - Naturalism/Materialism: The belief that physical reality is all there is—success is measured by possessions, status, achievements - Hedonism: Life is about maximizing pleasure and minimizing pain - Postmodernism/Relativism: "You do you," "Live your truth," all beliefs are equally valid - Deconstructionism: Progressive questioning of traditional faith that leads to abandonment rather than reconstruction Practical step: Identify one area where you're most influenced by non-biblical thinking. Commit to studying what Scripture says about that topic this week. 3. What "normal things" have you made into idols? ▼ God's prohibition in Eden was about eating—a normal, neutral, necessary activity. He was showing that sin isn't just about "bad" things, but about making anything more important than God. Examine: - Career success and professional recognition - Financial security and possessions - Relationships and approval from others - Comfort, entertainment, or leisure - Health, fitness, or appearance - Your children's success or your family's reputation None of these are inherently bad—but when they become the source of your identity, security, or purpose, they've become idols. 4. How can you "see people as God sees people" this week? ▼ The MTR Challenge: - On your commute, look at 10 people around you - Remember: only about 1 in 10 knows Christ in Hong Kong - Pray for all 10, since you don't know which one is the believer - Start seeing people not just as strangers, colleagues, or annoyances—but as souls who need the love of God At work or home: Who is one person you interact with regularly who doesn't know Christ? How might you reflect God's image to them this week?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Closing Prayer Prompt
chunks.text: "Father, we thank you that we're not trying to earn your approval. We're not trying to outweigh our bad with good. We're not trying to impress you. God, we thank you that life in all of its meaning means we are freed from the need to impress others, to manage our reputations. We are freed because of what you have said about us. You've made us in the imago Dei, the image of God. And so, God, we pray that we would reflect that image to those around us, to a world in need, to the 90%—would you use us to bring this good news to them? We thank you in Jesus' name. Amen."

sermons.title: Finding Purpose in Singleness, Marriage and Parenting - Island ECC

chunks.heading: How can you glorify God in this season of your life? / Biblical Foundation / Singleness / Marriage / Parenting / Church Community / Life Purpose
chunks.text: Whether single, married, or parenting, our purpose remains constant: to glorify God and enjoy Him forever From the Westminster Catechism: "Man's chief end is to glorify God and to enjoy him forever." This foundational truth guides how we live in every season of life. in whatever phase we find ourselves. ⛪ "They are not of the world, just as I am not of the world. Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth." John 17:16-17 🧘‍♂️ Undivided devotion to the Lord with freedom to serve intensely in ministry and mission. 1 Corinthians 7:35 💑 Two becoming one in sacrificial love, modeling Christ's relationship with the church. Ephesians 5:25 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Training up children in the way they should go, discipling the next generation. Proverbs 22:6 🤝 Supporting each life phase through biblical fellowship and mutual encouragement. Hebrews 10:24-25 🎯 Without God, life has no purpose. Without purpose, life has no meaning or hope. Rick Warren

chunks.heading: The Endless Cycle of "What's Next?" / 🔄 Worldview Rewiring
chunks.text: 🤔 World's Questions - When will you find someone? - When's the wedding? - When will you have kids? - When's the next baby? - Where will they go to school? - When will they get married? It never stops... ✨ Christian Response "How can you glorify God in this season of your life?" The question that matters most 🎯 Our Purpose Living on mission with purpose to glorify God In every season Life Phase ❌ World's Focus ✅ Christian Focus Singleness Independence, pleasure, temporary relationships Dependence on God, contentment, lasting community Marriage Happiness, individual autonomy, feelings-based love Holiness, mutual submission, covenant love Parenting Child-centered, success-oriented, flexible morality God-centered, character-oriented, biblical foundation

chunks.heading: 📖 Introduction: Not of This World
chunks.text: Jesus' prayer for believers to be set apart while in the world ▼ "I have given them your word, and the world has hated them because they are not of the world, just as I am not of the world. I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one. They are not of this world, just as I am not of the world. Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world." - John 17:14-18 We are called to be sanctified Set apart, made holy, different from the world around us while still being in the world as a light. This is a process of becoming more like Christ in our thoughts, actions, and character. - set apart, made holy, different - while remaining in this world as a light to others. 💡 Westminster Catechism (1640s): Q: What is the chief end of man? A: Man's chief end is to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever. 📚 Rick Warren's Insight: "Without God, life has no purpose. And without purpose, life has no meaning. Without meaning, life has no significance or hope." - The Purpose-Driven Life (50+ million copies sold)

chunks.heading: 🧘‍♂️ Singleness: Undivided Devotion
chunks.text: Paul's perspective on the gift of singleness ▼ "I want you to be free from anxieties. The unmarried man is anxious about the things of the Lord, how to please the Lord. But the married man is anxious about worldly things, how to please his wife, and his interests are divided... I say this for your own benefit, not to lay any restraint upon you, but to promote good order and to secure your undivided devotion to the Lord." - 1 Corinthians 7:32-35 📝 Pastor Tim's Experience: University Years: A married college pastor with two children once told him, "I wish I could have your life. I'm so busy with my wife and children, I barely have time to read or devote myself to God as much as I want to." Ministry in Hong Kong: Later, as a married father serving in youth ministry, Pastor Tim observed a single team member who could "go everywhere all at once" - checking Facebook to see him in five different places in one day, serving extensively. "You see what I'm talking about? All these phases. We have all these phases to think about, but at that particular time Paul is encouraging this idea of undivided devotion to God." 🚨 Real-Life Challenge: A visiting mother approached Pastor Tim: "My son doesn't go to this church, but he's been living in Hong Kong for months and going a little wild. Please call him and have coffee with him." This illustrates the concern when singleness becomes about exploring independence rather than growing dependence on God. Advantages of Singleness: - Undivided devotion to the Lord - Freedom for intense ministry and mission (like Youth with a Mission opportunities) - Time for deep community building through care groups - Rich worship experiences that can last "all hours of the night" - Opportunity to build spiritual foundation for future phases 🔄 Worldview Rewiring: ❌ World's View: - • Explore independence - • Pursue pleasure - • Temporary relationships - • Dating apps and casual connections ✅ Christian View: - • Grow dependence on God - • Find contentment in Christ - • Build supportive community - • Seek like-minded believers for friendship

chunks.heading: 💑 Marriage: Sacred Union of Two Sinners
chunks.text: More about holiness than happiness ▼ "Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord... Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her... This mystery is profound, and I am saying that it refers to Christ and the church." - Ephesians 5:22-33 💕 The Weather Channel Love Story: Early Dating: "We would talk all night on the phone. Literally all night. Back then we didn't have the internet, so I would watch the Weather Channel just to see when sunrise was. I'd be like, 'okay, sunrise is at 5:45, so we're going to talk until 5:45' just to prove we stayed up all night talking." Now: "I can't even stay awake past ten o'clock. I'm dead tired these days. I'm like, no, you're going to bed. I'm going to bed. We're not doing this." This illustrates the difference between "being in love" (the explosion) and "love" (the engine that runs the marriage). 💭 Key Insights: JC Ryle (Anglican Bishop): "Marriage is, after all, the union of two sinners and not of two angels." Gary Thomas (Sacred Marriage): "What if God designed marriage to make us holy more than to make us happy?" C.S. Lewis (Mere Christianity): "Love, as distinct from being in love, is not merely a feeling. It is a deep unity maintained by the will and deliberately strengthened by habit, reinforced by the grace which both partners ask and receive from God." ⚔️ Iron Sharpens Iron: When two people come together, even the most godly individuals bring their own baggage: cultural backgrounds, family backgrounds, traumas, different ways of thinking and communicating, different genders, all creating distractions in our Christian walk. "Nothing shows you how not perfect you are than marriage. It's a wonderful mirror to prove to you that you are not a perfect human being." Marriage as Ministry: - Mutual submission and sacrificial love - Daily choice to love (not just feeling) - Modeling Christ's love for the church - Serving together as a unified team in ministry - Creating a covenant relationship, not just a contract 🔄 Love vs. Being in Love: Being in love: The euphoric feeling, the explosion that started it all - when you can't think straight, write their name on everything. Love: The engine that runs the marriage - a habit strengthened by grace, choosing to love even when you don't like each other, just as you love yourself even when you don't like yourself.

chunks.heading: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Parenting: Discipling the Next Generation
chunks.text: Training children in the way they should go ▼ ⚖️ The Fifth Commandment: "Honor your father and mother, that your days may be long in the land that the Lord your God has given you." - Exodus 20:12 Pastor Tim humorously notes: "Luckily for us, when our children are born, they come out knowing this... Of course not. Our children are born with original sin just like we are." "Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it." - Proverbs 22:6 The Shema of Israel: "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might. And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way, and when you lie down, and when you rise." - Deuteronomy 6:4-7 💪 Why Parenting is Hard: Notice the word "train" in Proverbs 22:6. It's not just hope that a child will grow in the way they should go. It actually takes effort. It takes work. Why is parenting so hard? Because you're trying so hard. You're training. You're working. It's nonstop. It's every day. Parents as Home Pastors: - Training - Active, intentional effort (not hoping) - Discipling - Children become your primary disciples at home - Modeling - "They're not going to listen to what you say, they're going to listen to what you do" - Teaching Diligently - In house, walking, lying down, rising up - Releasing - Eventually launching them into their own mission 🤳 The Device Challenge: "You tell your kids, get off your devices, stop being on your devices, turn off the computer, turn off the phone, and then you pull out your phone and you're scrolling... We're terrible models in a lot of ways. But we need to remind ourselves that our kids are watching." 🎯 Parenting with Purpose: Create: A loving and safe home environment Disciple: Train them in godly character and biblical foundation Model: Live out your faith authentically before them Focus: God-centered family, character over success, biblical morality

chunks.heading: 🔄 Navigating Life Transitions
chunks.text: How focus changes through each phase ▼ 🎯 Progressive Focus: Singleness: Unfettered access to serve God and the world broadly Marriage: Focus narrows to your relationship with your spouse, but you can still serve as a unit Parenting: Focus narrows further to training children, preparing them for their future service as singles 🌱 Church Community Support: "That's what's the greatest joy of being a part of a church like this, is that we have this kind of support. To have families, to have married couples and to have singles living in the same kind of harmony that we all live in with the same type of focus and the same worldview."

chunks.heading: 🕰️ The Best Way to Spell Love
chunks.text: T-I-M-E Whether in singleness (time with God and community), marriage (time with spouse), or parenting (time with children) - relationships require intentional time and effort. "Relationships take time and effort, and the best way to spell love is time. Just being intentional, being focused, and giving your time and effort in those ways. And when you do that, you will find that your life becomes richer, that you will become happier... as you grow in holiness, as you grow in godliness, you will become more encouraged in life and more fulfilled."

chunks.heading: Jesus' Prayer for Believers / Our Ultimate Purpose
chunks.text: "I have given them your word, and the world has hated them because they are not of the world, just as I am not of the world. I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one. They are not of this world, just as I am not of the world. Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world." John 17:14-18 (NIV) Context: Jesus' high priestly prayer, praying for his disciples and all future believers to be sanctified (set apart) while living in the world as witnesses. "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all for the glory of God." 1 Corinthians 10:31 (NIV) Context: Paul's instruction about living all of life - even the most mundane activities - with the intentional purpose of glorifying God.

chunks.heading: The Gift of Singleness
chunks.text: "Now as a concession, not a command, I say this: I wish that all were as I myself am. But each has his own gift from God, one of one kind and one of another. To the unmarried and the widows I say that it is good for them to remain single, as I am. But if they cannot exercise self-control, they should marry. For it is better to marry than to burn with passion." 1 Corinthians 7:6-9 (NIV) "I want you to be free from anxieties. The unmarried man is anxious about the things of the Lord, how to please the Lord. But the married man is anxious about worldly things, how to please his wife, and his interests are divided." 1 Corinthians 7:32-33 (NIV) Context: Paul was responding to questions about sexual immorality and marriage. He advocates for singleness from his own experience, seeing it as enabling undivided devotion to God.

chunks.heading: Marriage as Sacred Mystery / Honor for Parents
chunks.text: "Wives, submit yourselves to your own husbands as you do to the Lord. For the husband is the head of the wife as Christ is the head of the church, his body, of which he is the Savior... Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her to make her holy, cleansing her by the washing with water through the word... This is a profound mystery—but I am talking about Christ and the church. However, each one of you also must love his wife as he loves himself, and the wife must respect her husband." Ephesians 5:22-25, 32-33 (NIV) Context: Part of Paul's household code, emphasizing mutual submission and sacrificial love as a reflection of Christ's relationship with the church. "Honor your father and your mother, so that you may live long in the land the Lord your God is giving you." Exodus 20:12 (NIV) Context: The Fifth Commandment - the first commandment with a promise attached, emphasizing the importance of family structure and respect.

chunks.heading: Training Children
chunks.text: "Start children off on the way they should go, and even when they are old they will not turn from it." Proverbs 22:6 (NIV) "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength. These commandments that I give you today are to be on your hearts. Impress them on your children. Talk about them when you sit at home and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you get up." Deuteronomy 6:4-7 (NIV) Context: The Shema - Israel's central confession of faith, emphasizing the total devotion to God that should be passed down through diligent teaching to children in all life situations.

chunks.heading: Supporting Each Other
chunks.text: "And let us consider how we may spur one another on toward love and good deeds, not giving up meeting together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging one another—and all the more as you see the Day approaching." Hebrews 10:24-25 (NIV) Context: The importance of Christian community in encouraging believers across all life phases to persevere in faith and good works.

chunks.heading: 🧘‍♂️For Those Who Are Single
chunks.text: 🎯 Focus Areas: - Spiritual Growth: Use this season for deep Bible study, prayer, and spiritual disciplines - Community Building: Invest in meaningful friendships and church relationships - Service & Mission: Take advantage of flexibility to serve in ministry, missions, or community outreach - Skill Development: Grow in areas that will benefit future relationships and ministry 🔄 Practical Steps: - • Join a care group or small group at church (like those at Island ECC) - • Consider short-term mission opportunities (Youth with a Mission, etc.) - • Volunteer in youth ministry or community service - • Find an older believer to mentor you - • Practice contentment in Christ daily - • Build community for finding future godly spouse if desired 💙 Addressing Loneliness: - • Remember that singleness doesn't mean isolation - • Cultivate deep friendships with fellow believers - • Engage in regular fellowship and worship experiences - • Serve others as a way of finding fulfillment

chunks.heading: 💑For Those Who Are Married
chunks.text: 💕 Relationship Priorities: - Daily Choice to Love: Practice love as an act of will, not just feeling - Mutual Submission: Both partners sacrificing for each other's good - Unified Mission: Serve together in church and community - Conflict Resolution: Address issues with grace and biblical principles 🔄 Practical Steps: - • Schedule regular one-on-one time together (not just watching TV) - • Pray together daily - • Find ways to serve as a couple in your church - • Study marriage books together (Sacred Marriage, etc.) - • Practice the 5 love languages intentionally - • Seek counseling when needed - don't wait for crisis - • Create device-free times for real connection ⚡ Navigating Challenges: - • Remember: You married a sinner, not an angel - • When you don't "like" your spouse, choose to love anyway - • Use conflicts as opportunities for iron to sharpen iron - • Focus on changing yourself, not your spouse

chunks.heading: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦For Those Who Are Parents
chunks.text: 👶 Discipleship Focus: - Home Discipleship: Be the primary spiritual influence in your children's lives - Character Training: Focus on godly character over worldly success - Authentic Modeling: Live out your faith consistently before them - Biblical Foundation: Root family values in Scripture 🔄 Practical Steps: - • Have regular family devotions (sit, walk, lie down, rise up) - • Teach children to pray and read Bible age-appropriately - • Discipline with love and biblical principles - • Create family traditions around faith - • Model device management - kids watch what you do - • Serve together as a family in your community - • Remember: They need to see authentic faith, not perfection 📚 Age-Appropriate Discipleship: - • Toddlers: Simple prayers, Bible songs, basic obedience - • Children: Bible stories, character lessons, family devotions - • Teens: Apologetics, real-world applications, mentoring relationships - • Young Adults: Launching them into their own faith journey

chunks.heading: 🔄Navigating Life Transitions
chunks.text: 🚪 From Single to Married - • Prepare for interests to become divided - • Learn to serve as a team, not individuals - • Understand marriage as ministry - • Expect iron-sharpening-iron refinement 👶 From Married to Parents - • Prepare for further narrowing of focus - • See children as your primary disciples - • Balance marriage and parenting priorities - • Train with intention, not just hope

chunks.heading: 🌟Universal Principles for All Seasons
chunks.text: ⏰ Time Investment "The best way to spell love is T-I-M-E." Relationships require intentional time investment, whether with God, spouse, children, or community. Quality time means focused, device-free attention. 🎯 Purpose-Driven Living In every season, ask: "How can I glorify God in this phase of life?" Let this guide your decisions, priorities, and responses to others' expectations. 🤝 Community Support Surround yourself with believers who encourage your faith journey, regardless of your current life phase. The church should support all phases with the same worldview. 📖 Word-Centered Ground all decisions and relationships in biblical truth. Let Scripture shape your worldview, not cultural expectations or family pressure.

chunks.heading: 🪞 Personal Reflection
chunks.text: 1. Current Season Assessment In what specific ways can you glorify God in your current season of life -- whether in singleness, marriage, or parenting? Consider: - What unique opportunities does your current phase offer? - How are you using your time to serve God and others? - What gifts and abilities can you leverage in this season? - How are you practicing contentment vs. longing for the next phase? 2. Worldview Alignment Are there any cultural expectations or personal desires that you need to realign with a Christ-centred perspective? How might doing so impact your relationships and purpose? Examine: - Where do you feel pressure from society's "what's next?" questions? - What dreams or goals might need biblical realignment? - How can you better reflect Christ in your relationships? - What worldly definitions of success do you need to surrender?

chunks.heading: 📋 Phase-Specific Questions
chunks.text: 🧘‍♂️If You're Single: - • How are you using this season to grow spiritually and build your foundation in Christ? - • What ministry opportunities are you pursuing that married people might not have time for? - • Are you building meaningful community relationships or just waiting for marriage? - • How do you practice contentment in Christ when others ask about your relationship status? - • If you desire marriage, how are you preparing yourself to be a godly spouse? - • How do you handle loneliness in healthy, God-honoring ways? 💑If You're Married: - • How are you choosing to love your spouse as an act of will when feelings fade? - • What does mutual submission look like in your daily interactions? - • How are you serving together as a unified team rather than two individuals? - • In what ways does your marriage reflect Christ's love for the church to others? - • What "worldly anxieties" distract you from your primary relationship focus? - • How do you handle conflict in ways that strengthen rather than damage your union? - • Are you pursuing happiness or holiness in your marriage? 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦If You're a Parent: - • How are you actively training (not just hoping) your children in godliness? - • What does it look like to be your children's primary pastor/discipler at home? - • How do you model authentic faith when you're struggling, tired, or imperfect? - • Are you creating a God-centered or child-centered family culture? - • How do you balance character development with academic/worldly success? - • What biblical truths are you teaching "when you sit, walk, lie down, and rise up"? - • How are you preparing to eventually release your children into their own faith journey?

chunks.heading: 🔄 For Those in Transition
chunks.text: 🚪 Entering New Phases: - • How are you grieving what you're leaving behind while embracing what's ahead? - • What lessons from your current phase will serve you in the next? - • How can you maintain your spiritual foundation during life transitions? - • What support do you need from your church community during this change? ⏰ For All Ages: - • How are you investing time (T-I-M-E) in the relationships that matter most? - • Where might you be conforming to worldly timelines instead of God's timing? - • How can you encourage others in different life phases without imposing expectations?

chunks.heading: 🎯 This Week's Action Steps
chunks.text: 💭 Deep Reflection Exercise: Spend 15 minutes in prayer asking God: "How can I better glorify You in my current season?" Write down what comes to mind and choose one specific action to implement this week. Consider: What would "undivided devotion" look like in your current circumstances? 📖 Bible Study Read 1 Corinthians 7 and Ephesians 5:22-33. Journal about how these passages speak to your current life phase. What practical steps do they suggest? 🤝 Community Share with a trusted friend or small group: "How can you pray for me to glorify God better in my current season?" Ask for accountability.

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Points
chunks.text: Thanksgiving: Thank God for the specific season of life He has you in right now, and for the unique opportunities it provides to serve Him. Confession: Ask forgiveness for areas where you've conformed to worldly expectations rather than biblical truth, or where you've been discontent with His timing. Supplication: Ask for wisdom to glorify God well in your relationships and daily choices. Pray for contentment and purpose in your current phase. Intercession: Pray for others in different life phases - singles longing for marriage, couples struggling in their relationship, parents feeling overwhelmed with training their children. Commitment: Ask God to help you love others well through intentional time investment, and to see every season as a gift for His glory.

sermons.title: Made To Worship | Psalm 95 | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎯 Key Theme / 📜 Core Scripture / 🎭 Central Question / 🌉 Spiritual Disciplines: Bridging Belief and Behavior / 🧩 Worship Components / 📋 Sermon Structure / 🎤 Main Sermon Content
chunks.text: Worship as whole-person engagement with God—involving heart (emotions), will (decisions), and mind (truth). Psalm 95 — The textbook for biblical worship, inviting God's people to sing, kneel, and hear His voice. "Are you good at worship?" — Not about vocal talent, but about cultivating worship as a spiritual discipline. Spiritual disciplines bridge the gap between what we believe and how we live. We sing "God, you're my only passion," but our lives don't always match up. The reality is we are all hypocrites to some degree—saying and singing things we don't fully live out. But spiritual disciplines help close that gap. Spiritual Disciplines Include: 📖 Scripture Reading 🙏 Prayer 🎵 Worship 💰 Giving 🍞 Fasting 📝 Memorization 🤝 Hospitality 💬 Evangelism By the grace of God, we institute patterns and habits so there's greater consistency between how we live and what we believe. ❤️ Heart (Emotions) Joy, affection, thanksgiving ✊ Will (Decisions) Bowing, kneeling, choosing 🧠 Mind (Truth) Hearing, reasoning, knowing Section Focus Key Scripture What is Worship? Definition and scope Psalm 95:1-2 Why Should We Worship? Made to worship; identity in Christ 1 Peter 2:9, Psalm 95:3 How to Improve Your Worship Community, Truth, Gospel Rest Psalm 95:6-11, Hebrews 4 🔍 What is Worship? ▼

chunks.heading: Definition of Worship
chunks.text: Worship is the act of ascribing ultimate value to something in such a way that engages your entire being: heart, will, and mind. "The word we have in English for worship comes from the Old English word, which combined worth and shape together." — Pastor Brett This concept rings true: what you worship will shape you. You become like that which you worship. "Come, let us sing for joy to the LORD; let us shout aloud to the Rock of our salvation. Let us come before him with thanksgiving and extol him with music and song." — Psalm 95:1-2 (NIV) 🌱 Worship is Transformative Worship is more than simply giving God what is rightly due Him. It's a transformative thing—as we engage in worship, God uses it to actually change who we are. "There is a beautiful byproduct for us as well. It's not just a duty, it's not just a song. But a yielded life, a worshiping life, is something that God takes and shapes and molds. Transformation occurs." Three Dimensions of Worship ❤️ Heart (Emotions) Worship engages our affections with joy, thanksgiving, and singing. It's dripping with emotion—not just intellectual assent. ✊ Will (Decisions) Worship involves volitional choices: bowing down, kneeling, deciding to set aside baggage and focus on God. "Come, let us bow down in worship, let us kneel before the LORD our Maker." — Psalm 95:6 (NIV) 🧠 Mind (Truth) Worship engages our intellect. It's a reasonable, rational faith that invites scrutiny and fills our minds with scriptural truth. "Today, if only you would hear his voice, 'Do not harden your hearts.'" — Psalm 95:7-8 (NIV) 💡 Key Insight: Wherever You Are, Think About Where You're Not God has wired each of us differently. Some are naturally emotional, others are analytical thinkers, and still others are action-oriented doers. Honor that—but don't hide behind it. "Don't just hide behind what is convenient for you. If you're an emotional person... maybe you need to also engage your mind. Maybe you need to memorize some scripture. If you're a thinking person... God made you to be emotional. Maybe that hasn't been nurtured in your life." — Pastor Brett 🙏 Why Should We Worship? ▼

chunks.heading: You Can't NOT Worship
chunks.text: We are always worshiping something. The issue is: What is the object of your worship? You were made to worship. "But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light." — 1 Peter 2:9 (NIV) ⚠️ Counterfeit Gods Psalm 95:3 says, "For the LORD is the great God, the great King above all gods." Most of us unknowingly give our lives to counterfeit gods—idols that promise fulfillment but never deliver. 🛋️ Comfort The pursuit of ease and financial security as ultimate values. 🎮 Control Structuring life around maintaining power and predictability. ⚡ Power Seeking influence, prestige, and dominance over others. 👥 Acceptance Living for others' approval and validation. 🪞 The Mirror of Erised: What Do You Really Desire? In the first Harry Potter story, there's a Mirror of Erised that shows you your deepest desires. When Harry looked, he saw his deceased parents. When Ron looked, he saw himself as a celebrated sports star. The question for us: If there were such a mirror for your soul, what would you see? What are your deepest longings? - Financial security and comfort? - Control over your circumstances? - Power, influence, and prestige? - Acceptance and approval from others? Most of us structure our lives around something without even realizing it. These become functional gods—counterfeit sources of meaning that promise fulfillment but can't deliver. "Whatever controls us is our Lord. The person who seeks power is controlled by power. The person who seeks acceptance is controlled by acceptance." — Becky Pippert Your ultimate problems are based on misguided worship. If you worship career, prestige, respect, or comfort—it won't yield what it promises. 📈 How to Improve Your Worship ▼

chunks.heading: Three Ways to Deepen Your Worship
chunks.text: 1️⃣ Community Psalm 95 uses plural pronouns throughout: let us sing, let us bow down, let us kneel. Corporate worship is not optional—it's essential. "The more diverse your worshiping community, the more accurately you will be able to comprehend God." — Tim Keller (paraphrased) 🌍 A Personal Example: Worship in Korea Pastor Brett shared about attending a conference in Korea where he was placed at a table with complete strangers from diverse backgrounds: - 🇭🇰 One woman from Hong Kong - 🇮🇳 An Indian man - 🇳🇬🇺🇸 A Nigerian woman living in New York - 🇳🇬🇬🇧 An African living in London - 🌍 Another African still living in Africa The Nigerian woman in New York spoke with profound gratitude about America—the opportunities, freedom, and blessings. Pastor Brett admitted his American perspective was quite different (thinking about school shootings and crime). The Indian man came from a Hindu caste background where hierarchy defined worth. Coming to faith, he realized everyone is made in the image of God—leveling the playing field and shattering racism. "I was blessed. I was changed. I was impacted... You get to see God more holistically, more fully, when you are teachable to other people's experience of who He is." 2️⃣ Truth Worship must be rooted in biblical truth. Don't worship a God of your own making. Throughout Psalm 95, David quotes Scripture about who God is. "In his hand are the depths of the earth, and the mountain peaks belong to him. The sea is his, for he made it, and his hands formed the dry land." — Psalm 95:4-5 (NIV) If your God never disagrees with you, you've made yourself God. Truth says, "I want to know what's right, and I know I'm not always right." 3️⃣ Gospel Sabbath Rest Psalm 95 ends with a warning about rest. Physical rest (Sabbath) is important, but Gospel rest is deeper. "Today, if only you would hear his voice, 'Do not harden your hearts as you did at Meribah... They will never enter my rest.'" — Psalm 95:7-8, 11 (NIV) Gospel Rest means: It is by grace you are saved. There's no amount of effort, pleasing God, or showing up at church that can make Him love you more. He loves you fully and completely as is. When you understand Gospel rest, worship isn't another good deed to earn God's favor. It's an expression of gratitude for all He's already done. You've already been loved and accepted fully in Christ.

chunks.heading: 📖 Bible Verses Referenced / ❤️ For Heart-Oriented People / 🧠 For Head-Oriented People / ✊ For Hands-Oriented People
chunks.text: 📜 Psalm 95 (Full Passage) ▼ 1 Come, let us sing for joy to the LORD; let us shout aloud to the Rock of our salvation. 2 Let us come before him with thanksgiving and extol him with music and song. 3 For the LORD is the great God, the great King above all gods. 4 In his hand are the depths of the earth, and the mountain peaks belong to him. 5 The sea is his, for he made it, and his hands formed the dry land. 6 Come, let us bow down in worship, let us kneel before the LORD our Maker; 7 for he is our God and we are the people of his pasture, the flock under his care. Today, if only you would hear his voice, 8 "Do not harden your hearts as you did at Meribah, as you did that day at Massah in the wilderness, 9 where your ancestors tested me; they tried me, though they had seen what I did. 10 For forty years I was angry with that generation; I said, 'They are a people whose hearts go astray, and they have not known my ways.' 11 So I declared on oath in my anger, 'They shall never enter my rest.'" — Psalm 95:1-11 (NIV) 📖 View Context & Commentary ✨ 1 Peter 2:9 ▼ "But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light." — 1 Peter 2:9 (NIV) Context: Peter describes the new identity of believers—chosen, priestly, holy, belonging to God. The purpose? To declare God's praises. We are made for worship. 🕊️ Hebrews 4 (Gospel Rest) ▼ "Therefore, since the promise of entering his rest still stands, let us be careful that none of you be found to have fallen short of it... There remains, then, a Sabbath-rest for the people of God; for anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his." — Hebrews 4:1, 9-10 (NIV, excerpts) Context: Hebrews 4 references Psalm 95 and expands on the concept of rest—not just physical Sabbath, but entering into the finished work of Christ. Gospel rest means ceasing from our own efforts to earn salvation. Scripture Theme Application Psalm 95:1-2 Heart engagement Worship involves joyful emotions Psalm 95:6-7 Will engagement Worship requires volitional decisions Psalm 95:7-8 Mind engagement Worship is hearing and obeying truth 1 Peter 2:9 Identity & purpose We are made to declare God's praises Hebrews 4 Gospel rest Worship flows from grace, not works You're naturally emotional and moved by worship music. That's a gift! But don't hide behind your strength. Challenge: Engage your mind. Memorize Scripture. Do a Bible study. Learn theological truths about God. You love outlines, diagrams, and theological precision. Excellent! But worship isn't just cognitive. Challenge: Nurture your emotions. God made you emotional—He is emotional. Learn to express feelings in worship and relationships. You're a doer. You make things happen. Great! But don't let busyness replace being. Challenge: Be still and know that He is God. Develop your emotional and intellectual life. Rest in His presence.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Actionable Steps for 2025 / Question 1 / Question 2 / 💬 Discussion Prompt
chunks.text: 1. Download the Island ECC Worship Playlist On Spotify, find the Island ECC worship playlist. Let worship songs become part of your weekly routine—not just a Sunday thing. 🎵 Search for Island ECC on Spotify → 2. Commit to 100% Church Attendance Corporate worship matters. Make it a priority. If you've started 2025 at 100% attendance, keep going! Community is non-negotiable for worship. 3. Identify Your Counterfeit God Honestly ask: What do I really worship? Is it comfort, control, power, or acceptance? Confess it. Bring it to God. Let Him be your ultimate value. "If you're worshiping a career, prestige, respect, or comfort—it's not going to yield for you that which it promises." 4. Rest in Gospel Grace Stop trying to earn God's love. You're already fully accepted in Christ. Let your worship be an overflow of gratitude, not a performance to impress. "For it is by grace you have been saved, through faith—and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God—not by works, so that no one can boast." — Ephesians 2:8-9 (NIV) What is one insight from the message today that stood out to you? Reflection Space: Take time to journal or discuss with your small group. Was it the concept of whole-person worship? The idea that you're always worshiping something? Gospel rest? What is one actionable step you can take to be "better at worship"? Consider these areas: - Will you engage your heart more? Your mind? Your will? - Will you prioritize corporate worship? - Will you ground your worship in biblical truth? - Will you rest in the gospel instead of striving? Share with your small group or a trusted friend: "Are you good at worship?" What does that question stir in you? How might you grow in this spiritual discipline? Remember: The goal isn't perfection, but transformation. As we yield ourselves to God in worship, He shapes us to become more like Christ.

sermons.title: What Are You Made For? | Island ECC Sermon
sermons.subtitle: 12 January 2025 | Island ECC | Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview / Core Message / Key Scripture / Big Idea / Action Step / 🌟 Sermon Highlights
chunks.text: 🎯 Discover your unique purpose through understanding your spiritual gifts, heart, aptitude, personality, and experience (SHAPE). 📖 Ephesians 2:8-10 - Saved by grace to do good works prepared in advance. 💎 You are God's masterpiece, uniquely created for specific good works that bring glory to Him. 🔑 Take the SHAPE assessment to discover your unique calling and align your life accordingly. - ✓ Your purpose is more than just existing—it's about bringing out God's flavors in this world - ✓ Salvation by grace is not the end—it's the beginning of living out your purpose - ✓ God has prepared specific good works for you to walk in - ✓ Your unique SHAPE reveals your calling and helps you serve effectively - ✓ Fan into flame your gifts and help others discover theirs

chunks.heading: 🎤 Main Sermon Content
chunks.text: 🚀 Introduction: What Are You Made For? ▼ Pastor Brett begins by asking a fundamental question that every person asks at some point: "What are you made for?" He illustrates this with a personal story about finding a mysterious object in his drawer—a mic stand attachment—and not knowing its purpose. This mirrors how many of us go through life without understanding our unique purpose. The sermon references ikigai, a Japanese concept of finding your life's purpose at the intersection of what you love, what the world needs, what you can be paid for, and what you're good at. While this framework is helpful, Pastor Brett emphasizes that from a Christian perspective, discovering your purpose goes much deeper. "Let me tell you why you are here. You're here to be salt seasoning that brings out the God flavors of this earth." Matthew 5:13 (MSG paraphrase) ⛪ Foundation: Saved by Grace, Created for Works ▼ Pastor Brett emphasizes the foundational truth of salvation before discussing purpose and calling: "For it is by grace you have been saved, through faith—and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God—not by works, so that no one can boast." Ephesians 2:8-9 (NIV) He stresses that salvation is entirely by grace—not earned through our efforts. When you stand before God and He asks, "Why should I let you into heaven?" your answer is simply: Jesus. Nothing you've done, no church attendance, no good deeds—only Jesus is perfect, and we trust in Him, not ourselves. However, Pastor Brett points out that the Greek text doesn't stop there. The translators added a period, but Paul continues: "For we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do." Ephesians 2:10 (NIV) This verse reveals that while works don't save us, God has prepared specific good works for each believer. You are God's poiema—His masterpiece, His work of art. James reinforces this: "Faith without works is dead" (James 2:26). The assumption is that genuine faith naturally produces good works. 🎯 The SHAPE Framework: Discovering Your Design ▼ Pastor Brett introduces the SHAPE acronym to help believers understand how God has uniquely designed them: 🎁 S - Spiritual Gifts Every believer receives at least one spiritual gift when they come to faith.

chunks.heading: 🎤 Main Sermon Content (pt 2)
chunks.text: These are supernatural abilities given by the Holy Spirit to build up the church and advance God's kingdom. "Each of you should use whatever gift you have received to serve others, as faithful stewards of God's grace in its various forms." 1 Peter 4:10 (NIV) Paul strongly emphasizes: "Now concerning spiritual gifts, brothers, I do not want you to be ignorant." (1 Corinthians 12:1). What a tragedy to be highly educated but ignorant about the greatest gift you'll ever receive! Pastor Brett shares that his spiritual gift is teaching. This doesn't mean he's the best teacher in the world, but he likes it, is competent at it, enjoys it, and feels gifted at it. Importantly, recognizing your gift isn't bragging—God gave it to you. ❤️ H - Heart (Passion) Your heart represents what you care about more than others do. It's a passion or concern that God has placed within you, sometimes for things that aren't explicitly commanded in Scripture. Pastor Brett uses the Apostle Paul as an example. Paul was so consumed with spreading the gospel that he suggested everyone should remain single like him to avoid distractions! Of course, he quickly retreated, acknowledging that "if you cannot control yourself, then they should marry, for it is better to marry than burn with passion" (1 Corinthians 7:9). But his heart's burning passion was clearly church planting and gospel advancement. Pastor Brett confesses: "I think everybody should adopt children." With 153 million orphans worldwide, he secretly believes everyone should adopt—even though he knows God hasn't called everyone to this. Similarly, one church member felt passionate about reducing plastic in the ocean and gave him a reusable water bottle. While Pastor Brett cares about environmental issues, it's not his burning passion. The key indicator: You secretly think everyone should care about your passion as much as you do. What issue makes your heart beat faster? Justice? Human trafficking? The elderly? Prison reform? The environment? Children's education? That's a clue to your heart. 💪 A - Aptitude (Talents) This is simply what you're good at—your natural talents and proficiencies. No false humility here; everyone is good at something. Pastor Brett shares a powerful story of a man who approached him and said: "Pastor Brett, I'm not good at leading Bible studies or teaching and preaching. But you know what I'm good at?

chunks.heading: 🎤 Main Sermon Content (pt 3)
chunks.text: Making money." He wasn't bragging; he recognized his unique ability to close deals and envision business opportunities. He asked Pastor Brett to tell him about needs in the church or ministry opportunities where he could help financially. Later, when Pastor Brett met a young man in mainland China who wanted theological education but couldn't afford it, he connected him with this businessman. The result? This couple funded 100% of a four-year theological degree so this young man could return and serve the church in China more effectively. The worship team is another example: they're good at music. While you may love music, if you're not good at it, that's not where you should serve. Pastor Brett jokes about begging Byron to let him sing: "It's not gonna happen, right?" 👤 P - Personality Your unique wiring matters. Understanding your personality helps you serve in roles that fit who God made you to be, not what drains or frustrates you. Key personality dimensions: - People-oriented vs. Task-oriented: Neither is better; they're just different - Fast-paced vs. Slow-paced: Pastor Brett's wife makes decisions instantly while he needs data and time to compare options - Outgoing vs. Introverted: Do people energize you or drain you? Do you restore by socializing or by reading alone? Pastor Brett and his wife are opposites: she's fast-paced, he's slow. In restaurants, she hands back the menu immediately ("I already know what I want"), while he wants to study all the options. She finishes eating while he's still deciding what to order! But together they make "informed and timely decisions"—her speed balanced by his thoroughness. The church uses personality assessments (Myers-Briggs, DiSC, Enneagram, StrengthsFinder, etc.) for staff to understand who would be good at what tasks, what work environments suit different people, and how to build effective teams. You should know yourself too. 📚 E - Experience Everything God has allowed in your life—successes, failures, disappointments, heartache, trauma—happened under His sovereign hand. Nothing is wasted in God's economy; He wants to redeem it all for good. "The Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our troubles, so that we can comfort those in any trouble with the comfort we ourselves receive from God." 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 (NIV) Notice "comfort" appears four times in this passage.

chunks.heading: 🎤 Main Sermon Content (pt 4)
chunks.text: God comforts you so that you can comfort others with the comfort He provides. This is why Island ECC runs a Divorce Care ministry—led by people who have been divorced. How fitting! God uses your experiences for good. Your experiences make you who you are right now. Perhaps there are others yearning to be seen, to be understood, who just need to know peace—to feel empathy from someone who has been there. Your experiences matter and can bring healing to others. 🦉 Three Wisdom Principles for Your Calling ▼ 1️⃣ God's Commands Are Not Optional There is a general blueprint for all Christians: to become more Christ-like. This means developing compassion, forgiveness, kindness, generosity, and other character qualities. You don't get to say, "I'm not wired to be compassionate." These are God's commands, not suggestions. This framework isn't meant to help you opt out of certain character qualities God wants to develop in all believers. The SHAPE assessment helps you understand your unique calling, but it doesn't excuse you from Christlikeness. 2️⃣ Manage Your Passion with Wisdom Just as Pastor Brett needs to realize not everybody needs to adopt children, you must recognize that your passion shouldn't become others' burden. When you're passionate about something, the tendency is to think everyone should be equally passionate. You can present your passion, care about it, recruit people for it, and inform others—but don't demand it takes front and center in their lives as well. Respect that God has given different people different hearts and callings. 3️⃣ Calling Provides Focus and Boundaries When you know who you are and what you're called to do, it helps you respond appropriately to what you shouldn't do or don't need to do. If you don't love kids, you can cross out children's ministry from your options—the church wants people who love kids serving there. Knowing yourself is freeing. You're supposed to live within your calling, make decisions based on it, refine it, schedule around it, care about it, and investigate ways to be faithful to what you care about. This clarity prevents burnout and enables joyful, effective service. 🔥 Conclusion: Fan Into Flame ▼ As Paul wrote to his young disciple Timothy: "I remind you to fan into flame the gift of God." 2 Timothy 1:6 (NIV) God has gifted you, but sometimes that gift can die down.

chunks.heading: 🎤 Main Sermon Content (pt 5)
chunks.text: You procrastinate, put it off, let good intentions win over good decisions. Paul says: Fan that flame. Let it grow. Make sure it takes preeminence in your life. But also fan into flame the gift of God in other people. When you see people operating in their gifts, encourage them! Pastor Brett shares an example of attending a dinner where the hostess had an incredible gift of hospitality—not just setting a beautiful table, but caring about the people at the table, meeting dietary needs, creating conversation by seating the right people together. He told her: "You're really good at hospitality." This kind of encouragement helps people see their gifts and do more of what God created them for. Your affirmation might be the catalyst someone needs to step into their calling. 📝 Action Steps: - Take the SHAPE assessment (available on the church app) - Ask God to reveal your unique design and calling - Align your schedule and decisions with your SHAPE - Fan into flame your own gifts through intentional practice and development - Encourage others by helping them see and develop their gifts "You are here for something far greater than just your life, but for the message that it sends, for the impact that it has, for the ripple effect that it will render on other people as well." - Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: Ephesians 2:8-10 - Saved by Grace for Good Works ▼ "For it is by grace you have been saved, through faith—and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God— not by works, so that no one can boast. For we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do." Ephesians 2:8-10 (NIV) Context: This is the foundational passage of the sermon. Paul is writing to the church in Ephesus, a predominantly pagan city. He explains the essence of salvation and immediately connects it to purpose. While salvation is entirely by grace through faith (not works), believers are created as God's masterpieces (Greek: poiema) to do good works that God prepared beforehand. Matthew 5:13 - Salt of the Earth ▼ "You are the salt of the earth. But if the salt loses its saltiness, how can it be made salty again? It is no longer good for anything, except to be thrown out and trampled underfoot." Matthew 5:13 (NIV) Eugene Peterson's Paraphrase (The Message): "Let me tell you why you are here. You're here to be salt seasoning that brings out the God flavors of this earth." Context: Jesus is teaching the Sermon on the Mount, explaining to His disciples their purpose and identity. Being "salt" means being a preserving, flavoring influence—bringing out God's character in the world around you. 1 Peter 4:10 - Using Your Spiritual Gifts ▼ "Each of you should use whatever gift you have received to serve others, as faithful stewards of God's grace in its various forms." 1 Peter 4:10 (NIV) Context: Peter is writing to persecuted Christians scattered throughout Asia Minor. He emphasizes that each believer has received a gift and is responsible for using it to serve others. These gifts are expressions of God's grace in different forms. 1 Corinthians 12:1 - Don't Be Ignorant About Gifts ▼ "Now concerning spiritual gifts, brothers, I do not want you to be ignorant." 1 Corinthians 12:1 (NIV) Context: Paul is addressing the Corinthian church's confusion about spiritual gifts. He strongly emphasizes that believers should not be ignorant about this crucial topic. The chapter goes on to explain the diversity of gifts and their purpose in the body of Christ. James 2:26 - Faith Without Works Is Dead ▼ "As the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without deeds is dead." James 2:26 (NIV) Context: James, the brother of Jesus, is writing to Jewish Christians scattered abroad. He addresses the relationship between faith and works—not contradicting Paul's teaching on salvation by grace, but emphasizing that genuine faith naturally produces good works as evidence. 1 Corinthians 7:8-9 - Paul's Heart for Gospel Work ▼ "Now to the unmarried and the widows I say: It is good for them to stay unmarried, as I do. But if they cannot control themselves, they should marry, for it is better to marry than to burn with passion." 1 Corinthians 7:8-9 (NIV) Context: Paul is answering questions about marriage. His personal preference for singleness stems from his consuming passion for gospel ministry—not because marriage is wrong. This illustrates how a person's unique "heart" (passion) can shape their perspective, though Paul wisely acknowledges not everyone shares his calling. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 - God Comforts to Comfort ▼ "Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our troubles, so that we can comfort those in any trouble with the comfort we ourselves receive from God." 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 (NIV) Context: Paul is writing about God's redemptive purposes in suffering. He emphasizes that God comforts us not just for our sake, but so we can comfort others. This passage appears four times with the word "comfort," showing God's cyclical pattern of redemption—using our experiences to minister to others. 2 Timothy 1:6 - Fan Into Flame Your Gift ▼ "For this reason I remind you to fan into flame the gift of God, which is in you through the laying on of my hands." 2 Timothy 1:6 (NIV) Context: Paul is writing his final letter to his spiritual son Timothy, encouraging him to remain faithful in ministry. The metaphor of "fanning into flame" suggests that spiritual gifts can diminish if neglected and need to be actively cultivated and exercised. Matthew 5:16 - Let Your Light Shine ▼ "In the same way, let your light shine before others, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven." Matthew 5:16 (NIV) Context: Continuing the Sermon on the Mount after calling believers "salt" and "light," Jesus emphasizes that good deeds should be visible and observable—not for self-glorification, but so that others will glorify God when they see believers living out their faith.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Understanding Your SHAPE
chunks.text: The SHAPE framework helps you discover how God has uniquely designed you for specific purposes. Each component works together to reveal your calling and guide you toward meaningful, fulfilling service. Component What It Means Key Questions to Ask 🎁 Spiritual Gifts Supernatural abilities given by the Holy Spirit when you believe in Christ • What do I do that feels natural and effective? • Where have I seen God work through me? • What ministry activities energize rather than drain me? ❤️ Heart Passions and concerns God has placed in you; what you care about deeply • What issues make me emotional or angry? • What do I think everyone should care about? • What needs in the world break my heart? 💪 Aptitude Natural talents, skills, and abilities you've developed over time • What am I naturally good at? • What do others frequently compliment me on? • What tasks come easily to me? 👤 Personality Your unique wiring—how God designed you to interact with the world • Am I people-oriented or task-oriented? • Do I make quick decisions or need time to process? • Do people energize or drain me? 📚 Experience Your life journey—successes, failures, pain, and lessons learned • What hardships have I overcome? • How has God comforted me in the past? • What life lessons could help others?

chunks.heading: 📱 Take the Assessment / ⚠️ Important Clarification / Take the Assessment / Pray for Clarity / Seek Feedback / Audit Your Schedule / Experiment / Set Boundaries / Fan Your Flame
chunks.text: The SHAPE assessment is available on the Island ECC app. It takes approximately 30 minutes and will provide you with a detailed profile of how God has uniquely designed you. 📍 To access: Open the app → Navigate to "More" (bottom menu) → Select "SHAPE Assessment" 💡 Common Spiritual Gifts ▼ 🎤 Teaching Ability to explain and apply Scripture clearly 🤝 Service Meeting practical needs and helping behind the scenes 💬 Encouragement Uplifting and motivating others in their faith 💰 Giving Generous stewardship and financial support 🎯 Leadership Guiding and directing others toward goals ❤️ Mercy Compassion for those who are suffering 💡 Wisdom Applying knowledge with godly insight 🏠 Hospitality Creating welcoming environments for connection 🙏 Faith Trusting God for the impossible 🧭 Administration Organizing people and resources effectively This sermon is NOT about volunteer recruitment for church programs. It's about something much bigger: discovering your God-given purpose and aligning your entire life—inside and outside the church—with how He made you. Your calling may be to use your gifts in your workplace, neighborhood, or other spheres of influence. 📝 Download the Island ECC app and complete the SHAPE assessment. Set aside 30 minutes of uninterrupted time to thoughtfully answer the questions. 🙏 Ask God to reveal how He's uniquely designed you. Request wisdom to see patterns in your life that point to your calling and purpose. 👥 Ask trusted friends, family, or mentors: "What do you see as my strengths? What activities do I seem most alive doing?" Others often see gifts we overlook. 📅 Review how you spend your time. Does your schedule reflect your SHAPE? Are you investing in areas aligned with how God made you, or constantly drained by misfit roles? 🔬 Try serving in different capacities. Notice what energizes vs. drains you. What feels like duty vs. delight? Use these experiences to refine your understanding. 🎯 Learn to say "no" to opportunities that don't align with your SHAPE, even if they're good. This creates space for God's best—what you're uniquely made for. 🔥 Intentionally develop your gifts. Take classes, find mentors, practice, and grow in areas where God has equipped you. Don't let your gifts atrophy through neglect. 💪

chunks.heading: Encourage Others
chunks.text: When you see someone operating in their gifts, tell them! Your affirmation might be the catalyst they need to step fully into their calling. 📖 Real-Life Examples from the Sermon ▼ 💼 The Businessman's Gift A church member approached Pastor Brett saying: "I'm not good at teaching or leading Bible studies, but I'm good at making money." He asked to be informed about financial needs so he could help. When Pastor Brett later mentioned a young man in China who needed funding for theological education, this businessman and his wife funded 100% of a four-year degree. Result: A minister equipped to serve the church in China more effectively. Lesson: Your aptitude (what you're good at) combined with your heart (what you care about) can create kingdom impact in unexpected ways—often outside traditional "church" ministry. 🏠 The Hospitality Expert Pastor Brett attended a dinner where the hostess demonstrated exceptional hospitality—not through elaborate decorations or expensive food, but by genuinely caring about the people present. She ensured dietary needs were met, facilitated connections between guests, and created an atmosphere where everyone felt valued. Lesson: Spiritual gifts manifest in everyday settings. Hospitality isn't just about entertaining—it's about using your gift to create spaces where people encounter God's love through genuine care. 🌊 The Environmental Advocate A woman gave Pastor Brett a reusable water bottle, passionate about reducing plastic waste in the ocean. While Pastor Brett cares about the environment, it's not his burning passion—but it clearly was hers. Lesson: Your heart (passion) will be unique to you. God places different concerns in different people's hearts. Don't judge or compare—celebrate that God cares about various needs through His diverse people.

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 1. What is your current understanding of your life's purpose? ▼ Take time to journal your honest answer. Have you been living with clarity about why you're here, or have you been drifting through a cycle of duty and relief? How does your current life align (or not align) with God's purposes for you? Dig Deeper: If someone asked you, "Why are you here on this planet?" what would you say? How much of your answer reflects God's purposes versus cultural expectations or personal ambitions? 2. What activities make you feel most alive and aligned with God's purposes? ▼ Think about times when you've felt deeply fulfilled—not just happy, but purposeful. What were you doing? Who were you with? What needs were you meeting? These moments often reveal clues to your SHAPE. Practical Exercise: List five times in the past year when you felt energized and purposeful. Look for common threads—these patterns reveal how God has wired you. 3. What issue or need in the world breaks your heart or stirs your passion? ▼ This question addresses the "H" in SHAPE—your heart. What injustice makes you angry? What suffering moves you to tears? What need do you secretly wish everyone cared about as much as you do? This passion is God-given and points toward your calling. Consider: Pastor Brett cares about orphans and adoption. Someone else cares about plastic in the ocean. Another person cares about prison reform. What's your thing? Don't dismiss it as insignificant—God placed it in your heart for a reason. 4. How has God used your past experiences (including pain) to shape who you are today? ▼ Reflect on the "E" in SHAPE—your experiences. God wastes nothing. Even hardships, failures, and trauma can be redeemed to comfort others who face similar struggles. How has God comforted you in ways that uniquely equip you to minister to others? Scripture Reminder: "God comforts us in all our troubles, so that we can comfort those in any trouble with the comfort we ourselves receive from God" (2 Corinthians 1:4). What comfort have you received that you can now share? 5. Are you currently serving in roles that drain you or energize you? Why? ▼ Jesus said, "My yoke is easy and my burden is light" (Matthew 11:30). If you're constantly exhausted and resentful in your service, you might be in a misfit role. Conversely, when you serve according to your SHAPE, it should feel life-giving, even when it's challenging. Self-Check: Make two lists—"What drains me" and "What energizes me." Be honest. Then prayerfully consider: How can you move toward more of what energizes you and less of what drains you? 6. Who in your life needs encouragement to discover or develop their gifts? ▼ Part of living out your purpose is helping others discover theirs. Think of someone you know who has a gift they may not recognize. How can you "fan into flame" their gift through specific, encouraging words? Action Challenge: This week, identify one person and write them a note or have a conversation specifically affirming a gift you see in them. Be specific about what you observe and how it impacts others. 7. What would change if you fully embraced that you are God's masterpiece? ▼ Ephesians 2:10 says you are God's poiema—His masterpiece, His work of art. How would your life look different if you truly believed this? What insecurities would lose their power? What risks might you take? What opportunities might you pursue? Meditate: You are not an accident. You are not average. You are not inadequate. You are God's unique creation, designed with intention for specific purposes. Let this truth sink deep into your identity. 8. What is one concrete step you will take this week to align your life with your SHAPE? ▼ Knowledge without action is incomplete. After reflecting on these questions and the sermon content, commit to one specific, measurable action this week. It could be taking the SHAPE assessment, having a conversation, saying "yes" to something aligned with your gifts, or saying "no" to something that isn't. Make It Specific: Don't just say "I'll think about it more." Write down exactly what you'll do, when you'll do it, and how you'll know you've completed it. Share your commitment with someone who can help keep you accountable.

sermons.title: People of God: But First, Look Inside | Island ECC
