Jesus said to him, "Shall I come and heal him?" The centurion replied, "Lord, I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. But just say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, 'Go,' and he goes; and that one, 'Come,' and he comes. I say to my servant, 'Do this,' and he does it." When Jesus heard this, he was amazed and said to those following him, "Truly I tell you, I have not found anyone in Israel with such great faith." ... Then Jesus said to the centurion, "Go! Let it be done just as you believed it would." And his servant was healed at that moment. Mark 6:1-6 (NIV) Jesus left there and went to his hometown, accompanied by his disciples. When the Sabbath came, he began to teach in the synagogue, and many who heard him were amazed. "Where did this man get these things?" they asked. "What's this wisdom that has been given him? What are these remarkable miracles he is performing? Isn't this the carpenter? Isn't this Mary's son and the brother of James, Joseph, Judas and Simon? Aren't his sisters here with us?" And they took offense at him. Jesus said to them, "A prophet is not without honor except in his own town, among his relatives and in his own home." He could not do any miracles there, except lay his hands on a few sick people and heal them. He was amazed at their lack of faith. John 14:1 (NIV) "Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me." Colossians 2:17 (NIV) These are a shadow of the things that were to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ. Philippians 2:10-11 (NIV) At the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father. "Increase my faith. Maybe this should be our prayer every day. God, give me spiritual eyes. Help me to see people as you see them." 🙏 Make "Increase my faith" your daily prayer. It acknowledges that spiritual growth is God's work in you, not your own achievement. It's a prayer of humility that says, "God, the distance between us is my fault. Expand my capacity to believe in You." 👁️ Ask God to help you see people as He sees them—as sheep without a shepherd rather than annoying obstacles on the MTR. Let compassion replace frustration. This shift in perspective is evidence of increasing faith. 💝 Be quick to forgive, quick to listen, eager to empathize, and slow to keep score or seek revenge. Not because offenders deserve it, but because your soul needs it. Unforgiveness erodes spiritual vitality. ⚓ When fear enters—financial instability, relational tension, uncertainty—remember your trust is in Jesus, not in stable circumstances. Faith isn't about decreasing fears; it's about increasing belief in the One who remains constant. 🚫 Examine your spiritual life honestly. Have you settled for routine religion? Limited God to certain areas? Made faith predictable and safe? The reality is found in Christ—intimate, organic, changing, adventurous, never merely routine. 🏛️ Whether it's a church building transition, job change, or life circumstance, don't just focus on accomplishing goals. Ask how God wants to transform you spiritually through the process. Increased faith is the real goal.

chunks.heading: 🌟 This Week's Challenge / Personal Reflection
chunks.text: Pray "Increase my faith" every morning this week. Journal one specific area where you recognize you've limited God or settled for shadow faith. Then ask Him to expand your capacity to believe in that area. Reflection Questions: - • Where do I most need increased faith right now? - • What "rooms" of my life have I kept God out of? - • Is my faith in circumstances or in Christ? - • Am I harboring unforgiveness that's eroding my spiritual vitality? "Faith is the beginning point of our journey with God. But it doesn't stop there. You are to live by faith so that you do not gratify the desires of the flesh."

chunks.heading: 🤔 Deep Dive Questions
chunks.text: 1. Identifying Your Faith Triggers Pastor Brett listed several situations that make us cry out "Increase my faith": big decisions, loss of control, fearful circumstances, and personal sacrifice. Which of these most resonates with where you are right now? What specific situation is exposing the limits of your faith? 2. The Forgiveness Test Jesus's command to forgive "seven times in a day" reveals how unforgiveness erodes our souls. He's less concerned about whether the offender is sincere than about the health of your heart. Is there someone you're withholding forgiveness from? How might harboring that unforgiveness be affecting your spiritual vitality and relationship with God? 3. Faith vs. Optimism The sermon emphasized that faith is not the same as optimism or positive thinking. Faith has an object—Jesus Christ. It's not about manifesting preferred outcomes but about trusting in the character of God. Have you been treating faith like optimism—hoping for good circumstances rather than trusting in Jesus regardless of circumstances? What would shift if you anchored your faith in Christ's character instead of your desired outcomes? 4. Shadow Faith Audit Using the image of Paul's dog barking at shadows, Pastor Brett warned against settling for "shadow faith"—a limited, predictable, routine version that looks real but lacks substance. The reality is found in Christ. In what areas of your spiritual life have you settled for going through religious motions rather than experiencing intimate relationship with Jesus? Where is your faith limited, predictable, or merely routine? 5. The Centurion's Understanding The Roman centurion amazed Jesus because he understood authority. He recognized that Jesus spoke with divine authority, representing God the Father, and that His words carried power to heal from a distance. How well do you understand Jesus's authority in your life? In what areas do you intellectually acknowledge His authority but practically live as if you're in control?

chunks.heading: ✍️ Journaling Prompts
chunks.text: Complete this prayer: "God, increase my faith in the area of _____________ because I recognize that I've been _____________." Identify gaps: "I find it easy to trust God with _____________, but I struggle to trust Him with _____________." The surrender question: "If God asked me to _____________, I would find it difficult because _____________. What does this reveal about where I need increased faith?"

chunks.heading: 🎯 Moving Forward
chunks.text: Pastor Brett closed with a powerful prayer that can become our own: "Father, we do pray that you would increase our faith. However mature or knowledgeable, educated we may be, God, we know that there are gaps. We know that there are deficiencies and they're ours, not yours. And so God, increase our faith, our ability to comprehend who you are, our ability to live our lives commensurate with that deep conviction of your character. God, to see spiritually as you see, to look upon the masses and to see them as sheep without a shepherd and to have compassion, not frustration. So God, increase our faith. Wherever you deem it needed, we welcome you in those places." This week, carry this prayer with you. Let it become as natural as breathing. And watch how God responds when His people humbly ask for what He's already eager to give: faith that sees Him clearly, trusts Him deeply, and follows Him completely.

sermons.title: Breaking Ground: Building People | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 📖 Message Overview / 🌟 Key Theme / 💡 Central Truth / 🏗️ Bold Faith Update / 1. The Gospel Is Really Good News / 2. The Woman at the Well: A Paradigm for Evangelism / 3. We Are Christ's Ambassadors / 4. The Church: God's Chosen Instrument
chunks.text: In this powerful message, Pastor Brett launches the "Breaking Ground" series as Island ECC prepares for their new location at Sunbeam Theater. Using the profound encounter between Jesus and the Samaritan woman at the well (John 4), he illustrates how the gospel transforms lives from darkness to light—and how we, as ambassadors of Christ, are called to share this life-changing message strategically, respectfully, creatively, and radically. The gospel is really good news that fundamentally changes people, families, workplaces, and communities. We are called to be conduits of this good news, initiating strategically and loving radically. "You were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord" (Ephesians 5:8). This radical transfer from darkness to light is prescriptive for every believer and fuels our mission. After 10 years of the Bold Faith campaign, Island ECC is breaking ground on the Sunbeam Theater renovation in March 2025, with an estimated move-in date of Q4 2027. Goal for 2025: raise $150 million. Not just a ticket to heaven, but a transformative power that changes hearts, relationships, and daily life. It brings value, purpose, freedom, hope, and forgiveness—shifting everything from darkness to light. Jesus' encounter with the Samaritan woman demonstrates how to bring good news to a lost world: initiating strategically, answering respectfully, teaching creatively, and loving radically. As official representatives with God's authority and message, we leverage everything we have to declare and demonstrate the gospel, ensuring other stories can be told. While God uses many means, the church is His primary instrument for change—personal, cultural, and global. Island ECC's new location represents a strategic opportunity to be a beacon in North Point and beyond.

chunks.heading: The Gospel Changes Everything
chunks.text: Pastor Brett begins by establishing a foundational truth: the gospel is good news—really good news. It's not merely about securing a heavenly destination; it's about God fundamentally changing who you are right now. "The gospel says that your relationships are going to get better. As you understand how God loves you, your capacity to love others is also going to expand. Everything gets better with the gospel." Paul's words in Ephesians 5:8 frame this transformation simply yet powerfully: "You were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord." This isn't just imagery—it's reality. Darkness can look like insecurity, confusion, enslavement to habits, purposelessness, or inner shame. Light brings value, freedom, energy, understanding, hope, and forgiveness.

chunks.heading: 🌊 The Woman at the Well: A Master Class in Evangelism / Initiated Strategically / Answered Respectfully / Taught Creatively / Loved Radically
chunks.text: Pastor Brett unpacks John 4, where Jesus meets a Samaritan woman at Jacob's well at noon—a divine appointment orchestrated by God himself. Despite deep cultural antagonism between Jews and Samaritans, Jesus deliberately goes through Samaria. What She Did: - Stereotyped: "You're a Jew and I'm a Samaritan woman. How can you ask me for a drink?" - Questioned: "Sir, you have nothing to draw with, and the well is deep. Where can you get this living water?" - Compared: "Are you greater than our father Jacob?" - Deflected: When Jesus revealed everything about her, she shifted to theology: "Where should we worship—this mountain or Jerusalem?" Her defenses were up. She had been hurt by men, rejected by society, and was carrying deep shame from five failed marriages and a current illicit relationship. Yet Jesus met her with something radically different. What He Did: Jesus purposefully placed himself at that well at that time. He created the divine appointment, orchestrating circumstances to reach one woman who desperately needed hope. "If you knew the gift of God and who it is that asks you for a drink, you would have asked him and he would have given you living water." He heard her questions without condescension. Using water—the one thing they had in common—Jesus bridged the gap between cosmic truth and everyday life. "Everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst." Despite knowing her entire sordid history, Jesus showed no judgment, no agenda, no exploitation. He offered honor, dignity, and transformative love.

chunks.heading: 🔄 Her Transformation
chunks.text: The text tells us something extraordinary: "Then, leaving her water jar, the woman went back to the town..." She came to the well at noon to avoid people because of her shame. She came for water. But after encountering Jesus, everything changed: "She left the jar—it's no longer important. It's no longer her mission. She came at noon to avoid the women going as a group because she's an outcast. She doesn't have friends. She avoids people because her shame is great. She tries to be small. And then Jesus leaves her feeling big." She went straight to the very people she had been avoiding and proclaimed: "Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Could this be the Messiah?" The very things she was hiding became her testimony. Her priorities completely shifted.

chunks.heading: 🚀 Our Mission as Ambassadors
chunks.text: Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:20: "We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us." We are official representatives with God's authority and message. Someone prayed for you, invited you, told you about Christ, sent you to a Christian school. You are here because of a chain of faithful witnesses. Now it's your turn to continue that chain. "You and I are here because somebody came before us. Somebody prayed for you. Somebody invited you... All under the beautiful tapestry of what God has woven together to be your story. But it doesn't end there. Because there's so many other stories yet to be told, yet to be written. And you and I get to play a part in that."

chunks.heading: 🏗️ Breaking Ground: The Bold Faith Vision
chunks.text: After 10 years of the Bold Faith campaign (launched in 2015), Island ECC is now at a historic inflection point. In January 2024, the church purchased the Sunbeam Theater for $750 million, putting down $300 million and securing a $450 million loan. The Timeline: - March 2025: Breaking ground / construction begins - Q4 2027: Estimated move-in date - 2025 Goal: Raise $150 million ($40M for mortgage, rest for revitalization) The facility includes over 100,000 square feet: the theater itself and the Kiu Fai Mansion (ground to 6th floor along King's Road), which will house Kids Club, Youth Ministry, Filipino Ministry, and more. Three floors (3-5) have been vacant for over 20 years—an unprecedented opportunity in Hong Kong. "We have a permanent location with signage outside and really be a true beacon in North Point and beyond... to say what do we want to do for God in the city."

chunks.heading: 📊 Where We Stand
chunks.text: As of February 21, 2025: - 1,981 people have given to Bold Faith - ~$1 million per month coming in from pledges and contributions - Gifts ranging from a missionary's $100 bill to checks with "so many zeros—10 million plus" "It was more than $100. The monetary amount was not what God was seeing, but a transfer of affection and faith... I want to be a part of what God is doing."

chunks.heading: 🙏 The Call / ✝️ Communion: Broken and Grateful
chunks.text: Pastor Brett's plea is simple and sincere: Pray. Ask God what you can do to be part of this historic moment. Whether you're fulfilling an existing pledge, making a new one, or contributing for the first time—this is about privilege and investment, not guilt or manipulation. "The gospel is really good news. It's so good that God has allowed us to believe it. And God asks that many others would have the same chance. Everyone deserves a chance to hear it and to be changed by it." The service concluded with communion, Pastor Brett noting the two shortest, most succinct verbs describing us: We are broken, and yet we are grateful. Christ was broken on our behalf so that we, though broken, could be forgiven, covered, and transformed.

chunks.heading: 🎬 Tim and Carrie Chen's Story
chunks.text: Before the sermon, the church shared the story of Tim and Carrie Chen, who have been with Island ECC since before it moved from Kowloon to Hong Kong Island 25 years ago. Their three sons—Joshua, and two others—were all born and raised at Island ECC. Key reflections from their video testimony: - From dropping off baby Joshua with a car seat, pacifier, and bottle to watching him grow in faith - Benefiting from resources at every stage: Kids Club materials for parents, youth ministry as kids questioned faith, and the preteen ministry created specifically for grades 5-6 - Moving from nomadic Sunday services (different locations each week) to a permanent space at 633 King's Road, which allowed real programming and community - Hoping the new Sunbeam location will provide even more space to serve the city, run events, and be "a true beacon" in North Point "The church even thought about: Okay, sixth graders a little bit young for youth. They don't quite fit in, but they're a little bit too old for Sunday school. They're getting a little bit bored. So the church created a preteen ministry for grades five to six... The fact that the church thinks about every segment of our community and thinks about ways to meet their needs is so incredible."

chunks.heading: 📜 Key Bible Verses
chunks.text: "You were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord." — Ephesians 5:8 (NIV) "The law and the prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the good news of the kingdom of God is being preached, and everyone is forcing their way into it." — Luke 16:16 (NIV) "We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us. We implore you on Christ's behalf: Be reconciled to God." — 2 Corinthians 5:20 (NIV)

chunks.heading: John 4: The Woman at the Well (Selected Portions) / 🎯 Initiate Strategically / 🤝 Answer Respectfully / 🎨 Teach Creatively / ❤️ Love Radically
chunks.text: "When a Samaritan woman came to draw water, Jesus said to her, 'Will you give me a drink?' (His disciples had gone into the town to buy food.) The Samaritan woman said to him, 'You are a Jew and I am a Samaritan woman. How can you ask me for a drink?' (For Jews do not associate with Samaritans.)" — John 4:7-9 (NIV) "Jesus answered her, 'If you knew the gift of God and who it is that asks you for a drink, you would have asked him and he would have given you living water.' 'Sir,' the woman said, 'you have nothing to draw with and the well is deep. Where can you get this living water?'" — John 4:10-11 (NIV) "Jesus answered, 'Everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life.'" — John 4:13-14 (NIV) "He told her, 'Go, call your husband and come back.' 'I have no husband,' she replied. Jesus said to her, 'You are right when you say you have no husband. The fact is, you have had five husbands, and the man you now have is not your husband. What you have just said is quite true.'" — John 4:16-18 (NIV) "'Sir,' the woman said, 'I can see that you are a prophet. Our ancestors worshiped on this mountain, but you Jews claim that the place where we must worship is in Jerusalem.'" — John 4:19-20 (NIV) "Then, leaving her water jar, the woman went back to the town and said to the people, 'Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Could this be the Messiah?'" — John 4:28-29 (NIV) "For two years now he [Paul] welcomed all who came to see him. He proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ—with all boldness and without hindrance!" — Acts 28:30-31 (NIV, paraphrased) "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing." — John 15:5 (NIV) What it means: Be intentional about reaching people who don't know Christ. Take risks. Go where they are, not just where it's comfortable. Action steps: - Pray for specific people by name who don't know Jesus - Create opportunities to connect (invite to coffee, events, Alpha) - Don't wait for the "perfect moment"—step out in faith What it means: Welcome questions, doubts, and criticism. Never push aside those who disagree. Action steps: - Listen more than you speak - Acknowledge legitimate questions without being defensive - Say "I don't know" when you don't—then research together - Invite skeptics to Alpha where there are "no wrong questions" What it means: Find bridges between cosmic truth and everyday life. Make God's word relevant and received. Action steps: - Use stories, metaphors, and everyday experiences - Connect faith to their actual struggles and questions - Don't default to "Christianese"—translate for your audience What it means: Show honor and dignity to everyone—rich, poor, single, married, regardless of background or sin. Action steps: - Serve without agenda or expectation - Welcome the outcast, the messy, the broken - Let your love be the first thing people notice, not your judgment

chunks.heading: 💭 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 1. From Darkness to Light: What specific areas of your life looked like "darkness" before Christ? What does "light" look like in those areas now? 2. Your Water Jar: What "water jar" might God be asking you to leave behind? What old priority, habit, or comfort zone needs to be set aside so you can fully embrace His mission? 3. Your Story: Who in your life doesn't yet know Jesus? How can you strategically, respectfully, creatively, and radically share the gospel with them? 4. Bold Faith: How is God calling you to participate in Island ECC's Bold Faith vision? Is it through financial giving, prayer, volunteering, or inviting others?

chunks.heading: Pray and Act / 📚 Additional Resources / Pre-2000: Kowloon Roots
chunks.text: - Pray daily for one specific person who doesn't know Jesus. Ask God to give you a strategic opportunity to share. - Identify your "well." Where do you naturally intersect with non-Christians? (Work, gym, hobby, neighborhood?) Be intentional there. - Practice radical love. This week, honor someone society deems "less than." Show them the dignity Jesus showed the Samaritan woman. - Seek God about Bold Faith. Ask Him specifically what your role is in Island ECC's next chapter. Write down what you sense and be ready to act. - Alpha Course: Invite skeptical friends to explore faith in a no-pressure environment - Island Bible Seminar (Today): Caring for cancer patients—practical Christ-centered support - Marriage Weekend (Coming Soon): Strengthen your marriage as a platform for gospel witness - Gather25 (Global Broadcast): Join the global church in prayer and unity Island ECC begins in Kowloon. Tim and Carrie Chen are among the original members worshiping on the mainland side of Hong Kong.

chunks.heading: ~2000: The Move to Hong Kong Island
chunks.text: A group of believers crosses the harbor to plant what becomes Island ECC. The church enters a nomadic phase—meeting in different locations each week, requiring constant setup and teardown. "Every week it was a newspaper ad or an email that told us where we would go. That made it really difficult for the church to offer any real programming... It was near impossible." — Tim Chen

chunks.heading: Move to 633 King's Road
chunks.text: Island ECC secures a permanent facility, floor by floor. Each new floor opens up new ministry opportunities—Kids Club, Youth, Tweens, Filipino Ministry, and more. "Even moving to six-three-three, it was a permanent space in the sense that we didn't have to set up and take down... And that already gave the church the ability to provide a space for the community." — Tim Chen

chunks.heading: 2015: Bold Faith Campaign Launches / January 2024: Sunbeam Theater Purchased / 2024-2025: Transition Year / March 2025: Breaking Ground / Q4 2027: Estimated Move-In / Initial Purchase (Jan 2024) / 2025 Goal / Current Giving / The Vision
chunks.text: Pastor Brett preaches the first "Trailblazer" sermon. The vision: save collectively so that one day, if kicked out or when the opportunity arises, Island ECC could secure a permanent location. "2015. That's a long time. And when we started it, it was a dream. Just an idea... I never would have imagined it would take this long. Ten years." — Pastor Brett Island ECC purchases the iconic Sunbeam Theater on King's Road, North Point for $750 million. The down payment of $300 million comes from a decade of faithful giving. A $450 million loan covers the balance. Existing tenants continue leasing portions of the facility. The church services the mortgage (~$40M/year) while finalizing revitalization plans. Over 1,981 people have contributed, with ~$1M/month coming in from pledges. Construction begins on the 100,000+ square foot facility, including the theater and Kiu Fai Mansion (ground-6th floor). Three floors (3-5) have been vacant for 20+ years. Island ECC anticipates moving into the fully renovated Sunbeam Theater—a permanent, visible beacon in North Point where the church can "proclaim with boldness and without hindrance." $750 million total - $300M down payment (from 10 years of savings) - $450M loan $150 million to raise - $40M: Service existing mortgage - $110M+: Revitalization costs (final figure TBD) 1,981 contributors - ~$1M/month from pledges - Gifts range from $100 (missionary) to $10M+ A Permanent Beacon - Open, welcoming corner atrium - Signage visible to all of North Point - Space for every ministry, every age

chunks.heading: 🏠 What the Sunbeam Means
chunks.text: "People know that there's a church there [at 633 King's Road], even though there's no signage on the outside... How much more amazing would it be that we have a permanent location with signage outside and really be a true beacon in North Point and beyond." — Tim Chen The Sunbeam Theater isn't just a building—it's a strategic platform for the gospel in one of Hong Kong's most densely populated areas. It represents: - Visibility: A public declaration that the church is open, welcoming, accessible - Longevity: A permanent home for generations to come - Hospitality: Space to invite, serve, and love the community - Programming: Room for Alpha, marriage weekends, cancer care seminars, kids/youth ministries, and more - Legacy: Building on 25+ years of faithfulness to impact the next 25+ years

chunks.heading: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Stories Like Tim and Carrie's
chunks.text: The Chen family embodies what Island ECC has always been about: building people. Their three sons grew up in the church, benefiting from: - Nursery care from one month old - Kids Club with age-specific parenting resources - Preteen ministry tailored for grades 5-6 - Youth ministry with adults walking alongside teenagers - A community of families with shared values "The fact that the church thinks about every segment of our community or congregation and thinks about ways to meet their needs is so incredible, and we're so thankful for that." — Carrie Chen The Sunbeam Theater will allow Island ECC to tell thousands more stories like the Chens'—stories of people encountering Jesus, being transformed, and raising the next generation to know and love God.

chunks.heading: 🎯 The Call to This Generation
chunks.text: Just as previous generations faithfully gave to build 633 King's Road, and before that crossed the harbor to plant Island ECC, this generation is called to step up for the Sunbeam. "You and I are here because somebody came before us... But it doesn't end there. Because there's so many other stories yet to be told, yet to be written. And you and I get to play a part in that." — Pastor Brett Next steps: - Pray and ask God what He's calling you to give - Attend next Sunday's service for detailed revitalization plans - Make or renew your pledge to Bold Faith - Invite others to be part of this historic moment

sermons.title: Breaking Ground: Building the City | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎯Sermon Overview / Key Themes / 🎵Worship Songs
chunks.text: In this powerful message, Pastor Brett casts vision for Island ECC's future while grounding us in the timeless truth that our generosity flows from understanding that everything belongs to God. 🏗️ Breaking Ground The physical project of moving to Sunbeam Theater represents a spiritual opportunity to expand kingdom impact in Hong Kong. 💝 Generous Stewardship Following David's example, we're called to be grateful managers of what God has entrusted to us, not owners. 🌃 City Impact Hong Kong's shiny exterior hides deep struggles. The gospel is the answer to poverty, anxiety, isolation, and despair. ⚖️ Perspective Shift "Everything in heaven and earth is yours." Recognizing God's ownership transforms how we give. "Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hands." — King David (1 Chronicles 29:14) - 🌱 Graves Into Gardens - 🙏 I Speak Jesus - ✨ Jireh - 🕊️ Living Hope

chunks.heading: Hong Kong: The Greatest City in the World
chunks.text: Pastor Brett opens with affection for Hong Kong—42 beaches, 564 skyscrapers, incredible food, Victoria Peak, and an iconic skyline (number 7 on the world list, though he thinks we should be #1!). It's vibrant, energetic, and exciting. But beneath the surface lie deep needs: 23% live below the poverty line, one in seven struggle with mental health, Hong Kong has the 7th highest divorce rate globally, and teen suicide rates reached an all-time high last year.

chunks.heading: The Task is Huge, But the Gospel is the Answer
chunks.text: "The answer to all of it is the gospel. I'm not being simplistic... The gospel brings hope, defines purpose and ambition, brings meaning into relationships, teaches us how to reconcile and forgive." Island ECC is positioned to offer more than band-aid solutions. Through tutoring, poverty relief, and community programs, we want to bring holistic transformation that says: You are made in the image of God.

chunks.heading: David's Story: The Unlikely King
chunks.text: Samuel was sent by God to anoint the next king from Jesse's family. Seven sons passed by—strong, capable, good-looking—but God said "no" to each. Finally, the youngest, David, tending sheep and not even invited to the tryout, was chosen. This moment defined David's perspective for life: God is the one who chooses. God is the one who provides. God is the source of everything.

chunks.heading: David's Perspective on Generosity / Not Generous Givers, But Grateful Managers
chunks.text: Fast forward to David in his mid-50s. He's king, he's established peace, and now he wants to build a temple for God. God says, "No, your son Solomon will build it—but you can prepare." David responds with remarkable humility: - He gives from the government's resources - He gives from his personal treasures - He challenges the leaders to give - The people rejoice and give willingly "Everything in heaven and earth is yours... Who am I, and who are my people, that we should be able to give as generously as this? Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hands." — David's prayer (1 Chronicles 29:11-14) The shift: We don't own and give. We are entrusted and manage. Everything we have—our lives, schedules, careers, bodies, bank accounts—belongs to God. We are stewards. "To whom much is given, much is required."

chunks.heading: The Vision: $150 Million in 12 Months
chunks.text: Island ECC is launching a generosity campaign with a goal of $150 million over 12 months: - $40 million to service the mortgage - $110 million for the first phase of building revitalization This requires unequal gifts but equal sacrifice. Not everyone gives the same amount, but everyone prayerfully considers what God is calling them to contribute. "Pray about it. It's between you and God. If you write zero, we'll respect that. Anything in between—pray."

chunks.heading: The Call: Leveraging Collective Generosity
chunks.text: "When we leverage our collective generosity, we maximize our kingdom impact. We can pool our resources and accomplish far more efficiently and effectively things that a smaller church simply couldn't do." This is about more than a building. It's about breaking through generational darkness, spiritual apathy, confusion, and despair with the hope of the gospel. "Don't make this weird. Money can get weird, and throw church into the mix and it can get new levels of weird. But what we're doing is collective generosity for maximum kingdom impact. That's not weird." — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: 1 Samuel 16:1, 10-12 (NIV)
chunks.text: "The Lord said to Samuel, 'How long will you mourn for Saul, since I have rejected him as king over Israel? Fill your horn with oil and be on your way; I am sending you to Jesse of Bethlehem. I have chosen one of his sons to be king.'... Jesse had seven of his sons pass before Samuel, but Samuel said to him, 'The Lord has not chosen these.' So he asked Jesse, 'Are these all the sons you have?' 'There is still the youngest,' Jesse answered. 'He is tending the sheep.' Samuel said, 'Send for him; we will not sit down until he arrives.' So he sent for him and had him brought in. He was glowing with health and had a fine appearance and handsome features. Then the Lord said, 'Rise and anoint him; this is the one.'" Context: God chooses David, the youngest and most unlikely son, to be the next king of Israel. This moment shapes David's lifelong perspective that God is the source of all blessing.

chunks.heading: 1 Chronicles 29:11-14 (NIV) / John 3:27 (NIV) / Luke 12:48 (NIV) / Matthew 16:18 (NIV) / Matthew 6:33 (NIV)
chunks.text: "Yours, Lord, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the majesty and the splendor, for everything in heaven and earth is yours. Yours, Lord, is the kingdom; you are exalted as head over all. Wealth and honor come from you; you are the ruler of all things. In your hands are strength and power to exalt and give strength to all. Now, our God, we give you thanks, and praise your glorious name. But who am I, and who are my people, that we should be able to give as generously as this? Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hands." Context: David's prayer after the people give generously toward building the temple. He recognizes that all wealth, honor, and opportunity to give come from God. "To this John replied, 'A person can receive only what is given them from heaven.'" Context: John the Baptist reminds his disciples that everything—position, blessing, opportunity—is a gift from God. "From everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded; and from the one who has been entrusted with much, much more will be asked." Context: Jesus teaches about faithful stewardship. We are managers, not owners, of what God entrusts to us. "And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it." Context: Jesus declares His intention to build His church. God is the one building; we are invited to participate. "But seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well." Context: Jesus teaches that when we prioritize God's kingdom, He provides what we need. The gospel addresses not just spiritual needs but holistic flourishing.

chunks.heading: 🌆Hong Kong by the Numbers / Goal: $150 Million (12 Months)
chunks.text: Hong Kong is incredible—but beneath the skyline are deep needs that only the gospel can truly address. 23% Live Below Poverty Line Nearly 1 in 4 Hong Kongers 1 in 7 Struggle with Mental Health Anxiety, depression, isolation 7th Highest Divorce Rate In the world 59K Special Needs Kids Up 105% in 10 years "The task is huge. But the opportunity is ours to represent a solution, hope. Beyond just fixing with a band-aid the immediate pressing issues... we want to go beyond that." $40 Million Mortgage servicing for Sunbeam Theater $110 Million First phase of building revitalization

chunks.heading: What Could This Look Like? / 💡Question 1 / 🚧Question 2
chunks.text: These are illustrative examples—everyone prays and decides their own commitment: 2 people @ $10M each $20M 10 people @ $5M each $50M 25 people @ $1M each $25M 250 people @ $100K each $25M 400 people @ $50K each $20M 1,000 people @ $10K each $10M Only 1,687 people in this scenario But over 5,000 call Island ECC home. It's doable. "Not the same gifts, unequal gifts, but equal sacrifice where we all look at our place and figure out what it is that we can do." What is one new insight you picked up from today's sermon? What is your greatest personal barrier to a life of joyful generosity?

chunks.heading: Key Takeaways / Prayer Prompt
chunks.text: 🙏 Everything Belongs to God David's perspective: "Everything in heaven and earth is yours." We don't own our resources—we manage them. Generosity flows from recognizing God as the source of all blessing. 🏗️ We're Building More Than a Building Sunbeam Theater is a tool for kingdom impact. The real goal is breaking through spiritual darkness with the hope of the gospel—addressing poverty, mental health, broken families, and loneliness in Hong Kong. 💝 From Generous Givers to Grateful Managers The shift in perspective: We're not generous people giving our stuff. We're grateful stewards returning what already belongs to God. "We have given you only what comes from your hands." ⚖️ Unequal Gifts, Equal Sacrifice God doesn't call everyone to give the same amount, but He invites all to pray and sacrifice. Some give $10 million, others give $10,000—what matters is prayerful obedience. 🌍 Maximum Collective Impact When we pool our resources as a big church, we can accomplish things a smaller church couldn't. Kids club serving 500 families, ministries to helpers, special needs families, community hubs—all made possible by collective generosity. "Don't make this weird. Pray about it. It is between you and God. We will respect whatever you decide. This is collective generosity for maximum kingdom impact." — Pastor Brett's closing encouragement "Lord, help me see my resources—my time, talents, and treasures—as gifts from You. Show me how You're calling me to steward them for Your kingdom. Give me a clear perspective that everything belongs to You, and the joy of returning what is already Yours. In Jesus' name, Amen."

sermons.title: Breaking Ground: Building Legacy | Island ECC

chunks.heading: A Call to Bold Faith 🔥
chunks.text: In the final installment of the "Breaking Ground" series, Island ECC explores what it means to build a legacy that transcends generations. As the church prepares for its move to the historic Sunbeam Theater location, Pastor Brett challenges us to embody the same boldness that characterized the early church—a boldness not rooted in personal courage, but in witnessing the power of the resurrected Christ.

chunks.heading: Key Themes 🗝️
chunks.text: - Resurrection Power: Peter and John's transformation from fearful deserters to fearless proclaimers - Provocative Faith: Speaking truth even when it challenges established religious systems - Generational Stewardship: Planting trees whose shade we may never fully enjoy - Bold Faith Campaign: Bring people to faith, Optimize training, Love the city, Deepen community "Previous generations plant trees and future generations enjoy the shade. What we are being asked to do is to plant a tree that future generations will benefit from." — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: The Context: A City Abuzz ⚡
chunks.text: Just weeks after the resurrection, Jerusalem is flooded with pilgrims for a feast celebration. The news of Jesus' death and resurrection has spread like wildfire. Peter and John—once terrified disciples who fled when Jesus was arrested—now stand emboldened, ready to proclaim a message that will threaten the very foundations of the religious establishment. What changed? They had seen Jesus alive. The resurrection wasn't just a miracle—it was undeniable proof that everything Jesus said was true. This wasn't about their courage; it was about witnessing God's power.

chunks.heading: The Proclamation: Repent & Be Baptized 💧
chunks.text: Peter's message to the crowd was provocative: "Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins." This wasn't just a call to spiritual renewal—it was a direct challenge to the temple system. Baptism in Jesus' name declared that forgiveness comes through Christ alone, not through animal sacrifices, not through the high priest, but through the blood of Jesus poured out once and for all. The result? 3,000 people were baptized that day. The church began with an explosion of faith.

chunks.heading: The Miracle: Dancing Lame Guy 🎉
chunks.text: Outside the temple gate sat a man who had been lame from birth—a familiar figure to all who passed by. In that culture, many believed his condition was divine punishment. Rabbis would even say, "Blessed be the righteous judge," effectively blaming the beggar for his own suffering. But Peter and John stopped. "Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk." The man didn't just walk—he danced. He leaped. He praised God publicly in the temple courts. This wasn't a quiet healing; it was a visible, undeniable testimony to the power of the risen Christ.

chunks.heading: The Opposition: You Killed the Author of Life ⚔️
chunks.text: With a crowd gathered, Peter preaches again: "You killed the author of life, but God raised him from the dead. We are witnesses of this." This was deeply offensive. The religious leaders had orchestrated Jesus' execution, and now Peter was publicly declaring their guilt—while simultaneously proclaiming that their entire sacrificial system had been rendered obsolete. 5,000 men believed. But the authorities arrested Peter and John. The message was spreading too fast, threatening too much power.

chunks.heading: The Trial: "We Cannot Stop Speaking" 🗣️
chunks.text: Brought before the religious leaders, Peter and John are warned to stop teaching in Jesus' name. But they respond with unwavering conviction: "As for us, we cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard." The leaders were astonished. These were "unschooled, ordinary men." What gave them such courage? The text tells us: They had been with Jesus. And they had the healed man standing right there as living proof. The authorities had no rebuttal. They threatened Peter and John again, then threw them in jail.

chunks.heading: The Prayer: "Give Us Boldness" 🙏
chunks.text: When Peter and John returned to the church and reported what had happened, the believers prayed together. But their prayer wasn't what you might expect. They didn't pray for safety. They didn't ask God to soften the hearts of their enemies or to give them wisdom to avoid conflict. Instead, they prayed: "Now, Lord, consider their threats and enable your servants to speak your word with great boldness." — Acts 4:29 They prayed for more boldness. They understood that the mission was bigger than their comfort, bigger than their safety. They were stewarding the gospel for future generations. And God answered. The place where they met was shaken. They were filled with the Holy Spirit. And they spoke the word of God boldly.

chunks.heading: The Bold Faith Campaign: BOLD Defined 📍
chunks.text: As Island ECC prepares for its permanent home at the Sunbeam Theater, the church is launching the Bold Faith Campaign—a call to invest not just financially, but spiritually and missionally in the future. The acronym BOLD captures the vision: Bring People to Faith 💒 ▼ A heart for the lost. Island ECC is committed to being outward-facing, welcoming all people from all backgrounds—broken, addicted, Buddhist, Muslim, or unchurched. The new location will feature an open cafe, community service hub, and redemptive retail spaces designed to engage the neighborhood seven days a week. The goal: Not to be a big church, but a healthy church that creatively and shrewdly shares the gospel in North Point and beyond. Optimize Training 📚 ▼ Equipping believers to integrate the gospel into every area of life. Through workshops, seminars, conferences, and classes (like the upcoming studies on Jude and fasting), Island ECC wants to help people apply biblical truth to their marriages, workplaces, and communities. The new space will provide unlimited access to host training events throughout the week—no longer constrained by rental agreements. Love the City ❤️ ▼ The Sunbeam Theater is an iconic Hong Kong landmark with deep cultural significance. Island ECC is committed to honoring that heritage by preserving its tangible and intangible history—partnering with artists, universities, and cultural institutions to create virtual museums, documentaries, and exhibitions. The new location will open its doors to the community, offering space, resources, and ministry presence on one of the city's busiest streets. Deepen Community 🤝 ▼ Rows are good, but circles are better. While Sunday gatherings are vital, true transformation happens in care groups—where people know your name, pray for you regularly, and walk through life's ups and downs with you. The new facility will provide dedicated space for small groups, youth ministry, kids club, and other community-building activities that foster authentic relationships.

chunks.heading: Scripture Foundation 📖
chunks.text: Acts 2:38 "Peter replied, 'Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.'" Acts 2:41 "Those who accepted his message were baptized, and about three thousand were added to their number that day." Acts 3:6 "Then Peter said, 'Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.'" Acts 3:8 "He jumped to his feet and began to walk. Then he went with them into the temple courts, walking and jumping, and praising God." Acts 3:15 "You killed the author of life, but God raised him from the dead. We are witnesses of this." Acts 4:12 "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." Acts 4:13 "When they saw the courage of Peter and John and realized that they were unschooled, ordinary men, they were astonished and they took note that these men had been with Jesus." Acts 4:20 "As for us, we cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard." Acts 4:24, 27-28 "When they heard this, they raised their voices together in prayer to God. 'Sovereign Lord,' they said, 'you made the heavens and the earth and the sea, and everything in them... Indeed Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed. They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen.'" Acts 4:29 "Now, Lord, consider their threats and enable your servants to speak your word with great boldness." Acts 4:31 "After they prayed, the place where they were meeting was shaken. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word of God boldly." Deuteronomy 31:8 "The Lord himself goes before you and will be with you; he will never leave you nor forsake you. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged."

chunks.heading: What Do You Pray About? 🤔
chunks.text: Pastor Brett asked a convicting question: If all of your prayers were answered, who would benefit the most? For most of us, the honest answer is: me. Our prayers revolve around our comfort, our promotion, our well-being, our problems. But the early church prayed differently. They prayed from a macro view—acknowledging God's sovereignty over all things—and then brought their specific circumstances to him. And instead of asking for safety or convenience, they asked for boldness to proclaim the gospel. Challenge: This week, evaluate your prayer life. Are you praying for your own comfort, or for God's kingdom to advance—even if it costs you something?

chunks.heading: Invest in Eternal Things 💎
chunks.text: There are only two things that are eternal: the Word of God and the souls of people. Everything else—possessions, careers, achievements—will fade. We get excited about new purchases, but that excitement is short-lived. Something else always becomes shinier. But investing in the gospel, in people's lives, in the church—that never loses its luster. It compounds across eternity. Application: Where are you investing your time, energy, and resources? Are you building a legacy that will outlast you, or are you chasing things that will rust and decay?

chunks.heading: Plant Trees You May Never Sit Under 🌳
chunks.text: The Chinese proverb says: "Previous generations plant trees and future generations enjoy the shade." You and I are enjoying shade today because someone before us—people we've never met—invested in the gospel, in the church, in missions. They prayed. They gave. They suffered. And we benefit. Now it's our turn. The Bold Faith campaign isn't just about a building—it's about ensuring that future generations have a place to encounter Christ, to be discipled, to be sent out into the world. Reflection: What legacy are you leaving? Will your children, grandchildren, and spiritual descendants benefit from the seeds you're planting today?

chunks.heading: Speak Boldly (Even When It's Costly) 🔥
chunks.text: Peter and John faced imprisonment, threats, and potential execution. But they couldn't stop talking about what they had seen and heard. Boldness isn't recklessness—it's confidence in the resurrection. It's believing that the message of Jesus is worth the risk. In our context, boldness might look like: - Sharing your faith with a colleague, even if it feels awkward - Inviting a friend to church, knowing they might say no - Standing for biblical truth in a culture that finds it offensive - Giving sacrificially to support the mission of the church Question: Where is God calling you to be bold? What step of faith is he asking you to take this week?

chunks.heading: Examine Your Heart ❤️
chunks.text: - 🔍 Am I living in light of the resurrection? Does my life reflect confidence that Jesus is alive, or am I living as if the gospel might be a nice idea but not transformative truth? - 💭 What am I praying for? Are my prayers centered on my comfort, or am I asking God to make me bold for his kingdom? - 🌱 What legacy am I leaving? Am I investing in things that will last—the Word and souls—or am I pouring my life into things that will fade? - 🗣️ Where am I holding back? Is there a conversation, a relationship, a step of faith where God is calling me to be bold, but I'm playing it safe?

chunks.heading: A Prayer for Boldness 🕊️
chunks.text: Sovereign Lord, you made the heavens and the earth and the sea, and everything in them. You are above all things, and yet you care deeply about our lives. We confess that we often pray for our own comfort, our own success, our own safety. But today, we ask for something different: Give us boldness. Give us courage to speak your word, even when it's costly. Give us faith to invest in your kingdom, even when we can't see the full return. Give us eyes to see beyond our own lives to the generations that will come after us. Help us to live as people who have been with Jesus—transformed by the resurrection, empowered by the Spirit, and sent into the world with a message that changes everything. In Jesus' name, Amen.

chunks.heading: Next Steps 👣 / Remember 🌟
chunks.text: - Pray about your role in the Bold Faith campaign. Ask God how he's calling you to participate—whether through financial giving, volunteering, or prayer. - Visit the Bold Faith website (iecc.hk/boldfaith) to learn more about the vision for the Sunbeam Theater location. - Invite someone to church this week. Be bold in sharing what God is doing in your life. - Join a care group if you're not already in one. Circles are better than rows—we need deep community to sustain bold faith. - Attend the Marriage Weekend (March 21-22) or sign up for the Jude class or Fasting workshop to continue growing in your faith. "As for us, we cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard." — Acts 4:20 May we be a generation that speaks boldly, gives generously, and plants trees whose shade we may never fully enjoy—all for the glory of God and the advancement of his kingdom. 🌱✨

sermons.title: I AM: The Light of the World | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: Pastor Kevin | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Context: The Festival of Tabernacles / 🎯 Key Theme / 📖 Main Scripture
chunks.text: The Festival of Tabernacles forms the backdrop for Jesus' bold declaration. During this festival, the Israelites erected massive columns in the temple court and lit enormous fires on top, creating a spectacular display of light visible throughout Jerusalem. These fires commemorated the pillar of fire that guided their ancestors through the wilderness. On the final day of the festival, when these fires were extinguished, Jesus stood up and declared: "I am the light of the world." Jesus claims to be the light that guides us, protects us, and dwells with us. This wasn't just a metaphor—it was a direct claim to divinity that paralleled God's presence in the Old Testament, which is why the religious leaders responded with such hostility. John 8:12 "When Jesus spoke again to the people, he said, 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.'"

chunks.heading: 🧭 The Light Guides Us
chunks.text: Just as the pillar of fire guided the Israelites through the wilderness, Jesus guides us through life. But this isn't about divine illumination for every minor decision—it's about living according to the principles and teachings Jesus gave us. - • Forgive as you have been forgiven - • Love your neighbor as yourself - • To be the greatest, be the servant of all - • Don't worry about what you'll wear or eat - • Let your yes be yes and your no be no - • Be faithful with little, be entrusted with more - • Don't judge lest you be judged - • Worship in spirit and truth "Jesus guides us by giving us a framework for how to live our lives so we can face any and every circumstance."

chunks.heading: 🛡️ The Light Protects Us
chunks.text: When the Egyptian army pursued the Israelites to the Red Sea, the pillar of fire threw the army into confusion and protected God's people. Similarly, Jesus protects us from spiritual forces of evil. Ephesians 6:12 "For our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world, and against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms." There is a very real spiritual battle happening around us. Satan is described as "the prince of this world," free to run amok temporarily. But the light of Jesus protects us by freeing us from our enslavement to sin and evil. 1 Thessalonians 5:5 "You are all children of the light and children of the day. We do not belong to the night or to the darkness." We don't belong to the darkness. If you don't have the light of Jesus, you belong to evil—shackled to sin, thinking you're free.

chunks.heading: 🏠 The Light Dwells With Us
chunks.text: For 40 years in the wilderness, God's glorious presence filled the tabernacle. Later, when Solomon built the temple, God's presence came and filled it. But when Israel fell into idolatry and disobedience, God's presence departed. Ezekiel 10:18-19 (excerpt) "Then the glory of the Lord departed from over the threshold of the temple... They stopped at the entrance of the east gate of the Lord's house, and the glory of the God of Israel was above them." By Jesus' time, the temple had been rebuilt, but God's presence was absent. For generations, the Jews prayed for God's presence to return. And then Jesus stood up and said, "I am the light. I've come back to dwell with you." He wasn't claiming to be a positive influence or a great teacher. He was claiming to be God himself—returned to guide, protect, and dwell among his people.

chunks.heading: ⚠️ The Tragic Response
chunks.text: The people of Israel responded to Jesus' claim with: - Judgment: "Your testimony is not valid" (John 8:13) - Dishonor: "Aren't we right in saying you are a Samaritan and demon-possessed?" (John 8:48) - Violence: They picked up stones to kill him (John 8:59) The very light they had been praying would return for centuries had to hide himself and slip away from the temple in darkness. How tragic. How ironic.

chunks.heading: 📖 Scripture References
chunks.text: John 7:2 - The Festival Setting ▼ "But when the Jewish festival of tabernacles was near, Jesus' brothers said to him, 'Leave Galilee and go to Judea, so that your disciples there may see the works you do. No one who wants to become a public figure acts in secret. Since you are doing these things, show yourself to the world.'" John 7:37 & John 8:12 - Jesus' Declaration ▼ John 7:37 - "On the last and greatest day of the festival, Jesus stood and said in a loud voice, 'Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink.'" John 8:12 - "When Jesus spoke again to the people, he said, 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.'" Exodus - The Pillar of Fire ▼ "By day the Lord went ahead of them in a pillar of cloud to guide them on their way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light, so that they could travel by day or by night." This pillar guided the Israelites for 40 years in the wilderness and protected them from their enemies at the Red Sea. Genesis 1 - Let There Be Light ▼ "In the beginning, God said, 'Let there be light.'" This light sparked all of creation. Everything we know and love has been created through this light. Light is the very first thing God spoke into existence. Ephesians 6:12 - The Spiritual Battle ▼ "For our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world, and against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms." 1 Thessalonians 5:5 - Children of Light ▼ "You are all children of the light and children of the day. We do not belong to the night or to the darkness." Note the word "belong"—if you don't have the light of Jesus, you belong to the darkness. The light of Jesus frees us from enslavement to evil. Ezekiel 10:18-19 - God's Presence Departs ▼ "Then the glory of the Lord departed from over the threshold of the temple and stopped above the cherubim. While I watched, the cherubim spread their wings and rose from the ground, and as they went, the wheels went with them. They stopped at the entrance of the east gate of the Lord's house, and the glory of the God of Israel was above them." This prophetic vision showed God's presence leaving the temple before Jerusalem fell to Babylon. When the temple was later rebuilt, God's presence did not return—until Jesus came.

chunks.heading: 📸 The Camera Exposure Analogy
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin shared a story from his recent vacation to New Zealand's Lake Pukaki, a certified dark sky reserve. When he tried to photograph the Milky Way with his iPhone, he got a disappointing dark image—but the professional photos showed stunning detail. The difference? Exposure time. The longer a camera lens is exposed to light, the more light it captures and the clearer the image becomes. "When we have the light of Jesus, we can all stand to increase our exposure to his light."

chunks.heading: 💡 Why Increase Your Exposure?
chunks.text: Greater exposure to God's light brings: - 🧭 Greater clarity over his guidance - Understanding how to apply his principles in your specific circumstances - 🛡️ Greater protection - From spiritual forces and deceptions you aren't even aware of - 🏠 Greater intimacy in his presence - Deeper relationship and transformation - ✨ Greater transformation - Becoming more like Jesus and more effective for his purposes 📅 If you're exposed 1 day/week: Add a second day—read your Bible, pray, join a care group, serve 📈 If you're exposed 2 days/week: Make it three days—add a midweek Bible study or coffee with Christian friends ⏰ If you have daily quiet time: Increase from 30 minutes to 35-40 minutes

chunks.heading: 🤔 Honest Self-Reflection
chunks.text: Before we judge the Israelites too harshly, Pastor Kevin challenges us to consider: Are we really that different? - • Do we truly want his guidance? (Living by his principles is inconvenient and requires sacrifice) - • Do we truly want his protection? (We live in a post-truth world that rejects absolute truth) - • Do we truly want his presence? (His light exposes the ugly parts of ourselves we'd rather ignore) "Sometimes we overcomplicate spirituality. Are you spending time with God? Can you increase the amount of time you spend with him?"

chunks.heading: 💭 Personal Reflection
chunks.text: 🔍 The Post-Truth World We Live In ▼ Pastor Kevin identified three characteristics of our current post-truth era: - Erosion of trust in traditional sources of truth (news, government, science, Scripture) - Prioritization of emotions over empirical evidence—if it doesn't feel right, we dismiss it - Widespread false information that we believe if it resonates with our feelings Result: We don't know right from wrong. We've abandoned Scripture as our source of truth and lean into our emotions, believing falsehoods that resonate with us. ⚔️ The Unseen Spiritual Battle ▼ John Mark Comer's book "Live No Lies" argues that Satan's main weapon is lies and deception. There is a sophisticated infrastructure—an intricate network of demons—working to compel us toward rebellion and unbelief. Can you, by yourself, stand up against this coalition of evil and deception? Pastor Kevin urges us to reconsider. "We need his protection to protect us from a spiritual reality and a spiritual battle that we cannot fully comprehend or even perceive." 🪞 The Mirror We Avoid ▼ Pastor Kevin shared a vulnerable story: "When I was in high school, I hated looking at myself in the mirror. I had pointy eyebrows and frequent pimples. Every time I flicked on the light switch and walked into the bathroom, I was confronted with this really gross and disgusting face." In the same way, the light of Jesus exposes the real ugliness in us—the muck, dirt, and sin we'd rather not see: - • Your anger and pride - • Your tendency to run from problems - • Your restless anxiety to control everything - • Your unhealthy codependency patterns "It's easier to turn a blind eye and move on with your life. But you can only be as efficient and effective as your resemblance to Jesus." 🌟 Part of God's Grand Plan ▼ All of us—Christian or not—have a desire for significance and purpose. Here's the beautiful message of the gospel: When you follow Jesus, you are brought into God's grand cosmic plan of redemption for the entire world. There's no greater purpose or cause you could ever be part of. But here's the key: You can only be as efficient as your resemblance to Jesus. The Christian life after salvation and baptism is dedicated to becoming more and more like Jesus. The best—and necessary—way to do this is to be in his presence, allowing his light to expose the dirt and sin in your heart so you can deal with it and progress. "This is what I believe is the most significant ministry of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit dwells within us to surface all these things under the surface that we wouldn't have otherwise touched so that we can be effective for his purposes." 🎁 The Simplicity of the Gospel ▼ How do we get the light? You follow him. You believe in him. You trust in him. John 8:12 "Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life." Salvation is by grace through faith in Jesus Christ alone. When you do this, you receive the light. That's the beautiful gospel message. But just because we have his light doesn't mean we allow that light to shine brightly. We can all stand to increase our exposure.

chunks.heading: 🙏 Closing Prayer Points
chunks.text: From Pastor Kevin's closing prayer: "Lord, we confess that there are many times and seasons where we have suppressed your light, walked away from your guidance, sought to defend ourselves without your protection, and tried to transform ourselves to be more like the world instead of more like Jesus." "Your light is so beautiful—so much greater than the Milky Way. And it's so much more personal. You invite us to experience the fullness of your light in this present life. It's available to us." "We want to be a city on a hill, a church shining your light in this city, this nation, and around the world. Help us do that. We want to be part of expanding your kingdom and glory."

sermons.title: I AM: The Door | Pastor Albert | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: 23 Mar 2025 • Pastor Albert • Island ECC

chunks.heading: Overview
chunks.text: Jesus declared, "I am the door." This powerful metaphor reveals that Jesus is the exclusive gateway to God and to a life of true meaning and abundance. Through the story of a man born blind and Jesus' confrontation with the Pharisees, we discover what it means to enter through the door that is Christ. John 10:9 (NIV) "I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out, and find pasture."

chunks.heading: Key Themes 🔑
chunks.text: - 🔹 Jesus as the Exclusive Door: He is the only way to God and salvation - 🔹 Protection and Provision: The shepherd lies at the entrance to protect his sheep - 🔹 Breaking Shame and Condemnation: "Neither this man nor his parents sinned" - 🔹 Recognizing the Shepherd's Voice: Knowing God through regular exposure to His Word - 🔹 The Open Door of Invitation: Jesus stands knocking, waiting for us to open

chunks.heading: Main Sermon
chunks.text: 🌟 Introduction: The Mystery of Doors ▼ Pastor Albert begins by sharing his love for Scotland and the excitement of entering old church doors and castle doors—never knowing what lies beyond. Sometimes a door leads to ruins, sometimes to magnificent mosaics or historic tombs. A door in China led to what he thought was a tea house but turned out to be someone's home! Doors are fascinating because they lead us somewhere—and Jesus uses this everyday image to teach us something profound about himself: "I am the door." 👁️ The Man Born Blind: Breaking Shame ▼ In John 9, Jesus encounters a man born blind. The disciples ask a shocking question: "Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?" "In Jesus' day, there was a belief that if something bad happened to you or your family, it must be a consequence of somebody's sin—either your own or your parents'." This meant the man lived under two layers of suffering: - The physical disability itself - The shame and condemnation—believing it was punishment for sin But Jesus breaks this chain of condemnation with powerful words: John 9:3 (NIV) "Neither this man nor his parents sinned," said Jesus, "but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him." For those carrying guilt: Jesus says to you today—it's not your fault. Break the chain of shame and self-condemnation. Turn your eyes to Jesus, let Him smile at you, let Him embrace you. 🩹 The Messy Process of Healing ▼ Jesus heals the man in an unusual way—He spits on the ground, makes mud, and puts it on the man's eyes. Then He tells him to wash in the Pool of Siloam. "All healings are messy. Imagine a broken bone needing surgery—both the surgery and recovery are messy. If you go to a counselor and open up, it's messy. If you need to reconcile with someone who betrayed you—it's beyond messy." If you're in a healing process right now, expect the messiness. It's the process you must go through. But the result? The man "came home seeing." ⚖️ The Pharisees: Gatekeepers or Obstacles? ▼ Instead of celebration, the Pharisees interrogate the healed man because Jesus healed on the Sabbath. They believed they were the gatekeepers of God's truth, the "door" through which people must pass to know God. "The Pharisees considered themselves God's representatives, gatekeepers of God's truth. They thought they were the door to God: 'If you want to know God, come through us.'" But the healed man gives a simple, profound testimony: John 9:25 (NIV) "Whether he is a sinner or not, I don't know. One thing I do know. I was blind but now I see!" (This became the famous lyric in "Amazing Grace") 🐑 The Sheep Pen Metaphor ▼ Jesus then tells a parable about sheep, shepherds, and gates. In ancient times, there were two types of sheep pens: Type 1: Public Sheep Pen (Urban Setting) Multiple shepherds use one common pen with a gatekeeper. Each morning, shepherds come and call their sheep by name. The sheep recognize their shepherd's voice and follow him out. John 10:3-4 (NIV) "The gatekeeper opens the gate for him, and the sheep listen to his voice. He calls his own sheep by name and leads them out. When he has brought out all his own, he goes on ahead of them, and his sheep follow him because they know his voice." Application: How do you recognize God's voice? By hearing it often and frequently. This is why we must read the Bible regularly—to familiarize ourselves with His voice. Type 2: Stone Sheep Pen (Rural Setting) In rural areas, a shepherd has his own stone pen with no gate and no gatekeeper. How does he protect the sheep from lions, bears, and wolves? "The shepherd sleeps at the entrance. He literally lays down his life at the doorway. The shepherd IS the gate." 🚪 "I Am the Door" ▼ John 10:7, 9-10 (NIV) "Therefore Jesus said again, 'Very truly I tell you, I am the gate for the sheep... I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out, and find pasture. The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.'" Jesus is saying: - I am THE door—not a door. He is exclusive. - I am the only door to God - I am the only door to salvation - Whoever enters through me will be saved - They will find protection (come in) - They will find provision (go out and find pasture) "Whatever is going on in your life, I am here to protect you. If there are wild animals coming to attack, I am here. If someone has to be hurt, it will be me. If someone has to die, it will be me. Because I lay down my life at the entrance for you." ✝️ The Cross: The Door Wide Open ▼ This shepherd metaphor points directly to the cross. Jesus didn't just talk about laying down His life—He did it. On the cross, Jesus became the door through which we can enter into relationship with God. The door that was closed in Genesis (when Adam and Eve were driven from Eden) is now wide open through Christ.

chunks.heading: Key Bible Verses
chunks.text: John 10:7 (NIV) Therefore Jesus said again, "Very truly I tell you, I am the gate for the sheep." John 10:9-10 (NIV) I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out, and find pasture. The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full. John 9:3 (NIV) "Neither this man nor his parents sinned," said Jesus, "but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him." John 10:3-4 (NIV) The gatekeeper opens the gate for him, and the sheep listen to his voice. He calls his own sheep by name and leads them out. When he has brought out all his own, he goes on ahead of them, and his sheep follow him because they know his voice. John 9:25 (NIV) He replied, "Whether he is a sinner or not, I don't know. One thing I do know. I was blind but now I see!" Revelation 3:20 (NIV) Here I am! I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with that person, and they with me. Romans 8:28 (NIV) And we know that in all things God works for the good of those who love him, who have been called according to his purpose. Ezekiel 34 (NIV - excerpt) [God rebukes the shepherds of Israel] "Woe to you shepherds of Israel who only take care of yourselves! Should not shepherds take care of the flock?... You have not strengthened the weak or healed the sick or bound up the injured..."

chunks.heading: Biblical Doors: Genesis to Revelation
chunks.text: Pastor Albert takes us on a journey through Scripture, showing how the metaphor of "the door" appears throughout the Bible. 🌳 Genesis 3 – The Door of Mercy After Adam and Eve sinned, God drove them from the Garden of Eden and placed a cherubim with a flaming sword to block the way to the tree of life. "Many think this is the door of punishment. But it's actually the door of mercy. God prevented them from eating from the tree of life and living eternally in their sinful state, separated from Him forever." Lesson: Sometimes God closes doors behind us not because He doesn't love us, but because He loves us too much. He redirects us to where He can reveal Himself. 🩸 Exodus 12 – The Door of Deliverance God commanded the Israelites to kill a lamb and paint its blood on their door frames. When the angel of death passed over Egypt, it would "pass over" any house marked with blood. Exodus 12:7, 13 (NIV) "Then they are to take some of the blood and put it on the sides and tops of the doorframes... The blood will be a sign for you on the houses where you are, and when I see the blood, I will pass over you." The Lamb → The Blood → The Door → Deliverance This is the Passover, which points forward to Christ—the Lamb of God whose blood delivers us from death. ✝️ John 10 – The Door of Salvation Jesus declares, "I am the door." He is the only way to God, the only way to salvation, the only way to abundant life. Exclusive claim: Not a door, but THE door. 🚪 Revelation 3:20 – The Door of Invitation Revelation 3:20 (NIV) "Here I am! I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with that person, and they with me." Important context: This verse is written to Christians in the church of Laodicea—believers who had become lukewarm and pushed Jesus to the margins of their lives. "Notice—there is no doorknob on the outside. The only way to open the door is from the inside. God will not violate your decision." Application for Christians: Before every meeting, every difficult conversation, every challenge—invite Jesus in. "Jesus, will you please come with me?" Make room for Him. Application for non-believers: Jesus is knocking on your door today. Will you open it? 🙌 Revelation 4 – The Door of Divine Worship In the future, we will see a door opened in heaven. When we pass through, we will see God on His throne, surrounded by angels, cherubim, elders—all worshiping Him. Revelation 4:1 (NIV) "After this I looked, and there before me was a door standing open in heaven." This is where we are going.

chunks.heading: For Those Carrying Shame 💔 / For Those in the Healing Process 🩹
chunks.text: "For many years you are condemning yourself. You feel like you are the reason why you and your family is suffering. I want you to hear Jesus' word: Neither this man nor his parents sinned. It's not your fault." Action Step: Today, break the chain of shame and self-condemnation. Turn your eyes away from yourself and back to Jesus. Let Him smile at you. Let Him embrace you. Expect the messiness. All healing—physical, emotional, relational—is messy. But stay in the process. The blind man washed in the Pool of Siloam and came home seeing.

chunks.heading: Learning to Recognize God's Voice 🎧 / Making Room for Jesus 🙏
chunks.text: John 10:27 (NIV) "My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me." How? The sheep recognize the shepherd's voice because they hear it every single day. If you want to recognize Jesus' voice: - Read the Bible regularly - Spend time in worship - Fellowship with other believers - Increase your exposure to God (Pastor Kevin's challenge from last week) "Anytime I go to a meeting or need to deal with something difficult, I will say, 'Jesus, will you please come with me?' I make room for Jesus." Practice this: Before your next challenge, conversation, or decision, pause and invite Jesus in. Don't keep Him standing outside the door of that area of your life.

chunks.heading: For Those Outside the Door 🚪 / 1. Breaking Condemnation 💭 / 2. Recognizing His Voice 🎧 / 3. Jesus at the Door 🚪 / 4. Protected and Provided For 🐑 / 5. Life to the Full 🌟
chunks.text: Jesus stands at the door of your heart and knocks. The doorknob is only on the inside—He will not force His way in. But He invites you: "I am the door. Whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out and find pasture. I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full." Will you open the door today? Are you carrying shame or condemnation about something in your life or family? Have you believed it's "your fault"? How does Jesus' statement "neither this man nor his parents sinned" speak to your situation? How often do you "hear" God's voice through Scripture, worship, and fellowship? What practical step can you take this week to increase your exposure to God? Where in your life have you kept Jesus outside the door? What areas have you not invited Him into? What would it look like to open that door today? Jesus says through Him we find both protection (come in) and provision (go out and find pasture). In what ways have you experienced Jesus' protection and provision in your life? Jesus came so we can have life "to the full" or "abundantly." What does abundant life in Christ look like for you? How is it different from the life the "thief" offers (stealing, killing, destroying)? Final Question: Jesus is standing at your door today. Would you like to invite Him in?

sermons.title: I AM: The Good Shepherd | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: 30 March 2025 | Pastor Eric | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🌟 Key Message
chunks.text: In John 10, Jesus continues His discourse from being the door to revealing Himself as the Good Shepherd. Using imagery deeply rooted in Old Testament prophecy and the familiar occupation of shepherding, Jesus declares to the religious leaders that He is the loving Messiah God's people had been waiting for. This message comes during the Festival of Dedication (Hanukkah), a time when Israel remembered pushing out bad leadership and reconsecrating the temple. In this context of longing for good leadership both spiritually and politically, Jesus audaciously claims: "I am the good shepherd."

chunks.heading: 🎯 Central Truths / 📍 Context & Setting
chunks.text: - • Jesus is the Good Shepherd who knows His sheep by name and lays down His life for them - • We are like sheep: easily lost, defenseless, and in need of a shepherd - • Bad shepherds feed themselves; good shepherds lay down their lives for the sheep - • The quality of our life depends on whose voice we listen to - • Jesus is the shepherd in sheep's clothing who died in our place When: Winter, during the Festival of Dedication (Hanukkah) Audience: Religious leaders who had failed in their God-given job to shepherd the people Atmosphere: Expectation for good leadership, remembering when Israel pushed out bad rulers, but currently under Roman occupation Spiritual Climate: "Winter" - spiritually cold, lacking flourishing, needing renewal

chunks.heading: Main Sermon
chunks.text: 🎬 Introduction: Understanding Context ▼ Pastor Eric begins by explaining how powerful statements gain their meaning from context. Just like Darth Vader's "I am your father" only has shock value when you know the story leading up to it, Jesus' "I am" statements are rich with meaning when we understand the context.
