This passage in John 10 continues from chapter 9, where Jesus addresses religious leaders who have failed as shepherds of God's people. The setting is winter during the Festival of Dedication (Hanukkah), a time commemorating when the Jewish people overthrew bad leadership 200 years prior and reconsecrated the temple after Antiochus Epiphanes desecrated it. There's an air of expectation—remembering past deliverance from bad leadership while currently under Roman rule, and internally experiencing spiritual leaders who aren't serving the people well. ⚠️ Bad Shepherds: The Indictment ▼ When Jesus says "I am the good shepherd," He's implicitly saying the current leaders are not. He compares them to hired hands who don't truly care for the sheep—they're in it for personal benefit and flee when danger comes. Jesus references Ezekiel 34, where God condemns Israel's shepherds: "You eat the fat, you clothe yourselves with the wool, you slaughter the fat ones, but you do not feed the sheep. The weak you have not strengthened, the sick you have not healed, the injured you have not bound up, the strayed you have not brought back, the lost you have not sought." This is a little bit of an awkward moment. Jesus walks in and says, "I am the good pastor." It's offensive, right? It's a lot offensive. But this is Jesus in love confronting the leaders who weren't doing what they should be doing. — Pastor Eric This is a call to anyone entrusted with "sheep"—whether a team at work, children, a care group, or aging parents. Good leadership means laying down your life, caring for those who go astray, healing the hurt, and nourishing others, sometimes giving of yourself so they can live. 🐑 We Are the Sheep ▼ Being called sheep is offensive at first, but then humbling. Drawing from shepherds-turned-pastors Phillip Keller and W. Phillip Keller, Pastor Eric describes sheep as: - Easily lost: No sense of direction, can't find their way even when the fold is in sight - Distracted: See luscious grass and wander off the path without meaning to - Panicky: When they realize they're lost, every sound becomes threatening, every shadow looks like the enemy - Defenseless: Can't run well, have no defensive weapons like horns or fangs - Helpless: Can't even get up when on their backs; will die without intervention We can replace these descriptions with ourselves: "I get easily lost and lose my sense of direction in life. I see something shiny, something new, something exciting, and soon I'm off the path. When I panic, everything becomes a threat. I feel defenseless, powerless to affect change in a world I cannot control." Left to my own devices, I often need rescuing. I'm hopeless in this greater world without someone interceding. And we find out that the lot of any one of us in life is dependent on who we allow to be the shepherd. — Pastor Eric 🔊 Listening to the Right Voice ▼ The imagery of the Good Shepherd finding His lost sheep is beautiful—the shepherd joyfully carries the lamb on his shoulders back home. But what the image doesn't show is what happened minutes before: the sheep running away, panicking, not recognizing that the shepherd is the best thing for it. Sometimes shepherds have to tackle sheep and bind their legs to rescue them. Even when a good God comes after us, we panic, fearing we might lose what we're chasing, not knowing enough to recognize the shepherd's swift movements are intended for our good. We can listen to a million different voices: accomplishment, achievement, production, mattering, security, being right. But Jesus calls us by name—and when He does, everything changes: - Saul, Saul — why are you persecuting me? - Martha, Martha — there's one thing that matters - Mary — at the empty tomb, one word and she sees Him - Lazarus — called out of death itself The Good Shepherd calls His sheep by name because He knows them, loves them, and is trying to rescue them. When we listen to His voice above all others, it changes everything. ✝️ The Good Shepherd in Sheep's Clothing ▼ Jesus warned of "wolves in sheep's clothing"—people who look like they're among you but whose intention is evil. But Jesus Himself is the opposite: the Shepherd in sheep's clothing. He is the Good Shepherd who laid down His life, who when the wolves came after the sheep, took the attack on Himself. He died in place of the other sheep. In His three decades of life, He was always the sheep who listened to the Good Shepherd (the Father), showing us what obedience, faithfulness, trust, and faith look like. He is the shepherd in sheep's clothing. He was the sheep who died in our place, and He was the sheep who showed us what obedience and faithfulness and trust and faith looked like by listening to His Father. — Pastor Eric Revelation beautifully captures this dual nature: "There is a lamb in the midst of the throne, He will be their shepherd, and He will guide them to springs of living water, and God will wipe away every tear from their eyes." It's freeing to admit we don't have it all together, that we can't control everything, that we make decisions out of fear. We're not made to be in control. There is One who is, who loves us, who died for us—and that's the Good Shepherd.

chunks.heading: Bible Verses
chunks.text: John 10:11-18 (NIV) "I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. The hired hand is not the shepherd and does not own the sheep. So when he sees the wolf coming, he abandons the sheep and runs away. Then the wolf attacks the flock and scatters it. The man runs away because he is a hired hand and cares nothing for the sheep. I am the good shepherd; I know my sheep and my sheep know me—just as the Father knows me and I know the Father—and I lay down my life for the sheep. I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen. I must bring them also. They too will listen to my voice, and there shall be one flock and one shepherd. The reason my Father loves me is that I lay down my life—only to take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down and authority to take it up again. This command I received from my Father." Ezekiel 34:2-6 (NIV) "This is what the Sovereign Lord says: Woe to you shepherds of Israel who only take care of yourselves! Should not shepherds take care of the flock? You eat the curds, clothe yourselves with the wool and slaughter the choice animals, but you do not take care of the flock. You have not strengthened the weak or healed the sick or bound up the injured. You have not brought back the strays or searched for the lost. You have ruled them harshly and brutally. So they were scattered because there was no shepherd, and when they were scattered they became food for all the wild animals. My sheep wandered over all the mountains and on every high hill. They were scattered over the whole earth, and no one searched or looked for them." Psalm 23:1-6 (NIV) "The Lord is my shepherd, I lack nothing. He makes me lie down in green pastures, he leads me beside quiet waters, he refreshes my soul. He guides me along the right paths for his name's sake. Even though I walk through the darkest valley, I will fear no evil, for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows. Surely your goodness and love will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever." Luke 15:4-6 (NIV) "Suppose one of you has a hundred sheep and loses one of them. Doesn't he leave the ninety-nine in the open country and go after the lost sheep until he finds it? And when he finds it, he joyfully puts it on his shoulders and goes home. Then he calls his friends and neighbors together and says, 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost sheep.'" Revelation 7:17 (NIV) "For the Lamb at the center of the throne will be their shepherd; 'he will lead them to springs of living water.' 'And God will wipe away every tear from their eyes.'" 1 Peter 2:25 (NIV) "For 'you were like sheep going astray,' but now you have returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls." 1 Peter 5:4 (NIV) "And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that will never fade away."

chunks.heading: The Life Without Lack
chunks.text: Dallas Willard's reflections on Psalm 23 reveal what it means to live under the care of the Good Shepherd. "The Lord is my shepherd" I'm in the care of someone else. I'm not the one in charge. I've taken my kingdom and surrendered it to the kingdom of God. I am living the with-God life. "I shall not want" That's the natural result. I shall not lack anything. That's what Jesus teaches: "Seek first his kingdom and his righteousness and everything else will be added." "He makes me lie down in green pastures" What kind of sheep lies down in a green pasture? A sheep that has eaten its fill. If a sheep is in a green pasture and she's not full, she'll be eating, not lying down. "He leads me beside still waters" A sheep that is being led beside still water is a sheep that is not thirsty. "He restores my soul" The broken depths of my soul are healed and reintegrated in a life in union with God, the eternal kind of life. "He leads me in paths of righteousness" The effect of the restoration of my soul is that I walk in paths of righteousness on his behalf, as a natural expression of my renewed inner nature. "I will fear no evil" A life without lack is one that carries no fear of evil. Our confidence in God soars far above wants and fears. Imagine what that would be like: no fear of life, aging or death, disease or hunger, no fear of any person or creature, not even the loss of all your possessions. "For you are with me" The complete sufficiency of the life without lack is based upon the presence of God. And he is most clearly and fully present to us in Jesus Christ, Emmanuel, God with us. "Your rod and your staff, they comfort me" I know from experience that the rod and the staff represent the shepherd's strength, his protective care. "You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies" Since I love my enemies, I would not feast upon a delicious meal in their presence and let them stand there hungry. The abundance of God's provision and safety in my life is so great, I would invite them to enjoy what God has prepared for me. "You anoint my head with oil" God is not only interested in my having something wonderful to eat, but in blessing me with a life that is full and free and powerful in him, including clothing, furnishings, joyful experiences, and deep relationships. "My cup overflows" I have more than my cup will hold, so much that I can be as generous as my shepherd without fear of ever running out. "Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life" This is the description of the eternal life available to us now in the kingdom of heaven: the abundant with-God life that comes from following the shepherd, where we dwell and abide with God in the fullness of his life, a life in which all the promises of Christ's gospel are realized. Because of this, we have no reason to be anxious. The world is a perfectly safe place for us to be. — Dallas Willard

chunks.heading: 🎯 For Those Who Lead
chunks.text: God calls us to lead like the Good Shepherd, not like the bad shepherds condemned in Ezekiel 34. Whether you lead a team at work, parent children, guide a care group, or care for aging parents, you are entrusted with "sheep." Good shepherds: - Lay down their lives for those entrusted to them - Strengthen the weak and heal the sick - Pursue those who go astray with love - Nourish and feed their flock - Sometimes give of themselves so others can live Bad shepherds: - Feed themselves at the expense of the sheep - Take money, prestige, anything that could go to others - Flee when danger comes - Care more about personal benefit than the flock Ask yourself: Am I leading with justice, fairness, care, and love in a way that emulates the Good Shepherd? Or am I acting like a hired hand who's in it for personal gain?

chunks.heading: 🔊 Whose Voice Are You Listening To?
chunks.text: We can listen to a million different voices in this world: - The voice of accomplishment and achievement - The voice that says you need to produce something to matter - The voice of security and being right - The voice of fear, greed, or pride - Voices that promise fulfillment but lead you astray But when the Good Shepherd calls your name—when He speaks specifically to you—everything changes. Remember how Jesus called: - Saul, Saul — confronting his misguided zeal - Martha, Martha — redirecting her busyness to what matters - Mary — revealing Himself at her point of deepest sorrow - Lazarus — calling him out of death itself Reflection Questions: - What voices currently have the most power in your life? - What would it look like to let God's voice have that power instead? - Where have you wandered off the path recently? - Can you hear the Good Shepherd calling your name?

chunks.heading: 🐑 Embrace Your Identity as a Sheep
chunks.text: It's humbling, but freeing, to admit: - I don't have it all together - I can't control everything - I get easily lost and distracted - I make decisions out of fear and respond out of panic - I need rescuing and cannot save myself But here's the good news: we're not made to be in control. There is One who is—who loves us, who died for us, who knows us by name, and who will never abandon us. The lot of any sheep depends on the type of shepherd who owns it. The quality of your life depends on whose voice you're listening to. Will you listen to the Good Shepherd?

chunks.heading: ✝️ Remember the Shepherd in Sheep's Clothing / Reflection & Prayer
chunks.text: Jesus is not a wolf in sheep's clothing. He is the Shepherd in sheep's clothing—the one who: - Laid down His life when wolves came after the sheep - Took the attack on Himself - Died in our place - Showed us what perfect obedience to the Father looks like - Rose again with authority over death As we approach Easter, remember that this Good Shepherd didn't just talk about laying down His life—He actually did it. And He had the authority to take it up again. This is the Shepherd we follow. This is the voice we listen to. This is the One who knows you by name and will never let you go. God, you are our great shepherd. It's incredible to read this psalm from a king in the highest position who rightfully humbles himself and says, "I am but a sheep under the great shepherd." — Pastor Eric's Closing Prayer

chunks.heading: 🙏 A Prayer to the Good Shepherd
chunks.text: God, help us, no matter our position in life, to humble ourselves and put ourselves under the chief shepherd. It is right, it is good to humble ourselves. Help us in our shepherding callings in life to shepherd with justice, fairness, care, and love in a way that emulates what you are like. Help us to listen to your voice when so many voices in this world are calling to us to chase something else, to leave the fold, to take a different path, to pursue temporary pleasure, only to realize we end up not with you. May you wreck those other voices of their power in our life. Detach them from our hearts, and may your voice and the power of it grow ever stronger in our hearts. May we love you, worship you, and follow you, our great shepherd. In Jesus' name, Amen.

chunks.heading: 💭 Personal Reflection Questions
chunks.text: About Being Sheep: - In what areas of your life do you feel most "lost" or "scattered" right now? - What "luscious grass" has recently distracted you from the path God has for you? - When you panic, what voices become loudest in your life? - Where do you need to admit you're powerless and need rescuing? About the Shepherd's Voice: - Can you hear Jesus calling your name? What might He be saying to you? - What voices currently have more power in your life than the Good Shepherd's voice? - How can you practically "listen" to God's voice this week? - What would change if you truly believed God knows you by name and cares for you? About Your Shepherding: - Who has God entrusted to your care (family, team, friends, etc.)? - Are you shepherding them like the Good Shepherd or like a hired hand? - Who in your circle has "gone astray" that you need to pursue with love? - Where might you be feeding yourself at the expense of those you're called to care for?

chunks.heading: 📝 This Week's Challenge
chunks.text: Give yourself a reminder this week to listen to His voice. Pastor Eric mentioned someone made a sheep cookie to remember the Good Shepherd. What reminder will you create? Consider: - Reading Psalm 23 every morning this week - Setting a daily alarm labeled "The Good Shepherd knows my name" - Finding a small sheep figurine or image to keep visible - Journaling about which voices you're listening to vs. which you should listen to - Memorizing John 10:14: "I am the good shepherd; I know my sheep and my sheep know me" Believe He is good and follow Him. My prayer, my benediction is that you would see the chief shepherd as the good shepherd, that you would hear his voice and you would listen. That you would know whether it comes in your calm or your distress, that it is for your good, that he loves you, that he knows you, and that he has the best for you. — Pastor Eric's Benediction

sermons.title: I AM: The Vine | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Central Message / Key Scripture
chunks.text: Jesus uses the metaphor of a vine and branches to teach the essential truth of Christian life: we must remain connected to Him. Just as branches cannot bear fruit apart from the vine, we cannot live fruitful spiritual lives apart from Christ. This isn't just about belief—it's about abiding, dwelling, staying constantly connected to our source of life. "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing." — John 15:5 (NIV)

chunks.heading: Main Points / Practical Takeaways
chunks.text: - ✅ Jesus is the True Vine - fulfilling Israel's failed vineyard - ✅ God the Father is the Gardener - who prunes and cultivates - ✅ We are the Branches - displaying fruit for God's glory - ✅ Remaining = Fruitfulness - connection produces spiritual growth - ✅ Pruning ≠ Punishment - removing what hinders, not what we need - ✅ The Command: Love - staying connected enables sacrificial love 🌱 Plant in Truth Abide in Jesus' words, fill your mind with Scripture, keep His command to love. 🌿 Tend Your Garden Practice spiritual disciplines daily—prayer, worship, community, service. ✂️ Trust the Gardener Let God prune what hinders your growth—habits, relationships, past burdens. 🌸 Bloom Together Grow in community—we're stronger when supporting each other.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤
chunks.text: 🌱 Introduction: The Best Gardener I Know ▼ Pastor Tim begins by sharing about his mother—the best gardener he knows. She worked tirelessly every weekend on her yard, meticulously caring for flowers and pulling weeds. After Tim got married, she helped set up his garden, but he neglected it during winter, thinking plants just naturally died and came back. When he asked his mom if plants needed water in winter, she responded with a profound question: "Do you need to drink water in winter?" This became a spiritual lesson—even in our spiritual winters and desert seasons, we need water. We need God. His mother also taught him about pruning—removing dead branches so plants can direct energy to living parts, producing more blossoms and fruit. This agricultural principle becomes the foundation for understanding Jesus' teaching about the vine. 🍇 "I Am the True Vine" - Historical Fulfillment ▼ When Jesus declares "I am the true vine" (John 15:1), His disciples immediately understood the weight of this statement. The word "true" packed their entire Old Testament history. Isaiah's Prophecy of the Failed Vineyard In Isaiah 5, God describes Israel as His vineyard—planted on a fertile hillside with choicest vines, a watchtower, and a winepress. He expected good grapes but received only bad fruit. "My loved one had a vineyard on a fertile hillside. He dug it up and cleared it of stones and planted it with the choicest vines... Then he looked for a crop of good grapes, but it yielded only bad fruit." — Isaiah 5:1-2 Because of their unfaithfulness, God removed His protection: "I will take away its hedge, and it will be destroyed; I will break down its wall, and it will be trampled. I will make it a wasteland, neither pruned nor cultivated, and briers and thorns will grow there." — Isaiah 5:5-6 The Hebrew Wordplay Isaiah uses powerful Hebrew wordplay contrasting what God wanted versus what He found: - Mishpat (justice/fairness) vs. Mispach (bloodshed/violence) - Tzedakah (righteousness) vs. Tze'akah (cries of distress) So close in sound, yet completely opposite in meaning—showing how Israel pursued the wrong things while thinking they were right. Hope of Restoration Despite judgment, Isaiah prophesied hope: "A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a branch will bear fruit.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 2)
chunks.text: The Spirit of the Lord will rest on him." — Isaiah 11:1-2 When Jesus says "I am the true vine," He's declaring: I am that promised shoot. I am the fulfillment. I am what Israel was meant to be. 🍷 Immediate Context: Fresh from Communion ▼ This teaching wasn't just historically significant—it was immediately visceral for the disciples. Just an hour before, they had shared communion with Jesus. They had tasted the wine representing His blood, the new covenant. After communion, Jesus said "Come, let us leave" (John 14:31). As they walked toward the Garden of Gethsemane— perhaps passing vineyards—Jesus gave this teaching. The taste of the choicest wine was still on their tongues. Jesus was saying: "I am the true vine. You just tasted what that means. This is the new covenant in my blood. I have redeemed Israel, and you are now part of this." ✂️ The Father as Gardener: Cutting and Pruning ▼ "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener. He cuts off every branch in me that bears no fruit, while every branch that does bear fruit he prunes so that it will be even more fruitful." — John 15:1-2 Two Actions of the Gardener 1. Cuts Off Fruitless Branches These represent people who claim to follow Christ but aren't truly connected—they fake it. Judas was one such example. Jesus said, "Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?" (Luke 6:46). Superficial connection leads to being cut off. 2. Prunes Fruitful Branches Critical truth: Pruning is NOT punishment. It's removing what we don't need so we can put more energy into what we do need. The Greek word is kathairo (καθαίρω) meaning "to cleanse" or "to purify"—where we get "catharsis." It's about releasing burdens, not bearing more. What Needs Pruning in Your Life? Pastor Tim invites us to reflect right now—we can all think of something: - 💭 A bad habit we're trying to shake - 🕰️ Part of our past we're holding onto - ⚠️ A toxic relationship causing harm - 🎭 Burdens or trauma we need catharsis from The promise: When we let God prune these things, we experience new growth—better growth. Our energy goes to what truly matters. 🔌 The Power of Remaining Connected ▼ "Remain in me, as I also remain in you.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 3)
chunks.text: No branch can bear fruit by itself; it must remain in the vine. Neither can you bear fruit unless you remain in me." — John 15:4 Modern Analogies Pastor Tim reimagines Jesus' teaching for our electric age: 💡 Power Supply Analogy "I am the power source, and you are the light bulbs. If you remain connected, you produce light. Disconnected, you do nothing." 🔋 Phone Charger Analogy (The Problem) "I am the ultimate charger, and you are the phones." But here's where it breaks down—phones can disconnect and still work... for a while. We do this too! We disconnect from Christ thinking, "I still have power. I can do things without Jesus." But eventually, the battery dies. That's when we rush back to church in crisis: "I need prayer! I need a care group!" We should stay connected constantly, not just when desperate. 📡 Wi-Fi Router Analogy (The Best One) "I am the ultimate Internet Service Provider, and you are the Wi-Fi routers." Without the Ethernet cable, routers do nothing. But when connected, they don't just receive—they serve. They reach out. They connect other devices to the main source. This is us! When connected to Christ, we serve others and bring them into connection too. It's not just about our connection—it's about expanding the network for God's glory. The Clear Command "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing." — John 15:5 Apart from Christ, we can do NOTHING. Not "some things with difficulty"—nothing of eternal value. But remaining in Him? We bear much fruit. Not just fruit—much fruit. 🍂 Withering vs. 🌸 Flourishing ▼ "If you do not remain in me, you are like a branch that is thrown away and withers; such branches are picked up, thrown into the fire and burned." — John 15:6 Pastor Tim shares a personal example: "We love grapes at home. Always have a bowl full. But if a grape falls off the vine, it withers. I hate withered grapes—they're nasty! Though strangely, I love raisins." When disconnected from the vine, branches wither. They become ugly, gross, useless. The same happens to our spiritual lives when we're not walking with Christ—we begin to fall apart, sometimes affecting us physically too.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 4)
chunks.text: But Remaining Brings Answered Prayer "If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you." — John 15:7 This isn't like rubbing a genie lamp. It's Jesus saying: "When you stay connected to me and understand my will, what you pray for will align with my purposes—and I will do it." It may not come exactly when or how we want, but God answers. Many of us have prayer journals showing how God has faithfully answered over years—sometimes differently than expected, but always good. 🌸 Bearing Fruit Shows We're Disciples ▼ "This is to my Father's glory, that you bear much fruit, showing yourselves to be my disciples." — John 15:8 Beautiful truth: Which part of a tree bears the fruit? The branches—not the trunk. We get the honor and privilege to be the most beautiful part of God's vine! Think of cherry blossoms in Japan—people travel from around the world to see their beauty. Why? Because the branches produce stunning blossoms. When we produce fruit, it's not for our glory—it's for the Father's glory. And that fruit proves to the world we are Jesus' disciples. It makes our faith attractive, drawing others to God. 🌳 Grafted In: The Tree of 40 Fruits ▼ The original covenant was with Israel. But through Christ, the new covenant includes everyone—Jews and Gentiles. We in Hong Kong, at "the ends of the earth," are now connected to this same vine! "You, though a wild olive shoot, have been grafted in among the others and now share in the nourishing sap from the olive root." — Romans 11:17 The Tree of 40 Fruit Pastor Tim shares about Sam Van Aken, an art professor at Syracuse University, who created an incredible grafting project: a single tree producing 40 different kinds of fruit. He chose 40 because it's a biblical number representing bounty. Through careful grafting, different varieties bloom at different times, so the tree constantly changes appearance—sometimes half looking dead while the other half blooms. Van Aken said: "These fruit are our story. None are indigenous—they were brought by people who valued them enough to carry them as a connection to their home." We Are That Tree All of us—multicultural, from different backgrounds—are grafted into one vine.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 5)
chunks.text: We produce different fruit (different gifts and service), bloom at different times, but we're all connected to the same root. Especially in Hong Kong's international context, we see this living picture: one vine, many branches, diverse fruit, all for God's glory. ❤️ The One Command: Love as Jesus Loved ▼ "As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Now remain in my love. If you keep my commands, you will remain in my love, just as I have kept my Father's commands and remain in his love." — John 15:9-10 We might think staying in God's love requires keeping many commands—all the effort, all the trying. But Jesus simplifies it to ONE command: "My command is this: Love each other as I have loved you." — John 15:12 Love—Sometimes Counter-Cultural This means: - 💔 Loving someone who doesn't love you back - 🤝 Serving someone who doesn't serve you - 😌 Treating people with kindness when they treat you poorly - 🕊️ Loving your enemies Often the opposite of what we feel like doing. But it's the truth of what we need to be doing. Not Just "As You Wish" but "As I Have" The Golden Rule says love others as you wish to be loved. But Jesus raises the bar: Love as I have loved you. "Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends." — John 15:13 Sacrificial love. Costly love. This is how Christ loved us—giving His life. And when we're rooted in Him, connected to Him, we receive the power to love this way too. 😊 The Promise: Complete Joy ▼ "I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete." — John 15:11 Jesus wants our joy to be complete—fully satisfied, lacking nothing. Not temporary happiness, but deep, abiding joy that comes from staying connected to Him. This joy flows naturally when we remain in Christ, when we love as He commanded, when we bear fruit for God's glory. The vine life is the joyful life.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 6)
chunks.text: 🚀 Staying Connected from Space ▼ Pastor Tim shares a powerful recent story: Butch Wilmore, an astronaut who was supposed to spend one week at the International Space Station but got stranded for nine months when his Boeing Starliner malfunctioned. At a press conference after returning to Earth, a reporter asked about attending church from space. Butch's answer was profound: "The Word of God can continually be filling me. I needed my pastors—the finest pastors on or off, in this case, the planet—and to tie in and to worship with my church family was vital. I mean, it's part of what makes me go. Not only that, I also tied into Grace Baptist Church in Mount Juliet, Tennessee... and I would watch their service as well every single week. And it was invigorating. It was part of what I need as a believer in Jesus Christ to continue that focus. It assisted me day in and day out because I need that fellowship. Even though it's fellowship from afar... I need it." Two Takeaways 1. We Have No Excuse Butch wasn't just out of the country—he was off the planet. And he attended two churches every week! We have absolutely no excuse to miss connecting with our church community. 2. "Tied In" Language He kept saying "I needed to be tied in." That's exactly what Jesus is talking about— staying connected, remaining in the vine. Even from orbit, Butch understood: I NEED this connection. 🛠️ Four Practical Steps to Stay Connected ▼ 1. 🌱 Plant Yourself in Truth Abide in Jesus' words. Keep His command to love one another. Fill your heart and mind with Scripture. Let God's Word be the soil you're planted in. This isn't just Sunday reading—it's daily nourishment. 2. 🌿 Tend Your Spiritual Garden Practice spiritual disciplines daily to stay rooted. Like gardening, this is never-ending. Weeds come. Pests attack. You must constantly tend: - • Pray consistently - • Read Scripture daily - • Join small groups/care groups - • Worship weekly (and beyond Sundays!) - • Serve in your church community 3. ✂️ Trust the Gardener to Prune Stay humble. Let God remove what hinders your spiritual fruitfulness. Our lives aren't in our control. God sees our weaknesses—things that drain us but don't benefit us. Surrender control.

chunks.heading: Full Sermon Teaching 🎤 (pt 7)
chunks.text: Let Him cut away: - • Destructive habits - • Toxic relationships - • Past trauma needing catharsis - • Anything distracting from fruitfulness 4. 🌸 Bloom Together in Faith We're stronger when we support and encourage each other in community. Sundays are amazing, but don't stop there. Connect throughout the week: - • Join a care group or small group - • Men's Fraternity / Women's Ministry - • Serve teams and ministries - • One-on-one discipleship Just as plants grow better together, we grow better in community.

chunks.heading: John 15:1-17 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Vine and the Branches 1 "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener. 2 He cuts off every branch in me that bears no fruit, while every branch that does bear fruit he prunes so that it will be even more fruitful. 3 You are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you. 4 Remain in me, as I also remain in you. No branch can bear fruit by itself; it must remain in the vine. Neither can you bear fruit unless you remain in me. 5 "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing. 6 If you do not remain in me, you are like a branch that is thrown away and withers; such branches are picked up, thrown into the fire and burned. 7 If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you. 8 This is to my Father's glory, that you bear much fruit, showing yourselves to be my disciples. 9 "As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Now remain in my love. 10 If you keep my commands, you will remain in my love, just as I have kept my Father's commands and remain in his love. 11 I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete. 12 My command is this: Love each other as I have loved you. 13 Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends. 14 You are my friends if you do what I command. 15 I no longer call you servants, because a servant does not know his master's business. Instead, I have called you friends, for everything that I learned from my Father I have made known to you. 16 You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit—fruit that will last—and so that whatever you ask in my name the Father will give you. 17 This is my command: Love each other."

chunks.heading: Isaiah 5:1-7 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Song of the Vineyard 1 I will sing for the one I love a song about his vineyard: My loved one had a vineyard on a fertile hillside. 2 He dug it up and cleared it of stones and planted it with the choicest vines. He built a watchtower in it and cut out a winepress as well. Then he looked for a crop of good grapes, but it yielded only bad fruit. 3 "Now you dwellers in Jerusalem and people of Judah, judge between me and my vineyard. 4 What more could have been done for my vineyard than I have done for it? When I looked for good grapes, why did it yield only bad? 5 Now I will tell you what I am going to do to my vineyard: I will take away its hedge, and it will be destroyed; I will break down its wall, and it will be trampled. 6 I will make it a wasteland, neither pruned nor cultivated, and briers and thorns will grow there. I will command the clouds not to rain on it." 7 The vineyard of the Lord Almighty is the nation of Israel, and the people of Judah are the vines he delighted in. And he looked for justice, but saw bloodshed; for righteousness, but heard cries of distress.

chunks.heading: Isaiah 11:1-2 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Branch from Jesse 1 A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit. 2 The Spirit of the Lord will rest on him—the Spirit of wisdom and of understanding, the Spirit of counsel and of might, the Spirit of the knowledge and fear of the Lord. This prophesies the Messiah—Jesus as the true vine that would come from David's line.

chunks.heading: Isaiah 27:2-6 (NIV) / Romans 11:17 (NIV) / Romans 8:28 (NIV) / Luke 6:46 (NIV) / Matthew 22:14 (NIV)
chunks.text: Restoration of the Vineyard 2 In that day— "Sing about a fruitful vineyard: 3 I, the Lord, watch over it; I water it continually. I guard it day and night so that no one may harm it. 4 I am not angry. If only there were briers and thorns confronting me! I would march against them in battle; I would set them all on fire. 5 Or else let them come to me for refuge; let them make peace with me, yes, let them make peace with me." 6 In days to come Jacob will take root, Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit. Grafted Into the Olive Tree If some of the branches have been broken off, and you, though a wild olive shoot, have been grafted in among the others and now share in the nourishing sap from the olive root... Paul explains how Gentiles (wild branches) are grafted into God's covenant people, sharing in the same vine. God Works All Things for Good And we know that in all things God works for the good of those who love him, who have been called according to his purpose. Referenced in worship—when we remain in Christ, God works everything for our good. Not Just Words, But Action "Why do you call me, 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?" Jesus challenges those who claim connection but show no fruit—they're like branches that will be cut off. Many Called, Few Chosen "For many are invited, but few are chosen." From the Parable of the Wedding Banquet—God chooses those who put Him first and truly respond to His invitation.

chunks.heading: Israel as God's Vineyard / From Judgment to Hope
chunks.text: Throughout the Old Testament, God used vineyard imagery to describe His relationship with Israel. The nation was meant to be His "choice vine"—carefully planted, protected, and cultivated to produce good fruit for His glory. However, Isaiah's prophecy (Isaiah 5) delivered a shocking indictment: despite God's meticulous care, Israel produced only "bad fruit"—injustice instead of justice, cries of distress instead of righteousness. The Hebrew wordplay emphasizes how close they came to what God wanted, yet how far they missed: - מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) - justice → מִשְׂפָּח (mispach) - bloodshed - צְדָקָה (tzedakah) - righteousness → צְעָקָה (tze'akah) - cries of distress God's response was to remove His hedge of protection, allowing the vineyard to be trampled and become wasteland. This was fulfilled through the Assyrian conquest and later Babylonian exile. Yet Isaiah also prophesied restoration (Isaiah 11:1-2, 27:2-6): a "shoot from the stump of Jesse" would come— a new branch bearing fruit, filled with God's Spirit. This pointed forward to the Messiah.

chunks.heading: Jesus: The True Vine
chunks.text: When Jesus declared "I am the true vine," His disciples would have immediately connected this to centuries of vineyard imagery. He was saying: "I am what Israel was meant to be. I fulfill what the nation failed to accomplish." The word "true" (ἀληθινός, alethinos) means genuine, real, authentic—not a shadow or type, but the reality itself. Jesus is the ultimate, perfect vine.

chunks.heading: Fresh from the Upper Room
chunks.text: This teaching came immediately after the Last Supper (John 13-14). Jesus had just instituted communion, saying "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (Luke 22:20). The disciples had literally tasted the wine—the fruit of the vine. As they walked from the upper room toward Gethsemane (John 14:31), possibly passing vineyards in the Kidron Valley, Jesus used this vivid, immediate illustration. The taste was still on their tongues. The new covenant was being established in real-time.

chunks.heading: Grafting: Ancient Practice, Spiritual Reality
chunks.text: Grafting was well-known in the ancient Mediterranean world. Farmers would splice branches from one plant onto another to improve fruit quality or rescue struggling vines. Paul uses this imagery in Romans 11:17-24 to explain how Gentiles (wild olive shoots) are grafted into the cultivated olive tree of God's covenant people. Normally, you'd graft cultivated branches onto wild rootstock— but Paul describes the reverse: wild branches grafted into a cultivated tree. This emphasizes God's supernatural grace. Today, the global Church is a living picture of this grafting—people from every nation, tribe, and tongue connected to one vine, sharing one life source: Jesus Christ.

chunks.heading: Viticulture in Jesus' Time / Practical Application 💡
chunks.text: Vineyards were central to the economy and culture of ancient Israel. Grapes were used for fresh eating, raisins, and wine production. Good vineyard management required: - 🪨 Clearing stones - preparing soil for planting - 🏗️ Building watchtowers - protection from thieves and animals - 🍷 Cutting winepresses - processing the harvest - ✂️ Regular pruning - removing dead wood to increase yield - 💧 Consistent watering - even in dry seasons Jesus' audience would have understood these practices intimately, making the metaphor powerfully accessible. Spiritual life requires the same diligence as physical cultivation. "Just as fruit reveals life within a branch, the love you share reveals the abiding presence of Christ in your life." — Pastor Tim

chunks.heading: Four Ways to Stay Connected
chunks.text: 🌱 1. Plant Yourself in Truth Abide in Jesus' words and keep His command to love one another. Fill your heart and mind with Scripture— this is the soil you're planted in. This Week: - 📖 Read John 15 daily, meditating on one verse each day - ✍️ Journal: "What does remaining in Christ look like for me today?" - 💭 Memorize John 15:5: "Apart from me you can do nothing" - ❤️ Identify one person to love sacrificially this week (even if difficult) 🌿 2. Tend Your Spiritual Garden Practice spiritual disciplines daily. Like gardening, this is never-ending work—weeds come, pests attack. You must constantly tend your spiritual life. Daily Spiritual Disciplines: Morning: - ☀️ Start with prayer & gratitude - 📖 Read Scripture (even 5 minutes) - 🎵 Worship through music Throughout Day: - 🙏 Pray without ceasing (1 Thess 5:17) - 💚 Practice loving actions - 📱 Limit distractions (phone, media) Evening: - 🌙 Reflect on God's faithfulness - 📝 Journal answered prayers - 😌 Confess & surrender burdens Weekly: - ⛪ Attend church (no excuses!) - 👥 Connect with care group - 🤝 Serve in a ministry ✂️ 3. Trust the Gardener to Prune You Stay humble and let God remove what hinders your spiritual fruitfulness. Remember: pruning isn't punishment— it's removing what you don't need so you can flourish. Self-Examination Questions: - 🔍 What habits drain my energy without producing spiritual fruit? - 💔 Are there toxic relationships I need to address or release? - ⏰ What am I prioritizing that keeps me from God? - 🎭 What burdens from my past do I need catharsis from? - 🧱 What fears or control issues am I holding onto instead of surrendering? Action: Choose one area to surrender to God this week. Pray specifically for His pruning work. Consider sharing with a trusted friend or care group leader for accountability. 🌸 4. Bloom Together in Faith We're stronger when we support and encourage each other in community. Just as plants grow better together, we flourish best in Christian fellowship. Community Connection Checklist: I attend church weekly (in-person or online with intention) I'm part of a care group or small group I serve in at least one church ministry I have an accountability partner or mentor I regularly check in on other believers (not just receiving support) Challenge: If you checked fewer than 3 boxes, reach out to your church this week to explore one new way to connect.

chunks.heading: Special Applications / Personal Reflection & Prayer 🙏
chunks.text: ❄️ If You're in a Spiritual Winter Remember Pastor Tim's lesson: "Do you need to drink water in winter?" Even in dry seasons, desert times, or spiritual winters, you still need God's living water. Don't neglect your connection thinking you can "come back when spring arrives." Remain in Christ especially during winter seasons—this is when roots grow deeper. 🌐 For Those Worshiping Online Like Butch Wilmore worshiping from the International Space Station, you can be "tied in" even from afar. But remember: online church is a supplement, not a replacement for in-person community when possible. Tips: Set aside dedicated worship time, minimize distractions, participate in comments/chat, and find ways to serve your local church remotely. 🕊️ Good Friday Practice: Fasting Island ECC is practicing fasting on Good Friday as a church. Consider skipping one meal and using that time for prayer, Scripture reading, or worship. Physical hunger becomes a reminder of our spiritual need for God. We then break fast together during the Good Friday service when we take communion—tasting the fruit of the true vine. Connection to sermon: Fasting is another form of pruning—removing physical nourishment temporarily to deepen spiritual nourishment. Take a moment of quiet before God. Imagine yourself as a branch connected to Jesus, the true vine. What does that connection feel like right now? Strong and vibrant? Weak and withering? Somewhere in between? Be honest with God. He already knows. He's the gardener who sees every branch, every leaf, every bud of fruit.

chunks.heading: Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 🌱 On Connection - • How connected do I feel to Jesus right now? What evidence of connection exists in my life? - • When do I feel most "plugged in" to Christ? When do I feel disconnected? - • Am I treating Jesus like a phone charger—connecting only when my "battery" is low? - • What would it look like for me to "abide" constantly, not just in crisis moments? ✂️ On Pruning - • What is God pruning from my life right now? How am I responding—with resistance or surrender? - • What habits, relationships, or priorities are draining my spiritual energy without producing fruit? - • Do I trust that God's pruning is for my good, not my harm? - • What past burdens or trauma do I need catharsis from? Am I willing to seek help (support groups, counseling, prayer)? 🍇 On Fruit-Bearing - • What fruit is visible in my life? Love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control? (Gal 5:22-23) - • Am I bearing fruit for God's glory or my own recognition? - • Do others see Christ in me through the love I show? - • Where am I trying to produce fruit in my own strength instead of remaining in Christ? ❤️ On Love - • Who is difficult for me to love right now? How is God calling me to love them "as Christ loved"? - • Am I loving sacrificially, or only when it's convenient? - • Where am I showing love in action, not just words? - • How can I serve someone this week who cannot serve me back? 🌸 On Community - • Am I "tied in" to a church community, or am I isolated? - • Who are my fellow branches helping me grow? Who am I helping? - • Do I view church as optional or vital? Am I making excuses to disconnect? - • How can I serve others in my church family this month?

chunks.heading: Guided Prayer
chunks.text: Pray through each section slowly. Let the Holy Spirit speak to your heart. 🙌 Adoration "Jesus, You are the true vine—the fulfillment of all God's promises. You are the source of all life, all fruitfulness, all spiritual nourishment. I worship You as the one who makes it possible for me to have a relationship with the Father. You are faithful, unchanging, and eternally alive." 😔 Confession "Lord, I confess the times I've tried to bear fruit in my own strength. Forgive me for disconnecting from You and thinking I could manage on my own. I confess [name specific areas where you've been spiritually disconnected]. I've called You 'Lord' with my words but not always with my actions. Cleanse me, prune me, and restore my connection to You." 🙏 Petition "Father, I ask that You help me remain in Christ daily—moment by moment. Give me discipline to tend my spiritual garden through prayer, Scripture, worship, and community. Grant me the humility to let You prune what needs removing from my life. Help me trust Your process, even when it's painful. Empower me to love sacrificially as Jesus loved me. Fill me with Your Spirit so I can bear much fruit for Your glory." 🙏 Intercession "Lord, I pray for my church community—that we would all remain connected to You as branches to a vine. I pray for those who are withering, disconnected, or in spiritual winter—bring them back to life. I pray for [name specific people God brings to mind] who need to experience Your love and connection. Use me as a branch to reach out and bring others into Your vineyard." 💚 Thanksgiving "Thank You, Jesus, for being the true vine. Thank You for the new covenant in Your blood that allows me to be grafted in. Thank You for the promise that when I remain in You, I will bear much fruit. Thank You for the community of believers around the world connected to the same vine. Thank You for the complete joy You offer. I am grateful for [name specific blessings or answered prayers]." ✨ Commitment "Lord, I commit to staying tied in to You this week. I commit to [name specific action from the application section]. I choose to trust You as my Gardener. I surrender control and invite Your pruning work. Help me bloom where I'm planted and serve the branches around me. May my life produce fruit that draws others to You. In Jesus' name, Amen."

chunks.heading: Scripture to Meditate On / 🌟 Final Encouragement
chunks.text: "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing." — John 15:5 Memorize this verse. Let it be your anchor when you're tempted to disconnect or rely on your own strength. "My command is this: Love each other as I have loved you." — John 15:12 Ask God daily: "How can I love someone today the way You love me?" You are not just any branch—you are part of the true vine. You've been chosen, grafted in, and positioned to bear beautiful fruit for God's glory. The same life that flows through Jesus flows through you when you remain connected. The same love, the same power, the same Spirit. Stay connected. Trust the Gardener. Love sacrificially. Bloom together. 🍇✨

sermons.title: I AM: The Way, The Truth and The Life | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Sermon Overview / Central Message
chunks.text: Do not let your heart be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me. — Jesus Christ, John 14:1 Jesus makes the most exclusive and radical claim in all of religious history: "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). This statement is not arrogant presumption—it is truth backed by the resurrection. Jesus doesn't offer Himself as a part-time hobby or spiritual supplement. He claims to be the exclusive pathway to God, the embodiment of truth itself, and the source of all genuine life. What troubles you right now? Jesus speaks directly into our anxieties, our burdens, our fears, saying: "Do not let your heart be troubled." We have responsibility for our emotional responses, and God equips us to face whatever troubles us through faith in Christ. 🛤️ The Way Jesus is not one path among many—He is THE way. The early church was known as "the Way" because of this exclusive claim. ✨ The Truth In a world of competing narratives and lies, Jesus embodies objective truth. "You will know the truth, and the truth will set you free." 🌱 The Life Christ is not interested in an hour on Sunday. He offers abundant, purposeful, eternal life that transforms everything.

chunks.heading: What Troubles You?
chunks.text: ▼ Jesus begins with a question that cuts to the heart: What troubles you right now? Not theoretically, but in this very moment—what feels heavy, burdensome, draining? He then gives a directive: "Do not let your heart be troubled" (John 14:1). The Greek grammar here is a present imperative passive—meaning "stop being troubled." This places responsibility on us for our emotional responses. Yet Jesus Himself was troubled. In John 13, knowing Judas would betray Him and Peter would deny Him, Scripture says "Jesus was troubled in spirit." So it's not about suppressing emotions or growing numb—it's about what we're troubled about and how we respond. God never commands us to do something without equipping us for it. We are not innocent bystanders to emotional bursts. We have capacity and responsibility to respond appropriately through faith.

chunks.heading: I AM the Way
chunks.text: ▼ When Jesus said "Where I am going, you cannot come," the disciples were confused. Thomas voiced what everyone was thinking: "Lord, we don't know where you're going, so how can we know the way?" Jesus responds not with logistics, but with identity: "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Jesus doesn't say the church is the way. He doesn't say having good theology is the way. Memorizing scripture is the way. Joining a care group is the way. All of these things are good, but these are a means to an end to help us encounter Jesus. — Pastor Brett This is an exclusive claim—offensive to modern ears. But it's backed by something unique: the resurrection. There are no bones of Jesus. No tomb where you can pay respects. He conquered death, which validates His claim to be the only way to God. The early church wasn't called "Christians" at first (that came later as an insult). They were known as "the Way"—followers of the one who claimed to be the exclusive path to the Father.

chunks.heading: I AM the Truth
chunks.text: ▼ Jesus said: "You will know the truth, and the truth will set you free" (John 8:32). The Jewish leaders were offended—"We're not slaves! We don't need freedom!" But Jesus pushed back: "Everyone who sins is a slave to sin." Then He escalated: "You belong to your father, the devil, and you want to carry out your father's desires. He was a murderer from the beginning... he is a liar and the father of lies" (John 8:44). There is a cosmic battle between truth and lies, light and darkness. God speaks truth. The enemy seeks to destroy through deception. We live on this battlefield daily, with both vying for our affection and attention. Truth is liberating. It provides clarity, removes instability, gives confidence. When we know what Jesus says is true, we can bank our entire life on it.

chunks.heading: I AM the Life
chunks.text: ▼ "For in him we live and move and have our being" (Acts 17:28). Jesus is not offering Himself as a part-time hobby to fit into your busy schedule. He's not interested in an hour and a half on Sunday only to be forgotten the other 142.5 hours of the week. Jesus says: "I am life." And when you come to Him, He is the "transcendental interferer" (C.S. Lewis)—He will disrupt everything, change your affections, shift what brings you joy. Christ, who is your life. — Colossians 3:4 You cannot separate secular and sacred. There is no "God's area" and "my area." All of life—career, relationships, passions, hobbies, money, schedule—comes under the beautiful umbrella of life in Christ. It's scary to surrender completely, to not hold back certain areas. But it's only in that all-out surrender that we realize: it's so worth it. He gives purpose, meaning, hope, and contentment. In God's economy, nothing is wasted.

chunks.heading: C.S. Lewis: Liar, Lunatic, or Lord?
chunks.text: ▼ C.S. Lewis, in his journey to faith detailed in Surprised by Joy, wrestled with Jesus's exclusive claims. He called Christ the "transcendental interferer." What mattered most of all was my deep-seated hatred of authority, my monstrous individualism, my lawlessness. No word in my vocabulary expressed hatred than the word interference. But Christianity placed at the center what then seemed to me a transcendental interferer. — C.S. Lewis, Surprised by Joy Lewis wanted an area—however small—where he could post a sign: "No admittance. This is my business alone." But God would not stay out. Otherwise He's not Lord of all, just Lord of some. Lewis famously presented three options for understanding Jesus's claims: Liar Jesus knew He was deceiving people but did it purposely with whimsical power. Lunatic He was crazy—didn't know He was lying, just went around making false claims. Lord His outlandish claims are actually true—validated by His resurrection from the dead. There are no bones of Jesus. No temple in Jerusalem where you can see His remains. If there were, we'd close the church. It would disprove everything. But the empty tomb backs up His claim: He is the way, the truth, and the life.

chunks.heading: Key Bible Verses / Modern Lies Versus Biblical Truth
chunks.text: John 14:1-6 (NIV) "Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me. My Father's house has many rooms; if that were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you? And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am. You know the way to the place where I am going." Thomas said to him, "Lord, we don't know where you are going, so how can we know the way?" Jesus answered, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." John 8:32 (NIV) "Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free." John 8:44 (NIV) "You belong to your father, the devil, and you want to carry out your father's desires. He was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks his native language, for he is a liar and the father of lies." Acts 9:2 (NIV) "...and asked him for letters to the synagogues in Damascus, so that if he found any there who belonged to the Way, whether men or women, he might take them as prisoners to Jerusalem." Acts 17:28 (NIV) "For in him we live and move and have our being." Colossians 3:4 (NIV) "When Christ, who is your life, appears, then you also will appear with him in glory." John 13:21 (NIV) "After he had said this, Jesus was troubled in spirit and testified, 'Very truly I tell you, one of you is going to betray me.'" Jesus said the enemy is the "father of lies" (John 8:44). We live on a battlefield where truth and deception compete for our hearts. Here are today's greatest lies exposed by Scripture:

chunks.heading: ❌ Independence is the ultimate pursuit
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: Don't need anybody. Be financially independent. Work for yourself. Do what you want, when you want, how you want. Don't accommodate anyone. The Truth: As parents, our goal isn't to make children independent from us—it's to transfer their dependence from us to God. To grow independent is to be stiff-necked, to resist authority, to resent submitting to anyone else's desires. This is anti-Christian. "We are members of one body. When one suffers, we all suffer. When one rejoices, we all rejoice." We were created for interdependence, not independence.

chunks.heading: ❌ Vulnerability is weakness
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: Don't let people see your weakness. Present an image. Manage your reputation. Show your wins, victories, strengths—then pretend to be humble. But don't actually let people in. The Truth: For the Christian, vulnerability IS strength. There's life in being honest, authentic, real. You can admit weakness because you're secure in your faith in Jesus Christ. You don't have to manage a reputation when God is your defender. "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:9).

chunks.heading: ❌ Sex can be casual or recreational
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: "Friends with benefits." Sex is just physical recreation with no deeper implications. The Truth: Sex designed by God is to be monogamous, soul-melting intimacy between two people in marriage. It is sacred, not casual. It always has spiritual and emotional implications. "Flee from sexual immorality. All other sins a person commits are outside the body, but whoever sins sexually, sins against their own body" (1 Corinthians 6:18).

chunks.heading: ❌ Sabbath rest is outdated and unrealistic
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: "I'm too important to take a day off. I'm just so busy." We wear busyness as a badge of honor. The Truth: We should be embarrassed, even ashamed. God, the Creator of the universe, worked six days and rested one. When you say "I can't," you're saying you're more important than God. Don't be a fool. You need rest. Sabbath is a display of faith—saying "I can take a break for 24 hours and the world doesn't fall apart. I don't lose my job." If you have a crisis, please don't have it on Friday (Pastor Brett's day off)!

chunks.heading: ❌ Private sin isn't harmful
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: If my sin only affects me, if nobody knows about it, it doesn't have a ripple effect. The Truth: It ALWAYS has a ripple effect. You cannot sin in a vacuum. Secret sin shapes your character, hardens your heart, damages your relationships with God and others—even when hidden. "Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (Hebrews 4:13).

chunks.heading: ❌ Relationships can be formed efficiently
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: We're Hong Kong—we love efficiency! Let's do relationships faster. Schedule quality time. "Hey 13-year-old son, 6:30-7:00 tonight, let's have quality time where you unveil your soul to me." The Truth: No 13-year-old will do that. You have to invest quantity time, and then you might get small nuggets of quality time. Marriage? You gotta spend time together—good time, bad time, easy time, hard time, talk time, goofing-off time. TIME. Relationships cannot be squeezed into an efficiency paradigm. Love takes time. Trust takes time. Intimacy takes time.

chunks.heading: ❌ Live your truth
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: So popular. So many people say it. I kind of know what people mean... The Truth: But listen—you don't have a truth. There IS truth. And you should live according to truth, but you don't get to declare what is true. You don't get to "live your truth" unless it IS God's truth. "I am the way and the TRUTH and the life" (John 14:6). Jesus doesn't say "live your truth"—He says "I AM the truth." Truth is objective, not subjective.

chunks.heading: ❌ All roads lead to God
chunks.text: ▼ The Lie: All religions are basically the same. All paths lead to the same mountaintop. It doesn't matter what you believe as long as you're sincere. The Truth: That's really emotionally satisfying, isn't it? But God told us that's not how it works. Jesus is unique among all gods. Only He died and rose again. Only He conquered death. Yes, all roads lead to the same mountaintop—but not all faiths lead to the same God. "No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). If it weren't necessary to go tell people about Jesus, we could save a lot of money and time. But we go because it's necessary. That's why missions matter. Perhaps the most famous modern poem is Invictus by William Henley. It ends: "I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul." Great poem. Horrible theology. Great poem, bad mantra for your life. — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: 💭 Reflect on Your Troubles
chunks.text: Jesus asks: What troubles you right now? Not theoretically—what feels heavy as you sit here today? He commands: "Do not let your heart be troubled." You have responsibility for your emotional responses. What would it look like to bring that trouble to Jesus instead of carrying it alone? Journaling prompt: Write down what troubles you most right now. Then write a prayer surrendering it to Jesus, the one who was troubled for you. 🛤️ Embrace the Exclusive Way Stop apologizing for the exclusivity of Jesus. His resurrection backs up His claim. How can you graciously but confidently share that Jesus is THE way—not A way—with someone this week? 🔍 Identify the Lies You Believe Review the list of modern lies. Which one resonates most with you? Where have you been living by cultural wisdom instead of biblical truth? Confess it and ask God to renew your mind. 🚪 Remove the "No Admittance" Signs C.S. Lewis wanted areas of his life where God couldn't interfere. What area are you holding back from Jesus? Career? Finances? A relationship? A secret sin? Invite Him into ALL of it. 🌍 Live Under the Umbrella of Life Stop separating "secular" and "sacred." Christ IS your life—not just your Sunday life. How does knowing Jesus transform how you work, parent, spend money, or use your time? 🙌 Cultivate Gratitude for the Gospel You know the only way to heaven. Why weren't you born in a remote village without access to the gospel? Take time this week to worship God for His kindness in revealing Himself to you. ✉️ Invite Someone to Easter Many friends will never follow you to church—except Easter (the "CEOs": Christmas and Easter Only). Jesus loved them and died for them. Pray about who to invite to Good Friday or Easter Sunday services. Jesus is not offering himself as a part-time hobby. Jesus is not interested in squeezing out an hour and a half of your schedule only to be forgotten the other hundred and forty-two and a half hours of the week. Jesus says, I am life. — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: Prayer & Reflection
chunks.text: Father in heaven, we are grateful for your word that it is true. We are grateful for Jesus, who endured such hostility against sinners, who did not lose hope, so that the mission could be fulfilled—that he would be crucified, and that he would be raised from the dead, proving to an on-looking world that he is unique. — Pastor Brett's Closing Prayer

chunks.heading: 🙏 Reflect & Respond
chunks.text: Questions for Meditation - 1. What does it mean for you personally that Jesus is THE way, not just A way? - 2. Which modern lie from today's sermon do you most struggle with? - 3. Is there an area of your life where you've posted a "No Admittance" sign to God? - 4. How does Jesus being "your life" change how you approach Monday morning? - 5. Who is one person you can invite to experience the hope of Easter this week? A Prayer to Pray Jesus, You are the Way when I feel lost. You are the Truth when I'm surrounded by lies. You are the Life when everything feels dead and meaningless. I confess I've tried to keep parts of my life separate from You—my career, my relationships, my secret struggles. But You are Lord of ALL, not Lord of some. I surrender every area to You today. Help me stop being troubled by things that don't matter eternally, and instead trust You with everything that does. Thank You for dying and rising to prove You are who You say You are. I believe. Help my unbelief. In Your name, Amen. 📅 This Holy Week As we approach Good Friday and Easter Sunday, consider a spiritual discipline: Fast during Friday lunch. Don't just skip the meal—use that time to pray, read Scripture, or take a reflective walk. Then break your fast with communion at the Good Friday service (4pm or 6pm). 🌍 Mission Heart Island ECC is never inward-looking. While preparing for a new building, we're also sending mission teams to Thailand, Vietnam, and beyond. Consider: What if God is calling YOU to go on a short-term mission trip this year? Check the brochure. Pray about it. And enjoy the free Vietnamese coffee at the Global Outreach booth! 💬 Share Your Story If this sermon impacted you, share it. Tag a friend who needs to hear that Jesus is the way, the truth, and the life. The early church was known as "the Way"—let's keep living and sharing that exclusive, beautiful truth.

chunks.heading: ✝️ The Heart of the Gospel
chunks.text: As emotionally unsatisfying as exclusivity may be, think what it means for YOU that God revealed Himself to you. There's only one way to heaven, and you KNOW it. Why weren't you born in a remote village without a church, Christian, or Bible? We may not have complete answers, but when we think about ourselves, we're filled with gratitude. We worship a God who loved us enough to send His Son as THE way, THE truth, and THE life.

sermons.title: I AM: The Resurrection and The Life | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Central Message 🎯 / Key Scripture: John 11:25-26 📖
chunks.text: The resurrection of Jesus is not just a future promise—it is a present reality that transforms how we think, live, and hope today. Jesus declares, "I am the resurrection and the life," offering renewal to the dead and broken places in our lives right now. ▼ "Jesus said to her, 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die; and whoever lives by believing in me will never die. Do you believe this?'"

chunks.heading: Main Themes 🌟
chunks.text: ▼ 1. The Historical Reality of Resurrection 🏺 Jesus died on the cross and rose on the third day, appearing to over 500 witnesses who could verify His identity. 2. Future Promise ⏳ We will conquer death, receive glorified bodies, and experience a renewed world—all through believing in Jesus Christ. 3. Present Reality 💫 Resurrection power is available today to transform our thinking, our living, and our hoping.

chunks.heading: Three Areas Needing Resurrection Life 🔄 / Introduction: The Foundation of Faith 🏛️
chunks.text: ▼ 1. Resurrection Life to Think Differently 🧠 God can create new neural pathways, replacing destructive patterns with life-giving instincts and responses. 2. Resurrection Life to Live Differently 🚶 We are freed from the rulership of sin—our lives can be characterized by selflessness, humility, and love instead of selfishness and pride. 3. Resurrection Life to Hope Differently 🌅 Jesus offers an ultimate hope that never disappoints, replacing our worship of comfort, power, approval, and control with true satisfaction in Him. Pastor Kevin opens by reminding us that our entire Christian faith hinges on the resurrection. As Paul wrote, if Jesus had not risen, our faith would be futile and we would be the most pitied of all people. The resurrection is not optional—it is essential.

chunks.heading: Addressing Doubts: Did Jesus Really Die? ⚖️ / The Future Promise: What Resurrection Looks Like 🌈
chunks.text: Two common questions challenge the resurrection: • Did Jesus actually die? Roman crucifixion was highly effective. Victims suffocated to death, and if they lasted too long, soldiers broke their legs to hasten death. Jesus could not have survived. • Did the disciples lie? Jesus appeared to over 500 people who could have debunked any impersonator. None denied Him. The resurrection is historically credible. The Old Testament prophesied that death would be "swallowed up forever" (Isaiah 25:8). The New Testament reveals specifics: • We will receive glorified, imperishable bodies raised in power and glory (1 Corinthians 15) • We will live in a renewed heaven and earth (Revelation 21) • This promise is accessed simply by believing in Jesus

chunks.heading: The Story of Lazarus: Present Resurrection Power 🕊️
chunks.text: Jesus visits Mary and Martha after their brother Lazarus dies. Martha responds with religious statements: "I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day." But she's hiding her disappointment—Jesus was only two miles away and could have come earlier. Many of us do the same. We lean on abstract theological truths to avoid facing our pain and disappointment. We don't truly believe God cares about the small details of our lives. But Jesus reveals Himself: "I am the resurrection and the life.""I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25) - Jesus doesn't just bring resurrection in the future; He IS resurrection. His very presence brings life to dead things now. Then He raises Lazarus from the tomb after four days—demonstrating that His resurrection power is not just for the future, but for today.

chunks.heading: Three Areas Needing Resurrection Today 🌱 / The Challenge: Do You Believe? 🤔
chunks.text: 1. Resurrection Life to Think Differently 🧠 We make over 20,000 decisions daily, 90% subconsciously. Our brain creates neural pathwaysNeural pathways are like roads through a forest. The more you walk a path, the clearer it becomes. Our brains naturally follow the most well-worn paths—which can be destructive patterns of rage, defensiveness, or judgment.—patterns that can be destructive (rage, defensiveness, judgment). But Romans 12:2 promises transformation: "Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind." God's Spirit creates new pathways—new ways of responding that give life. 2. Resurrection Life to Live Differently 🚶 Every human has a fundamental problem: we are born sinful. There are two levels: • Acts of sin (stealing, lying, cheating) • Capital-S Sin (the orientation of our lives toward ourselves—selfishness, pride, self-glorification) Even if you never commit an act of sin, you're still ruled by Sin. But Romans 6 declares: "Our old self was crucified with him... that we should no longer be slaves to sin." Jesus unbinds our shackles. Will we still sin? Yes. But our lives are no longer characterized by selfishness and pride—they're characterized by selflessness and humility. 3. Resurrection Life to Hope Differently 🌅 We all have hopes: career success, relationships, ideal life, successful kids. But none of these hopes can sustain us long-term. The day after becoming partner, you feel empty. You set your sights higher. Our deepest desires fall into four core idolsThe four core idols are: Comfort (pursuing pleasure and the ideal life), Power (seeking authority and influence), Approval (craving love and affection), and Control (managing every circumstance to ensure success).: comfort, power, approval, and control. But Jesus says, "Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty" (John 6:35). When you truly follow Jesus, you discover that the ideal life is found in Him. You possess power over death itself. You are lavished with the love of God. And the God who controls the universe directs your every step. This is the ultimate hope that never disappoints. Martha believed in future resurrection but didn't believe in present resurrection power. Do you believe God's resurrection power extends to your life right now? To your inner thought life? Your struggle with sin? Your disappointments? Your dashed hopes? The application is simple yet profound: Do you believe? And if so, how will you live differently?

chunks.heading: Bible Verses 📜 / The Central Question ❓
chunks.text: John 11:25-26 (NIV) "Jesus said to her, 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die; and whoever lives by believing in me will never die. Do you believe this?'" 1 Corinthians 15:21-22 (NIV) "For since death came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man. For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive." Isaiah 25:8 (NIV) "He will swallow up death forever. The Sovereign Lord will wipe away the tears from all faces; he will remove his people's disgrace from all the earth." 1 Corinthians 15:42-44 (NIV) "So will it be with the resurrection of the dead. The body that is sown is perishable, it is raised imperishable; it is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power; it is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body." Revelation 21:1 (NIV) "Then I saw 'a new heaven and a new earth,' for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away." John 11:17-27 (NIV) - The Lazarus Story "On his arrival, Jesus found that Lazarus had already been in the tomb for four days. Now Bethany was less than two miles from Jerusalem, and many Jews had come to Martha and Mary to comfort them in the loss of their brother. When Martha heard that Jesus was coming, she went out to meet him, but Mary stayed at home. 'Lord,' Martha said to Jesus, 'if you had been here, my brother would not have died. But I know that even now God will give you whatever you ask.' Jesus said to her, 'Your brother will rise again.' Martha answered, 'I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day.' Jesus said to her, 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die; and whoever lives by believing in me will never die. Do you believe this?' 'Yes, Lord,' she replied, 'I believe that you are the Messiah, the Son of God, who is to come into the world.'" Romans 12:2 (NIV) "Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind." Romans 6:6-7 (NIV) "For we know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin—because anyone who has died has been set free from sin." John 6:35 (NIV) "Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty." John 11:38-44 (NIV) - Lazarus Raised "Jesus, once more deeply moved, came to the tomb. It was a cave with a stone laid across the entrance. 'Take away the stone,' he said. 'But, Lord,' said Martha, the sister of the dead man, 'by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days.' Then Jesus said, 'Did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God?' So they took away the stone. Then Jesus looked up and said, 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me. I knew that you always hear me, but I said this for the benefit of the people standing here, that they may believe that you sent me.' When he had said this, Jesus called in a loud voice, 'Lazarus, come out!' The dead man came out, his hands and feet wrapped with strips of linen, and a cloth around his face. Jesus said to them, 'Take off the grave clothes and let him go.'" Do you believe? This Easter Sunday challenges us not just to affirm the historical resurrection of Jesus, but to believe that His resurrection power is available for us today—in the specific, practical areas of our lives.

chunks.heading: For Those Who Don't Yet Follow Jesus 🕊️
chunks.text: ▼ Pastor Kevin extends an invitation to make the greatest decision of your life by answering two questions quietly in your heart: 1. Do you believe that Jesus, who died for your sins, is alive and has risen from the grave? 2. Will you accept Him as your Savior and Lord over your life? This resurrection life—the power to conquer death, receive a glorified body, and live in a renewed world—begins when you simply believe.

chunks.heading: For Committed Followers of Jesus 🔥
chunks.text: ▼ If you already follow Jesus, the challenge is this: Do you believe God's resurrection power extends to your life right now? Reflect on these questions: • Do you believe there is resurrection power over your inner thought life? • Do you believe there is resurrection power over your struggle with sin? • Do you believe there is resurrection power over your disappointments? • Do you believe there is resurrection power over your dashed hopes? Like Martha, many of us believe in future resurrection but struggle to believe in present resurrection power. We hide behind abstract theological truths because we're afraid of being disappointed again. The invitation: Submit the broken and dead places in your life to Jesus, fully believing that He can renew them—whether it's a broken relationship, a habit you can't break, a quirk that prevents genuine connection, or an overwhelming sense of loneliness.

chunks.heading: Practical Steps This Week 👣 / Remember 💭
