chunks.text: ▼ 1. Identify one "dead place" in your life that needs resurrection power. Name it specifically. 2. Ask yourself: "Do I truly believe Jesus can bring life to this area?" Be honest about your doubt. 3. Pray daily: "Jesus, I believe you have resurrection power. Help my unbelief. I submit [specific area] to you, trusting that you can renew it." 4. Watch for signs of breakthrough and resist the temptation to dismiss them (like the pastor's friend exploring faith). 5. Live differently: If you believe in resurrection power, your daily choices should reflect that belief. What will change in how you think, live, and hope? Jesus didn't just tell Martha, "Your brother will rise in the future." He said, "I AM the resurrection." He brought Lazarus back to life that very day. His power is not just for the distant future—it's for your life today. The question is: Do you believe?

chunks.heading: Closing Prayer from Pastor Kevin 🙏
chunks.text: "Jesus, that challenge you gave to Martha is something that I know we ought to be thinking about all the time. Because as much as it is easy to say we believe that you died for our sins and rose from the grave, I think a lot of us do not truly believe that you can fix the broken places in our lives. And so, Lord, we do submit these before you. We recognize that 2,000 years ago, you demonstrated that you had power over death. And you demonstrated that everything you taught was going to be true. And right now we are trying to hold on to those words. But we want to hold on to them dearly. And so we ask you, Lord, to perhaps if we haven't been fully believing in our daily lives, Lord, that we would submit those to you, fully believing that you will renew those things. Whether it's a broken relationship, it's a habit that we can't break, or it's a quirk that we have that keeps us from having genuine relationships. Where it's this overwhelming sense of loneliness we feel, and we don't know why it is that nobody seems to love us. Lord, there are strongholds that we all have that on this resurrection day we believe that you can renew. So God, I know that as much as we say these things in our mind, we need to be able to live them out. Help us, Lord. Help us in our unbelief. Help us gradually, every single day, to come to a more certain belief that your resurrection power is not just for the future. But it is for us individually, and it is for us even now. We love you, Jesus. We pray this in your powerful name. Amen."

chunks.heading: Personal Reflection Questions 🤔
chunks.text: ▼ 1. The Resurrection's Reality How firmly do I believe in the historical resurrection of Jesus? What doubts linger, and how can I address them? 2. My Martha Moments When have I hidden behind religious statements to avoid facing disappointment with God? What was I truly feeling beneath those words? 3. Dismissing Miracles Like the pastor dismissing signs of his friend's genuine faith, when have I refused to see God's work because I didn't want to be disappointed? What breakthrough might I be dismissing right now? 4. Neural Pathways What destructive patterns (rage, defensiveness, judgment) have become "well-worn paths" in my brain? Can I identify when and how these were formed? 5. Capital-S Sin Beyond my acts of sin, in what ways is my life still oriented toward myself? Where do I see selfishness masquerading as good deeds? 6. My Core Idol Which of the four idols (comfort, power, approval, control) do I most worship? How has this idol failed to satisfy me? 7. The Dead Places What area of my life feels most "dead" right now—like Lazarus in the tomb for four days? Do I truly believe Jesus can bring resurrection power to that specific place? 8. Living Differently If I genuinely believed in present resurrection power, what would change about how I live tomorrow? Next week? This year?

chunks.heading: For Further Study 📚
chunks.text: ▼ Related Bible Passages: • John 20:1-31 (The resurrection appearances) • 1 Corinthians 15:1-58 (The resurrection chapter) • Romans 6:1-14 (Dead to sin, alive in Christ) • Ephesians 2:1-10 (Made alive in Christ) • Colossians 3:1-17 (Set your hearts on things above) Books Mentioned: • Mistborn by Brandon Sanderson (illustration of freedom from enslavement) Additional Resources: • Wes Huff (biblical historian) on the resurrection's credibility • Island ECC's "Quick Fix" sermon series (on the four core idols)

chunks.heading: The Call to Belief ✨
chunks.text: This Easter Sunday is not just a celebration of an event 2,000 years ago. It's an invitation to experience resurrection power today. Jesus is not just offering you life after death—He's offering you life in the midst of death. Life in the midst of broken thinking, enslaved living, and false hopes. The tomb is empty. Death has been conquered. And the same power that raised Jesus from the grave is available to raise the dead places in your life right now. Do you believe?

sermons.title: Jehovah Shalom: The Lord is Peace | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Sermon Overview / Key Themes / Main Scripture References
chunks.text: In this powerful message, we explore one of God's names: Jehovah Shalom — The Lord is Peace. This peace isn't merely the absence of conflict; it's a deep, abiding contentment available to every Christ follower, even in the midst of chaos. "What comes into our minds when we think about God is the most important thing about us." — A.W. Tozer Identity in God's Declaration You are what God declares you to be — not what circumstances suggest or what you achieve for yourself. The Cycle of Israel The Book of Judges reveals a 400-year pattern: prosperity → idolatry → oppression → deliverance. Sound familiar? Gideon's Transformation From hiding in fear to becoming a mighty warrior — all because God spoke truth over his life. The Mind Governed by the Spirit Peace comes from setting our minds on what the Spirit desires, not on worldly chaos. - 📜 Exodus 3 — God reveals "I AM" - 📜 Judges 6-8 — Gideon's story - 📜 Romans 8:5-6 — Mind set on the Spirit - 📜 Isaiah 26:3 — Perfect peace - 📜 John 16:33 — Peace in tribulation

chunks.heading: Understanding God's Names
chunks.text: We're beginning a six-week series called Known, exploring different names God uses to reveal aspects of His character. This premise is foundational: what you think about God is the most important thing about you. Most of us lead with other labels — our career, marital status, achievements. But fundamentally, what you think about God drives your motivation, contentment, and understanding of how the world works. Here's the catch: What you think about God is probably wrong. Why? Because God is infinite and you are finite. Your capacity to comprehend Him accurately is limited. Plus, we live in a fallen world tainted by sin and imperfection, which clouds our conclusions about how life works. The challenge — and opportunity — is to examine what we think about God and let Scripture shape our understanding more accurately.

chunks.heading: The Names of God
chunks.text: El or Elohim appears over 2,000 times in the Old Testament, a common word for God. Then in Exodus 3, Moses asks God, "Who should I tell them you are?" God responds: "Tell them I AM THAT I AM" — meaning "I am present, I always have been, I always will be." From that encounter came Yahweh, often abbreviated and replaced with Adonai (Lord) by Jewish people who believed saying the name would minimize His character. In the 4th century BC, Jewish scribes created Jehovah by inserting vowels from Adonai into Yahweh. While not an original biblical word, it's accurate and meaningful. God uses many names because no single name can fully describe Him: - El Roi — The God Who Sees - El Shaddai — God Almighty - Jehovah Jireh — The Lord Will Provide - Jehovah Nissi — The Lord Our Banner - Jehovah Shalom — The Lord is Peace

chunks.heading: Gideon's Story: From Fear to Faith
chunks.text: Today's focus is Jehovah Shalom. The Hebrew word shalom means more than just "peace" as absence of conflict. It's a deep, abiding contentment and wholeness in the midst of chaos. This name is revealed in the Book of Judges, characterized by the phrase: "Everyone did what was right in his own eyes." Israel cycled through this pattern for 400+ years: Prosperity → Idolatry → Oppression → Deliverance → Prosperity... Enter Gideon, one of twelve major judges. We find him hiding in a winepress, threshing wheat to save it from Midianite plunderers. He's fearful, lacking courage, totally afraid. Then an angel appears and says: "The Lord is with you, mighty warrior." Let's be clear: nothing in Gideon's life says "mighty warrior." He's hiding in fear. But God speaks prophetically into his life. Gideon must now choose: Will I step into this label? Will I believe what God says about me? Or will I let circumstances rule my life?

chunks.heading: You Are What God Declares
chunks.text: You are what God declares you to be. God says so much about who you are. Rather than embracing His label, we often reject it or try to achieve something different ourselves. Some settle for less, playing a negative tape: "I'm insecure, I don't belong." But God says: "You are My daughter, My son. You have dignity, value, and worth because you're made in My image." Others create more than what God says: "I got this, God. I'll carve my own way, create my own dignity." This leads to anxiety: What's my next trick? How long can I impress? What happens when my achievements are past? This is why many face depression at retirement — their worth was tethered to career achievement rather than God's declaration.

chunks.heading: Gideon's Honest Questions / 1. Looking at Circumstances / 2. Making Comparisons / 3. Becoming Critical
chunks.text: Gideon responds with brilliantly honest pushback: "Pardon me, my lord, but if the Lord is with us, why has all this happened to us? Where are all His wonders our ancestors told us about? The Lord has abandoned us!" He looks at circumstances and bases God's presence on whether things look favorable. Sound familiar? We look at world chaos, financial markets, relationships, careers and think, "God, are You on the throne? Have You noticed?" Gideon makes three mistakes we often make: ▼ He judges God's presence by visible outcomes. But God's presence isn't contingent on favorable circumstances. The irony? He's speaking to an angel of God while saying "Where is God?" ▼ "Where are the wonders like our ancestors experienced?" Comparison is a black hole — it breeds envy or pride. We're told repeatedly to fix our eyes on Jesus, not on others' blessings or lack thereof. ▼ His feelings turn into criticism of God's ways, timing, and character. He brings his baggage to the conversation: "You've abandoned us!" This is a raw journal entry we get to witness.

chunks.heading: God's Response
chunks.text: God doesn't rebuke Gideon's honesty. Instead: "Go in the strength you have and save Israel out of Midian's hand. Am I not sending you?" Gideon protests: "My clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my family!" He's resisting the call, pointing out to God (as if God doesn't know) why he's the wrong choice. Then God speaks words that change everything: "I will be with you, and you will strike down all the Midianites, leaving none alive."

chunks.heading: The Sign and the Peace
chunks.text: Gideon asks for verification — a sincere request God always answers. (Though we don't get to determine how He answers!) God provides an extravagant sign: fire consumes Gideon's offering. Then God says: "Peace! Do not be afraid. You are not going to die." So Gideon built an altar and called it Jehovah Shalom — The Lord is Peace. Gideon found peace when he realized God was with him. Not in a general "God is everywhere" way, but in a particular, specific, and personal way. It's not enough to know God exists — we must know God is with us and for us.

chunks.heading: How Do We Know God Is With Us? / Peace in Tribulation
chunks.text: If God's presence leads to God's peace, how can we be assured? Romans 8 gives us clarity: "Those who live according to the flesh have their minds set on what the flesh desires. But those who live according to the Spirit have their minds set on what the Spirit desires. The mind governed by the flesh is death, but the mind governed by the Spirit is life and peace." Peace requires a mindset shift. It's taking our faith from a Sunday hobby to a 24/7 perspective. Not secluding ourselves into monasteries, but bringing God into every area — work, relationships, schedules, hobbies. We do our work "heartily as unto the Lord rather than people." It's seeing life through the lens of the One who made life. This isn't burdensome — it's clarity, a beautiful light way to see everything. Isaiah promises: "You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on You, because he trusts in You." Jesus gives us gritty reality: "I have told you these things, so that in Me you may have peace. In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world." Following Christ isn't an escape from trouble — it's the presence of God and the shalom of peace in the midst of chaos.

chunks.heading: The Orchestra Illustration
chunks.text: Think of an orchestra warm-up: utter chaos, screeching, discordant noise. You paid money and think, "Couldn't you have done this before I came?" But then the conductor walks out. Instruments are stilled. He commands attention. Harmony occurs. Suddenly, chaos becomes a symphony. That's what God does in our lives. The chaos, noise, anxiety, stress — they don't disappear. But when He takes the stage, when we set our minds on what He says, He makes music from it. He infuses peace in the midst of it. Even the most troublesome things gain purpose and meaning. Life isn't easy. But there can be shalom in the midst of it.

chunks.heading: (intro)
chunks.text: Judges 6:12-16 (NIV) "When the angel of the LORD appeared to Gideon, he said, 'The LORD is with you, mighty warrior.' 'Pardon me, my lord,' Gideon replied, 'but if the LORD is with us, why has all this happened to us? Where are all his wonders that our ancestors told us about when they said, "Did not the LORD bring us up out of Egypt?" But now the LORD has abandoned us and given us into the hand of Midian.' The LORD turned to him and said, 'Go in the strength you have and save Israel out of Midian's hand. Am I not sending you?' 'Pardon me, my lord,' Gideon replied, 'but how can I save Israel? My clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my family.' The LORD answered, 'I will be with you, and you will strike down all the Midianites, leaving none alive.'" Judges 6:23-24 (NIV) "But the LORD said to him, 'Peace! Do not be afraid. You are not going to die.' So Gideon built an altar to the LORD there and called it The LORD Is Peace. To this day it stands in Ophrah of the Abiezrites." Romans 8:5-6 (NIV) "Those who live according to the flesh have their minds set on what the flesh desires; but those who live in accordance with the Spirit have their minds set on what the Spirit desires. The mind governed by the flesh is death, but the mind governed by the Spirit is life and peace." Isaiah 26:3 (NIV) "You will keep in perfect peace those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you." John 16:33 (NIV) "I have told you these things, so that in me you may have peace. In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world." Exodus 3:14 (NIV) "God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM. This is what you are to say to the Israelites: "I AM has sent me to you."'"

chunks.heading: Understanding God's Names
chunks.text: God reveals Himself through many names, each unveiling a different aspect of His character. No single name can fully describe Him because He is infinite and we are finite. El / Elohim Meaning: God (general term) Used: Over 2,000 times in the Old Testament Significance: The foundational name for deity, used for both the true God and pagan gods Yahweh (YHWH) Meaning: "I AM THAT I AM" — the self-existent one Origin: Exodus 3, God's revelation to Moses Significance: God's personal, covenant name declaring His eternal presence and faithfulness Jehovah Origin: Created in 4th century BC by combining YHWH consonants with Adonai vowels Significance: While not original, it's accurate and meaningful, preserving reverence for God's name Adonai Meaning: Lord, Master Usage: Substituted for YHWH out of reverence Significance: Emphasizes God's sovereignty and our submission

chunks.heading: Compound Names Explored in This Series
chunks.text: Jehovah Shalom — The Lord is Peace Revealed in Judges 6:24 when Gideon built an altar after God assured him, "Peace! Do not be afraid." This peace (shalom) is deep, abiding contentment and wholeness even in chaos — not merely absence of conflict. Jehovah Jireh — The Lord Will Provide Abraham named the place where God provided a ram in place of Isaac (Genesis 22:14). Reveals God as our ultimate provider in impossible situations. Jehovah Nissi — The Lord Our Banner Moses built an altar after victory over the Amalekites (Exodus 17:15). God is our rallying point, our victory, our identity in spiritual warfare. El Roi — The God Who Sees Hagar named God this in Genesis 16:13 when fleeing Sarah. God sees us in our distress, isolation, and desperation — we are never invisible to Him. El Shaddai — God Almighty Used in Genesis 17:1 when God made covenant with Abraham. Emphasizes God's all-sufficiency, power, and ability to accomplish His promises.

chunks.heading: Why Multiple Names Matter / Living in Jehovah Shalom
chunks.text: Each name reveals a layer of God's character that our finite minds can grasp. Together, they paint a fuller picture of who God is: - Provider when we lack - Peace when we're anxious - Victory when we're defeated - Observer when we're alone - Almighty when we're weak As you encounter different seasons of life, different names of God will resonate more deeply. This is by design — He meets us exactly where we are. How do we move from knowing about God's peace to actually experiencing it in our daily chaos?

chunks.heading: 1. Embrace Your God-Given Identity
chunks.text: You are what God declares you to be — not what circumstances suggest or what you achieve for yourself. Practical Steps: - Identify the negative tapes playing in your mind (insecurity, unworthiness, comparison) - Counter each with what Scripture says: "I am loved" (1 John 3:1), "I am chosen" (1 Peter 2:9), "I am God's workmanship" (Ephesians 2:10) - Write these truths down and speak them aloud when anxiety rises - Stop striving to create your own dignity through achievement or approval

chunks.heading: 2. Shift Your Mindset
chunks.text: "The mind governed by the Spirit is life and peace." — Romans 8:6 From Sunday Faith to 24/7 Perspective: - At work: Do your tasks "heartily as unto the Lord" (Colossians 3:23) — excellence becomes worship, not reputation management - In relationships: See people as image-bearers of God, not as means to your ends - Facing decisions: Ask "What does God think about this?" not just "What do I want?" - In crisis: Remember God is the conductor — He makes music from chaos

chunks.heading: 3. Stop the Comparison Trap
chunks.text: Gideon compared his present to Israel's past. We compare ourselves to others' blessings or achievements. Both destroy peace. Instead: - Fix your eyes on Jesus (Hebrews 12:2), not on others' journeys - Celebrate others' wins without diminishing your own value - Practice gratitude for what God has specifically given you - Recognize that your season is unique — God's timing is perfect

chunks.heading: 4. Ask for Signs (Sincerely) / 5. Bring Honest Doubts to God
chunks.text: If you sincerely want to know God is with you, He will always show you. Every single time. But remember: - God answers on His terms, not ours — don't bargain ("If you give me X, I'll follow") - Be open to unexpected answers — He may reveal Himself in ways you didn't anticipate - Journal your requests and watch for answers — you'll build faith muscles - God's goal is revealing Himself for who He is, not giving goodies Gideon's raw honesty didn't offend God. Neither will yours. Practice Authentic Prayer: - "God, where are You in this situation?" - "I'm afraid and I don't feel like a mighty warrior" - "The world is chaotic — are You still on the throne?" - "I want to believe, but I need help with my unbelief"

chunks.heading: 6. Expect Trouble, Experience Peace
chunks.text: "In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world." — John 16:33 Reframe your expectations: - Faith doesn't exempt you from hardship — it anchors you through it - Shalom isn't circumstances improving; it's your soul being at rest regardless - When chaos comes (and it will), remind yourself: "God is still with me, God is still for me" - The conductor is on the stage — trust Him to make harmony from discord

chunks.heading: This Week's Challenge / What label am I living under? / Where am I looking for peace?
chunks.text: Build Your Altar Gideon built an altar and named it "Jehovah Shalom" to remember God's presence and peace. Your turn: Create a tangible reminder of God's peace in your life. - Write "Jehovah Shalom" on a card and place it where you'll see it daily - Set a phone reminder to pause at noon and say, "God is with me" - Journal one instance this week where you chose God's truth over anxious feelings - Share with one person how God has shown you peace in chaos ▼ Like Gideon hiding in a winepress, where are you hiding? What negative identity have you accepted that contradicts what God says about you? God's Declaration: "You are My beloved child, chosen, redeemed, forgiven, empowered." Reflection: What would change in your life if you truly believed God's label for you instead of the world's? ▼ Am I seeking peace through control, achievement, relationships, or possessions? Or am I setting my mind on the Spirit? Honest Assessment: - What circumstance, if changed, would give me peace right now? - What am I believing will satisfy my restlessness? - How often do I turn to God first versus as a last resort?

chunks.heading: What chaos needs a conductor?
chunks.text: ▼ What area of your life feels like discordant noise right now? Career uncertainty? Relationship tension? Financial stress? Health concerns? Invitation: Visualize God stepping onto the stage of that chaos. What would it look like for Him to bring harmony? Prayer: "God, I invite You to take the conductor's stand in [specific situation]. I trust You to make music from this mess."

chunks.heading: Am I comparing or criticizing?
chunks.text: ▼ Gideon compared his present to Israel's past and criticized God's absence. Where am I doing the same? Examine: - Whose life am I comparing mine to? - What past season am I romanticizing? - What am I criticizing God for: His timing? His methods? His silence? Shift: "God, help me fix my eyes on You, not on others. Help me trust Your timing even when I don't understand."

chunks.heading: Guided Prayer
chunks.text: Pray this aloud, slowly, allowing each phrase to sink into your soul: Jehovah Shalom, Lord of Peace, I confess I've sought peace in fleeting things — in control, in achievement, in people's approval. Forgive me for looking to the world for what only You can provide. I acknowledge: You are with me. Not in a distant, general way, but intimately, personally, specifically. You see me. You know me. You are for me. I receive: The identity You've declared over me. I am not defined by my failures, my fears, or my circumstances. I am Your beloved child, mighty in Your strength, equipped by Your Spirit. I surrender: My need to understand everything. My compulsion to compare. My criticism when life doesn't make sense. I trust You are the Conductor, making music from my chaos. I set my mind: On what the Spirit desires, not on the noise of this world. I choose to see my work, relationships, and challenges through Your lens. Transform my perspective. I ask: Show me You are with me this week. I want to know You, not just Your gifts. Reveal Yourself in ways I cannot miss. I'm listening. Jehovah Shalom, anchor my soul in Your unshakable peace. In Jesus' name, Amen.

chunks.heading: Scripture Memory
chunks.text: Isaiah 26:3 "You will keep in perfect peace those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you." 📝 Journal Prompt Complete this sentence: "If I truly believed God is with me and for me, I would stop _____________ and start _____________." Write your answer, then pray it back to God. Ask Him for the courage to make that shift this week.

sermons.title: Jehovah Nissi: The Lord is My Banner | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Sermon Overview
chunks.text: In this powerful message, Pastor Rick explores the name Jehovah Nissi (The Lord is My Banner) revealed during Israel's first military battle in the wilderness. Through the story in Exodus 17, we discover how God provides victory not through human strength or strategy, but through His divine presence and power. The sermon connects an ancient battle memorial to modern faith, showing how raising God's name high—like Moses raising his staff—brings victory in our spiritual battles. Just as the Marines at Iwo Jima raised a flag symbolizing uncommon valor, Moses raised the staff of God, and from that ugliness of war came a glorious moment of divine triumph. Out of the ugliness of war comes some glorious moments. — Pastor Rick

chunks.heading: Key Themes
chunks.text: Divine Sovereignty meets Human Responsibility: God provides victory, but we must participate through obedience and faithful action. The staff had no magic power—it required both divine appointment and obedient use with right internal motives. God's Visibility is Unignorable: Like a banner raised high on a pole, God makes Himself known. His presence cannot be overlooked, and He actively pursues relationship with each of us. Victory Worth Commemorating: Moses built an altar immediately after the battle to celebrate, give thanks, and pledge commitment—three essential responses when God grants us victory. From Grumbling to Glory: The Israelites went from complaining about water to witnessing God's provision in battle. Our grumbling reveals lost confidence in God's goodness, but God remains faithful even when we forget who He is.

chunks.heading: A Memorial of Uncommon Valor
chunks.text: The Battle of Iwo Jima in 1945 produced one of the most iconic images in American history: six Marines raising the flag on Mount Suribachi. That photograph, and the bronze statue it inspired, carries an inscription in gold letters: "Uncommon valor was a common virtue" along with the Marine Corps motto "Semper Fidelis" (Always Faithful). Pastor Rick notes three important elements of this memorial: it took more than one person to raise the pole, the pole was raised high so people could see what was on top, and on top was a flag celebrating victory for a country. These same elements appear in our story from Exodus.

chunks.heading: Israel's First Battle
chunks.text: The story begins with an unprovoked attack. The Amalekites ambushed the Israelites—a ragtag group of former slaves who had never fought a battle. They had no military experience, no battle plans, no strategy. They traveled with women and children who had been attacked earlier when they fell behind. Moses said to Joshua, "Choose some of our men, and let's go out and fight the Amalek army. Tomorrow I will stand on top of the hill with the staff that got in my hands." — Exodus 17:9

chunks.heading: The Staff of God's Presence
chunks.text: Before this battle, there was another critical story. The people were grumbling—actually questioning whether God was with them at all, asking "Is the Lord among us or not?" They were thirsty, impatient, and complaining that Moses had led them out of Egypt to die in the wilderness. God instructed Moses: "Go out in front of the people. Take some of the elders of Israel and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile and go, and I will stand there before you on the rock of Horeb." Moses struck the rock, water flowed miraculously, and God provided what the people needed. This same staff—used to part the Red Sea, to bring plagues on Egypt, to bring water from rock—would now be raised in battle. Pastor Rick demonstrated with a physical staff, explaining that this was no magic wand. The staff was an external and visible symbol that meant nothing unless it was divinely appointed and used obediently with right internal motives.

chunks.heading: The Battle on Two Fronts
chunks.text: The narrative focuses not on the fighting itself, but on what happened on the hill. As long as Moses held up his hands with the staff, Israel prevailed. When his hands grew tired and lowered, the Amalekites gained advantage. When Moses' hands grew tired, they took a stone and put it under him, and Aaron and Hur held his hands up, one on one side and one on the other, so that his hands remained steady until sunset. — Exodus 17:12 The battle was won because God provided the victory. The presence of God, symbolized by the raised staff, secured triumph. It took teamwork—Aaron and Hur supporting Moses when he grew tired. This represents both divine sovereignty and human responsibility working together.

chunks.heading: The Church as Banner-Bearers
chunks.text: Pastor Rick applied this directly to the church: "We need faithful leaders who are God-appointed. But we need those who follow and support, and we call those here volunteers. This is a church built on volunteers who, under God's sovereignty, are responsible for the church and how Christ's name is proclaimed." Whether passing out bulletins, working production, playing music, leading care groups, helping with kids' club—every volunteer is the church, holding up the banner of Christ's name together.

chunks.heading: Building an Altar
chunks.text: After the victory, Moses built an altar and called it "Jehovah Nissi"—The Lord is My Banner. This altar symbolized three things: 1. Celebration of Victory: Moses didn't celebrate until there was actual victory. Taking time to celebrate victories in our lives—individually and collectively—is essential. The church does this annually at Easter, celebrating the ultimate victory of the cross and resurrection. 2. Thanksgiving for God's Provision: The altar wasn't about their leadership or participation. It was genuine thanks for what only God could provide. We must pause to thank God when something good happens that He has provided. 3. Pledge of Commitment: An altar represents our pledge to do our part. In baptism, people celebrate victory over what bound them, thank God for what He provided that they couldn't provide themselves, and pledge their commitment to follow Jesus. We should create towers of granite in our memory. — Commentary on Exodus

chunks.heading: Towers of Granite in Our Memory
chunks.text: Pastor Rick shared a personal "tower of granite"—a moment when circumstances were so far beyond him that he knew God had provided. As a college junior committed to following Jesus but vowed never to be a pastor like his father, he signed up for summer missions working with children at camps. Upon arrival, the vicar announced that each team member would preach once during the summer—and Rick would start the next night. When Rick refused, the vicar gave him two choices: preach Sunday night, or be put on a plane home that Sunday afternoon. "This is a tower of granite in my life because it pushed me with a godly man not knowing any of my story to push me ahead, and I would not have been a pastor and a preacher if it hadn't been for that moment when everything changed for me."

chunks.heading: Understanding Jehovah
chunks.text: Before exploring the full name "Jehovah Nissi," Pastor Rick clarified the confusing history of God's name: Lord (capitalized) is the mainstream English translation representing Yahweh. The capitals distinguish it from "lord" with lowercase letters. Yahweh is the proper name of God, made up of four Hebrew consonants (Y-H-W-H), called the tetragrammaton. Many godly Hebrews wouldn't speak this name, possibly because of the commandment not to take the Lord's name in vain. Jehovah developed over centuries by taking vowels from "Adonai" (meaning Lord) and adding them to the four consonants of Yahweh. When Latinized, Y became J and W became V, giving us "Jehovah." Jehovah is the great miracle maker, the unrivaled wonder worker. No one can be likened to him. He is alone in Wonderland, the creator and worker of true marvels compared with which all other remarkable things are like child's play. — Charles Spurgeon A thousand lives are not equal to the eternal life which abides in Jehovah's smile. — Charles Spurgeon God communicates through new names so we know how He cares about us. He's not distant—He creates life and wants to be with us. He reveals Himself through different names that capture aspects of the one God because He wants intimacy and invites us to know Him.

chunks.heading: The Lord is My Banner
chunks.text: The word "banner" (nissi) comes from a Hebrew stem meaning "a sign that can be seen from afar off." Banners were used by tribal groups, cities, and armies to identify themselves, rally troops, and encourage people to do battle. Pastor Rick connected this to sports, showing a Chicago Bulls championship banner. "These are banners that are very visible when you walk in the auditorium. They commemorate greatness, honor special contributions like Michael Jordan's, and attract people to the team. That's what banners do." Four aspects of God are captured in "Jehovah Nissi": 1. God's Visibility is Unignorable: Banners are meant to be seen. God wants us to know He's here. He's available to you, and you can't ignore Him—He'll come after you because He loves you and wants you to have life. 2. God's Provision of Victory is Worth Commemorating: The name came after a battle. There's no doubt it was God's provision, and that victory deserves celebration. When you have victories, remember it's God who provides them. 3. God's Presence is to be Celebrated and Honored: "You have given a banner to those who honor you, that it might be lifted up because of the truth" (Psalm 60). We honor the fact of God's presence in our daily life and serve as representatives of who God is. 4. God's Character is Attractional: Banners function to attract attention, gather people together, and summon them to be seen by others. God has a gravitational pull—a tug for all who would believe. The church loves being attractional because God's character is attractional.

chunks.heading: From Old Testament to New Testament
chunks.text: Pastor Rick then showed a video of Island ECC's Kids Club singing "His Banner Over Me Is Love." The children sang about being brought to the banqueting table where God's banner over them is love, being lifted into heavenly places. "That's our kids. They helped me deliver the sermon because I really believe that captures what the altar is about, what the name is about—that the Lord is our banner. We take it from the Old Testament of the Israelites into the New Testament, where we believe in Christ and what Christ gives for us." A new commandment I give you, that you love one another, just as I loved you. You also are to love one another. By this all people will know that you are my disciples if you have love for one another. — Jesus, John 13:34-35 This changes or deepens what happened with Moses and the Israelites. The Lord is our banner because we know Jesus Christ, and we are to love the way He loved us. There's tremendous power in that.

chunks.heading: Your Battles and God's Banner
chunks.text: Pastor Rick closed with direct application: "I don't know what kind of things you have in your life, but we all have times when we have battles going on. We all have times we grumble about those. We all have struggles, situations that are beyond us." "In the midst of that, please remember the name of God, Jehovah Nissi. If it's a spiritual battle and God wants you to fight it, He will be there for you. He will lead you in that way. Don't take on battles if God's not leading you—not spiritual ones." "For us, the staff that is lifted up is the cross. So much changed about life because of the cross. It is lifted high because we know what happened after that death on the cross—the resurrection. The cross and the empty tomb suffice as the most concrete expression of what God did in Christ, and the resurrection assures us of final victory in our life. Jehovah Nissi." The worship team then led the congregation in worship, singing: "There's no other God like Jehovah. His arm never tires. There's no other God like Jehovah. Call the name, call the name Jehovah. All glory and praise is yours. Call on him, Jehovah Nissi, fight our battles."

chunks.heading: Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: Exodus 17:9 "Moses said to Joshua, 'Choose some of our men and go out to fight the Amalekites. Tomorrow I will stand on top of the hill with the staff of God in my hands.'" Exodus 17:5-6 "The LORD answered Moses, 'Go out in front of the people. Take with you some of the elders of Israel and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile, and go. I will stand there before you by the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock, and water will come out of it for the people to drink.'" Exodus 17:11-12 "As long as Moses held up his hands, the Israelites were winning, but whenever he lowered his hands, the Amalekites were winning. When Moses' hands grew tired, they took a stone and put it under him and he sat on it. Aaron and Hur held his hands up—one on one side, one on the other—so that his hands remained steady till sunset." Exodus 17:13 "So Joshua overcame the Amalekite army with the sword." Exodus 17:15 "Moses built an altar and called it The LORD is my Banner." Exodus 17:16 "He said, 'Because hands were lifted up against the throne of the LORD, the LORD will be at war against the Amalekites from generation to generation.'" James 5:9 "Don't grumble against one another, brothers and sisters, or you will be judged. The Judge is standing at the door!" John 13:34-35 "A new command I give you: Love one another. As I have loved you, so you must love one another. By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another." Psalm 60:4 "But for those who fear you, you have raised a banner to be unfurled against the bow."

chunks.heading: The Journey of God's Name
chunks.text: YHWH (Yahweh) - The Tetragrammaton The proper name of God in Hebrew consists of four consonants: Y-H-W-H (יהוה). This is the personal, covenant name of God revealed to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14-15). Out of reverence, many Jewish people would not pronounce this name aloud. Adonai - Lord As a substitute for the unspoken name YHWH, Jewish readers would say "Adonai" (meaning "Lord" or "Master") when they encountered the four consonants in Scripture. This practice preserved the sanctity of God's personal name. Jehovah - A Medieval Rendering Over centuries, scribes combined the consonants of YHWH with the vowels of Adonai, creating a hybrid form. When this was Latinized in medieval times, Y became J and W became V, resulting in "Jehovah." While not the original pronunciation, it became a reverent way to refer to God's covenant name. LORD (Capitalized) - English Translation In most English Bibles, when you see "LORD" in all capital letters, it represents the Hebrew name YHWH/Yahweh. This distinguishes it from "Lord" (with lowercase letters), which usually translates Adonai or refers to Jesus as Lord in the New Testament.

chunks.heading: Jehovah Nissi: The Lord is My Banner
chunks.text: Nissi (נִסִּי) comes from the Hebrew root meaning "to lift up" or "a sign/standard that can be seen from afar." In ancient times, banners served multiple purposes in military and civic life. What is a Banner in Ancient Context? ▼ In the ancient Near East, banners were raised high on poles and served several critical functions: - Identification: They marked tribal groups, cities, or armies with distinct symbols or colors - Rally Point: Soldiers could locate their unit and regroup around the banner during battle - Encouragement: A raised banner signaled that the battle was still being fought and victory was possible - Victory Declaration: After battle, the banner celebrated triumph and commemorated the win The banner had to be raised high enough to be seen from a distance, requiring teamwork and strength to keep it elevated throughout the battle—just as Moses needed Aaron and Hur's help. Four Characteristics of Jehovah Nissi ▼ 1. God's Visibility is Unignorable Like a banner raised high that demands attention, God makes Himself known. He doesn't hide from us but actively reveals His presence. Pastor Rick created the word "unignorable" to capture this—God's availability is so persistent that even when we try to ignore Him, He pursues us because He loves us and wants us to have life. 2. God's Provision of Victory is Worth Commemorating This name emerged after God provided victory in battle. Moses didn't build the altar during the fight—he waited until God had delivered triumph. When God gives us victories (spiritual, relational, circumstantial), we should pause to commemorate them, creating "towers of granite in our memory" that we can return to when faith wavers. 3. God's Presence is to be Celebrated and Honored The banner represents God's abiding presence with His people. We don't just acknowledge it intellectually—we celebrate it, honor it, and let it shape how we live. As Psalm 60:4 says, God has given a banner to those who honor Him, that it might be lifted up because of truth. 4. God's Character is Attractional Banners draw people together, creating a gathering point. God's character has this same gravitational pull. He doesn't coerce or manipulate, but His love, faithfulness, and goodness naturally attract people to Him. The church embraces this by being attractional—helping others see and be drawn to God's character. Other Names of God in Scripture ▼ God reveals different aspects of His character through various names: - Jehovah Jireh - The Lord Will Provide (Genesis 22:14) - Jehovah Rapha - The Lord Who Heals (Exodus 15:26) - Jehovah Shalom - The Lord is Peace (Judges 6:24) - Jehovah Raah - The Lord is My Shepherd (Psalm 23:1) - Jehovah Tsidkenu - The Lord Our Righteousness (Jeremiah 23:6) - Jehovah Shammah - The Lord is There (Ezekiel 48:35) Each name invites us into deeper intimacy with God, revealing how He relates to us in specific circumstances and meets particular needs. Jehovah is the great miracle maker, the unrivaled wonder worker. No one can be likened to him. He is alone in Wonderland, the creator and worker of true marvels compared with which all other remarkable things are like child's play. — Charles Spurgeon A thousand lives are not equal to the eternal life which abides in Jehovah's smile. Think of that—Jehovah smiles, and just in the smile of this great Jehovah God, it's like a thousand lives, an eternal life. That's the power of Jehovah's name. — Charles Spurgeon

chunks.heading: From Grumbling to Gratitude
chunks.text: Before the battle, the Israelites were grumbling against God and Moses. They questioned whether God was even with them: "Is the LORD among us or not?" (Exodus 17:7). This wasn't simple complaining—it was losing trust in God's character, goodness, and faithfulness. We do this constantly. When life gets difficult, when prayers seem unanswered, when circumstances don't make sense, we gogguzō (the Greek word for grumble that sounds like grumbling). We forget who God is and what He's already done. Application: Take inventory of your current "grumbling points." Where have you been questioning God's presence, provision, or goodness? Now contrast that with your personal "towers of granite"—times when God's intervention was undeniable. Let those memories rebuild your trust.

chunks.heading: Divine Sovereignty and Human Responsibility
chunks.text: The staff Moses raised was divinely appointed—God commanded its use. But it required human obedience to lift it, hold it high, and keep it raised even when tired. Victory came through the partnership of God's power and human participation. The staff is no magic wand. The external and visible symbol means nothing unless it is divinely appointed and the obedient use of the external form is accompanied by internal motives of the heart and spirit. — Biblical Commentary This applies directly to church life. God sovereignly works, but He invites our participation. At Island ECC, volunteers aren't just "helpers"—they're essential partners in God's work. Whether greeting, serving in production, teaching children, leading groups, or playing music, volunteers lift high the banner of Christ's name together. Application: Where is God inviting you to participate in His work? What "staff" has He placed in your hand that needs to be lifted high? Remember, it often requires teamwork (like Aaron and Hur supporting Moses). Who could support you, and whom could you support?

chunks.heading: Building Altars of Remembrance
chunks.text: Moses didn't celebrate during the battle or before victory was secured. He waited until God had delivered triumph, then immediately built an altar. This altar served three purposes: 1. Celebration of Victory - We need to pause and celebrate when God gives victories. The church does this corporately (like Easter), but individuals should do it personally. When has God recently given you a win—spiritual, relational, professional, or personal? Have you stopped to celebrate it? 2. Thanksgiving for God's Provision - The altar directed praise to God, not to human effort. When something good happens, our first response should be gratitude to God. This isn't about denying our role, but recognizing the ultimate source. 3. Pledge of Commitment - Altars represented renewed dedication. Baptism functions this way for Christians—celebrating victory over sin, thanking God for salvation we couldn't earn, and pledging to follow Jesus. Application: Create your own "altar" this week. This could be journaling about a recent victory and thanking God, sharing a testimony with your small group, or taking a specific step of obedience as a pledge of commitment. Make it concrete and memorable.

chunks.heading: Towers of Granite in Your Memory
chunks.text: Pastor Rick shared his personal "tower of granite"—the summer missions experience where a vicar forced him to preach, changing the entire trajectory of his life. These are moments when circumstances are so far beyond us that we know God provided. Application: Identify and write down your top three "towers of granite"—times when God's intervention was unmistakable. Keep this list somewhere accessible. When you're tempted to grumble or doubt God's presence, return to these memories. They're your personal Ebenezer stones (1 Samuel 7:12), markers of God's faithfulness.

chunks.heading: Fighting Spiritual Battles
chunks.text: Not all battles are ours to fight. The Israelites didn't go looking for war—they were attacked. God led them into this battle and provided the strategy. Pastor Rick warns: "If it's a spiritual battle and God wants you to fight it, He will be there for you. He will lead you in that way. Don't take on battles if God's not leading you—not spiritual ones." Many Christians exhaust themselves fighting battles God never asked them to fight. Others avoid battles God has clearly called them to engage. Discernment is crucial. Application: What battles are you currently fighting? Ask yourself these questions: - Did God lead me into this, or did I choose it myself? - Am I fighting in my own strength, or am I looking to God's "staff" (His presence and power)? - Do I have "Aaron and Hur" support, or am I isolated? - Is this battle about God's kingdom, or my ego/preferences? If you're in a God-ordained battle, keep the banner raised. If you're fighting your own war, consider laying down your weapons.

chunks.heading: The Cross as Our Banner
chunks.text: Pastor Rick concluded: "For us, the staff that is lifted up is the cross. The cross and the empty tomb suffice as the most concrete expression of what God did in Christ, and the resurrection assures us of final victory in our life. Jehovah Nissi." In the Old Testament, Moses raised a wooden staff. In the New Testament, God raised His Son on a wooden cross. That cross—followed by the empty tomb—is the ultimate banner of God's victory over sin, death, and Satan. Application: How does the cross function as your banner this week? When you face temptation, the cross reminds you that sin's power is broken. When you face fear, the resurrection assures you that death has lost its sting. When you feel unworthy, the cross proves God's love for you. Keep the cross raised high in your thoughts, your prayers, and your worship.

chunks.heading: His Banner Over Me Is Love
chunks.text: The children's song captures the New Testament reality: Christ's banner over us is love. Jesus gave a new commandment: "Love one another as I have loved you" (John 13:34-35). This is how people will recognize His disciples. Application: Who in your life needs to experience Christ's love through you this week? Consider these categories: - The Grumbler - Someone who's complaining and questioning God's goodness. Can you point them to a "tower of granite"? - The Weary - Someone whose arms are tired from holding up their "staff." Can you be their Aaron and Hur? - The Victory-Needer - Someone in a battle. Can you pray for them, encourage them, or help them see God's presence? - The Celebrator - Someone who's experienced a win. Can you help them build an "altar" and give God glory?

chunks.heading: Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 🤔 When have I grumbled against God? ▼ The Israelites asked, "Is the LORD among us or not?" Reflect on times when you've questioned God's presence, goodness, or faithfulness. What circumstances triggered that grumbling? Looking back now, can you see evidence of God's presence that you missed in the moment? Journal Prompt: Write a letter to God expressing your honest frustrations and questions, then write His response to you based on His character revealed in Scripture. ⛰️ What are my "towers of granite"? ▼ Identify 2-3 specific times when circumstances were so far beyond you that you knew God had intervened. These become anchors for your faith when you're tempted to doubt. Describe each tower: - What was the situation? - What did you need that you couldn't provide for yourself? - How did God show up? - What did this reveal about God's character? 🙌 Where do I need Aaron and Hur support? ▼ Moses couldn't keep his arms raised alone. He needed support from Aaron and Hur. What battles are you fighting where your arms are getting tired? Who could help hold them up? Conversely, whose arms could you help hold up right now? Who in your community is weary and needs someone to stand beside them? Action Step: Reach out to someone this week—either to ask for support or to offer it. 🏛️ What altar do I need to build? ▼ An altar serves three purposes: celebrating victory, giving thanks for God's provision, and pledging commitment. Where do you need to build an altar in your life right now? - Celebrate: What recent victory has God given you that you haven't properly celebrated? - Thank: What provision from God have you taken for granted without expressing gratitude? - Pledge: What commitment is God calling you to make in response to His faithfulness? ⚔️ What battles should I stop fighting? ▼ The Israelites didn't choose this battle—they were attacked, and God gave them strategy for victory. But sometimes we fight battles God never asked us to fight. Evaluate your current battles: - Is this battle driven by God's leading or my ego? - Am I fighting in my strength or looking to God's presence for victory? - Is this advancing God's kingdom or my preferences? - Has God given me specific strategy, or am I just swinging wildly? Consider whether you need to lay down weapons in some areas and pick them up in others. ❤️ How is Christ's banner of love over me? ▼ The children sang, "His banner over me is love." The New Testament reveals that Christ's love is the ultimate banner. How are you experiencing that love right now? Jesus said, "Love one another as I have loved you" (John 13:34-35). Who needs to experience Christ's love through you this week? Be specific. Challenge: Identify one person and one specific way you'll demonstrate Christ's banner of love to them before this week ends.

chunks.heading: Prayer Guide
chunks.text: Prayer of Acknowledgment Jehovah Nissi, You are my banner. You are visible, powerful, and present. Forgive me for the times I've grumbled and questioned whether You were with me. Thank You for the towers of granite in my memory that prove Your faithfulness. Help me return to those memories when doubt creeps in. Prayer for Current Battles Lord, I bring before You the battles I'm facing: [name them specifically]. Show me which of these You've called me to fight and which I need to lay down. For the battles You've ordained, give me Your strategy. Raise the banner of Your presence high so I can see it clearly. Send me Aaron and Hur support—people who will stand with me when my arms grow tired. Prayer of Gratitude Thank You, Father, for victories You've already given. I celebrate [name specific victories] and acknowledge that they came from Your hand, not my strength. I build an altar of remembrance and give You all glory. May these memories sustain me in future battles. Prayer of Commitment Jesus, Your cross is the ultimate banner raised high. You defeated sin, death, and Satan through Your sacrifice and resurrection. I pledge to live under that banner—to love as You loved, to serve as You served, to sacrifice as You sacrificed. Make me a banner-bearer who points others to Your love. Use me to hold up the arms of the weary and to celebrate with those who've experienced victory. Closing Benediction Jehovah Nissi, fight my battles. Raise Your banner high over my life. Let Your banner over me be love—love that transforms me, love that flows through me, love that draws others to You. There is no other God like You. Your arm never tires. All glory and praise is Yours. In Jesus' name, Amen.

chunks.heading: Scripture Memory
chunks.text: Choose one of these verses to memorize this week: Exodus 17:15 "Moses built an altar and called it The LORD is my Banner." John 13:34-35 "A new command I give you: Love one another. As I have loved you, so you must love one another. By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another." Psalm 60:4 "But for those who fear you, you have raised a banner to be unfurled against the bow."

sermons.title: 🩺 Jehovah Rapha: The Lord Who Heals | Bitter Waters to Sweet

chunks.heading: (intro) / The Core Challenge / Bitter Waters / The Wood / Sweet Waters
chunks.text: ❓ We all want healing, but few of us get it. How do we reconcile this with God as Jehovah Rapha? 🏺 The Israelites at Marah couldn't drink the bitter water - a picture of our fallen world 🪵 God showed Moses a piece of wood that transformed bitter waters to sweet 💧 The water became fit to drink - God reveals: "I am the Lord who heals you"

chunks.heading: The Difficult Question: Why Doesn't God Always Heal?
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin begins with a vulnerable confession: when first assigned this message on God as healer, he thought it would be uplifting for Mother's Day. But then reality hit - "we all want healing, few of us get it." Personal Stories of Loss - • A 20-year-old man losing his battle to cancer, dying a week later - • His brother's friend, 24, with rhabdomyosarcoma - despite many churches praying, she died February 9th - • The painful question: "Is this a cruel joke? How is God healer when he doesn't heal?" 💭 How This Affects Our Faith & Prayer Surface Level Impact: We don't know how to pray for sick people. Should we be bold and declare healing? Should we be more measured? Deeper Level Impact: It can lead to a crisis of faith. If God reveals himself as healer but doesn't heal those closest to us, is God real?

chunks.heading: Point 1: Original Sin Leads to Bitterness
chunks.text: The bitter waters at Marah represent the bitter existence we experience because of the fall. When Adam and Eve ate from the tree, the whole world was cursed with three major consequences: 🐍 Curse 1: Enmity Conflict between humans and the animal kingdom - nature no longer cooperates with us 👥 Curse 2: Relational Pain Difficulty in childbirth and child-rearing, conflict in all human relationships 🌿 Curse 3: Thorns & Death The ground produces thorns and thistles, and death enters the world 🌍 The Whole Creation Groans "We know that the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time." - Romans 8:22 Every wilting flower, every dying tree, every destructive storm is creation groaning under the bondage of death. 🏥 Modern Culture's Disconnect from Sin & Sickness Ancient cultures understood there was a connection between sin and sickness. Even Jesus acknowledged this when he told the healed man at Bethesda: "See, you are well again. Stop sinning or something worse may happen to you." Modern medicine has separated physical sickness from sin entirely. But while your specific sickness may not be related to your personal sin, it is certainly connected to original sin - the corruption that entered when death and decay invaded God's perfect creation. Important: This doesn't mean your illness is punishment for something you've done. It means we live in a fallen world where death and disease exist because of humanity's initial rebellion against God.

chunks.heading: Point 2: There Will Be Complete Healing of Bitterness
chunks.text: Just as God purified the bitter waters at Marah by having Moses throw in a piece of wood, God promises to reverse all the curses of the fall. This is the great hope of scripture - complete restoration is coming. 🌟 Isaiah 65: Picture of Restoration ✓ Death Reversed: "For as the days of a tree, so will be the days of my people" ✓ Nature Cooperates: "They will not labor in vain" - thorns and thistles reversed ✓ Relationships Healed: "Nor will they bear children doomed to misfortune" ✓ Animal Kingdom Peace: "The wolf and lamb will feed together, the lion will eat straw like an ox" 📜 From Old Covenant Rules to New Covenant Faith Old Covenant (Exodus 15:26): "If you listen carefully... if you do what is right... if you pay attention... if you keep all his decrees, I will not bring on you any of the diseases..." Four conditional "if" statements - essentially requiring perfect obedience to hundreds of rules and regulations. New Covenant: Much simpler - just have faith in Jesus Christ. Faith that Jesus died for your sins and rose from the grave. This is the promise given to all who confess faith in Jesus. The Israelites failed to keep the old covenant (see the golden calf in Exodus 32, just chapters later). The new covenant isn't based on our performance but on Jesus' perfect performance for us.

chunks.heading: Point 3: Some Are Healed and Get a Glimpse of Glory
chunks.text: God does heal physically today! In the 24 hours before preaching this sermon, Pastor Kevin had two friends approach him with chronic disabilities who experienced miraculous pain relief during prayer. ⚡ Recent Healing Testimonies - • Friend with chronic shoulder pain - couldn't lift arm past shoulder - pain lifted during prayer - • Friend with debilitating back pain - could barely stand - felt pain completely leave during prayer - • Kevin reflects: "It was like God was saying, 'I know you're preaching this, but I need you to see and believe that I can heal.'" 🔍 Jesus' Healing Ministry in Perspective Jesus healed many people, but for every one person he healed, thousands he didn't heal. For every one person he raised from the dead (three total), thousands remained in the grave. But those who experienced his healing got a precious foretaste, a glimpse of the future where there is no more pain and destruction. 👀 Biblical Examples of Foretastes Lazarus (raised from dead): Got to experience a glimpse of a world without death - though he would eventually die again, he tasted the resurrection power that awaits all believers. Woman with 12-year bleeding: When she touched Jesus' robe and was instantly healed, she experienced a foretaste of a world without disease and sickness. If you experience healing today, treasure it and share the story. You've been given a gracious gift of experiencing what the future restoration will be like for everyone who belongs to Jesus.

chunks.heading: Point 4: Healing and Faith Don't Always Correlate
chunks.text: Many of us carry hidden legalism that says "If I do this, then God will do that." This thinking can make us blame ourselves when healing doesn't come. ⚠️ The Danger of Legalistic Thinking - • "If I pray hard enough, God will heal me" - • "If I believe strongly enough, God will heal me" - • "If I'm holy and righteous enough, God will heal me" - • "If healing doesn't come, it's because I'm not doing these things right" ✋ Rejecting False Guilt If you're not able to "pick up your mat and walk" right now... If you've been wheelchair bound all your life and can't get up when someone prays for you... It's NOT because you don't pray hard enough or have too much doubt. This is just legalism taking on a different form. 💝 For Those Still Waiting for Healing Pastor Kevin researched many faithful ministers who struggled with chronic illness and died young - David Brainerd (29), Robert Murray McShane (29), Oswald Chambers (43), William Borden (25), Eric Liddell (43). "In times of illness, when the body is weak and the spirit feels burdened, I find solace in the assurance that Christ is my constant companion. He has borne my sorrows and understands my pain." - Robert Murray McShane What tied all their writings together: an overwhelming awareness of Jesus as their constant companion. Suffering stripped away distractions so they could be acutely aware of Jesus' presence. The Apostle Paul's Experience: "Three times I pleaded with the Lord to take it away from me. But he said to me, 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.'" - 2 Corinthians 12:8-9 Paul never got his prayer for healing answered, but perhaps he got the best gift of all - this overwhelming awareness of Christ as his constant companion.

chunks.heading: Primary Text: Exodus 15:22-26 / The Three Curses from Genesis 3
chunks.text: "Then Moses led Israel from the Red Sea and they went into the desert of Shur. For three days they traveled in the desert without finding water. When they came to Marah, they could not drink its water because it was bitter. That is why the place is called Marah. So the people grumbled against Moses, saying, 'What are we to drink?' Then Moses cried out to the Lord, and the Lord showed him a piece of wood. He threw it into the water, and the water became fit to drink. There the Lord issued a ruling and instruction for them and put them to the test. He said, 'If you listen carefully to the Lord your God and do what is right in his eyes, if you pay attention to his commands and keep all his decrees, I will not bring on you any of the diseases I brought on the Egyptians, for I am the Lord, who heals you.'" 📖 View Historical Context & Application Romans 8:22 "We know that the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time." View Context Isaiah 65:19-25 "No longer will they build houses and others live in them... for as the days of a tree, so will be the days of my people..." View Full Vision 2 Corinthians 12:8-9 "Three times I pleaded with the Lord to take it away from me. But he said to me, 'My grace is sufficient for you...'" Paul's Thorn Exodus 2:23-25 "The Israelites groaned in their slavery and cried out, and their cry for help... went up to God." Slavery Context 🐍 Human vs. Animal Conflict "Enmity between your offspring and hers" - Nature no longer cooperates with humanity 👥 Relational Pain "Pain in childbearing" and child-rearing - All human relationships affected by conflict 🌿 Thorns & Death "Thorns and thistles" - Creation corrupted, death enters the world

chunks.heading: When Someone You Love Is Sick
chunks.text: ✋ What NOT to Say - • "You just need more faith" - • "God will definitely heal you" - • "There must be unconfessed sin" - • "If you really believed, you'd be healed" 💙 What TO Say & Do - • "I'm praying for you and believing with you" - • "God sees you and is with you in this" - • "Can I bring you a meal or help practically?" - • Simply be present - don't feel pressure to have answers

chunks.heading: How to Pray for Healing
chunks.text: 🙏 Balanced Prayer Approach - • Be Bold: Ask for God's healing power to be displayed - • Be Humble: "Your will be done, not mine" - • Be Honest: Express your real feelings and fears to God - • Be Hopeful: Remember the future promise of complete healing 🎯 Prayer Focus Points - • Medical wisdom for doctors and treatments - • Peace and comfort for the suffering person - • Strength and hope for family members - • God's presence to be felt tangibly - • If healing comes, that it points people to Jesus

chunks.heading: Dealing with Unanswered Prayer
chunks.text: 🛡️ Reject These Lies - • "God doesn't love me" - • "I don't have enough faith" - • "I must have done something wrong" - • "Prayer doesn't work" ✅ Embrace These Truths - • God is with you in your suffering - • You live in a fallen world - this isn't God's final design - • Complete healing is promised in the future - • Your pain has purpose, even if you can't see it - • Jesus understands suffering intimately

chunks.heading: If You Experience Healing
chunks.text: 🎁 Treasure the Gift You have experienced a foretaste of the future restoration. This is a precious privilege - a glimpse of what life will be like when all curses are reversed. 📢 Share Your Story - • Tell others about God's goodness to you - • Encourage those still waiting for healing - • Use your story to point people to Jesus - • Remember that this points to an even greater future healing 🤲 Stay Humble Your healing wasn't because you had more faith or were more righteous than others. It was God's grace giving you a preview of coming restoration.

chunks.heading: Core Reflection Questions / Spiritual Health Check
chunks.text: 🤔 About Your View of God - • How has your understanding of God as "healer" changed after this message? - • Where do you see evidence of the "three curses" in your own life or the world around you? - • What "bitter water" situations are you currently facing? 💭 About Your Prayers - • How do you typically pray when someone is sick? - • Have you ever blamed yourself for "unanswered" prayer? How so? - • What would change in your prayer life if you truly believed healing and faith don't always correlate? 💔 About Your Pain - • What chronic struggle (physical, emotional, relational) do you need to bring to Jesus as your "constant companion"? - • How might your current suffering actually be stripping away distractions to help you see Jesus more clearly? - • In what ways do you identify with "groaning" as described in Romans 8:22? 🌅 About Future Hope - • Which aspect of the Isaiah 65 restoration are you most looking forward to? - • Have you experienced any "foretastes" of healing or restoration in your life? - • How does the promise of future complete healing change how you view your current struggles? Legalism Check: Do you ever think healing depends on your spiritual performance? Often Sometimes Rarely Faith Foundation: Is your hope in healing or in the Healer? Mostly in healing Mostly in the Healer Companion Awareness: How real is Jesus' presence to you in difficulty? Feels distant Sometimes real Very real

chunks.heading: Growth Planning
chunks.text: This Week I Will... - Pray for someone who is sick, focusing on God's presence with them rather than demanding healing - Identify and confess any legalistic thinking about healing in my life - Meditate on Isaiah 65's vision of restoration when I feel overwhelmed by brokenness - Practice being aware of Jesus as my "constant companion" in daily struggles Scripture to Memorize "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." - 2 Corinthians 12:9

chunks.heading: 🙏 Closing Prayer
chunks.text: "God, we come before you with heavy hearts, knowing many of us need your miraculous hand of healing. We pray that if it's in your will, you would give us glimpses of the future where there's no more pain and sickness. But we find our strength in knowing what you have promised - that there will be a future where all bitterness is done away with. Help us find encouragement in knowing that you are our constant companion. When we cry, when the pain seems too much, remind us that you are right there with us. Help us fix our eyes upon you, Jehovah Rapha, believing that even if our circumstances don't communicate this truth, we know there will be complete healing in the future. In Jesus' name, Amen."

sermons.title: El Roi & Jehovah Jireh: The God Who Sees and Provides | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 👁️ El Roi / 🙌 Jehovah Jireh / 📝 Sermon Summary
chunks.text: The God Who Sees Hebrew: אֵל רֳאִי The Lord Will Provide Hebrew: יְהוָה יִרְאֶה This powerful message explores two profound names of God through the intertwined stories of Hagar and Abraham. We discover how God meets us in our deepest vulnerabilities, sees our pain, and provides for our needs. Part 1 (Jessica): The story of Hagar, an Egyptian slave who encountered God in the desert and named Him "El Roi" – the God who sees. In her abandonment and desperation, God saw her, heard her cry, and promised her freedom. Part 2 (Pastor Tim): The story of Abraham and Isaac on Mount Moriah, where God tested Abraham's faith and provided a ram as a substitute. Here we encounter "Jehovah Jireh" – the Lord who provides, foreshadowing the ultimate provision of Jesus.

chunks.heading: 🔑 Key Themes
chunks.text: - 🌊 God Sees Our Vulnerability: Even when we feel invisible, abandoned, or alone, God sees us completely - 💧 God Hears Our Cry: He is attentive to our pain and responds with compassion - 🕊️ God Provides Freedom: He works even through injustice to bring about redemption - 🐏 God Provides a Substitute: The ram in the thicket points to Jesus, our ultimate provision - ⛰️ Faith is Tested and Proven: Abraham's obedience revealed the genuineness of his faith

chunks.heading: Part 1: El Roi – The God Who Sees (Jessica)
chunks.text: 📜 The Promise to Abraham At 75 years old, Abram received God's call to leave his homeland with an incredible promise: he would become the father of nations. There was just one problem – he had no children. For nine years, Abram and Sarai (65 years old when called) wandered, waiting for this promise to be fulfilled. 💡 Sarai's Solution Sarai proposed a culturally acceptable solution: Abram could have a child through her Egyptian slave Hagar. In ancient Near Eastern culture, any child born to a slave belonged to the master and mistress. Though this seems shocking to us, it was reasonable in their context. "Abram, you're to be the father of nations, but I cannot bear children. Take Hagar – she's young. Any child born to her will be my child." ⚡ Conflict and Mistreatment When Hagar became pregnant, she began to disdain Sarai (slavery, forced marriage, forced pregnancy provide pretty clear reasons). The power dynamic shifted, and Sarai became angry. When she complained to Abram, he responded: "Your slave, your problem." Sarai's solution was to mistreat Hagar so severely that a pregnant slave with no resources chose to flee to the desert. 👁️ God Sees Hagar Genesis 16:7-13 – The angel of the Lord found Hagar in the desert and asked, "Where have you come from, and where are you going?" God asked this question not because He didn't know, but to bring Hagar clarity. She knew where she'd come from, but had no idea where she was going. But God did. God promised Hagar: "I will increase your descendants so much that they will be too numerous to count... You will give birth to a son. Name him Ishmael, for the Lord has heard your misery." This was the same promise given to Abram! A lowly slave girl would become the mother of nations. Her son would be "like a wild donkey" – meaning free. In a culture where slave children were slaves for life, this was miraculous. 🏷️ Hagar Names God Remarkably, Hagar didn't name God "El Shema" (God who hears), even though God said He heard her affliction. Instead, she named Him El Roi – "You are the God who sees me." "This is even more personal because when she names God 'God sees me,' what she's saying is that God has looked at her heart. He knows the deepest desire, the longing that she wants. God has seen her personally. He knows her pain and he knows her heart, and he knows that she desires freedom." – Jessica 🚪 God Works for Freedom Years later, after Sarah gave birth to Isaac, she demanded that Abraham dismiss Hagar and Ishmael. Though this troubled Abraham, God told him to follow through. Here we see God's beautiful work: He used Sarah's disdain and even injustice to grant Hagar and Ishmael the freedom she longed for. However, Abraham sent them into the desert with only one skin of water (perhaps 15 liters) and bread – barely enough for three days in the desert heat. When the water ran out, Hagar put Ishmael under a bush because she couldn't bear to watch him die. 💧 God Sees and Provides Genesis 21:17-19 – God heard the boy crying, and the angel called to Hagar: "Do not be afraid. God has heard the boy... Lift him up, for I will make him into a great nation." Then God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water. El Roi comes in our deepest vulnerabilities and pain. He doesn't just see – He enters into it and provides what we need.

chunks.heading: Part 2: Jehovah Jireh – The Lord Will Provide (Pastor Tim)
chunks.text: 🎯 Abraham's Journey of Faith Abraham and Sarah were called to leave their homeland at 75 and 65 years old – a huge act of faith. But their journey included moments of doubt: lying to Pharaoh about Sarah being his sister, taking matters into their own hands with Hagar, and laughing at God's promise of a son in their old age. When Isaac (whose name means "one who laughs") was finally born, Abraham was 100 years old. After waiting a century for this promise, they finally had their son. ⚡ The Ultimate Test Genesis 22:1-2 – "After these things, God tested Abraham and said... 'Take your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah and offer him there as a burnt offering.'" Notice the parallel language: "Your son, your only son, whom you love" echoes John 3:16 – "God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son." This test foreshadows God's ultimate provision of Jesus. "I can't imagine a greater test than that which the Lord applied to Abraham... Surely on this occasion he was tried ten times in one." – Charles Spurgeon 🌅 Immediate Obedience "Abraham rose early in the morning" and immediately obeyed. Through all his experiences – even when he tried to take control – he had learned that God ultimately provides. His experience proved God's faithfulness. Isaac, old enough to carry his own wood and understand what was happening, noticed they had everything except a lamb. Abraham's response revealed complete faith: "God will provide a lamb for us, my son." 🛡️ Abraham's Dual Faith Abraham believed two things simultaneously: - God would spare Isaac – He would provide a substitute - Even if Isaac died – God would resurrect him, because God's promise was "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" (Hebrews 11:17-19) 🐏 The Provision Revealed Genesis 22:11-14 – Just as Abraham reached for the knife, the angel stopped him: "Do not lay your hand on the boy... for now I know that you fear God." Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught in a thicket. He took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering instead of his son. "So Abraham called the name of that place, 'The Lord will provide' – Jehovah Jireh." ✅ Faith Proven Genuine This wasn't about proving Abraham had faith – Genesis 15 already said he "believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness." This test revealed how much his faith had grown. As James writes, "faith without works is dead" – genuine faith produces fruit. "Abraham believed God and was credited to him as righteousness. But in Genesis 22, Abraham obeyed God and his faith was proven genuine." – Pastor Tim

chunks.heading: 🔗 The Connection: Vision & Provision
chunks.text: God's vision leads to His provision. Two different sons in two different situations: Hagar with Ishmael needing water and survival, Abraham with Isaac facing the ultimate test of faith. In both stories, we see the same truth: God sees what we need and provides at exactly the right time. El Roi and Jehovah Jireh are not separate attributes but two sides of the same divine nature. First God sees, then He sees to it that our needs are met.

chunks.heading: Key Scripture Passages 📖
chunks.text: Genesis 15:1-6 – God's Promise to Abram › After this, the word of the Lord came to Abram in a vision: "Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, your very great reward." But Abram said, "Sovereign Lord, what can you give me since I remain childless and the one who will inherit my estate is Eliezer of Damascus?" And Abram said, "You have given me no children; so a servant in my household will be my heir." Then the word of the Lord came to him: "This man will not be your heir, but a son who is your own flesh and blood will be your heir." He took him outside and said, "Look up at the sky and count the stars—if indeed you can count them." Then he said to him, "So shall your offspring be." Abram believed the Lord, and he credited it to him as righteousness. Genesis 16:7-13 – Hagar Names God "El Roi" › The angel of the Lord found Hagar near a spring in the desert; it was the spring that is beside the road to Shur. And he said, "Hagar, slave of Sarai, where have you come from, and where are you going?" "I'm running away from my mistress Sarai," she answered. Then the angel of the Lord told her, "Go back to your mistress and submit to her." The angel added, "I will increase your descendants so much that they will be too numerous to count." The angel of the Lord also said to her: "You are now pregnant and you will give birth to a son. You shall name him Ishmael, for the Lord has heard of your misery. He will be a wild donkey of a man; his hand will be against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and he will live in hostility toward all his brothers." She gave this name to the Lord who spoke to her: "You are the God who sees me," for she said, "I have now seen the One who sees me." Genesis 21:14-21 – God Provides Water for Hagar › Early the next morning Abraham took some food and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar. He set them on her shoulders and then sent her off with the boy.

chunks.heading: Key Scripture Passages 📖 (pt 2)
chunks.text: She went on her way and wandered in the Desert of Beersheba. When the water in the skin was gone, she put the boy under one of the bushes. Then she went off and sat down about a bowshot away, for she thought, "I cannot watch the boy die." And as she sat there, she began to sob. God heard the boy crying, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven and said to her, "What is the matter, Hagar? Do not be afraid; God has heard the boy crying as he lies there.
