Complete Breakdown No love, no respect, not even friendship anymore ⏰ Act Now: Like plugging a hole in a ship, you can't make excuses. The moment you recognize contempt, address it with full urgency and priority.

chunks.heading: 🔧 Step 3: Use the Three-Step Apology
chunks.text: ✅ The Framework That Works ▼ Step Action Example 1️⃣ Name It Identify what happened Ensure both agree "When I rolled my eyes during our fight... I saw you clam up and shut down." 2️⃣ Own It Take full responsibility Acknowledge their feelings "I'm sorry. That was judgmental and rude. It made you feel like nothing." 3️⃣ Fix It State what you'll do differently Be concrete & clear "Next time we argue, I'll listen without judgment and try to understand you." 💡 Remember: - Won't solve everything, but moves you forward ✅ - Order doesn't matter - just include all 3 📝 - Practice makes it less awkward 🔄 ❌ False Apologies to Avoid ▼ Type Sounds Like Why It Fails 🔀 Deflector "Sorry I yelled, BUT you were late" Shifts blame Minimizes issue 🌫️ Generalizer "Sorry if I upset you in any way" Don't know what you did Just want them happy 👊 Backhander "Sorry for my grudge, but I never respected you" Insults while apologizing Makes you feel better, not them 🚩 Red Flag: If you say "but" → You're deflecting!

chunks.heading: ✝️ Step 4: Ground Yourself in the Gospel
chunks.text: 🌟 The Gospel as the Greatest Equalizer ▼ Why the gospel matters for overcoming contempt: Only through Jesus Christ can we achieve the wholesale perspective change needed to rid ourselves of contempt. Gospel Truth How It Changes Our View We're All Sinners Eliminates superiority - we're not better than those who've wronged us. We've all been alienated from God by sin and rebellion. We've All Been Forgiven God paid an extravagant cost (His only Son) to reconcile us. Who are we to withhold forgiveness from others? We're All Beloved We must see others as God sees them - not as pathogens, but as beloved children of God worthy of repair. ✝️ The Gospel Reframe: The person you're in conflict with is not a pathogen to avoid but a person loved by God, just as you are. Both of you are sinners. Both of you are forgiven. Both of you are precious in His sight. This changes everything.

chunks.heading: 🙏 Step 5: Take Responsibility "As Far as It Depends on You"
chunks.text: "If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone." Romans 12:18 (NIV) 💪 What You Control vs. What You Don't ▼ What You Control What You Don't Control How you view the other person How they respond to your repair attempt Your attitude toward them Whether they accept your apology Whether you attempt repair The outcome of the relationship Taking responsibility for your part Whether they take responsibility for theirs Pursuing reconciliation with urgency and priority Whether reconciliation actually happens The Reality Check: - Sometimes you'll attempt repair and get no response at all - Sometimes they'll respond, but not in the way you prefer - Sometimes the best outcome is just that you don't hate each other - Sometimes conflict is a series of back-and-forths where both contributed to the rupture 💪 Your Responsibility: As far as it depends on you, do what you can. Change your attitude if you can. View them as people, as children of God you should repair with, not as pathogens. You're responsible for your effort and your heart posture, not their response.

chunks.heading: 📋 Action Checklist
chunks.text: This Week: - ☐ Identify one relationship where contempt might be present - ☐ Reflect on whether you see that person as beloved by God or as a "pathogen" - ☐ Ask God to reveal your part in the rupture of that relationship - ☐ Practice the three-step apology with someone (doesn't have to be perfect) - ☐ Don't wait - pursue repair with urgency and priority - ☐ Remember: You're responsible for your effort, not their response

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: Use these questions for personal reflection, small group discussion, or journaling. Take your time with each one, asking the Holy Spirit to reveal truth and give you courage to act. 🔍 Understanding Contempt in Your Life ▼ - Who comes to mind when you think about someone you've been avoiding or struggling to be around? What feelings arise when you think about that person? - Are there any relationships where you've noticed signs of contempt - eye rolling (literal or mental), sarcasm, name-calling, or viewing them primarily through what they've done wrong? Be specific. - In what ways have you viewed someone as a "pathogen" to avoid rather than as a person or child of God? How did that dehumanization happen? - Think of a broken relationship. How have you contributed to its rupture? What part have you played, even if someone else initiated the conflict? - Where have you been treating relationships transactionally - asking "How does this benefit me?" rather than seeing people for who they are? ⚡ Examining Your Approach to Repair ▼ - When conflict arises, do you tend to address it immediately or delay hoping it will blow over? What motivates your approach? - Have you ever prioritized worship (church attendance, prayer, devotions) over reconciling with someone you've wronged or who has something against you? What does that reveal about your priorities? - Which false apology do you tend to use most: the Deflector ("I'm sorry, but..."), the Generalizer ("I'm sorry if I upset you in any way"), or the Backhander (using apology to insult)? Why do you think that is? - What makes it difficult for you to apologize with urgency and sincerity? Is it embarrassment, pride, fear of vulnerability, or something else? - Think of relationships you've let fall into disrepair because repair seemed too hard. What was the cost of taking the "path of least resistance"? ✝️ The Gospel and Contempt ▼ - Do you truly believe that the person you're in conflict with is as loved by God as you are? If not, what makes it hard to believe that? - How does remembering that "we're all sinners" challenge any sense of superiority you might have toward someone who's wronged you? - God paid an extravagant cost (His only Son) to reconcile with you when you were His enemy. How does that truth impact your willingness to pursue costly reconciliation with others? - In what ways have you been like the person waiting for someone else to apologize, not realizing you might be harboring contempt as the "victim"? - The sermon said that only through Christ can we achieve the perspective change needed to overcome contempt. What would it look like for you to rely on Christ's power rather than your own strength in a specific broken relationship? 🛠️ Moving Toward Action ▼ - Who is one person you need to pursue repair with urgently? What specific action will you take this week? - Practice the three-step apology: Can you name what happened, take responsibility for your part and their feelings, and state what you'll do differently? Write it out for one specific situation. - What are you afraid will happen if you pursue repair? How does "as far as it depends on you" free you from that fear? - The sermon mentioned that the strongest relationships are those that weather the sticky stuff together. What sticky situation is God inviting you to walk through rather than avoid? - What would change in your most strained relationship if you consistently viewed that person as a beloved child of God rather than through the lens of what they've done or how they make you feel? 🙏 For Those Waiting for Others to Apologize ▼ - How long have you been waiting for someone to apologize to you? What has that waiting cost you emotionally and spiritually? - Can you identify contempt (that mixture of anger and disgust) in your own heart toward the person who wronged you? How has that contempt affected you? - Even if you didn't initiate the wrong, how have you contributed to the ongoing rupture through your response? Can you take responsibility for that part? - What would it look like to see the person who hurt you as someone who is also broken, also a sinner, also in need of God's grace - just like you? - The sermon said that victims often exhibit contempt first. Are you willing to address your own contempt even while waiting for the other person to make things right? What might that look like? 💭 Going Deeper ▼ - Matthew 5:23-24 says to leave your gift at the altar and be reconciled before worshiping. Is there anyone you need to reconcile with before you can worship God in spirit and truth? Why is reconciliation a prerequisite for true worship? - The sermon described how contempt progresses from small annoyances to complete relational breakdown (the Sally and Joe example). At what stage is your most difficult relationship? What would urgent intervention look like? - Dr. Arthur Brooks said contempt is anger mixed with disgust. When you're angry with someone, it shows you care. But when disgust enters, you dehumanize them. How can you preserve the caring anger while eliminating the dehumanizing disgust? - The Gottmans' research showed they could predict divorce with 90% accuracy based on contempt. What does this tell you about the urgency of addressing contempt in any close relationship (not just marriage)? - Pastor Kevin said his deepest relationships are those that "get through the sticky stuff." What sticky situation are you currently facing that could become an opportunity for deeper connection if you choose repair over avoidance? - "Contempt will exact its full price" (Matthew 5:26). What is contempt currently costing you? What might it cost you if left unaddressed? - How does the truth that "as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone" (Romans 12:18) both free you and challenge you in your most difficult relationships? 🙏 Prayer Focus: Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any contempt in your heart toward specific people. Pray for the courage to name it, take responsibility for your part, and state what you'll do differently. Ask God to help you see others as He sees them - as beloved children worthy of repair, not pathogens to avoid.

sermons.title: The Sticky Stuff: Discouragement | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: A sevenfold strategy to dismantle discouragement in your relationships

chunks.heading: The Context / Key Scripture Foundation / What Discouragement Looks Like
chunks.text: In Exodus 6, God spoke to Moses with incredible promises: "I will bring you out. I will free you. I will redeem you. I will take you as my own people." Yet when Moses delivered this good news to the Israelites, they did not listen because of their discouragement. Their hearts had become so weary that they couldn't even receive relief. This reminds us that discouragement can be destructive — not just in our personal walk with God, but in our relationships with others. Psalm 42:5 "Why am I so discouraged? Why is my heart so sad? I will put my hope in God!" 2 Corinthians 4:1, 16 "Therefore, since through God's mercy we have this ministry, we do not lose heart... Therefore we do not lose heart." - 😔 Resigned — "I guess this is it. This is as good as it gets." - 😴 Lethargic — Lack of energy, momentum, thoughtfulness, care - 😞 Despondent — "It is what it is" What relationship in your life could really benefit from you bringing some encouragement to it?

chunks.heading: Introduction: Good News They Couldn't Hear
chunks.text: Moses reported God's promise to the Israelites, but they did not listen to him because of their discouragement (Exodus 6:9). They couldn't even hear good news. Their hearts had become so discouraged that they could not receive relief. This reminds us: discouragement can be destructive in your personal walk with God and in your relationships with others. If your heart has grown weary, if hope has faded, if your courage is lacking, that will impact those around you.

chunks.heading: Paul's Credibility
chunks.text: The Apostle Paul gives us an incredible model for not losing heart. Consider what he endured: - Five times received 39 lashes - Three times beaten with rods - Three times shipwrecked - A night and day in the open sea - Often gone without sleep Yet through all this, Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 4: "We do not lose heart." If he endured that without losing heart, perhaps our struggles aren't so insurmountable.

chunks.heading: Relational Discouragement Is Not Inevitable
chunks.text: It's not God's design for relationships to become lifeless, for marriages to become mere roommate arrangements, for friendships to fade into polite distance. That kind of weighty relationship where you're not giving into or receiving from is: - Not healthy for anyone involved - Not sustainable over time - Not God-approved — not His will for you It is not God's will for you as a follower of Christ — one who has greatly benefited from a God who pursued and loved you in spite of everything — to turn to those around you and give up, to concede defeat, to give less than you could out of discouragement.

chunks.heading: The Whole Chapter Devoted to This
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians chapter 4 is entirely devoted to not losing heart. Paul begins: "We do not lose heart" (v.1) and concludes: "Therefore we do not lose heart" (v.16). Between these bookends, he details from his personal experience how he, of all people, doesn't lose heart. "If you look at the world, you'll be distressed. If you look within, you'll be depressed. But if you look at Christ, you'll be at rest." — Corrie ten Boom

chunks.heading: The Warning / ⚡ Sevenfold Strategy
chunks.text: Galatians 6:9 warns us: "Let us not get tired of doing what is right. For after a while we will reap a harvest of blessing if we don't get discouraged and give up." If you don't have a plan to fight discouragement, if you don't have an antidote for a world that gives you plenty of data to be discouraged, if you don't have a strategy for your own soul to fight through it, then you'll give up — on people, on hope, on meaning. From 2 Corinthians 4, Paul gives us seven practical ways to dismantle discouragement in our lives and relationships. 1

chunks.heading: Make Much of God's Mercy
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:1 "Since through God's mercy we have this ministry, we do not lose heart." Paul starts at the obvious: everything is by God's grace and mercy. He knew this well as one grateful he didn't receive the retribution he deserved. God spared him and gave him what he didn't earn. You have received the same lavish, unearned, permanent acceptance from a holy God who enjoys you, delights over you, dances over you. Application: Make much of God's mercy. Not just knowing it intellectually ("Yeah, I know God loves me"), but living in light of that truth. Exaggerate it. Focus on it. Fixate on it. Build it into your life. 💡 When you worship, are you thinking about the words? Are you singing them as your own prayer? When you taste something delicious (like pastor tacos!), do you see it as God's goodness? 2

chunks.heading: Be Authentic
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:2 "Rather, we have renounced secret and shameful ways. We do not use deception, nor do we distort the word of God." Paul commends himself — not arrogantly, but authentically. "I present to you me. Warts and all. I am what I am. I don't try to be something I'm not." The world has enough plastic. Metaphorically, there's too much fake. You get to be real. You're made in the image of God. Application: Grow in your comfort level with who you are. Cut the umbilical cord of pretending, of managing your reputation, of being overly concerned about what others think. It's exhausting. 💡 If you're not comfortable with who you are, if you're not living authentically, you're providing the world more plastic. The church has the privilege of understanding we've been forgiven much and accepted in spite of who we are — therefore we can be freed from being enslaved to others' opinions. 3

chunks.heading: Combat the Tendency to Make Life About You
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:5 "For what we preach is not ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, and ourselves as servants, for Jesus' sake." Paul says, "This is not about me. My life's mission is that I would decrease and He would increase. That God would be made great. That my life would be lived for Jesus' sake." Never has our world had such a focused, tailor-made, algorithmed strategy to make you think about you more. Your tendency is already to think about yourself enough. Application: Have a plan to combat it. Walk into a room and think: Who can I bless? Who looks like they need someone to talk to? Who can I bring life to? Who can I compliment? Who can I encourage? 💡 One approach sucks life from a room; the other injects life into it. Who do you want to be? 4

chunks.heading: Embrace Your Limitations
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:7 "But we have this treasure in jars of clay to show that this all-surpassing power is from God and not from us." Paul calls us "jars of clay" — clay pots that are easily broken and chipped. That's the fragility of our personal life. You and I have limitations. Most of us spend so much life trying to hide our limitations, excuse them, improve upon them, or pretend they don't exist. But Paul says: You are a jar of clay. 2 Corinthians 12:10 "For the sake of Christ, then, I am content with weaknesses, insults, hardships, persecutions, calamities. For when I am weak, then I am strong." Content with weakness. This doesn't mean you're not ambitious or that you don't try hard. But you recognize God has made you in a certain way. In Ephesians 4, it says He has "measured out, apportioned his grace to us." 💡 Spiritual maturity means not only being aware of your weaknesses, but seeing the redemptive beauty in them. "I'm never going to be Tim Keller. God's will is for me to be Brad Hillier. And God's will is for you to be you." 5

chunks.heading: Leverage Your Pain for a Greater Good
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:8-9 "We are hard-pressed on every side, but not crushed; perplexed, but not in despair; persecuted, but not abandoned; struck down, but not destroyed." Paul says, "All of this is for your benefit." When you encounter disappointment, heartache, struggle you didn't see coming, it's a secret of the Christian life to understand it — not deny it, not over-spiritualize it ("Oh, but God is good!") — but to look it square in the face and then say: God is good. Application: We all have pain. You can either hide in it, sulk in it, circle the drain with it — or you can process it over time and use it for something greater than yourself. 💡 Paul didn't just whine: "I was shipwrecked, beaten, lost..." He said, "All this happened for your good — so you'd see the credibility of the gospel, that I didn't give up easy." The gospel gets credibility because Paul stood the course and endured. 6

chunks.heading: Renew Yourself Daily
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:16 "Therefore we do not lose heart. Though outwardly we are wasting away, yet inwardly we are being renewed day by day." Paul points out: You and I are souls. We are not bodies that contain souls — we are souls. In the unseen world, the most important aspect of who we are (our soul) is being changed. While our body decays, our soul is being renewed. But notice the frequency: day by day. Not week by week. Not just on Sundays. We need something grander than a Sunday opportunity to refresh our souls. Application: Think about God daily. Pray daily. Recognize you are a soul daily. Cultivate patterns and habits — reactions to the world — that remind you of who you are. 💡 Make your faith pervasive. Look at the crowd on the MTR and remember 10% of people in Hong Kong know Christ. Spot nine people and think, They're real people. Maybe I should pray for them instead of doom scrolling. 7

chunks.heading: Discipline Yourself to See the Unseen
chunks.text: 2 Corinthians 4:17-18 "For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all. So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." Recognize the reality that you are a soul. Know that prayer matters (you don't see it). Know that God speaks (even though you may not hear it audibly). The unseen world is as real as anything. Oxygen is unseen, and you believe in it. Application: Believe in a greater unseen world where God dwells, moves, speaks, engages, delights, dances over you. Don't get sucked into the tangible only — that leads to discouragement. If your life is just deadlines, careers, paying bills — it's meaningless, unless you see a God who is in all of it. But it requires discipline. "If you look at the world, you'll be distressed. If you look within, you'll be depressed. But if you look at Christ, you'll be at rest." — Corrie ten Boom 💡 The pace we live, the distractions we have, the way we can easily numb our senses — say no to that anesthesia. Be alert. Be alive.

chunks.heading: Exodus 6:6-9 — The Context of Discouragement
chunks.text: ▼ "Therefore, say to the Israelites: 'I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians. I will free you from being slaves to them, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with mighty acts of judgment. I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God, who brought you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians. And I will bring you to the land I swore with uplifted hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac and to Jacob. I will give it to you as a possession. I am the LORD.' Moses reported this to the Israelites, but they did not listen to him because of their discouragement and harsh labor." Context: God promises deliverance, but the people are too discouraged to even hear the good news.

chunks.heading: Psalm 42:5-6 — David's Self-Awareness
chunks.text: ▼ "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Why so disturbed within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him, my Savior and my God. My soul is downcast within me; therefore I will remember you from the land of the Jordan, the heights of Hermon—from Mount Mizar." Context: David acknowledges his discouragement and directs himself toward hope in God.

chunks.heading: 2 Corinthians 11:23-27 — Paul's Hardships
chunks.text: ▼ "...I have worked much harder, been in prison more frequently, been flogged more severely, and been exposed to death again and again. Five times I received from the Jews the forty lashes minus one. Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was pelted with stones, three times I was shipwrecked, I spent a night and a day in the open sea, I have been constantly on the move. I have been in danger from rivers, in danger from bandits, in danger from my fellow Jews, in danger from Gentiles; in danger in the city, in danger in the country, in danger at sea; and in danger from false believers. I have labored and toiled and have often gone without sleep; I have known hunger and thirst and have often gone without food; I have been cold and naked." Context: Paul's credibility — he endured all this yet did not lose heart.

chunks.heading: 2 Corinthians 4:1-18 — The Foundation Text / 2 Corinthians 12:10 — Strength in Weakness / Ephesians 4:7 — Apportioned Grace / Galatians 6:9 — Don't Give Up
chunks.text: ▼ Verse 1: "Therefore, since through God's mercy we have this ministry, we do not lose heart." Verse 2: "Rather, we have renounced secret and shameful ways; we do not use deception, nor do we distort the word of God. On the contrary, by setting forth the truth plainly we commend ourselves to everyone's conscience in the sight of God." Verse 5: "For what we preach is not ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, and ourselves as your servants for Jesus' sake." Verse 7: "But we have this treasure in jars of clay to show that this all-surpassing power is from God and not from us." Verses 8-9: "We are hard pressed on every side, but not crushed; perplexed, but not in despair; persecuted, but not abandoned; struck down, but not destroyed." Verse 16: "Therefore we do not lose heart. Though outwardly we are wasting away, yet inwardly we are being renewed day by day." Verses 17-18: "For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all. So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." Context: This entire chapter is devoted to the concept of not losing heart. ▼ "That is why, for Christ's sake, I delight in weaknesses, in insults, in hardships, in persecutions, in difficulties. For when I am weak, then I am strong." Context: Content with weakness — spiritual maturity sees redemptive beauty in limitations. ▼ "But to each one of us grace has been given as Christ apportioned it." Context: God has measured out and apportioned His grace to each of us differently. ▼ "Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up." Context: Without a strategy to fight discouragement, we'll give up — on people, hope, and meaning.

chunks.heading: Questions to Ponder
chunks.text: 1. Identifying Discouragement What relationship (or relationships) in your life might be described as lifeless or in need of an injection of vitality, energy, thoughtfulness, and care? - Marriage? - Parent-child relationship? - Friendship? - Colleague relationship? 2. Signs of Relational Discouragement Have you become: - Resigned? "I guess this is as good as it gets..." - Lethargic? Just phoning it in, lacking energy and care - Despondent? "It is what it is" 3. Making Much of God's Mercy Do you truly live in light of God's lavish, unearned mercy? Or have you relegated "God loves me" to intellectual knowledge without daily experiential reality? 4. Authenticity Check Are you comfortable with who you are? Or are you exhausting yourself by managing your reputation, pretending, and being enslaved to others' opinions? 5. Who Are You Living For? When you walk into a room, do you think: - "Who's here? Am I dressed appropriately? What will I eat?" (Life about you) - "Who can I bless? Who needs encouragement? Who can I bring life to?" (Life for Jesus' sake) 6. Embracing Your Limitations What weaknesses are you trying to hide, excuse, or improve upon instead of being content with how God has made you? Can you see the redemptive beauty in your limitations? 7. Leveraging Pain What pain or struggle are you currently facing? Are you hiding in it and circling the drain — or are you processing it and asking God how to use it for something greater than yourself? 8. Daily Renewal What patterns and habits do you have for daily renewal? - Do you think about God daily? - Do you pray daily? - Do you recognize you are a soul daily? 9. Seeing the Unseen Are you disciplining yourself to see the unseen world — where God dwells, moves, speaks, delights, and dances over you? Or are you getting sucked into the tangible only (deadlines, careers, bills)?

chunks.heading: 📝 Action Steps
chunks.text: - Identify one relationship that needs attention this week. What specific action can you take to inject life into it? - Practice making much of God's mercy — set reminders to thank God for specific things throughout your day (tastes, sights, relationships, breath). - Be intentionally authentic — share something real with someone this week instead of managing your image. - Enter a room differently — next social gathering, ask yourself "Who can I bless?" instead of "How do I look?" - Name one weakness and choose to be content with it, seeing how God might use it for His glory. - Journal about a current pain — ask God how He might leverage it for a greater good. - Establish a daily renewal practice — morning prayer, scripture reading, worship, journaling (pick one to start). - Practice seeing the unseen — pray for 9 people on the MTR instead of scrolling. Look for God's movement in ordinary moments. Don't settle. Don't give up. Don't be discouraged. For God has not given up on you, and He's certainly not discouraged with you.

sermons.title: The Sticky Stuff: Disagreement | Island ECC

chunks.heading: Sermon Overview / Key Themes
chunks.text: Repairing relationships is one of the toughest things we'll do in life. And yet, it's required from us all. Though we love building new relationships, maintaining damaged relationships can be daunting. This sermon explores practical, biblical skills to navigate conflict and pursue reconciliation in our relationships. 56 Current global conflicts 92 Countries involved 69% Of conflicts are perpetual 🕊️ Peacemaking vs. Peacekeeping Jesus calls us to be peacemakers, not just peacekeepers. Making peace requires initiative, courage, and biblical wisdom. 🎯 Reconciliation Over Resolution The goal isn't always to solve every disagreement, but to restore relationship and pursue intimacy despite differences. 💪 Practical Skills Eight biblical strategies for navigating conflict that lead to growth, health, and deeper connection.

chunks.heading: 1. Make the First Move 🚀 / 2. Reject Your Excuses 🚫 / 3. Recognize Your Lack 🙏
chunks.text: Stop waiting. Stop keeping score. God made the first move toward you—let that empower you to pursue reconciliation, regardless of who's "more wrong." "If you, while going, remember that someone, you have an angst against someone, there's unresolved conflict against someone, leave your offering there. And then first go and be reconciled to them." — Matthew 5 Reconciliation is more important than worship. Better to work it out in the lobby than sing with bitterness in your heart. We all have excuses: "I tried before," "They're more mature," "It won't work." Don't let fear win. Fear leads to hiding, but you are secure in God's love. Their rejection may sting, but it doesn't define you. Pursue peace anyway. Conflict makes everything fuzzy. You need God's wisdom to see clearly, to respond well, to know what to say. James 1:5 "If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to you." Conflict is a blessing when it increases your dependence on God.

chunks.heading: 4. Consider Acceptance ✅
chunks.text: Not everything is worth fighting about. Paul addressed the early church's debate about meat sacrificed to idols by calling some matters "disputable." Romans 14:1 "Accept the one whose faith is weak, without quarreling over disputable matters." John Gottman's research: 69% of marital conflict is perpetual—it won't be resolved. You can fight about it forever, or you can accept. This isn't denial or avoidance. It's a decision: I release this. I accept you as you are. Forgiveness granted, reconciliation established.

chunks.heading: 5. Own Your Part 🪞
chunks.text: Even if you think it's only 1% your fault, own it. Most of us think the problem is 80% them, 20% us. Guess what? They think the same thing. James 4:1 "What causes fights and quarrels among you? Don't they come from your desires that battle within you?" If you are at peace with God, very little can disrupt that. The source of quarrels is often what's happening within us.

chunks.heading: 6. Listen Beyond the Words 👂
chunks.text: Listen to the heart. Understand the woundedness, the baggage, the context. If we saw what they're carrying, we'd be more gracious. James 1:19 "My dear brothers and sisters, take note of this: Everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak and slow to become angry." Two ears, one mouth—use them proportionately. Listening well automatically makes you slow to speak and slow to anger.

chunks.heading: 7. Guard Your Mouth 🗣️
chunks.text: Words create. God spoke the world into existence; you're made in His image. Your words have power to wound or heal. Ephesians 4:15 "Speak the truth in love." Weapons of Mass Destruction (WMD): - The "D" word (divorce) — never use it - Comparisons — "You're just like your dad" or "Be more like your sister" - "Always" and "Never" — rarely true, always destructive Don't hide behind your personality. "I'm just direct" isn't an excuse. Neither is "I'm just quiet." We all need to speak truth in love.

chunks.heading: 8. Focus on Reconciliation, Not Resolution 🤝
chunks.text: You may never agree. But you can agree to disagree and still be reconciled. Forgiveness was not God's goal. God sent Jesus to die so we could be forgiven—but that was a means to an end. Reconciliation was the goal: intimacy, connection, relationship restored. Forgiveness can happen from a distance. Reconciliation requires proximity. As much as it depends on you, pursue reconciliation.

chunks.heading: Key Bible Verses / 🎯 Who Comes to Mind?
chunks.text: Matthew 5:9 "Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God." Matthew 5:23-24 "Therefore, if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother or sister has something against you, leave your gift there in front of the altar. First go and be reconciled to them; then come and offer your gift." 1 John 4:20 "Whoever claims to love God yet hates a brother or sister is a liar. For whoever does not love their brother and sister, whom they have seen, cannot love God, whom they have not seen." 1 Peter 3:7 "Husbands, in the same way be considerate as you live with your wives, and treat them with respect as the weaker partner and as heirs with you of the gracious gift of life, so that nothing will hinder your prayers." James 3:18 "Peacemakers who sow in peace reap a harvest of righteousness." James 1:5 "If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to you." Romans 14:1 "Accept the one whose faith is weak, without quarreling over disputable matters." James 4:1 "What causes fights and quarrels among you? Don't they come from your desires that battle within you?" Proverbs 13:10 "Where there is strife, there is pride, but wisdom is found in those who take advice." James 1:19 "My dear brothers and sisters, take note of this: Everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak and slow to become angry." Ephesians 4:15 "Instead, speaking the truth in love, we will grow to become in every respect the mature body of him who is the head, that is, Christ." Proverbs 12:18 "The words of the reckless pierce like swords, but the tongue of the wise brings healing." Ephesians 4:29 "Do not let any unwholesome talk come out of your mouths, but only what is helpful for building others up according to their needs, that it may benefit those who listen." Romans 12:18 "If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone." 2 Corinthians 5:18-20 "All this is from God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation: that God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ, not counting people's sins against them. And he has committed to us the message of reconciliation. We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us. We implore you on Christ's behalf: Be reconciled to God." 1 John 4:19 "We love because he first loved us." ▼ As you listened to this message, did someone come to mind? A strained relationship? An unresolved conflict? Don't wait. Make the first move. Stop keeping score. God made the first move toward you—now you go first. - Write the text - Make the call - Send the email - Have the conversation

chunks.heading: 🪞 What's Your Conflict Style? / 💬 Establish WMD Boundaries
chunks.text: ▼ How do you typically handle conflict? Are you: - Porcupine: Defensive, prickly, quick to protect yourself? - Tortoise: Withdrawn, waiting it out, hoping it passes? - Lizard: So conflict-averse you'd lose a limb to escape? - Mimic Octopus: Camouflaging, blending in, avoiding confrontation? - Bonobo Chimp: Using charm and affection to distract? Whatever your default, know that biblical skills can be learned. You can grow in peacemaking. ▼ What are your relational "weapons of mass destruction"—words you should never say? Have a conversation with your spouse, family members, or close friends. Agree together: - No divorce threats - No toxic comparisons - No "always" or "never" accusations - No name-calling or character attacks When you know the boundaries, you protect the relationship even in heated moments.

chunks.heading: ✅ Practice Acceptance
chunks.text: ▼ Is there something you've been fighting about for years? Something that's 69% likely to be perpetual? Maybe it's time to stop fighting and start accepting. Not denial. Not passivity. But a conscious decision: "I release this. I accept you as you are. This is no longer a source of conflict between us." Acceptance paves the way for reconciliation. You stop keeping score. You throw away the scorecard.

chunks.heading: 🙏 Ask for Wisdom
chunks.text: ▼ Before your next difficult conversation, pray James 1:5: "If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to you." Ask God: - How should I approach this person? - What should I say? - What should I own? - What should I accept? - Help me listen beyond the words

chunks.heading: Three Consequences of Unresolved Conflict / The Heart of the Gospel
chunks.text: 1. Disrupts Your Peace with God 🕊️ You cannot have peace with God and unresolved conflict with someone else. They're incompatible. 2. Disrupts Your Prayer Life 🙏 Peter says treating people poorly—especially your spouse—will hinder your prayers. God says: Don't pray. Do. Fix it first. 3. Disrupts Your Personal Joy 😔 You reap what you sow. If you sow peace, you reap peace. If you sow bitterness, you reap a harvest of misery. Forgiveness was not God's goal. Reconciliation was. God didn't just want to forgive you from a distance—He wanted intimacy, connection, relationship restored. Forgiveness is a means. Reconciliation is the end. Don't stop at "I forgive you." Pursue proximity. Pursue peace. Pursue relationship.

chunks.heading: Closing Prayer (from the sermon)
chunks.text: "Father, we are grateful for your love for us in spite of who we are. While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us. It was in our worst state that you deemed us worthy of your sacrificial love. And yes, we are forgiven through Christ, but more importantly, we are reconciled. And so as we live in the afterglow of that beautiful substitutionary death that we have received, God, we pray that that would somehow free us up in our relationships to be a beacon of peace and hope to others, that we could sow a life of making peace with all of those around us. We have access to you. We have resources through your spirit. We have been forgiven much. And so certainly we can do the same. We thank you in the name of Jesus who died for us. Amen."

chunks.heading: Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 1. What conflict have you been avoiding? What excuse have you been using? 2. Which of the 8 strategies do you most need to apply right now? 3. What "WMD words" do you need to commit to never saying again? 4. Is there a perpetual problem you need to accept rather than keep fighting about? 5. Who will you reach out to this week to pursue reconciliation?

sermons.title: The Sticky Stuff: Resentment | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview / 1. Release the Resentment / 2. Give Grace / 3. Embrace Empathy / 🔑 Key Definitions
chunks.text: Series: Sticking Together Through the Sticky Stuff Theme: Understanding and releasing resentment through forgiveness Key Insight: Resentment only hurts one person—you. It's like drinking poison hoping someone else gets sick, or living rent-free in your head while guarding a prison door. Put away bitterness, wrath, and anger. It's not worth the cortisol—the stress hormone that deteriorates us emotionally, spiritually, and physically. Holding resentment paralyzes us and keeps us from moving forward. Be kind and tender-hearted to one another. Don't keep records of wrongs or rights. Show grace freely, knowing that one day we'll need it too. Forgive as God in Christ forgave you. Put yourself in others' shoes. Ask: Would I want mercy if I lost control, broke a promise, or failed again? Resentment: A feeling of indignant displeasure or persistent ill will at something regarded as a wrong, insult, or injury. Living Rent-Free: An expression meaning something negative (usually a person or memory) occupies your mind constantly without "paying rent"—you can't stop thinking about it.

chunks.heading: 💬 The Full Message
chunks.text: ⚾ Opening Story: The Angry Coach ▼ Pastor Tim shared a personal story from his youth playing baseball. He had a coach who used negative reinforcement—ridiculing players to "build them up." During one game, Tim made a mistake that cost the team the win. His coach yelled at him publicly in front of parents, teammates, and classmates. But then the assistant coach kicked a chair in anger. The head coach, not seeing who did it, assumed it was Tim and came over screaming in his face, cursing and putting him down—all for something he didn't do. Tim froze, too scared to correct him, and just took the verbal assault. This incident planted seeds of resentment. When the coach left the next year to coach another team, Tim was happy every time they lost. "Yeah, he deserves it," he thought. The resentment felt justified, but it kept Tim paralyzed emotionally. This illustrates how resentment feels right but doesn't make anything right. It feels justified but keeps us paralyzed. 🧠 What is Resentment? ▼ Definition: A feeling of indignant displeasure or persistent ill will at something regarded as a wrong, insult, or injury. "Holding resentment is like letting someone you despise live rent-free in your head." — Anne Landers "Resentment is like drinking poison and hoping the other person gets sick." "Resentment is a prison. When you put someone in your jail cell of hatred, you're stuck guarding the door." Physical Effects: Resentment raises cortisol (stress hormone) which can lead to: - Higher blood pressure - Weakened immune system - Digestive issues - Sleep disturbances - Depression Our brains replay hurtful memories, and they can't distinguish between real and imagined experiences. Each replay triggers the same stress response as the original event. 📝 Keeping Records of Wrongs (and Rights) ▼ We're all excellent archivists—we keep detailed records of how others have wronged us. But we're also good at keeping records of how right we are and how well we're doing compared to others. "We judge ourselves by our intentions and others by their behavior." — Stephen Covey When someone messes up, we think: "They're a bad person." When we mess up, we say: "But I had good intentions. I didn't mean to." Where We Build Resentment: - Co-workers: "I worked hard on this project.

chunks.heading: 💬 The Full Message (pt 2)
chunks.text: Why are they getting credit?" - Friends: "I'm always the one to text first. They never initiate." - Family: "I'm always coordinating everything. Why don't you help?" - Spouses: "I do all the chores. Why aren't you pulling your weight?" - Kids: "After everything I've given you, you disrespect me like this?" - Bosses: "I'm loyal. I work hard. Why no raise or promotion?" - Teachers/Students: "I do all my work and follow rules. Why so hard on me?" - Exes: "I was kind and loving. Why did they leave?" - Neighbors: "I say hi every morning. They don't even look at me." - Ourselves: Dwelling on past regrets and mistakes, unable to forgive ourselves. - God: "I come to church, pray, read my Bible, serve. Why aren't you answering my prayers?" 😤 How We Normally Handle Resentment ▼ - Keep it in: We hold on to it, nurse it, replay it over and over. - Explode (to the wrong person): We vent to friends instead of addressing the issue. - Keep score: We maintain mental checklists of wrongs and rights. - Passive aggression: "Well, somebody showed up for work today." - Fake nice: "Good to see you" while secretly wishing they'd leave. - Victimize: We rehearse the hurt and convince ourselves we deserve to feel angry. 📖 The Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15) ▼ This famous parable is usually focused on the younger brother who squanders his inheritance and returns home humbled. But the story is really about two brothers and a father. The Older Brother's Resentment: When the younger brother returns and the father throws a huge celebration, the older brother refuses to join. He's been working faithfully for years, never disobeying, and yet his father never threw him a party. He explodes at his father: "Look! All these years I've been slaving for you and never disobeyed your orders. Yet you never gave me even a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends. But when this son of yours who has squandered your property with prostitutes comes home, you kill the fattened calf for him!" Notice the passive aggression: "this son of yours"—not "my brother." The Father's Response: "My son, you are always with me, and everything I have is yours.

chunks.heading: 💬 The Full Message (pt 3)
chunks.text: But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found." Key Insight: Grace welcomed both brothers in, but resentment kept the older brother out. He refused the joy because he couldn't let go of his bitterness. "You will always feel that you are owed more than you are getting. You will always see someone doing better than you in some aspect of life and will ask, 'Why this person and not me, after all I've done?' This resentment is your own fault. It's caused not by the prosperity of the other person, but by your own effort to control life through your performance." — Tim Keller, The Prodigal God ✝️ The Only Way Out: Forgiveness ▼ The question we must ask: What's more important—your resentment or your relationship? Common Objections to Forgiveness: "But they haven't even apologized!" Just like resentment has nothing to do with them, forgiveness also has nothing to do with them. They don't need to know you've forgiven them. You just do it. "If I forgive them, it's like saying it's okay." No. It's still wrong. Forgiveness is not saying "it's okay." It's releasing yourself from the poison of resentment. It's grace, not approval. "Forgiveness means reconciliation, right?" No. Forgiveness is not reconciliation. You can forgive in your heart and still maintain healthy boundaries. "What if they do it again?" You can forgive and still set boundaries. Forgiveness doesn't mean giving unlimited access to hurt you again. "I've forgiven them, but I keep thinking about it." Jesus said forgive "70 times seven" (Matthew 18:22). Sometimes we only get hurt once, but we replay it hundreds of times. Each time we replay it, we need to remind ourselves: "I've already forgiven them." Forgiveness is a gift to yourself, not the other person. It's not letting them off the hook—it's freeing yourself from the prison. "Mercy and forgiveness must be free and unmerited to the wrongdoer. If the wrongdoer has to do something to merit it, then it isn't mercy. But forgiveness always comes at a cost to the one granting the forgiveness." — Tim Keller, The Prodigal God Jesus' cost was the cross. Our cost is letting go of our pride and taking the first step toward healing.

chunks.heading: 💬 The Full Message (pt 4)
chunks.text: 🔚 The Rest of the Story ▼ Five or six years after the baseball incident, Tim had become a Christian. He was asked to play drums at his church's men's conference. As he sat behind the drum set, guess who walked through the door? His former coach—unmistakable, a mountain of a man. He sat in the front row. Tim thought, "God is about to send lightning down because that man is here." But then the music started. This burly, angry coach fell to his knees, raised his hands, and worshiped with tears streaming down his face. Tim couldn't believe what he was seeing. This mean, intimidating man was surrendering his heart to God. Instantly, Tim felt a wave of forgiveness. He began to imagine what his coach must be going through. What struggles brought him to his knees? Later, through God's providence (and humor), Tim ended up in a Bible study with this coach. Over weeks, he saw a completely different man—vulnerable in prayer, humble, growing in faith. One day, Tim had to ask: "Coach, do you remember that game where you yelled at me in front of everyone?" The coach's response: "Nope." He had no memory of it. The event that tormented Tim for years meant nothing to the coach. Tim had been drinking the poison, guarding the prison door, for an event the other person didn't even remember. But then the coach said something that shocked Tim even more: "Tim, I was a jerk back then, and I've really been working on that." He had truly changed. God had transformed him. The coach passed away last year. When Tim saw the Facebook tributes—stories of a great teacher, a caring coach, wonderful memories—he realized how much God had changed this man. The lesson: Holding on to resentment doesn't help anybody. It only hurts you.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Bible Passages
chunks.text: 1 Corinthians 13:4-5 (NIV) "Love is patient, love is kind. It does not envy, it does not boast, it is not proud. It does not dishonor others, it is not self-seeking, it is not easily angered, it keeps no record of wrongs." This is the famous "love chapter" often read at weddings, but it's for every relationship. Love is not just a feeling—it's an action. And love keeps no record of wrongs. Luke 15:25-32 (NIV) - The Older Brother "Meanwhile, the older son was in the field. When he came near the house, he heard music and dancing. So he called one of the servants and asked him what was going on. 'Your brother has come,' he replied, 'and your father has killed the fattened calf because he has him back safe and sound.' The older brother became angry and refused to go in. So his father went out and pleaded with him. But he answered his father, 'Look! All these years I've been slaving for you and never disobeyed your orders. Yet you never gave me even a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends. But when this son of yours who has squandered your property with prostitutes comes home, you kill the fattened calf for him!' 'My son,' the father said, 'you are always with me, and everything I have is yours. But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.'" The older brother represents those who've been faithful but harbor resentment. Grace opened the door to celebration, but his bitterness kept him outside. Ephesians 4:31-32 (NIV) "Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and slander be put away from you, along with all malice. Be kind to one another, tender-hearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you." A clear command: release the bitterness, show kindness, and forgive as God forgave us through Christ. Luke 23:34 (NIV) - Jesus on the Cross "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." Jesus forgave freely while being crucified. He didn't wait for an apology. If Jesus can forgive us while dying on the cross, we can forgive those who hurt our pride. Matthew 18:21-22 (NIV) - Seventy Times Seven "Then Peter came to Jesus and asked, 'Lord, how many times shall I forgive my brother or sister who sins against me? Up to seven times?' Jesus answered, 'I tell you, not seven times, but seventy-seven times.'" Forgiveness isn't a one-time event. We may need to forgive the same offense repeatedly as our minds replay the hurt.

chunks.heading: 1. Release the Resentment / 2. Give Grace / 3. Embrace Empathy
chunks.text: "Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and slander be put away from you along with all malice." (Ephesians 4:31) Why? It's not worth the cortisol. Resentment deteriorates us emotionally, spiritually, and physically. It paralyzes us and keeps us from moving forward. Action: Name the person or situation. Acknowledge the hurt. Then consciously choose to release it. You might need to do this many times as memories resurface. "Be kind to one another, tender-hearted." (Ephesians 4:32) Why? One day we'll need it. We all make mistakes. We all hurt people. We judge ourselves by our intentions but others by their behavior. Grace levels the playing field. Action: Stop keeping score of wrongs and rights. Extend kindness even when it's not deserved—just as God does for us. "Forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you." (Ephesians 4:32) Why? We've all been there. We've all needed forgiveness. Empathy helps us see the person behind the offense. Action: Ask yourself: Would I want mercy if I lost control? If I broke a promise? If I failed again? Put yourself in their shoes. Imagine what they're going through.

chunks.heading: 💭 Questions to Ask Yourself
chunks.text: - Is there someone I've locked in a jail cell of resentment? Am I guarding the door? - Am I drinking poison hoping someone else gets sick? - Who is living rent-free in my head? - What's more important—my resentment or my relationship? - Would I want mercy if I were in their shoes? - Am I keeping a record of their wrongs? Am I keeping a record of my rights? - What is holding on to this resentment costing me emotionally, spiritually, and physically?

chunks.heading: 🛠️ Practical Steps This Week
chunks.text: - Identify: Write down the name of one person you resent and why. - Acknowledge the cost: Consider what holding this resentment is doing to you. - Choose forgiveness: Say out loud, "I forgive [name] for [what they did]." - Set boundaries if needed: Forgiveness doesn't mean unlimited access. Protect yourself wisely. - Repeat as necessary: Each time the memory resurfaces, remind yourself, "I've already forgiven them." - Pray for them: Ask God to bless them and help you see them through His eyes.

chunks.heading: 🙏 Closing Prayer & Reflection
chunks.text: God, we thank you so much for your love for us. We thank you for your forgiveness. We thank you for your mercy. We thank you that you don't keep any records of wrongs against us. And God, I pray now as we think about our fellow brothers and sisters, our family members, our friends, our coworkers, our bosses—anyone that we are in touch with on a regular basis—God, I do pray that we would be able to show that same grace and mercy that you've given us to those around us. I pray that we could release this resentment that we may be holding on to. That we could give the grace that's necessary to restore a relationship. And ultimately that we can be empathetic with one another, knowing that you empathize with us. You've forgiven us, and we are to forgive others. So God, we thank you for this. I thank you for this time of sitting with us and processing it, and I pray that all of us could benefit from it in some way. Of your powerful word, the story of the older brother, of Paul's words to us, his instruction to us, I pray that we would ruminate on that rather than the resentment. We pray all this in Jesus' name. Amen.

chunks.heading: ✨ Final Thoughts
chunks.text: The cross that Jesus died on is proof to us that God keeps no record of wrongs. It's proof to us that we are not condemned. The cross shows us that Jesus freely forgave us. He wasn't on the cross saying, "When they apologize, then I'll forgive them." He didn't say, "After everything I've done for them, and they're going to act like this?" No. Jesus said: "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do." If Jesus can forgive us while being crucified, we can forgive those who hurt our pride. Forgiveness doesn't rewrite the past, but it does open a door to a better future.

chunks.heading: 📝 Journaling Prompts
chunks.text: - Who am I holding a grudge against right now? What happened? - How is this resentment affecting my emotional, spiritual, and physical health? - What would it look like to release this person from my "prison"? - Am I keeping records of my own rights and goodness? How does that fuel my resentment? - What does God's forgiveness toward me teach me about forgiving others? - What boundaries do I need to set to protect myself while still choosing forgiveness?

sermons.title: The Sticky Stuff: Spiritual Imbalance | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: Navigating Different Spiritual Seasons in Relationships

chunks.heading: 🎯 Core Message / 🔑 Key Principles / 📍 Main Scriptures / 🌍 Context: Ancient Corinth
chunks.text: God has made us all unique, so in relationships we will encounter others in different spiritual seasons with different practices. Paul's wisdom to the Corinthians shows us how to navigate spiritual imbalances with grace, humility, and love. - Open your heart with vulnerability - Close your mouth and trust God's work - Use your freedom to serve others - Celebrate different expressions of faith - 1 Corinthians 9:19-23 - 2 Corinthians 6:11-13 - 2 Corinthians 7:8-9 - 1 Corinthians 8:1-13 A diverse port city much like Hong Kong—economically thriving, culturally complex, religiously pluralistic. The church there struggled with spiritual imbalances as Jewish believers and Gentile converts tried to follow Jesus together.

chunks.heading: Understanding Corinth: A City Like Hong Kong
chunks.text: Imagine a city that is culturally diverse, religiously diverse, a port city facilitating trade—an economic hub. It's highly urbanized, politically complex, with a spectrum of social classes. You might be thinking Hong Kong, but the answer is ancient Corinth. Corinth existed on a narrow strip of land (an isthmus) between two seas. Ships would dock there, unload cargo, and take three or four days to cross—creating a "Wan Chai situation" with sailors waiting around. This created a booming, ethically complicated city with diverse people from all backgrounds. "Paul, in his second missionary journey, left Athens and went to Corinth and stayed there for about a year and a half, 18 months. While he was there, he met Aquila and Priscilla, who had fled Rome, and they all joined in a trade together—they were tent makers." The church in Corinth was beautifully diverse and complicated. You had people from Jewish backgrounds who had sought holiness their whole life, and people who were basically not Jewish, who had no faith or had worshipped other gods. They were coming together around a table trying to explore what it meant that Jesus died and resurrected.

chunks.heading: Paul's Heart: The Foundation of Relationship
chunks.text: Before we dive into principles, we need to understand Paul's motivation. He writes: "For though I am free from all, I have made myself a servant to all. I have become all things to all people that by all means I might save some. I do it all for the sake of the gospel, that I may share with them in its blessings." (1 Corinthians 9:19, 22-23) Paul's heart was that they would share in the blessings of the gospel together—experience joy and wholeness, being renewed and made new together. That's probably your heart too for the relationships that come to mind as you think about imbalance. Be kind to yourself. These conversations can be tough. You've had missteps, spoken too soon, weren't as patient. But at the root of it is a heart saying, "I feel like we're missing it. There's more. There's good that awaits us."

chunks.heading: Posture: I'm For You
chunks.text: Paul says, "We have spoken freely to you, Corinthians. Our heart is wide open" (2 Corinthians 6:11). As the authority figure, Paul took the first step of vulnerability. He didn't have to do this—he was in charge. But he opened his heart and shared his life with them. Even this hardcore man—who was dragged outside cities by mobs, stoned, shipwrecked, snake-bitten—this man had tears as he wrote a difficult letter to the Corinthians. He regretted sending it momentarily, wondering if it would be too harsh. "For even if I made you grieve with my letter, I do not regret it, though I did regret it. For I see that the letter grieved you, though only for a while. As it is, I rejoice, not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved into repenting." (2 Corinthians 7:8-9) Opening your heart is important for making connection and preventing defensiveness. When you open up your heart, it's a soft start to a difficult conversation that helps it go much further. I've found through experience that when I get frustrated or angry and stew in it, conversations don't go well. But if I step back and look beyond that layer to see what I'm actually sad about—disappointment or "I wish things were this way"—conversations go much better. We need to have conversations because we have a God who has conversations. Our God loves us more than anyone else, and yet He is the most direct and straightforward. He lovingly tells us what is and what isn't, doesn't sugarcoat it—and He sticks around. We need to emulate that.

chunks.heading: Posture: I Am Not God
chunks.text: This may seem opposite, but it is. The book of Proverbs says three times that it's better to live on a corner of the roof, in a desert, or underneath a drip than to live with a nagging wife. Being annoying is probably one of the least Christ-like things you can do. The idea is: when you open your heart and share, then it's known. Sometimes after that, the best thing you can do is to be quiet and not pester. You are not accountable for anyone's actions. You are not in control. You cannot change anyone. This has to be a work of God to open the heart. Repeat after me: I am not in control. Repeat after Scripture: Be still and know that He is God. Paul says, "We've spoken freely to you Corinthians, our heart is wide open. You are not restricted by us, but you are restricted in your own affections. In return, would you widen your hearts also?" (2 Corinthians 6:11-13) Sometimes your heart-sharing is not reciprocated—that's real life. God is the only one that changes hearts.

chunks.heading: The Rescuer vs. Ambassador Paradigm
chunks.text: A lot of us in relationships think we're the rescuer, the hero of our own story. But that's not our job in relationships, care groups, or marriage. Our job is not to change people—that's God's job. Our job is to come alongside someone as their advocate, their ambassador, their supporter, and help lift them up and point them to the One who can truly do the work from the inside out. When you can step back, pray for someone, show up and be faithful without nagging or criticizing, you're showing that you trust God is in control. You want change to come from God doing a work in their heart, not from behavior modification because they fear your retribution.

chunks.heading: Posture: My Freedom Is a Gift to Others
chunks.text: Paul speaks to a debate in the first century about meat sacrificed to idols. Some people were okay with eating it; others felt it went against their conscience because it drew their heart back to what they'd been saved from. "Now, concerning food offered to idols, we know all of us possess knowledge, but knowledge puffs up, but love builds up... Take care that this right of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to those who are weak." (1 Corinthians 8:1, 9) Use your freedom not to say, "I have the right to do this," but to ask, "How can I bless? Love builds up, knowledge puffs up." Have you ever protected someone, helped someone when you knew they were vulnerable or sensitive? How good does that feel? That's the kind of love Paul is talking about. We should always be moving toward greater humility and servanthood in how we serve others in relationships. Many of us are in friendships, care groups, or relationships with people who have been hurt by the church, or who have sensitive backgrounds, or who are vulnerable to certain triggers. We need to say, "It doesn't matter if it doesn't matter to me or I'm good with God with this. How can I come to your level to protect you, serve you, love you in a way that shows I'm sensitive to that?"

chunks.heading: Posture: God's Work in Others May Look Different
chunks.text: In 1 Corinthians, the church was arguing: "I follow Paul." "I follow Apollos." "I follow Cephas." Paul responds, "Is Christ divided?" People were drawn to different teachers, different expressions of Christianity. They grew differently. Their hearts were touched by different aspects of God. The way I connect with God is very different than the way my wife connects. There's overlap, but there's difference too. This happens in care groups. We gather with 12 people and may all want or need different things in the moment. We have to listen and be sensitive. We can't assume, "Oh, that person didn't memorize the book of James this week, so they're not really a Christian." God is not our own box. He is bigger. His faith is bigger. He is doing way more work than we're aware of. Our calling is to be active in looking for God at work and celebrating that. That is a posture of generosity—relational generosity, humility—that opens your heart to God to say, "God, how are you at work in this person? Show me. I'm not looking for what they did wrong or criticism opportunities. I'm looking to see where You are at work, and I'm going to celebrate that." That posture is good for your soul, especially if there's sticky stuff you're navigating in relationships.

chunks.heading: Key Bible Verses / Practical Application
chunks.text: 1 Corinthians 9:19-23 (NIV) ▼ Paul's Heart for Ministry "Though I am free and belong to no one, I have made myself a slave to everyone, to win as many as possible. To the Jews I became like a Jew, to win the Jews. To those under the law I became like one under the law (though I myself am not under the law), so as to win those under the law. To those not having the law I became like one not having the law (though I am not free from God's law but am under Christ's law), so as to win those not having the law. To the weak I became weak, to win the weak. I have become all things to all people so that by all possible means I might save some. I do all this for the sake of the gospel, that I may share in its blessings." 2 Corinthians 6:11-13 (NIV) ▼ Opening Your Heart "We have spoken freely to you, Corinthians, and opened wide our hearts to you. We are not withholding our affection from you, but you are withholding yours from us. As a fair exchange—I speak as to my children—open wide your hearts also." 2 Corinthians 7:8-9 (NIV) ▼ Godly Grief Leads to Repentance "Even if I caused you sorrow by my letter, I do not regret it. Though I did regret it—I see that my letter hurt you, but only for a little while—yet now I am happy, not because you were made sorry, but because your sorrow led you to repentance. For you became sorrowful as God intended and so were not harmed in any way by us." 1 Corinthians 8:1, 9 (NIV) ▼ Knowledge vs. Love "Now about food sacrificed to idols: We know that 'We all possess knowledge.' But knowledge puffs up while love builds up... Be careful, however, that the exercise of your rights does not become a stumbling block to the weak." Proverbs 21:9, 25:24, 27:15 (NIV) ▼ The Danger of Nagging "Better to live on a corner of the roof than share a house with a quarrelsome wife." (21:9) "Better to live on a corner of the roof than share a house with a quarrelsome wife." (25:24) "A quarrelsome wife is like the dripping of a leaky roof in a rainstorm." (27:15) 1️⃣

chunks.heading: Open Your Heart (I'm For You)
chunks.text: Healthy relationships require conversation. You have to open your mouth and your heart, even though it's probably the last thing you want to do sometimes. Build trust and intimacy by going below the surface and sharing both the good stuff and the difficult stuff. Take the first step of vulnerability. Don't wait for perfect conditions. Share your struggles, not just your conclusions. Let people see the grays between the black and whites of life. 🤔 Reflection: Instead of starting with anger or frustration, can you identify what you're actually sad or disappointed about underneath? Start there. 2️⃣

chunks.heading: Close Your Mouth (I Am Not God)
chunks.text: After you've opened your heart and shared, sometimes the most godly thing you can do is be quiet. Recognize that you cannot change anyone—only God can do heart work. Your spouse, friend, or family member is not restricted by you, but by their own affections. Trust God's timing and work. Don't nag, pester, or criticize. Instead, pray faithfully and live out the gospel in close proximity to them so they can see God's fruit growing in your life. 🙏 Practice: Identify one relationship where you've been trying to be the "rescuer." How can you shift to being an "ambassador" who points them to God instead? 3️⃣

chunks.heading: Use Your Freedom to Free Others (My Freedom Is a Gift)
chunks.text: What God has freed you from or saved you from is not meant to create superiority over others or burden them. Instead, your freedom allows you to go down and connect with others in their vulnerability. Ask: How can I bless? Use your freedom not to assert your rights, but to protect, serve, and love others who may be sensitive or vulnerable to things that don't impact you. 💝 Example: If alcohol isn't a struggle for you but someone in your care group is working to overcome addiction, choose not to drink around them. Love builds up; knowledge puffs up. 4️⃣

chunks.heading: Celebrate Different Expressions of Faith / 🪞 The Central Question
chunks.text: God's work in others may look different than God's work in you. The way you connect with God may be very different from how your spouse, friend, or care group member connects with Him. Don't assume everyone should worship, pray, or grow exactly like you do. Be actively curious. Look for where God is at work in others, even if it's different from your experience. Celebrate growth wherever you find it. Ask God to show you what He's doing in others' lives. 🌱 Challenge: This week, ask someone in your life: "How has God been speaking to you lately?" Listen without judgment or comparison. As you navigate spiritual imbalances in relationships, continually ask yourself: What do my posture and actions communicate about who God is? Do your actions reflect the gospel—God's grace, truth, patience, and unconditional love? Or are they more about you, your preferences, your timeline, your way of doing things?

chunks.heading: 💭 For Those in Spiritually Unequal Relationships
chunks.text: If you're married to a non-Christian: The biblical call is to stay in that covenant and love your spouse with the love of Jesus. Open your heart, share, and then perhaps the most godly thing is to close your mouth and live out the gospel so your spouse sees the fruit of the Spirit growing in you. If you're dating someone with different spiritual convictions: Hit pause and ask, "Does this relationship help me pursue what I say is ultimate in my life—knowing and serving God?" All relationships are meant to point us to our greatest relationship with Him.

chunks.heading: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Family Dynamics
chunks.text: Whether you're a first-generation Christian praying for your parents to come to faith, or a parent hoping your adult children would encounter Jesus, remember: - ✅ You can control: Your prayers, your posture, your faithfulness, your love - ❌ You cannot control: Their response, their timeline, their heart change Are you resting in God's sovereignty, or are you anxiously trying to force change?

chunks.heading: 🤝 Care Group & Church Community
chunks.text: In your small group or church relationships, recognize that everyone is on a different trajectory of spiritual growth. Some are brand new to faith; others have walked with God for decades. Some connect through worship; others through Bible study; others through service. Am I looking for God at work in others' lives, even when it looks different from my experience? Choose the posture of generosity—celebrating growth wherever you find it, not comparing or criticizing.

chunks.heading: ⚖️ The Unequally Yoked Question
chunks.text: Paul's teaching about being "unequally yoked" isn't about superiority—it's about trajectory. The question for any relationship or endeavor is: Does this relationship help me pursue my trajectory of knowing God and serving Him, or does it pull me away from what I was designed for? If you're not a Christian reading this: The gospel isn't scary for your relationship—it's the greatest thing. It's a loving impetus that nudges people toward being kinder, more patient, seeking understanding, considering others' needs. Every marriage, every friendship, is better with Jesus in it.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Personal Inventory / A City Much Like Hong Kong
chunks.text: This week, take inventory: - 1. Where have I been trying to be the "rescuer" instead of the "ambassador"? - 2. In which relationship do I need to open my heart more vulnerably? - 3. Where do I need to close my mouth and trust God's work? - 4. How can I use my freedom to bless someone who's vulnerable or sensitive? - 5. Whose spiritual growth do I need to celebrate, even if it looks different from mine? To understand Paul's wisdom to the Corinthians, we need to understand the city itself. Corinth was located on a narrow strip of land (an isthmus) between the Aegean Sea and the Ionian Sea in southern Greece. This strategic location made it a booming economic hub.

chunks.heading: 🚢 The Port City Economy / ⚔️ Political & Social Complexity
chunks.text: Instead of sailing around the dangerous southern coast of Greece, ships would dock at Corinth and unload their cargo. It would take three or four days for the cargo (and sometimes the ships themselves) to be transported across the six-kilometer channel. This created a thriving trade economy. In the late 19th century, the Corinth Canal was finally carved through, but in Paul's day, the overland transport system meant sailors had several days to wait—leading to the development of a morally complex city with numerous vices and temptations. Corinth had a diverse population with multiple social classes, from wealthy merchants to slaves. After Jerusalem was ransacked in 70 A.D., the Roman Emperor Nero brought about 6,000 Jewish slaves to Corinth to attempt building the canal—adding another layer of cultural complexity to an already diverse city.

chunks.heading: 🏛️ Religious Diversity
chunks.text: The city was filled with temples to various Greek gods. The Temple of Aphrodite, located on Acro-Corinth (a hill overlooking the city), employed about 1,000 women—many of them slaves—as temple prostitutes. This was considered a form of worship in the pagan religious system. This created an environment where sexual ethics, religious practices, and cultural norms were vastly different from the Jewish monotheistic tradition that some in the church came from.

chunks.heading: 🎮 The Isthmian Games
chunks.text: Every two years, Corinth hosted the Isthmian Games, similar to the Olympics. These weren't casual sporting events—Greeks were passionate about athletics, and the games drew huge crowds requiring massive amounts of temporary housing (hence Paul's tent-making trade). This athletic culture influenced Paul's language. When he writes to the Corinthians about "striving to win the prize," "gaining a wreath as a victory," and "disciplining and beating your body for a certain goal," he's using metaphors everyone in Corinth would immediately understand.

chunks.heading: ⛪ The Church's Challenge
chunks.text: Into this complex, diverse, morally complicated city came the gospel. The church in Corinth was made up of: - Jewish believers who had sought holiness their whole lives and had deep religious traditions - Gentile converts who had no faith background or had worshipped other gods and had very different practices - Different economic classes from slaves to wealthy merchants - Various ethical backgrounds including those saved from sexual immorality and temple prostitution They were trying to figure out together what it meant that Jesus died and resurrected and they could have new life. They had different ways of expressing and understanding their faith, different gifts, different beliefs, different pasts—and they were all trying to follow Jesus together in the same community. "Paul was coming around a church in a big city trying to unify under Jesus a beautifully diverse and complicated group of people who had different pasts and different understandings. And he was trying to bring them toward faith further in Jesus and to grow and to meld together."

chunks.heading: 📍 Why This Matters for Us
chunks.text: Just like ancient Corinth, Hong Kong (and many modern cities) is: - Economically thriving and diverse - Culturally complex with multiple worldviews - Religiously pluralistic - Fast-paced and opportunity-driven The challenges the Corinthian church faced—spiritual imbalances, different expressions of faith, cultural tensions, moral confusion—are challenges we still face today. Paul's wisdom remains remarkably relevant for navigating relationships in our complex, diverse world.

sermons.title: The Sticky Stuff: Annoyance | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎯 Sermon Overview / Key Themes / 1. Choose to Rise Above the Offense / 2. Don't Stop Forgiving / 3. Refuse to Play Games / 4. Disengage When Necessary / 5. Establish Clear Boundaries / 6. Stay on the High Ground
chunks.text: We all have people in our lives who drive us crazy—those who sap our energy, rob our joy, and leave us feeling angry, guilty, frustrated, and confused. This message explores what God says about dealing with the annoying people in our lives while maintaining our faith and integrity. - Boundaries are Biblical: From Genesis 1 onward, God establishes boundaries to bring order from chaos and peace from disorder. - Boundaries Require Balance: We need to avoid being either too rigid or too passive in our relational boundaries. - Boundaries Protect Peace: Healthy boundaries honor God while protecting our emotional and spiritual well-being. - Self-Examination is Critical: Annoying relationships often reveal our own inadequacies and need for spiritual growth. Develop thick skin with a tender heart. Practice being "unoffendable"—don't let every slight or annoyance take up residence in your mind and rob you of joy. "It is to one's glory to overlook an offense." (Proverbs 19:11) Forgiveness isn't optional. Jesus commanded us to forgive "seventy times seven"—meaning continuously. You need to forgive more than anyone needs to be forgiven. "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." (Luke 23:34) Don't engage in manipulation, guilt trips, or endless arguments. Like Jesus before Pilate, sometimes the wisest response is no response at all. "We have renounced secret and shameful ways. We do not use deception, nor do we distort the Word of God." (2 Corinthians 4:2) Some people thrive on conflict. Don't fuel their fire. Remember: it takes two people to have an argument. "Just as charcoal and wood keep a fire going, a quarrelsome person keeps an argument going." (Proverbs 26:21) Boundaries are for you, not others. You can't control their behavior, but you can control how you respond and what you allow in your life. "Have nothing to do with such people." (2 Timothy 3:5) Don't retaliate. Don't gossip. Don't return insult for insult. You can have boundaries and still be a blessing. "Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good." (Romans 12:21)

chunks.heading: 🌅 God Establishes Boundaries from the Beginning
chunks.text: ▼ In Genesis 1, before creation, the earth was "formless and empty, darkness was over the surface of the deep." God's first act was to create a boundary—separating light from darkness, bringing order from chaos.
