chunks.heading: 🎹 The Piano Metaphor
chunks.text: Learning "Linus and Lucy" from Charlie Brown Christmas taught Pastor Tim about holding two things simultaneously. The left hand plays the low, rumbling bass—representing daily struggles, monotony, and pain. The right hand plays the bright, dancing melody—representing joy, celebration, and hope. Life requires us to play both hands at once. The struggles don't stop for Christmas. The bills don't pause. But God's goodness continues throughout all of it.

chunks.heading: 😭 Permission to Grieve / 🔥 Trials Refine Our Faith / 🌊 Kissing the Wave
chunks.text: Psalm 88 is the most depressing psalm in the Bible—and it's okay. It ends with: "Darkness is my closest friend" (Psalm 88:18). The psalmist offers no resolution, no turn to praise, just raw pain expressed to God. Even Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane said: "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death" (Matthew 26:38). It's okay to go there. God doesn't just hear our praise—He hears our pain. The important thing is that we're still talking to God, even in the pit of despair. James 1:2-4 teaches us to "consider it pure joy" when we face trials. Not because the trials themselves are good, but because: - Testing produces perseverance - Perseverance leads to maturity - Maturity makes us complete, lacking nothing Like gold refined by fire (1 Peter 1:6-7), our struggles remove impurities and make our faith more genuine. "I've learned to kiss the wave that throws me against the rock of ages." — Charles Spurgeon The waves of hardship are powerful and dangerous, but they push us closer to God—the everlasting Rock. We can count these trials as joy because they bring us nearer to Him.

chunks.heading: ✅ Choose to Rejoice
chunks.text: Just as marriage requires choosing to love even when feelings fade, our faith requires choosing joy even when we don't feel like it. This week, when struggles hit—whether a difficult bill, bad news, or ongoing hardship—make the choice to rejoice. Not because the pain is good, but because God is with you in it, building your character and drawing you closer to Himself. "Suffering is unbearable if you aren't certain that God is for you and with you." — Tim Keller, Walking with God Through Pain and Suffering

chunks.heading: Key Bible Verses 📜
chunks.text: Matthew 1:18-19 (NIV) This is how the birth of Jesus the Messiah came about: His mother Mary was pledged to be married to Joseph, but before they came together, she was found to be pregnant through the Holy Spirit. Because Joseph her husband was faithful to the law, and yet did not want to expose her to public disgrace, he had in mind to divorce her quietly. Luke 1:30 (NIV) But the angel said to her, "Do not be afraid, Mary; you have found favor with God." Luke 2:4-7 (NIV) So Joseph also went up from the town of Nazareth in Galilee to Judea, to Bethlehem the town of David, because he belonged to the house and line of David. He went there to register with Mary, who was pledged to be married to him and was expecting a child. While they were there, the time came for the baby to be born, and she gave birth to her firstborn, a son. She wrapped him in cloths and placed him in a manger, because there was no guest room available for them. James 1:2-4 (NIV) Consider it pure joy, my brothers and sisters, whenever you face trials of many kinds, because you know that the testing of your faith produces perseverance. Let perseverance finish its work so that you may be mature and complete, not lacking anything. Romans 5:1-4 (NIV) Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ... Not only so, but we also glory in our sufferings, because we know that suffering produces perseverance; perseverance, character; and character, hope. 1 Peter 1:6-7 (NIV) In all this you greatly rejoice, though now for a little while you may have had to suffer grief in all kinds of trials. These have come so that the proven genuineness of your faith—of greater worth than gold, which perishes even though refined by fire—may result in praise, glory and honor when Jesus Christ is revealed. Psalm 88:18 (NIV) You have taken from me friend and neighbor—darkness is my closest friend. Psalm 42:5, 11 (NIV) Why, my soul, are you downcast? Why so disturbed within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him, my Savior and my God. Matthew 26:38 (NIV) Then he said to them, "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch with me." Psalm 34:18 (NIV) The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit. Matthew 11:28 (NIV) Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.

chunks.heading: Key Lessons 💡 / 📚 A School for the Soul
chunks.text: 🙏 Dependence Struggles teach us to trust God completely, especially when we hit rock-bottom moments and have nowhere else to turn. 💎 Humility Our character is refined through hardship, making us more humble and moldable in God's hands. 💪 Endurance We grow stronger through challenges, building spiritual muscle that prepares us for future trials. ✨ Transformation Struggles shape us to become more like Christ, mirroring His perseverance through suffering. ✅ Validation Our faith is proven genuine through trials, like gold refined by fire that emerges purer. 🎯 Choice Joy is not a feeling but a decision. We actively choose to rejoice even when we don't feel like it. Martin Luther described suffering as a "school" that teaches us in ways prosperity cannot. Through hardship, our faith moves from something we talk about to something deeply embedded in our soul—tested, proven, and real.

chunks.heading: 🔥 The Refining Process
chunks.text: Like a goldsmith heating gold to remove impurities, God uses trials to purify our faith. The heat is intense, but the result is a more precious, genuine faith that reflects His glory. "While other worldviews lead us to sit in the midst of life's joys foreseeing the coming sorrows, Christianity empowers its people to sit in the midst of the world's sorrows, tasting the coming joy." — Tim Keller

chunks.heading: Three Core Truths to Hold / 📝 Journal Your Two Hands / 🙏 Pray Like the Psalmists / 💬 Share Your Struggle / 🎯 Practice the "Choice" Moment / 📖 Read Tim Keller's Book
chunks.text: 💔 The Lord is Close to the Brokenhearted "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit." (Psalm 34:18) When you're in pain, God doesn't distance Himself—He draws near. Your suffering doesn't repel Him; it invites His comfort and presence. ⚖️ Trials Test, but God Gives Rest "Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest." (Matthew 11:28) The trials will test you—they're designed to. But God offers rest in the midst of them. Not escape, but peace. Not avoidance, but His presence. ✨ In Every Struggle, Make the Choice to Rejoice "Consider it pure joy, my brothers and sisters, whenever you face trials of many kinds." (James 1:2) Joy is not a feeling that happens to us—it's a choice we make. When the bill arrives, when the diagnosis comes, when the relationship breaks, choose joy. Not because the circumstance is good, but because God is good and He's with you in it. Take time each day to write down both: - Left Hand (Struggles): What's hard right now? What's weighing on you? - Right Hand (God's Goodness): Where do you see God's presence, even faintly, in those struggles? Practice holding both truths simultaneously without dismissing either. Give yourself permission to bring your raw emotions to God. Use Psalm 88 as a template—pour out your pain honestly. Then follow with Psalm 42—remind your soul to hope in God. Identify one trusted person this week and be honest about what you're going through. Don't pretend it's fine. Community carries burdens together (Galatians 6:2). When a struggle hits this week—big or small—pause before reacting. Verbally say: "This is hard, but God is good. I choose to count this as joy because it's building endurance in me." Walking with God Through Pain and Suffering is available at Quench (Island ECC's bookstore on the first floor). It provides theological depth and practical wisdom for navigating hardship. "Nonstop joy doesn't mean no problems. It means choosing to see God's presence in both the celebrations and the struggles, playing both hands of the piano simultaneously." — Inspired by Pastor Tim's message

chunks.heading: Reflection Questions 🤔 / 💭 For Those in Deep Pain / 🎁 For Those Experiencing Joy
chunks.text: 🔍 Question 1: Where Do You See God? In the struggles you are facing right now, where do you see God's presence and goodness even if it is hard to recognize? Reflection Prompt: Consider the recent tragedy in your city, or a personal hardship you're facing. What small moments of grace have appeared? Where have you seen community, love, or unexpected provision? Sometimes God's presence is in the friend who showed up, the strength you didn't know you had, or the peace that doesn't make logical sense. 🌱 Question 2: How Are Trials Shaping You? How could your current trials be shaping your faith, character, or endurance for the future, and how might you choose to count it all as joy in the process? Reflection Prompt: Think about a past hardship that, looking back, clearly strengthened you or deepened your faith. What similarities exist between that experience and what you're facing now? What character qualities (patience, humility, dependence on God) might be forming in you through this current trial? How can you actively choose to view this as a "credit" rather than a "debit" in your spiritual account? If you're in a season of profound grief or struggle, it's okay to rest in Psalm 88. You don't have to force yourself to "find the silver lining." Simply stay in conversation with God, even if it's just to say, "I'm here, and I'm hurting." If you're in a good season, how can you support someone in their struggle? How can you be God's presence to them? Consider reaching out to someone you know is hurting and simply being present with them. "My soul is downcast within me; therefore I will remember you." — Psalm 42:6 (NIV)

chunks.heading: Prayer for the Week
chunks.text: "God, we thank You that You enter into our pain. Help us to hold both hands—the struggles and Your goodness—simultaneously. Teach us to choose joy not because the trial is good, but because You are good and You are with us. Build in us perseverance, character, and hope. We choose to rejoice, even when we don't feel like it, because we trust that You are refining us. Amen."

sermons.title: Rejoice in Opposition | Island ECC Sermon

chunks.heading: 🌟 The Desired Response / ⚠️ The Dreadful Response / 🆘 The Desperate Response / 🎯 Central Question / Introduction: Two Great Loves
chunks.text: The Wise Men Despite doubt and uncertainty, they held fast to their conviction. They faced an unprepared Jerusalem, a hostile king, and manipulation—yet they pressed on to worship Jesus. "When they saw the star, they were overjoyed. On coming to the house they saw the child with his mother Mary, and they bowed down and worshipped him." King Herod He responded to opposition by building monuments of power, control, and security. His Herodium stood 5km from Bethlehem—an artificial mountain of glory that now lies in ruins. "We all build our own Herodiums. We all build our own artificial mountains of glory." We Must Remember The Israelites were desperate—like their ancestors in Egypt. Only by recognizing our depravity and desperate need for a Savior can we truly worship like the wise men. "The more we recognize the reality of our depravity, the greater our need for Jesus Christ." "Will you choose Jesus as king of your life? Or will you clutch onto your thrones as kings of your own lives?" Pastor Kevin shares his passion for Christmas and astronomy, setting the stage for exploring the Star of Bethlehem—where these two loves converge in the Christmas story. "Sometimes following God can feel like you're on the wrong path because everything in your life is falling apart."

chunks.heading: The Wise Men's Journey
chunks.text: The wise men had only astrological signs—Jupiter circling Regulus three times. Yet this was enough to compel them on a 5-6 month journey to Jerusalem. Their First Doubt: When they arrived in Jerusalem, the city was unprepared and disturbed by the news. The king knew nothing. The priests had to search the scriptures. "Why is it that everybody in this country... treats this newborn king with so much hostility and contempt?"

chunks.heading: Opposition Today / Herod: The World's Response
chunks.text: Opposition isn't always overt persecution. It's often having our belief systems dismissed and met with suspicion: - "You sure you want to believe in this Jesus person?" - "You telling me that a virgin gave birth?" - "You want me to believe that a star stopped over Bethlehem?" These questions chip away at our belief systems until tragedy strikes and they become more convincing. Herod was history's greatest builder—temples, cities, fortresses, aqueducts. He literally moved mountains for his own glory. His Herodium: A massive fortress-mountain built 5km from Bethlehem, standing defiantly against the newborn king. "The world teaches us that the way you respond to opposition is that you amass for yourselves these monuments of power, control, status and security."

chunks.heading: Remembering Desperation / The Final Contrast / ⚖️ The Choice / 🔭 The Star of Bethlehem: An Astronomical Investigation
chunks.text: Matthew draws parallels to Moses and Egypt—the same desperation, the same need for a savior. But Jesus rescues us from something far worse: our depravity. Depravity: Not just making mistakes, but every fiber of our being in rebellion against God. We had no capacity to even desire God. "Unless we understand the true nature of our former state, then our salvation is not going to be all that great." Today, all of Herod's monuments lay in ruins. The temple is replaced by a mosque. Masada and Caesarea crumble. Tour buses drive past the forgotten Herodium to visit little Bethlehem. "The crown jewel of Herod's legacy sits forgotten as people drive on by to go visit the little town of Bethlehem." If we choose to be kings of our own lives, we choose a very temporary kingdom. If we choose Jesus, we choose an eternal kingdom where the last shall be first and the least shall be the greatest. Based on research by Rick Larson's documentary "The Star of Bethlehem"

chunks.heading: Problem #1: How did they know a king was born in Israel?
chunks.text: ▼ What the wise men saw was not actually a star—it was what the ancients called a "wandering star," which we now know as planets. Around 3 BC: - Jupiter (the King Planet - largest of all planets) - Passed by Regulus (the King Star) - Within the constellation Leo (the Lion) - In a triple conjunction (passing three times) Jerusalem was the capital of Judah, which was represented by a lion. So: The King Planet + circling the King Star + in the constellation of a Lion = A new king born in Israel For people in Babylon who looked to the stars for messages, this was not far-fetched.

chunks.heading: Problem #2: How was it the brightest star they'd ever seen?
chunks.text: ▼ About nine months after Caesar's census (around the time Jesus was born): - Venus caught up with Jupiter in the night sky - The two planets came so close they appeared as one single star to the naked eye - The light from both planets amplified each other - It became the brightest thing they had ever seen in their lifetime This was their cue. This is when they knew something had happened. So they began their 5-6 month journey to Jerusalem.

chunks.heading: Problem #3: How does a star stop over a location?
chunks.text: ▼ Retrograde Motion: Planets don't move in a linear fashion across the night sky. They move in a loopy direction because Earth's orbit is faster or slower than other planets. Example: Like driving on a highway next to a slower car—it looks like that car is moving backwards. That's retrograde motion. When the wise men arrived in Jerusalem around November/December 2 BC: - Jupiter was moving across the sky - It stopped in retrograde motion - Directly over Bethlehem Computer programs that recreate the ancient night sky confirm this astronomical phenomenon occurred exactly as described.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Pastor Kevin's Theory: The Complete Timeline
chunks.text: - In Babylon: The wise men see Jupiter circle Regulus three times in the constellation Leo → They know a king will be born in Judah - Nine months later: Venus catches up to Jupiter, creating the brightest star ever seen → The king is born - 5-6 months journey: They travel from Babylon to Jerusalem - Arrival in Jerusalem: Jupiter moves ahead of them in retrograde motion and stops over Bethlehem The guiding star was none other than the planet Jupiter 🪐

chunks.heading: Matthew 2:1-3 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Wise Men Arrive "After Jesus was born in Bethlehem in Judea, during the time of King Herod, Magi from the east came to Jerusalem and asked, 'Where is the one who has been born king of the Jews? We saw his star when it rose and have come to worship him.' When King Herod heard this he was disturbed, and all Jerusalem with him."

chunks.heading: Matthew 2:4-6 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Priests Search Scripture "When he had called together all the people's chief priests and teachers of the law, he asked them where the Messiah was to be born. 'In Bethlehem in Judea,' they replied, 'for this is what the prophet has written: "But you, Bethlehem, in the land of Judah, are by no means least among the rulers of Judah; for out of you will come a ruler who will shepherd my people Israel."'"

chunks.heading: Matthew 2:7-12 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Journey to Bethlehem "Then Herod called the Magi secretly and found out from them the exact time the star had appeared. He sent them to Bethlehem and said, 'Go and search carefully for the child. As soon as you find him, report to me, so that I too may go and worship him.'" "After they had heard the king, they went on their way, and the star they had seen when it rose went ahead of them until it stopped over the place where the child was. When they saw the star, they were overjoyed." "On coming to the house, they saw the child with his mother Mary, and they bowed down and worshiped him. Then they opened their treasures and presented him with gifts of gold, frankincense and myrrh. And having been warned in a dream not to go back to Herod, they returned to their country by another route."

chunks.heading: Matthew 2:13-15 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Escape to Egypt "When they had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream. 'Get up,' he said, 'take the child and his mother and escape to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you, for Herod is going to search for the child to kill him.' So he got up, took the child and his mother during the night and left for Egypt, where he stayed until the death of Herod. And so was fulfilled what the Lord had said through the prophet: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.'"

chunks.heading: Matthew 2:16-18 (NIV) / Isaiah 9:2 (NIV)
chunks.text: Herod's Massacre "When Herod realized that he had been outwitted by the Magi, he was furious, and he gave orders to kill all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under, in accordance with the time he had learned from the Magi. Then what was said through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled: 'A voice is heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more.'" The Promise of Light "The people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of deep darkness a light has dawned." This verse appears throughout the Advent series, reminding us that hope came in darkness.

chunks.heading: Colossians 1:15-17 (NIV)
chunks.text: The Supremacy of Christ "He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities—all things were created through him and for him. And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together." Proclaimed at the end of the service, declaring Jesus as the eternal King.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Recognize Your Herodiums / ⚠️ Understanding Opposition
chunks.text: We all build our own artificial mountains of glory. The world conditions us to do this—if we aren't achieving, making an impact, leaving a legacy, creating a name for ourselves, we're failing at life. Ask yourself: - What monuments of power, control, status, and security am I building? - What am I clutching onto that says "I don't need saving"? - Where am I trying to be king of my own life instead of surrendering to Jesus as King? Opposition isn't always persecution. Often it's more subtle: - Dismissal: Having your beliefs treated as foolish or backwards - Doubt: Questions that chip away at your faith ("You sure you believe that?") - Isolation: Feeling alone when the world around you doesn't celebrate what you celebrate - Tragedy: When hard times make those doubting questions more convincing

chunks.heading: ✨ The Path to Conviction
chunks.text: How do we move from being like Herod to being like the wise men? Remember your desperation. - Recognize the true weight of depravity—not just "making mistakes" but being in complete rebellion against God - Understand that you had no capacity to even desire God on your own - Marvel at the miracle of your salvation—that God pursued you when you couldn't pursue Him - Remember you are co-heirs with Christ, adopted as sons and daughters "The more we recognize the reality of our depravity, the greater our need for Jesus Christ."

chunks.heading: 🙏 For Those Who've Lost Their Fire
chunks.text: If you feel stagnant in your faith, if you've lost that initial passion: You may have lost sight of your own depravity. - We've lost sight of our desperate state and how much we need a Savior - We've started thinking too highly of ourselves - We've begun clutching our achievements and legacies - We're essentially saying, "We don't need saving" The solution: Return to the reality of who you were without Christ and the miracle of what He's done for you.

chunks.heading: 🎯 The Central Choice
chunks.text: Will you choose Jesus as king of your life? Or will you clutch onto your throne as king of your own life? Remember the contrast: 👑 King of Your Own Life A very temporary kingdom. All monuments eventually crumble. Even Herod's greatest works lie forgotten in ruins. ✝️ Jesus as King An eternal kingdom. No death, no sin. The last shall be first. The least shall be greatest.

chunks.heading: 1. What forms of opposition are you currently encountering?
chunks.text: Consider the opposition that challenges your resolve and faith: - Are people dismissing or questioning your beliefs? - Are you facing uncertainty about the path God has you on? - Are circumstances making you doubt whether you've heard from God correctly? - Are you experiencing the discomfort of being "out of step" with the world around you? Take a moment to write or pray through your answer:

chunks.heading: 2. What are the various Herodiums you have built in your own life?
chunks.text: Think about your own "artificial mountains of glory": - Career achievements: Are you building your identity on professional success? - Financial security: Is your net worth your safety net instead of God? - Social status: Are you curating an image or reputation to maintain? - Family legacy: Are you building a name for yourself and your children? - Ministry impact: Even in serving God, are you building monuments to yourself? - Control systems: What structures have you created to feel safe and in control? Be honest—what are you clutching onto?

chunks.heading: 3. Have you recognized the reality of your depravity?
chunks.text: This is the key to passionate, steadfast faith: - Do you see your sinfulness as just "falling short" or as complete rebellion? - Do you understand you had no capacity to even desire God without His intervention? - Can you marvel at the miracle that God pursued you when you couldn't pursue Him? - Does your salvation feel like a ticket to heaven or an incredible rescue from death? "Greater than any gift you could have ever received is God sending his son to be your Savior King to rescue you from depravity, to make you co-heirs with him, to adopt you into his family as sons and daughters." Reflect on the weight of what has been accomplished for you:

chunks.heading: 🌟 Closing Prayer
chunks.text: "This Christmas week, may we come and bow at his feet as the wise men did and worship him, no matter the opposition that is around us." Pray that God would: - Help you identify and tear down the monuments you've created for yourself - Give you direction in how to live for Him instead of building spotlights on yourself - Speak that still, quiet voice in your heart about how to respond to Him in faith - Help you live a worshipful life no matter the opposition around you

chunks.heading: 📚 Additional Resources
chunks.text: - 🎥 The Star of Bethlehem Documentary by Rick Larson Explore the astronomical evidence discussed in this sermon - 🎄 Christmas Eve Services Rejoice In The Hope - Candlelight Services at Island ECC - 💝 Love Offering Support global outreach projects including Faith Comes by Hearing's Vision 2033 - 🫀 Journey of the Heart Workshop Starting January 8, 2026 - A 6-week workshop on paying attention to your heart

sermons.title: The Weary World Rejoices: Rejoice in Anticipation | Island ECC

chunks.heading: 📖 Sermon Overview / Date / Speaker / Church / 🎵 Worship Songs
chunks.text: As the excitement of Christmas fades, many experience a sense of withdrawal. Yet, did you know that Advent was originally intended as a season to anticipate Christ's second coming? The Christian life is meant to be a continual Advent, an ongoing longing for His return. Unfortunately, many of us lose that fervor. How can we cultivate a renewed eagerness and hunger for His return in our everyday lives? 📅 28 Dec 2025 🎤 Pastor Kevin ⛪ Island ECC - Bless God - Revelation Song - King of Kings - The Blood - He Who Is To Come

chunks.heading: Introduction: The Post-Christmas Letdown / The Honest Question: Are We Really Eager?
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin shares his struggle with post-Christmas withdrawal—not missing Christmas Day itself, but the Advent season of anticipation. He reveals that Advent wasn't always just about preparing for Christmas Day, but originally had three purposes: - ✝️ Celebrate Jesus coming into the world in human form - 💝 Recognize Jesus coming into our lives to transform and save us - 👑 Prepare for His second coming as King "Advent is meant to be a season to remind us and compel us towards a lifelong anticipation of Jesus. That excitement that we have for Christmas Day, we should have that same excitement, if not more, for the return of Jesus." Pastor Kevin confesses: "We're more excited about the next iPhone release than we are about Jesus coming back." He contrasts this with biblical authors like Paul, who wrote of eagerly awaiting Christ's return, and John Calvin, who spoke of hungering for that great day. "Do the words hunger and eager describe your anticipation of Jesus?"

chunks.heading: 🔑 Ingredient #1: Keep Working
chunks.text: From Matthew 24:36-51, Jesus teaches about the unknown timing of His return through the parable of the faithful and wicked servants. Matthew 24:44-46 (NIV): "So you also must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour when you do not expect him. Who then is the faithful and wise servant, whom the master has put in charge of the servants in his household to give them their food at the proper time? It will be good for that servant whose master finds him doing so when he returns." Key insight: The faithful servant keeps working because he expects his master to show up at any moment. The wicked servant stops working because he expects his master won't come at all. What is our work? Before Jesus ascended, He commanded: "You will be my witnesses" (Acts 1:8). Our task is to: - 📢 Share the gospel with others - 💖 Live out the gospel in love, forgiveness, and humility - 🍞 "Feed His sheep" as Jesus instructed Peter "The more we do it, the more we will long for his return, the more excited we will be for his return. If you want to have a lifelong anticipation for the return of Jesus, do a good job with the task that God has given to you."

chunks.heading: 🔑 Ingredient #2: Preparation / The Affection Analogy: Visiting Mother
chunks.text: From Matthew 25:1-13, Jesus teaches through the parable of the ten virgins (bridesmaids). Matthew 25:1-4 (NIV): "At that time the kingdom of heaven will be like ten virgins who took their lamps and went out to meet the bridegroom. Five of them were foolish and five were wise. The foolish ones took their lamps but did not take any oil with them. The wise ones, however, took oil in jars along with their lamps." The difference? Both groups fell asleep. But the wise had extra oil—they were prepared for a delay. The unprepared ones ran out and missed the wedding feast entirely. Two Hints About Spiritual Preparation: Hint #1: It's About Your Heart The bridegroom says to the unprepared: "I don't know you" (Matthew 25:12). This echoes Jesus' warning in Matthew 7:21-23 about those who prophesy and perform miracles in His name, yet Jesus says, "I never knew you." "You can do a lot of things for God, but not really know him. If you serve on the worship team, you show up and you play these songs, but these songs are just chords to you... You can do all these things and still not know him, and still be spiritually dry." Hint #2: It Cannot Be Done for You The wise bridesmaids couldn't share their oil. Spiritual preparation is personal—it's between you and God alone. "If you're being dragged here to church by your family or your spouse, that's great. But your internal heart change, your belief in God, that's on you and you alone. If your prayer life is relegated to when the pastor prays at the end of a sermon, you need to develop your own prayer life. You got to get your own oil." Summary: Spiritual preparation is about your affection for God that cannot be faked by action and cannot be outsourced to another person. Pastor Kevin illustrates with family relationships: - Close relationship: Mom visits → through the roof with excitement - Complicated relationship: Mom visits → nervous anticipation - Estranged relationship: Mom visits → indifference (don't believe she'll actually come) "Your affection determines your readiness. Your affection for God determines your readiness and your anticipation."

chunks.heading: Practical Advice: Quantity Time is Quality Time
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin shares relationship wisdom he received when dating his wife Emily: "Quantity time is quality time." Grand dates are wonderful, but nothing beats daily presence. Applied to our relationship with God: "Nothing beats the day in, day out quantity time that you spend with him. What if reading the Bible wasn't just getting through a Bible reading plan? What if you read the Bible to understand God more? What if you paused after getting to the verse of the day on your phone and you asked God, 'Lord, what do you want to show me about yourself?'" On prayer: "What if prayer was also a time when you can enjoy his presence? If you've never had a time of prayer where you just stayed silent, not really think anything, and enjoyed your time with God, I'm telling you, you are missing out." Approach spiritual disciplines with relationship as the goal, not just completion.

chunks.heading: Closing Illustration: The Apple Watch
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin confesses his material desire for an Apple Watch Ultra 3 and his love for the research phase—the anticipation itself. When his wife suggested delaying the purchase for a future model with better features, she said: "The greatest gift I can give you this Christmas, Kevin, is more time for anticipation." The principle: Anticipating the future changes your present. It gives excitement, purpose, direction, and joy. "When the focus of our anticipation is the coming of Jesus, you will feel all these feelings more profoundly. They will be more long-lasting. If you struggle with loneliness, with emptiness, you're living a life devoid of joy and hope—can I invite you to embrace the anticipation of Christ? To let the hope of Jesus ignite in you a perpetual Advent."

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Bible Verses / 💡 Practical Application / Be a Faithful Witness / Get Your Own Oil / Read for Relationship / Pray for Presence / Quantity Time = Quality Time / Grow Your Affection / 🎁 The Greatest Gift
chunks.text: Matthew 24:36-39 (NIV) "But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father. As it was in the days of Noah, so it will be at the coming of the Son of Man. For in the days before the flood, people were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, up to the day Noah entered the ark; and they knew nothing about what would happen until the flood came and took them all away. That is how it will be at the coming of the Son of Man." Matthew 24:42-44 (NIV) "Therefore keep watch, because you do not know on what day your Lord will come. But understand this: If the owner of the house had known at what time of night the thief was coming, he would have kept watch and would not have let his house be broken into. So you also must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour when you do not expect him." Matthew 24:45-51 (NIV) "Who then is the faithful and wise servant, whom the master has put in charge of the servants in his household to give them their food at the proper time? It will be good for that servant whose master finds him doing so when he returns. Truly I tell you, he will put him in charge of all his possessions. But suppose that servant is wicked and says to himself, 'My master is staying away a long time,' and he then begins to beat his fellow servants and to eat and drink with drunkards. The master of that servant will come on a day when he does not expect him and at an hour he is not aware of. He will cut him to pieces and assign him a place with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth." Matthew 25:1-13 (NIV) "At that time the kingdom of heaven will be like ten virgins who took their lamps and went out to meet the bridegroom. Five of them were foolish and five were wise. The foolish ones took their lamps but did not take any oil with them. The wise ones, however, took oil in jars along with their lamps. The bridegroom was a long time in coming, and they all became drowsy and fell asleep. At midnight the cry rang out: 'Here's the bridegroom! Come out to meet him!' Then all the virgins woke up and trimmed their lamps. The foolish ones said to the wise, 'Give us some of your oil; our lamps are going out.' 'No,' they replied, 'there may not be enough for both us and you. Instead, go to those who sell oil and buy some for yourselves.' But while they were on their way to buy the oil, the bridegroom arrived. The virgins who were ready went in with him to the wedding banquet. And the door was shut. Later the others also came. 'Lord, Lord,' they said, 'open the door for us!' But he replied, 'Truly I tell you, I don't know you.' Therefore keep watch, because you do not know the day or the hour." Acts 1:8 (NIV) "But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you; and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth." Matthew 7:21-23 (NIV) "Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven. Many will say to me on that day, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name and in your name drive out demons and in your name perform many miracles?' Then I will tell them plainly, 'I never knew you. Away from me, you evildoers!'" 🎯 Don't wait for the "perfect moment" to share the gospel. The more diligently you do the work God has given you, the more excited you'll be to give account when Jesus returns. Share with urgency and love. 🕯️ Spiritual preparation cannot be outsourced. Develop your own prayer life, your own Bible reading routine, your own relationship with God. Don't rely on corporate worship alone—cultivate daily intimacy with Jesus. 📖 Approach the Bible not as a checklist, but as God's way of revealing Himself to you. Ask: "Lord, what do you want to show me about yourself today?" Let Scripture deepen your affection for Him. 🙏 Prayer isn't just bringing requests to God. Try silent prayer—just enjoying His presence without words. This builds affection and transforms prayer from duty to delight. ⏰ Grand spiritual experiences are wonderful, but daily consistency wins. Spend time with God every single day—even if brief. Relationship is built in the ordinary moments. 💝 The greater your affection for God, the greater your anticipation for Jesus' return. Prioritize activities that deepen your love for Him—worship, community, service, reflection. "Anticipating the future changes your present. It gives you excitement, purpose, something to think about and to fixate your attention on, gives you a pep in your step. When the focus of our anticipation is the coming of Jesus, you will feel all these feelings more profoundly." — Pastor Kevin

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions / 📝 Personal Commitment
chunks.text: 1. What is the greatest obstacle that keeps you from living a life of anticipation for the return of Jesus? ▼ Consider these possibilities: - Comfort in earthly life—things are "good enough" that you don't long for something better - Busyness and distraction—too focused on immediate concerns to think about eternal realities - Doubt or uncertainty—not fully convinced Jesus will actually return - Fear of judgment—worried about giving account rather than excited about reunion - Weak affection for God—the relationship isn't close enough to produce genuine longing Reflection prompts: - Am I more excited about upcoming earthly events (vacations, purchases, promotions) than about Jesus' return? - When I think about Jesus coming back, what's my primary emotion: excitement, anxiety, or indifference? - What would need to change in my life for me to genuinely long for His return? 2. What is one thing you plan on doing in the new year to grow your affection towards God? ▼ Ideas from the sermon: - Daily Bible reading with the goal of understanding God better, not just completing a plan - Silent prayer to simply enjoy God's presence without bringing requests - Faithful witnessing by sharing the gospel with a specific friend or family member - Serving others as a way to "feed His sheep" and live out the gospel - Morning/evening routine dedicated to spending quantity time with God Make it specific: - What exactly will you do? (e.g., "Read one chapter before breakfast") - When will you do it? (e.g., "Every morning at 6:30 AM") - How will you track it? (e.g., "Journal one insight per day") - Who will keep you accountable? (e.g., "Share progress with care group weekly") Remember: Approach this with relationship as the goal. The purpose isn't to check a box, but to grow closer to God so that your affection deepens and your anticipation for His return intensifies. Write down your commitment for 2026. Share it with a trusted friend or care group member. "In 2026, I will grow my affection for God by..." ✍️ (Take a moment to write this down)

sermons.title: Soul Garden: Above All Else | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: Guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it

chunks.heading: 📖 Sermon Overview / Date / Speaker / Series / Church
chunks.text: "The wisest man who ever lived and the Creator of our souls agree on this: who we are in our heart and how we care for our heart will determine the course of our life." — Pastor Eric We would be wise to lean in and pay close attention to our heart—the thing from which our whole life flows. In this first message of the Soul Garden series, Pastor Eric explores why Scripture commands us to guard our hearts above all else, what the heart really encompasses in biblical understanding, and how we can begin the daily practice of paying attention to our inner life. 📅 4 Jan 2026 🎤 Pastor Eric 📚 Soul Garden ⛪ Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎵 Worship Songs
chunks.text: - Won't Stop Now - Made For More - Worthy Of It All - A Thousand Hallelujahs 🌿 Series Introduction The Soul Garden series invites us to spend several weeks journeying through Scripture to understand our hearts. Just like a garden needs watching over, nourishing, watering, protecting, and pruning for growth and health—so do our hearts. This series uses the garden metaphor to explore how we can guard, care for, preserve, and protect our inner life.

chunks.heading: Introduction: The Complexity of the Heart
chunks.text: Pastor Eric opens by illustrating how the English word "heart" carries so many meanings: - "I gave you my heart" — I love you - "You broke my heart" — you no longer love me - "Change of heart" — thinking differently - "Cross my heart" — I promise I'm telling the truth - "Heart of gold" — they are kind - "Lost heart" — gave up - "Played with heart" — never stopped fighting - "Wholeheartedly" — full effort "The heart is complex, but it is central. As we dive into looking at the heart with the lens of spiritual vitality—wholeness, holiness, happiness, flourishing, and joy—it will be worth it."

chunks.heading: The Garden Metaphor
chunks.text: The series uses the framework of a garden to talk about guarding and caring for our hearts: 🌻 Just Like a Garden... A garden needs watching over, nourishing, watering, protecting, and pruning for growth, life, and health. So do our hearts. This could be one of the wisest and most important things we do this year—one that changes our trajectory for many years.

chunks.heading: The Key Passage: Proverbs 4:20-27
chunks.text: "My son, pay attention to what I say. Turn your ear to my words. Do not let them out of your sight, and keep them within your heart, for they are life to those who find them, and health to one's whole body. Above all else, guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it." — Proverbs 4:20-23 (NIV) The Question: If the wisest man who ever lived (Solomon) and the One who created you (Jesus) teamed up to give you advice, what would it be? Solomon says: "Above all else" — above everything — pay attention, watch, guard, keep your heart. Jesus says: "Love the Lord your God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength" — the greatest commandment. "Both the wisest one who ever lived and the one who's wiser than him are in agreement. This isn't advice—it's a command."

chunks.heading: What Is in Your Heart Matters Most
chunks.text: Pastor Eric references Dallas Willard's book Renovation of the Heart: "What is in our heart matters more than anything else for who we become and what becomes of us." Key insight: Who you are on the inside—your inner self, your inner sense of self—is who you really are. This is what God is concerned with. This is what He is transforming. 💡 The Revolution of the Heart Dallas Willard argues that Jesus' revolution wasn't about organizations, institutions, laws, or rules. His revolution was a revolution of the heart. The only way for real change is to have change on the inside—not just modifying behavior to appear good, holy, or clean.

chunks.heading: The Biblical Understanding of "Heart" / What We Love / What We Know / What We Choose
chunks.text: In Hebrew culture and Old Testament writings, the heart (לֵב, lev) is much more holistic than our modern Western thinking. It encompasses: - Desires and affections - Thoughts and intentions - Will and choices - Emotions—all working together as your unified inner self The word "heart" appears over 1,000 times in Scripture. Anything that appears that often is something God wants us to know about! ❤️ Our affections, what we seek, yearn for, want, desire 🧠 Our thoughts, intentions, imaginations ⚡ Our will—ability to say yes or no, face temptation, pursue discipline

chunks.heading: Managing the Outside vs. The Inside
chunks.text: Pastor Eric shares a personal story about getting frustrated with his kids. Outwardly, he managed his response—but inwardly, he noticed something different: "The quickness with which I got frustrated inside and the intensity with which I got frustrated at a normal thing was different than I had ever encountered. It caught me off guard. I was like, 'Whoa, that's in there.'" Key insight: We've all learned to manage what we do on the outside. We put on a face, carry a persona, learn not to react. But who we are on the inside is a better reflection of who we really are. 🎭 There's No Such Thing as "Blowing It" Scripture would say there's no such thing as "blowing it." What comes out was already in you. When something erupts that seems "out of character," it may just be out of character for how you've managed situations before. The Bible calls us to transformation on the inside—not just behavior modification on the outside.

chunks.heading: How to Guard Your Heart: Preserve & Protect
chunks.text: Craig Troxel, in his book With All Your Heart, uses helpful language about what it means to guard or "keep" your heart: 👁️ Keep an Eye ON Your Heart Pay attention to what's going on inside. Watch over the growth. What's happening within your heart? 🛡️ Keep an Eye OUT FOR Your Heart Be aware of external influences. What environment are you in? What are you giving access to your heart? Like a parent keeps an eye on their kids (health, studies) and keeps an eye out for their kids (oncoming cars, harm)—we do both for our hearts. "The greatest difficulty in conversion is to win the heart to God. But the greatest difficulty after conversion is to keep the heart with God." — John Flavel (Puritan author)

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Bible Verses / 💡 Practical Application / What We Love / What We Know
chunks.text: Proverbs 4:20-23 (NIV) "My son, pay attention to what I say; turn your ear to my words. Do not let them out of your sight, keep them within your heart; for they are life to those who find them and health to one's whole body. Above all else, guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it." Proverbs 4:24-27 (NIV) "Keep your mouth free of perversity; keep corrupt talk far from your lips. Let your eyes look straight ahead; fix your gaze directly before you. Give careful thought to the paths for your feet and be steadfast in all your ways. Do not turn to the right or the left; keep your foot from evil." Psalm 139:23-24 (NIV) "Search me, God, and know my heart; test me and know my anxious thoughts. See if there is any offensive way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting." Psalm 121:1-8 (ESV) "I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come? My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth. He will not let your foot be moved; he who keeps you will not slumber... The LORD is your keeper... The LORD will keep you from all evil; he will keep your life." Proverbs 19:21 (NIV) "Many are the plans in a person's heart, but it is the LORD's purpose that prevails." Jude 1:24-25 (ESV) "Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy, to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen." "The Lord is your keeper. The Lord is your shade on your right hand... The Lord will keep your going out and your coming in from this time forth and forevermore." — Psalm 121:5, 8 Pastor Eric provides daily reflection questions to help us become aware of what's going on in our hearts. These are available on the church app and sermon page. ❤️ - What desires did I give attention to today that aren't healthy for me? - Was there any conflict between what I desired and what I feel God wants from me? - What was my overriding emotion today? How did that serve me? 🧠 - What thoughts dominated my mind today? - How did dwelling on those thoughts influence my feelings or actions? - Did any truth of God or Scripture guide my thought life today? - Was there any media or conversations that influenced my thought life? ⚡

chunks.heading: What We Choose
chunks.text: - What choices did I make today that reflect God's desires for me? - Were there moments where I chose convenience over integrity? - Did I seek God's guidance in the choices I made today? 📝 Daily Practice Set aside a few minutes at the end of each day to reflect on these questions. You don't have to answer all nine—even engaging with a few will help you become more aware of what's going on in your heart. The goal is awareness. As our days progress so fast, we may miss important signals if we don't stop and reflect.

chunks.heading: 🙏 The Prayer to Pray
chunks.text: "Search me, God, and know my heart; test me and know my anxious thoughts. See if there is any offensive way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting." — Psalm 139:23-24 This is a terrifying but transformative prayer. It takes deep trust and humility to invite God to search your heart. But He wants good for you, and it's a safe place to be honest.

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: 1. How would you compare the way you guard your heart to how you guard other valuable things? ▼ Consider how much effort you put into guarding: - Your finances — bank accounts, investments, budgets - Your career — reputation, networking, skill development - Your physical health — exercise, diet, medical check-ups - Your reputation — what others think of you Honest assessment: Does your effort to guard your heart reflect the priority Scripture says it should be given? Is your heart getting "above all else" treatment? 2. What is one way you can preserve and protect your heart better this week? ▼ To preserve (keep an eye ON): - Daily reflection using the "What we love / know / choose" questions - Journaling your emotions, thoughts, and decisions - Prayer time specifically for heart examination - Talking with a trusted friend about what you're noticing inside To protect (keep an eye OUT FOR): - Evaluating what media you're consuming - Assessing which relationships are influencing you - Setting boundaries on environments that harm your heart - Creating space for silence and Scripture Remember: The Keeper of your heart is with you. You're not doing this alone. God is sovereign, in control, and empowering you through His Holy Spirit. 3. Is there something in your heart that has surfaced recently that caught you off guard? ▼ Pastor Eric shared how an unusual intensity of frustration caught him by surprise. Sometimes things surface that we didn't know were there: - An unexpected burst of anger or resentment - A desire you didn't realize was so strong - Fear or anxiety that seemed disproportionate - Jealousy, comparison, or contempt toward someone - Apathy toward things that should matter Next step: Rather than being alarmed, see this as valuable data. Bring it to God in prayer. Ask the Holy Spirit to help you understand what's going on and where it came from. This is the beginning of heart transformation.

chunks.heading: 🌱 Coming Up in Soul Garden
chunks.text: This is just the beginning of a multi-week journey exploring our hearts through Scripture. Future weeks will explore: - What is God's vision for the garden of your heart? - What pruning needs to happen? - What kind of soil are we planted in (community)? - What do we do with the fruit and blessings? - What might be out to harm our heart? "We live from our hearts. That's where life is found, from what comes within." — Dallas Willard, Renovation of the Heart

sermons.title: Soul Garden: Where Are We Growing? | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: Desire is the engine of our hearts—follow the right light source

chunks.heading: 📖 Sermon Overview / Date / Speaker / Series / Church
chunks.text: "Our hearts grow in the direction of our desires. Whatever is brightest in your life is shaping how you grow." — Pastor Tim Desire is the engine of our hearts. We are always growing toward something. And while desire itself is not a bad thing, it can slowly become aimed at the wrong things. In this second message of the Soul Garden series, Pastor Tim explores how our hearts are shaped by what we love and how God invites us to refocus our desires so we keep growing in the right direction—like a sunflower following the sun. 📅 11 Jan 2026 🎤 Pastor Tim 📚 Soul Garden (Part 2) ⛪ Island ECC

chunks.heading: 🎵 Worship Songs
chunks.text: - Christ Is The Center - Another Like You - Still - Lord I Need You 🌻 The Sunflower Principle A sunflower tracks the sun's movement across the sky every single day. At night, when the sun sets in the west, it turns back east, waiting for the sun to return. What an amazing metaphor for our lives—following the main light source, turning our attention back to God each morning.

chunks.heading: Introduction: Our Hearts Grow Toward Our Desires
chunks.text: Pastor Tim begins with the theme verse for the Soul Garden series: "Above all else, guard your heart for everything you do flows from it." — Proverbs 4:23 (NIV) Everything we do in life flows from our heart. We need to protect and guard our heart to make sure we know where it's growing towards. Our hearts grow in the direction of our desires.

chunks.heading: The iPhone Revolution: A Cautionary Tale
chunks.text: Nineteen years ago, Apple announced the iPhone—a revolutionary device. Pastor Tim remembers being excited, telling his wife Cindy all the possibilities. But fast forward almost 20 years: "This revolutionary device has kind of become a ball and chain... A lot of companies are fighting for our attention. The most valuable commodity in this world now is our time and attention." 💡 The Algorithm of Our Lives A person who worked at a large social media company was quoted in the New York Times: "I am convinced the devil lives in our phones." This isn't a message about the evils of technology—it's about thinking about what our attention is giving power to. At the end of the year, these companies give us a "gift"—Spotify Wrapped, YouTube Rewind—showing us exactly where we devoted all our time. Often we look and think: "That's what I devoted all of my time and attention to?"

chunks.heading: Treasure and Heart: Jesus' Teaching
chunks.text: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus speaks about material things: "Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal... For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." — Matthew 6:19-21 (NIV) What we pay attention to constitutes our inner lives. Our desires—the things we're attracted to, the things we chase—are shaping our hearts and moving them in that direction.

chunks.heading: Broken Cisterns: The Prophet Jeremiah's Warning
chunks.text: The prophet Jeremiah spoke to people who were outwardly religious but inwardly wavering: "My people have committed two sins: They have forsaken me, the spring of living water, and have dug their own cisterns, broken cisterns that cannot hold water." — Jeremiah 2:13 (NIV) They forsook God who provides unlimited fresh water and created man-made water receptacles that crack, break, and run dry. 🚰 Tim Keller on Idols "An idol is anything more important to you than God. Anything that absorbs your heart and imagination more than God. Anything you seek to give you what only God can give."

chunks.heading: The Crooked Forest: A Lesson in Growth
chunks.text: Pastor Tim shows a fascinating picture of a forest in Poland where trees grow crooked. The consensus is that man intervened and made these trees grow this way—perhaps for chairs or curved boat lumber. 🌲 The Crooked Forest (Krzywy Las), Poland Located near Gryfino in West Pomerania, this unique grove contains approximately 400 Scots pine trees planted around 1930. Each tree exhibits a distinctive 90-degree bend at its base, all curving northward before straightening upwards. While the exact cause remains a mystery, theories suggest human intervention to cultivate curved timber for boat building or furniture. The surrounding forest consists of straight pines, making this formation even more striking—a powerful visual reminder of how outside influences can shape our growth. 🌲 What Happens When We Get Out of the Way Notice in the photo: as man got out of the way, the trees started growing back towards the correct direction—toward their main light source above. If we would just get out of our own way, put ourselves in the backseat, and focus on things above, we would begin growing in the right direction. When Our Desires Drift... 🎮 Control replaces Christ We think we can do things better than God 📱 Distraction replaces Devotion So many things pull us away 🛡️ Safety replaces Surrender We want comfort over God's will 📋 Productivity replaces Presence Doing FOR God, not WITH God 😐 Apathy replaces Awe We lose our wonder of who God is

chunks.heading: Refocusing on Jesus
chunks.text: "Let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith." — Hebrews 12:1-2 (NIV) Jesus says: "The eye is the lamp of the body. So if your eye is healthy, your whole body will be full of light." (Matthew 6:22) Our focus is very important. Everything we're bringing into our lives tells us where our light is. Our focus will feed our soul and help us grow the right way.

chunks.heading: The Pizza to Broccoli Transformation
chunks.text: Pastor Tim shares a personal story about his love for pizza—especially the all-you-can-eat pizza buffet near his university. But as he got older and started eating healthier: "I remember one specific night... I looked at the broccoli and my mouth started watering. For broccoli! And it hit me—what has happened to me? The same feeling I had for pizza is now for broccoli!" 🍕➡️🥦 God Changes Our Taste God doesn't take away our hunger—He changes our taste. If we keep chasing the wrong thing, He can help us change our taste for the right thing. If we enter into devotion with Him and focus our eyes on Christ, God can transform what we crave. "Taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the one who takes refuge in him." — Psalm 34:8 (NIV)

chunks.heading: The Sunflower: Following the True Light
chunks.text: 🌻 A sunflower tracks the sun's movement across the sky. At night, when the sun sets in the west, the flower turns back east, waiting for the sun to return. "The sunflower doesn't follow a lamp. It doesn't follow a candle. It follows the main source of light, the sun. God is saying: I'm the source of light that you need to follow. Stop looking at these other things that will never fill you." "Whatever is brightest in your life is shaping how you grow." Many of us have become desensitized—staring at our phones (a flashlight!) every night. But God is bright for us like the sun is bright for the sunflower. If we trust in God as our true light source, we will grow towards Him. "The greatest enemy of hunger for God is not poison, but apple pie. It is not the banquet of the wicked that dulls our appetite for heaven, but the endless nibbling at the table of the world." — John Piper, A Hunger for God

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Bible Verses / 💡 Practical Application / The Live • Look • Lean Framework / Live / Look / Lean / Start Your Day with God / Audit Your Attention / Change Your Taste / 🌿 Touch Grass / 🙏 A Prayer for Direction
chunks.text: Proverbs 4:23 (NIV) "Above all else, guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it." Matthew 6:19-21 (NIV) "Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal, but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven... For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." Jeremiah 2:13 (NIV) "My people have committed two sins: They have forsaken me, the spring of living water, and have dug their own cisterns, broken cisterns that cannot hold water." Genesis 3:6 (NIV) "When the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it." Romans 1:25 (NIV) "They exchanged the truth about God for a lie, and worshiped and served created things rather than the Creator—who is forever praised." Psalm 37:4 (NIV) "Delight yourself in the Lord, and he will give you the desires of your heart." Colossians 3:2 (NIV) "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth." Hebrews 12:1-2 (NIV) "Let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith." Matthew 6:22 (NIV) "The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are healthy, your whole body will be full of light." Psalm 34:8 (NIV) "Taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the one who takes refuge in him." Matthew 5:6 (NIV) "Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled." Psalm 27:4 (NIV) "One thing I ask from the Lord, this only do I seek: that I may dwell in the house of the Lord all the days of my life, to gaze on the beauty of the Lord and to seek him in his temple." "God is most glorified when we are most satisfied in him." — John Piper Pastor Tim summarizes the sermon with a beautiful framework drawn from Psalm 27:4—David's single request to dwell with God, gaze at His beauty, and seek Him. 1 Dwell with God Let your heart live at home with Him. Dwell in His presence daily—not just on Sundays. 2 Gaze at His Wonder Fix your eyes on the beauty of the Lord. Be inspired and in awe of who God is. 3 Seek Desires That Honor God Lean toward Him with everything you do. Let your desires be directed toward Him. 🌅 Like the sunflower turning east overnight to await the sunrise, prepare your heart each evening and morning to focus on God first—before the phone, before the emails, before the distractions. 📵 What has captured your attention this week? Review your screen time, your browsing history, your "year in review." Are these things moving your heart toward God or away from Him? 🍽️ Start hungering for the right things. Spend time in God's word, in prayer, in community. Over time, God will change your taste so that righteousness becomes what you crave. One of Pastor Tim's favorite new Gen Z slang terms is "touch grass"—go offline, turn off your computer, go outside and literally touch grass. Go into nature, explore God's beautiful creation—not on a screen, but IRL (in real life). Maybe you'll see a sunflower. "God, if we are distracted, if our desires are off course, if we are hungering for the wrong things—change our tastes. Help us to taste and see that you are good, and may you truly be the center of our lives. Help us to look to you as our source and grow in the right direction." — Pastor Tim's closing prayer

chunks.heading: 🤔 Reflection Questions / 🌱 Coming Up in Soul Garden
chunks.text: 📋 Questions from the Sermon - What has quietly become bigger than God in my heart, even if I would never say it out loud? What am I treasuring right now? - What can I do this week to help me refocus on God to make sure I'm growing in the right direction? 1. What is my heart growing towards right now? ▼ This is the key question Pastor Tim asks us to leave with. Take an honest assessment: - Where does most of my attention go each day? - What fills my mind when I have free time? - What would my "year in review" reveal about my priorities? - If my heart is like a plant, what "light source" am I growing toward? Remember: "Whatever is brightest in your life is shaping how you grow." 2. What "broken cisterns" have I been digging? ▼ Jeremiah says we forsake the spring of living water for broken cisterns that can't hold water. Consider: - What am I looking to for fulfillment that isn't God? - What absorbs my heart and imagination more than God does? - What do I seek to give me what only God can give? (Security? Identity? Worth? Love?) - Are there idols in my life that I wouldn't call "idols" but have become more important than God? Note: These idols may not be evil things—they could be work, entertainment, relationships, or even ministry. The question is whether they've become bigger than God. 3. Am I like the crooked trees or the sunflower? ▼ Pastor Tim presented two powerful images: 🌲 The Crooked Forest Trees that grew crooked because man intervened—but when left alone, they grew back toward the light. 🌻 The Sunflower Follows the sun all day, then turns back east at night to await the sunrise—never following a lamp or candle. Ask yourself: - Have I been growing crooked because of outside influences? - What would it look like to "get out of my own way" and grow toward God? - Am I following the main light source, or distracted by smaller lights? 4. What tastes need to change in my life? ▼ Pastor Tim's story about pizza and broccoli illustrates how God can change our desires: "God doesn't take away our hunger—He changes our taste." Reflect: - What spiritual "pizza" am I still craving that isn't good for my soul? - What spiritual "broccoli" do I need to develop a taste for? - Am I willing to let God transform what I hunger for? - What steps can I take to "taste and see that the Lord is good"? 5. How can I Live, Look, and Lean toward God this week? ▼ Based on Psalm 27:4, create a practical plan: LIVE — Dwell with God - Start each day in God's presence before checking your phone - Create rhythms of prayer throughout the day - Be present in community worship on Sundays LOOK — Gaze at God's Wonder - Spend time in Scripture, looking at who God is - Go outside and appreciate His creation ("touch grass") - Reflect on His faithfulness in your life LEAN — Seek Desires That Honor God - Audit where your attention goes this week - Reduce time on things that pull you away from God - Invest time in what cultivates hunger for righteousness "Our hearts are restless until they rest in you." — Augustine The Soul Garden series continues as we explore how to guard our hearts by gardening our souls. Join us next week! ← Previous: Above All Else (Part 1)

sermons.title: Soul Garden: Pruning For Life | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "Every branch that does bear fruit He prunes so that it will be even more fruitful."

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview / 🎯 Two Main Questions Explored / 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: Central Theme: God disciplines us through pruning—not as punishment, but to make us more fruitful. Learning to recognize when God is pruning us and how to actively engage in the process is essential for spiritual growth. Key Metaphor: Just as a gardener prunes branches to redirect energy and produce more fruit, God removes even good things from our lives to help us become more fruitful in evangelism, holiness, righteousness, and peace. ❓ Question 1 How do we know when God's discipline is happening to us? 🛠️ Question 2 What are we supposed to do when we're going through God's discipline? - John 15:1-2 - Jesus as the vine, pruning for fruitfulness - Hebrews 12:10-11 - God's discipline produces holiness, righteousness, and peace

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways
chunks.text: - Not all difficulties are God's discipline - Sometimes bad things happen because the world is broken or there's spiritual attack. - Look for specific results - God's pruning produces: better witness, holiness, righteousness, and peace. - Discipline is continuous, not instant - God brings us to similar places and circumstances to give us opportunities to respond differently. - We must actively engage - Through reflection, connecting difficulties to sin patterns, and tracking long-term themes in our lives. - Remain in His love - Everything flows from staying connected to Jesus as the vine.

chunks.heading: 🌱 Understanding Pruning
chunks.text: Pruning is the process by which you cut away dead or dying branches to make room for new and fruitful branches. But why do certain branches become less fruitful? Plants need three things: - 💧 Water - ☀️ Sunlight - 🌱 Nutrients from the soil When these resources are impeded or cut off, branches become less fruitful. Some examples: - Competition: Too many branches on one side competing for the same resources - Blocking light: Branches growing inward or crossing over, blocking sunlight - Disease: Infected branches that need removal to preserve the rest of the plant - Scarcity: During droughts, pruning redirects limited resources to the most productive branches The Principle: Pruning redirects energy to make the plant fruitful. Similarly, God prunes us by removing even good things to redirect our energy, time, and focus to make us fruitful.

chunks.heading: 🍇 What is the "Fruit" God Seeks?
chunks.text: In John 15, the primary fruit Jesus refers to is people won to Christ through evangelism and discipleship. The Greek word karpos refers to tangible, harvestable fruit—not just internal character qualities. However, Hebrews 12 adds three more dimensions of fruitfulness: - Holiness - Sharing in God's attributes, reflecting His otherworldly love and wisdom - Righteousness - Living the life God calls us to, naturally and eagerly - Peace - Being in right relationship with God, where everything is as it should be

chunks.heading: 🚫 Two Misconceptions About Discipline
chunks.text: Misconception #1: Every Difficulty is God's Discipline The Truth: Not everything hard in life is God's discipline. Sometimes: - The world is simply broken (consequences of sin in general) - There's spiritual attack or oppression - It's just life being difficult How to Discern: Look for the four specific results mentioned above. If a difficulty is producing those outcomes, it may be God's pruning. Misconception #2: Discipline is Quick, Immediate, and Punitive The Truth: Hebrews 12:11 says discipline produces fruit "later on" and for those who are "trained by it." This means: - There's a time lag - You might not see results immediately - It's repetitive - Training happens over time, not in a single event - It's formative, not punitive - God is shaping you, not just punishing you

chunks.heading: 🎬 The Lion King and "Hakuna Matata"
chunks.text: Pastor Kevin shared a powerful illustration from The Lion King: Simba runs away from his past, feeling shame and guilt over his father's death. His friends teach him "Hakuna Matata"—no worries, forget the past. But the moral of the story is actually the opposite: you must confront your past to truly become who you're meant to be. Many of us adopt a "Hakuna Matata" approach to healing—we try to quickly move on, cut off our past, and avoid dealing with difficult patterns. But this approach doesn't work.

chunks.heading: 🔗 Family Systems Theory: "Cutoff"
chunks.text: In counseling, there's a concept called cutoff—when people try to heal by cutting out their past, thinking it's quick and painless. But: Cutoff doesn't cut off anything—it seals your past. You don't eliminate your past; you freeze it. All your patterns of interacting, responding, and attaching are still drawn from your frozen past. Example: If you were avoidant as a child because of tough parents, even in a loving community, you'll still be instinctively avoidant. Your subconscious patterns sabotage your best intentions to be vulnerable.

chunks.heading: 🔄 God's Continuous Discipline
chunks.text: God's discipline isn't about cutting off your past—it's about bringing you back to similar places and circumstances so you can confront patterns and respond differently. Personal Example: Pastor Kevin shared how his desire to be liked led him to always say "yes" first, even when he was too busy. This caused him to drop responsibilities, often affecting his family. God kept bringing him to situations where people asked him to do things, giving him opportunities to respond differently. When Pastor Kevin first joined Island ECC, one of the staff ethos was literally titled "Yes, First." (meaning say yes to new ideas and figure out how later). For him, this was God bringing him to a similar circumstance to work through his people-pleasing pattern.

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages / 🔍 Additional References
chunks.text: 📖 John 15:1-2 (NIV) "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener. He cuts off every branch in me that bears no fruit, while every branch that does bear fruit he prunes so that it will be even more fruitful." 📖 John 15:5 (NIV) "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing." 📖 John 15:9 (NIV) "As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Now remain in my love." 📖 Hebrews 12:7-8 (NIV) "Endure hardship as discipline; God is treating you as his children. For what children are not disciplined by their father? If you are not disciplined—and everyone undergoes discipline—then you are not legitimate, not true sons and daughters at all." 📖 Hebrews 12:10-11 (NIV) "They disciplined us for a little while as they thought best; but God disciplines us for our good, in order that we may share in his holiness. No discipline seems pleasant at the time, but painful. Later on, however, it produces a harvest of righteousness and peace for those who have been trained by it." 📖 Hebrews 12:12-13 (NIV) "Therefore, strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees. Make level paths for your feet, so that the lame may not be disabled, but rather healed." - Isaiah 35 - "Strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees" (holding on to God's promises) - Proverbs - "Make level paths" (removing obstacles that cause stumbling)

chunks.heading: ❌ Breaking Down Misconceptions
chunks.text: ❌ Misconception #1: Every difficulty is God's discipline ▼ Why This is Wrong: Not every hard thing you experience is God disciplining you. Sometimes: - The world is broken - We live in a fallen world affected by sin - Spiritual attack - The enemy opposes God's people - Natural consequences - Choices have outcomes unrelated to divine discipline How to Discern: Look for these four specific results that come from God's discipline: - Better Witness - Are you more effective in evangelism and discipleship? - Holiness - Do you exhibit God's otherworldly attributes (love, wisdom, patience)? - Righteousness - Do you naturally desire to live as God calls you to? - Peace - Is your relationship with God being restored? Practical Step: Engage in reflection to see if a difficulty is tied to sin in your life. For example: Were you fired because you were hard to work with, unapologetic, arrogant? Is that tied to pride lurking in your life? ❌ Misconception #2: Discipline is quick, immediate, and punitive ▼ Why This is Wrong: Hebrews 12:11 says discipline produces fruit "later on" for those who are "trained by it." This reveals two key truths: 1. There's a Time Lag You might be going through something difficult right now, but you may not see the results immediately. You could be standing in the gap, and the fruit will come later. 2. It's Repetitive Training Training isn't a single event—it's consistent, repetitive, and intentional. You don't run once and call it training. Similarly, spiritual discipline happens over and over. We Don't Like This: We want change to be immediate. We try to short-circuit the process by making transformation instant—cutting off all past relationships, starting fresh, thinking we're completely new. But this approach leaves many things undealt with. The Pattern: God brings you to similar places and similar circumstances to give you opportunities to respond differently. This is how you truly heal and grow. 💡 The "Hakuna Matata" Trap ▼ Many of us who grew up in the 90s absorbed "Hakuna Matata" as a life philosophy without realizing it: Hakuna matata, what a wonderful phrase. Hakuna matata, ain't no passing craze. It means no worries for the rest of your days. It's our problem-free philosophy. We thought the message was: Forget your worries. Don't care about your past. Just cut everything off and move on. But That's Not The Real Moral: The actual message of The Lion King is that you must confront your past to truly assume your full identity and become who you're meant to be. Family Systems Theory: "Cutoff" This counseling concept describes what happens when we try to heal by cutting off our past: - We think cutoff eliminates the past - Reality - It only seals and freezes the past - Result - All our old patterns of interaction remain, drawn from our frozen past Example: If you were avoidant as a child, you'll still be avoidant in new communities—even loving ones—because the instinct is subconscious and sabotages your best intentions.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Practice Reflection
chunks.text: We cannot be passive onlookers of our own pruning process. We must actively engage. The primary way to be active is through reflection. 📝 Short-Range Reflection When facing a difficulty, reflect to see if it's tied to sin in your life. Connect the circumstance to deeper patterns. Example: "Was I fired because I was hard to work with, unapologetic, or arrogant? Is this tied to pride?" 🔭 Long-Range Reflection Zoom out and look for recurring themes. Take inventory at the end of the year or beginning of a new year. Question: "Are there similar lessons God keeps teaching me? Similar places He keeps bringing me?"

chunks.heading: 💪 Two Active Responses (Hebrews 12:12-13)
chunks.text: 1. Strengthen Your Feeble Arms and Weak Knees This phrase from Isaiah 35 means: Hold on to the promises of God. - It's not about mustering willpower - It's about clinging to what God has said, even when everything around you is crumbling - God promises to be with you and develop character in you despite your doubts 2. Make Level Paths for Your Feet This phrase from Proverbs means: Remove obstacles along your path. - Create new habits intentionally - Build new relationships strategically - Develop new decision-making patterns - Purpose: So when you come to that similar place and circumstance again, you can respond differently Both actions are only possible through consistent reflection. That's how you stay active in God's pruning process and respond differently when opportunities arise.

chunks.heading: 🌿 The Foundation: Remain in His Love
chunks.text: 📖 John 15:5, 9 "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing... As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Now remain in my love." Everything depends on remaining in Christ's love. Without this foundation, all the advice about reflection and growth is meaningless.

chunks.heading: Why "Remain in His Love" Matters:
chunks.text: - It's the main driver for evangelism - Otherwise, what are you doing it for? - It's the main driver for character development - These traits make you more Christ-like; if not for love of Him, what's the purpose? - It makes everything else possible - Confronting your past, reflecting deeply, changing patterns—these are only possible when rooted in His love - It makes growth natural - When your love for Him is genuine, the difficult work of pruning becomes something you naturally desire If you remain in His love, not only are you able to do the hard work of growth—those things will be second nature. Those things you will naturally desire to do if your love for Him is genuine.

chunks.heading: ✅ Action Steps Summary
