It wasn't easy — it was God's doing. 📖 Ezra 5:5 (NIV) "But the eye of their God was watching over the elders of the Jews, and they were not stopped until a report could go to Darius and his written reply be received." Even during opposition, God didn't leave them alone. He watched over them, buying them time to continue building. 📖 Ezra 6:22 (NIV) "For seven days they celebrated with joy the Festival of Unleavened Bread, because the Lord had filled them with joy by changing the attitude of the king of Assyria so that he assisted them in the work on the house of God, the God of Israel." God even enabled the celebration by filling their hearts with joy and turning the heart of the king to support them. Our Bold Faith Project: We might look at the move to Sunbeam Theater as Island ECC's project. But the book of Ezra reminds us that it is first and foremost God's project. He placed this vision in the hearts of our elders and pastors. He opened the door for Sunbeam. And every time you've contributed, you've been responding to God.

chunks.heading: 🏺 The People's Response: Set Apart for God
chunks.text: After the temple was completed, the Israelites didn't just move in and worship however they wanted. They were determined to renew themselves. 📖 Ezra 6:18 (NIV) "And they installed the priests in their divisions and the Levites in their groups for the service of God at Jerusalem, according to what is written in the Book of Moses." They went back to study Moses' instructions to make sure they would worship God the way God wanted to be worshipped — not however was easy or convenient. 📖 Ezra 6:21 (NIV) "So the Israelites who had returned from the exile ate it, together with all who had separated themselves from the unclean practices of their Gentile neighbors in order to seek the Lord, the God of Israel." Being set apart meant separating from unclean practices. The Hebrew word for "holy" literally means "set apart." They understood that a holy God requires holy worshippers.

chunks.heading: 🏺 The Golden Bowl
chunks.text: Zelda uses a vivid analogy: imagine a golden bowl that is reserved for the Lord — set apart, used only by God and for God, so that nothing unclean has ever entered it. That is what it means to be holy. And that is what the Israelites were striving for: to set themselves apart so their worship would be pleasing to the Lord.

chunks.heading: 📜 This Isn't Just an Old Testament Idea
chunks.text: The concept of being set apart appears powerfully in the New Testament too: 📖 Hebrews 12:1-2 (NIV) "Therefore, since we are surrounded by such a great cloud of witnesses, let us throw off everything that hinders and the sin that so easily entangles. And let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith." To run the race well, we must cast off everything that hinders — the same idea of being set apart, of keeping our eyes fixed on Jesus with focus and intentionality. 📖 2 Timothy 2:20-21 (NIV) "In a large house there are articles not only of gold and silver, but also of wood and clay; some are for special purposes and some for common use. Those who cleanse themselves from the latter will be instruments for special purposes, made holy, useful to the Master and prepared to do any good work." It doesn't matter what kind of vessel you are or have been. If you say to God, "I want to be set apart for you," He will sanctify you and make you useful for His purposes. 🏺 Common Use Used for ordinary purposes. Not set apart. Mixed with unclean practices. → 🏆 Special Purposes Made holy. Set apart for God. Useful to the Master. Ready for every good work. The Greek verb for "set apart" (sanctified) is in the passive voice: We don't sanctify ourselves — God sanctifies us when we say, "God, I want to be set apart for you." Our part is the willingness; His part is the transformation.

chunks.heading: 💡 Two Guiding Questions
chunks.text: Zelda closes with two practical guiding questions to help us grow into people who are set apart for God. Much of how we act and react is on autopilot. To be set apart requires developing new habits: 🤔 What does God think of this? Pause before your decisions. Would He be pleased with the choices you're making? Would He be pleased with the way you're reacting and interacting with people? 🙏 How would God want me to be? Would He want you to be kind? Gracious? Humble? When we form the habit of being mindful of God and His ways, we are well on the path of being set apart. Your Chinese New Year Pledge: Say to God: "I want to be renewed. I want to be the one set apart for God, so that I'm ready for every good work." It's going to take more than a small group of committed individuals — God is inviting all of us to partner with Him.

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: Sermon 1 — Hill Chau 📖 Ezra 4:2 (NIV) "Let us help you build because, like you, we seek your God and have been sacrificing to him since the time of Esarhaddon king of Assyria, who brought us here." The opposition's first tactic: deception. They pretended to worship the same God and offered to join the rebuilding. The Israelites saw through it and drew a clear line. 📖 Ezra 4:3 (NIV) "But Zerubbabel, Joshua and the rest of the heads of the families of Israel answered, 'You have no part with us in building a temple to our God. We alone will build it for the Lord, the God of Israel, as King Cyrus, the king of Persia, commanded us.'" A firm, clear boundary. The Israelites recognized the deception and refused to be entangled with the opposition. 📖 Ezra 4:4-5 (NIV) "Then the peoples around them set out to discourage the people of Judah and make them afraid to go on building. They bribed officials to work against them and frustrated their plans during the entire reign of Cyrus king of Persia and down to the reign of Darius king of Persia." After deception failed, the enemies escalated: discouragement, fear, bribery, and long-term frustration spanning the reigns of multiple kings. 📖 Ezra 4:24 (NIV) "Thus the work on the house of God in Jerusalem came to a standstill until the second year of the reign of Darius king of Persia." The opposition temporarily succeeded. The building stopped for years — but God's plan was not derailed, only delayed. 📖 Ephesians 6:11 (NIV) "Put on the full armor of God, so that you can take your stand against the devil's schemes." The Greek phrase "take your stand" is a military term for holding one's position. We stand ready with anticipation of attack, equipped with God's armor. 📖 Ezra 5:11 (NIV) "This is the answer they gave us: 'We are the servants of the God of heaven and earth, and we are rebuilding the temple that was built many years ago, one that a great king of Israel built and finished.'" When challenged about their authority, the Israelites didn't cite an earthly king. They declared their identity: servants of the God of heaven and earth. Sermon 2 — Zelda Cheung 📖 Ezra 1:1 (NIV) "In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, in order to fulfill the word of the Lord spoken by Jeremiah, the Lord moved the heart of Cyrus king of Persia to make a proclamation throughout his realm and also to put it in writing." God initiated the entire rebuilding project. He moved the heart of a pagan king to fulfill His promise. 📖 Ezra 1:5 (NIV) "Then the family heads of Judah and Benjamin, and the priests and Levites — everyone whose heart God had moved — prepared to go up and build the house of the Lord in Jerusalem." God moved their hearts to leave settled lives in Babylon and return to a ruined Jerusalem — a bold, God-initiated decision. 📖 Ezra 5:5 (NIV) "But the eye of their God was watching over the elders of the Jews, and they were not stopped until a report could go to Darius and his written reply be received." God protected the progress even during opposition, buying them time to keep building. 📖 Ezra 6:18 (NIV) "And they installed the priests in their divisions and the Levites in their groups for the service of God at Jerusalem, according to what is written in the Book of Moses." The Israelites studied God's Word to worship correctly — not however was easy or convenient, but the way God prescribed. 📖 Ezra 6:21 (NIV) "So the Israelites who had returned from the exile ate it, together with all who had separated themselves from the unclean practices of their Gentile neighbors in order to seek the Lord, the God of Israel." Holiness means being set apart — separating from unclean practices to seek the Lord wholeheartedly. 📖 Ezra 6:22 (NIV) "For seven days they celebrated with joy the Festival of Unleavened Bread, because the Lord had filled them with joy by changing the attitude of the king of Assyria so that he assisted them in the work on the house of God, the God of Israel." Even the joy of celebration was enabled by God. He changed the king's heart so they could celebrate freely. 📖 Hebrews 12:1-2 (NIV) "Therefore, since we are surrounded by such a great cloud of witnesses, let us throw off everything that hinders and the sin that so easily entangles. And let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith." Being set apart means casting off hindrances and sin so we can run the race with focus, determination, and intentionality — eyes fixed on Jesus. 📖 2 Timothy 2:20-21 (NIV) "In a large house there are articles not only of gold and silver, but also of wood and clay; some are for special purposes and some for common use. Those who cleanse themselves from the latter will be instruments for special purposes, made holy, useful to the Master and prepared to do any good work." Paul's vessel analogy: it doesn't matter what kind of vessel we are. All vessels can be useful to the Master if we are willing to be set apart. The Greek verb "sanctified" is passive — God does the transforming when we offer ourselves.

chunks.heading: 🏇 From Sermon 1: Persevere Through Opposition
chunks.text: 👀 Identify Your Opponent's Tactics The enemy tailors tactics to each person. What excuses do you use to avoid God's calling? What fears hold you back? Name them so they lose their power over you. 🙏 Pray Before, During, and After Prayer is the most important fuel of ministry. Like the Alpha team, pray before every endeavor, during it, and after it. Don't wait for a crisis to start praying. ⚔️ Put on the Full Armor of God Read Ephesians 6:10-18 this week. Understand each piece of armor. Stand with anticipation of attack — not in fear, but in readiness. Know how to defend and fight back. 🆔 Anchor Your Identity When challenged, don't cite your achievements or earthly credentials. Know who you serve: the God of heaven and earth. That identity cannot be shaken by any opposition.

chunks.heading: ✨ From Sermon 2: Partner with God
chunks.text: 🤝 Recognize God's Initiative The Bold Faith project, your ministry involvement, your faith journey — these are responses to God's moving in your heart. You're not just volunteering; you're partnering with the Creator. 🏺 Be a Vessel for Special Purposes Say to God: "I want to be set apart for you." It doesn't matter what kind of vessel you've been. God will sanctify you when you offer yourself willingly. ⏸️ Develop the Habit of Pausing Before decisions, reactions, and interactions — pause and ask: "What does God think of this? Would He be pleased?" This simple discipline puts you on the path of holiness. 🏃 Throw Off What Hinders Identify the things that distract you from running the race — the sin that so easily entangles, the habits that pull you away from God. Cast them off and fix your eyes on Jesus.

chunks.heading: 📋 Putting It All Together: The Ezra Journey
chunks.text: Over these two Chinese New Year Sundays, we've traced the entire arc of Ezra 1-6: 1 Return & Rebuild God moves hearts. The people respond with faith and action (Ezra 1-3) 2 Face Opposition The enemy attacks with every tactic. Persevere through prayer, armor, and identity (Ezra 4) 3 Complete & Renew The temple is finished. God's people are set apart for every good work (Ezra 5-6) 🏮 As we step into the Year of the Horse: May we have the strength and determination of the horse — not carrying burdens alone, but running free together as God's people. May we persevere through opposition, be set apart for God, and be ready for every good work He has prepared for us.

chunks.heading: 💭 Reflection Questions
chunks.text: These questions are drawn from the two sermons and the official YouTube reflection questions. Consider journaling your responses or discussing with your care group. Sermon 1 — Racing Through Opposition - What tactic(s) does the opposition use against you? Is it deception — making you think you don't need to serve? Discouragement — telling you your efforts don't matter? Fear — that you'll fail or say the wrong thing? Name the specific tactic that has been most effective against you. - How do you persevere through opposition while living out God's calling? What practical steps — prayer habits, accountability partners, spiritual disciplines — help you keep going when the opposition is strong? - What did God call you to do, and what made you stop? Hill could find a thousand reasons not to share the gospel. What calling have you set aside? What excuses have you been making? Is it time to pick it back up? - Are you a spectator in the stands, or are you playing your part on the field? If Christians don't share Jesus, who will? What would it look like for you to step onto the field this week — even in a small way? Sermon 2 — New Year, New You - God is inviting you to partner with Him. What are some ways you can partner with God for His Kingdom? It's going to take more than a small group of committed individuals. God is inviting all of us. How are you responding to that invitation? - What specific changes can you implement this year to help you be set apart for God? Think about habits, relationships, priorities, and practices. What needs to be thrown off? What needs to be embraced? - "What does God think of this? Would He be pleased?" How often do you pause to ask this question in your daily life? What would change if you made it a regular habit before your decisions, reactions, and interactions? - What kind of vessel have you been — and what kind do you want to become? Paul says it doesn't matter what kind of vessel you are. If you cleanse yourself, you will be useful to the Master. Are you ready to say, "God, I want to be set apart for you"? 🐴 Chinese New Year Prayer: Father God, we are grateful that you invite each and every one of us to partner with you. You know our fears, our worries, and our hesitation. Yet you are the God who transforms. Open our eyes to see the privilege of partnering with you. Teach us to be set apart — to throw off everything that hinders, to cast aside the sin that entangles, and to fix our eyes on Jesus. Make us instruments for special purposes, ready for every good work. May we persevere through every opposition, anchored in the knowledge that we are servants of the God of heaven and earth. In the name of Jesus Christ, Amen. Next Week: A new sermon series begins — "The Way" — overlapping with the Lent season. Come ready for a fresh journey!

sermons.title: The Way: Ordinary People | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men."

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: Jesus strategically chose twelve ordinary, unschooled people — fishermen, a tax collector, a political activist — not the religious elite, not the educated scholars. He created something new from something unremarkable, and He continues to call ordinary people like us to extraordinary purpose: be with Him, be changed by Him, and impact the world for Him. Series Opener: This is the first sermon in "The Way" — a Lent series exploring what it means to follow Jesus as the early church understood it. Before they were called "Christians," believers were known as followers of the Way (Acts 9:2; 24:14), because Jesus declared: "I am the way" (John 14:6).

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Mark 3:13-15 (NIV) "Jesus went up on a mountainside and called to him those he wanted, and they came to him. He appointed twelve that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach." 📖 John 14:6 (NIV) "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - Jesus chose ordinary people on purpose — Not by circumstance, but strategically. Fishermen, tax collectors, political activists — nobody with pedigree or theological training. - "Appointed" means "created" — The Greek word used is the same root as Genesis 1. Jesus was creating something new from twelve, reminiscent of the twelve tribes of Israel. - Our primary calling is to be with Jesus — Before mission, before service, before preaching. A relational, organic, ongoing connection with God through Christ. - Jesus is relational — The Trinity itself is community. Revelation 3:20: He doesn't demand performance — He wants to dine with you. - Who you're with determines who you are — Community shapes character. Jesus calls us to be around people who sharpen us spiritually. - Maturity is measured by love — Not years, not knowledge, not sacrifice. Do you love God and love people more than you used to? - God uses ordinary for extraordinary — "Do not despise small beginnings" (Zechariah 4:10). Faithfulness in the small, mundane, unnoticed things is what God calls extraordinary. 🛤️ The Way Lent series introduction → 🎣 12 Ordinary People Mark 3 — strategic selection → ❤️ Be With Him Relationship, not religion → 🌍 Impact Your World Ordinary faithfulness

chunks.heading: 📖 Introducing "The Way"
chunks.text: The early church wasn't originally called "the church" — it was called the Way. This title traces directly to Jesus himself, who provocatively declared in John 14:6: "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." This claim is exclusive and radical: not a way, but the way. Not all roads lead to the same mountaintop. Jesus uniquely, because of who He was, the life He led, and most importantly His power over death — His resurrection — positioned Himself as the bridge back to a holy God. In Acts, the Apostle Paul refers to the church as "the Way" five times — both as Saul the persecutor and later as Paul the believer. Standing before Festus on trial, he would say: "I am a follower of the Way." Lent Series: For the next six weeks leading up to Easter, this series dives into Scripture to consider what it means to follow the way of Jesus — His life, His ministry, the claims He made, and the uniqueness of the gospel.

chunks.heading: Why Jesus Avoided the Crowds
chunks.text: As Jesus began His public ministry around age 30, He healed many people in miraculous ways. Naturally, His reputation grew and massive crowds followed. You'd think this is exactly what a revolutionary leader would want. But oddly, Jesus repudiated the crowds. He even had a small boat ready as an escape plan. Why? Because the people were coming for the wrong reason. They sought the healing more than the healer. The physical had taken over the spiritual. Make no mistake, God cares about your physical well-being. You are created in the image of God with a physical body. But the greater tragedy is when we focus only on the physical at the expense of the spiritual. — Pastor Brett Jesus' mission was to seek and to save the lost — to make sure people understood their spiritual condition before a holy God. There were only two options: live a perfect life (impossible) or trust in a perfect Savior in Jesus.

chunks.heading: The Twelve Disciples
chunks.text: So Jesus went up on a mountainside and called to Him those He wanted. Not the theologians, not the educated elite — but ordinary, blue-collar, unschooled people. Peter Fisherman James Fisherman John Fisherman Andrew Fisherman Philip — Bartholomew — Matthew Tax Collector Thomas — James Son of Alphaeus Thaddeus — Simon Zealot (Political) Judas Treasurer "Appointed" = "Created": The Greek word translated "appointed" has this idea of creating something — the same root as the Hebrew word in Genesis when God created the world. Jesus wasn't just choosing; He was creating something new. Twelve apostles, reminiscent of twelve tribes — something familiar, yet entirely new.

chunks.heading: ❤️ Our Primary Calling: Be With Him
chunks.text: 📖 Mark 3:14 (NIV) "He appointed twelve that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach." The first description of their calling: not to perform, not to serve — but to be with Jesus. Our primary calling is to be with Jesus in a relational, organic, ongoing way. God invites us not to a religion, not to a list of do's and don'ts, not to a lifestyle meant to earn His approval — but into a relationship. That is incredibly radical, both in Jesus' day and ours. Pastor Brett shared: "I am a Christian, and if somebody asks I say yes. But my preference is to say I'm a follower of Christ. A Christian is simply someone saying, I follow Christ — who He is, what He taught, who He revealed God to be. Not just a historical figure to study, but the revelation of God made known in Jesus that we are invited to have a relationship with."

chunks.heading: Jesus Is Relational
chunks.text: The whole Godhead — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — is relational. In Genesis 1, before mankind existed, they have a conversation: "Let us make mankind in our image." "Us" and "our" — a plurality within the Godhead, community and relationship existing even before creation. 📖 Revelation 3:20 (NIV) "Here I am! I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with that person, and they with me." Not "I will correct their bad behavior." Not "I will demand submission." Jesus says: I will dine with you. Food in every culture is intimate. Think of Chinese New Year — who you eat with matters.

chunks.heading: Who You're With Determines Who You Are
chunks.text: You become like those you hang out with. Every parent knows this — it's why you ask your eight-year-old about their new friend's family. It's why you choose certain schools. It's why some come to church: "I want my kid to be around good people." Jesus says: Be around me. Because my mission is that you would become more like me. That's the invitation into community — small groups, service teams, fellowships — people who know your name and sharpen you spiritually.

chunks.heading: 📏 Measuring Christian Maturity
chunks.text: How would you measure spiritual maturity? Some might say years, knowledge, sacrifice. But Jesus puts it simply: The Litmus Test: Do you love God? Do you love people? More than you used to. It's why worship songs are carefully chosen — to slow our distractions, focus our affections, stir what is true, remind us of God's character. And loving God vertically should always spill into loving people horizontally. If you don't do the vertical, you can't do the horizontal. It's not multiple choice — it's a package.

chunks.heading: 🔄 Being Transformed
chunks.text: 📖 2 Corinthians 3:18 (NIV) "And we all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory, which comes from the Lord, who is the Spirit." Your purpose, regardless of vocation, is to be made like Christ — an ever-ongoing metamorphosis, changing you from the inside out. The fruit of the Spirit — love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control — God is producing change in all nine areas. You don't get to pick. Pastor Brett shared his own transformation: as a kid he went to church twice a year — Mother's Day and Easter — and hated it. He thought, "When I'm old enough, I will never come to this place." Now he loves every expression of church. God changed his heart.

chunks.heading: 🌍 Our Mission: Impact the World
chunks.text: 📖 Matthew 4:19 (NIV) "Come, follow me," Jesus said, "and I will send you out to fish for people." It's a promise and a package. Follow Him, and He will use you. Not optional — it's both invitation and deployment. God could have made rocks cry out the gospel. But He chose you over a rock. We are His ambassadors, His mouthpiece — faulty, inconsistent, and flawed as we are. God knows that and still says: "I'm going with you."

chunks.heading: 🌱 The Glory of Small Things
chunks.text: 📖 Zechariah 4:10 (NIV) "Who dares despise the day of small things, since the seven eyes of the LORD that range throughout the earth will rejoice when they see the chosen capstone in the hand of Zerubbabel?" God is in the small things. When you're exhausted but sit down to read a Bible story to your child at night — God sees it and it matters. When you greet the doorman by name and give them a blessing — God sees it. The mundane, the ordinary, the not-applauded activities are where God rushes in and says: "I will use the small things." An extraordinary life is obtained by doing ordinary things for God. Nobody wants to be called ordinary — but it might be extraordinary because it was exhibited in ordinary ways over a lifetime, for the glory of God. — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: 📖 John 14:6 (NIV) "Jesus answered, 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'" The foundational claim that gave the early church its name — "the Way." Jesus claims exclusivity as the bridge to a holy God. 📖 Mark 3:7-8 (NIV) "Jesus withdrew with his disciples to the lake, and a large crowd from Galilee followed. When they heard about all he was doing, many people came to him." The beginning of Jesus' public ministry — crowds following for physical healing, but Jesus seeking to redirect them to spiritual wholeness. 📖 Mark 3:9 (NIV) "Because of the crowd he told his disciples to have a small boat ready for him, to keep the people from crowding him." Jesus' "escape plan" — He avoided the crowds because they sought the healing more than the healer. 📖 Mark 3:13-15 (NIV) "Jesus went up on a mountainside and called to him those he wanted, and they came to him. He appointed twelve that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach and to have authority to drive out demons." The primary passage: Jesus strategically calling twelve ordinary people, creating something new — "appointed" sharing the same Greek root as "to create." 📖 Genesis 1:26 (NIV) "Then God said, 'Let us make mankind in our image, in our likeness.'" "Us" and "our" — the plurality within the Godhead, revealing that God is inherently relational. The Trinity in conversation before creation. 📖 Revelation 3:20 (NIV) "Here I am! I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with that person, and they with me." Jesus' intimate invitation: not to correct or demand, but to dine — the most relational act across every culture. 📖 2 Corinthians 3:18 (NIV) "And we all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory, which comes from the Lord, who is the Spirit." The ongoing metamorphosis: we are being changed from the inside out, becoming more like Christ with ever-increasing glory. 📖 Galatians 5:22-23 (NIV) "But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, forbearance, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness and self-control. Against such things there is no law." Nine aspects of spiritual fruit. God is producing change in all of them — you don't get to pick and choose. 📖 Matthew 4:19 (NIV) "'Come, follow me,' Jesus said, 'and I will send you out to fish for people.'" A promise and a package: follow Him (relationship), and He will use you (mission). Both halves are inseparable. 📖 Zechariah 4:10 (NIV) "Who dares despise the day of small things, since the seven eyes of the LORD that range throughout the earth will rejoice when they see the chosen capstone in the hand of Zerubbabel?" Small beginnings matter. The ordinary, unnoticed acts of faithfulness — God sees them and rejoices.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Additional References
chunks.text: - John 15 (NIV) — "Abide in the vine" — being filled with God's Spirit gives life, energy, conviction, and comfort - Matthew 5:13-16 (NIV) — Salt and light of the world — referenced in the closing by Byron: "by our works, people will give glory to God our Father" - Acts 9:2; 24:14 — Paul refers to the church as "the Way" — both as persecutor and believer

chunks.heading: 🎯 Living It Out / 🎯 This Week's Challenge / 📝 From the Sermon
chunks.text: This sermon isn't abstract theology — it's a personal invitation. Jesus calls ordinary people into an extraordinary adventure. Here's how to respond this week. 🙏 Cultivate "With-ness" Your primary calling is to be with Jesus. Before you serve, before you strategize, before you do — just be. Spend time in prayer, in Scripture, in silence. Practice awareness of His presence throughout your day. 🤝 Invest in Community You become like those you're around. Join a small group. Serve on a team. Find people who know your name and who sharpen you spiritually. Don't do the faith journey alone. ❤️ Measure by Love Ask yourself honestly: Do I love God more than I used to? Do I love people more than I used to? Not knowledge, not years — love is the litmus test of maturity. 🌱 Embrace the Ordinary Read the bedtime story when you're exhausted. Greet the doorman by name. Show up consistently. The mundane, faithful, unnoticed acts — that's where God does His greatest work. 🗣️ Speak Up Your colleagues deserve an informed decision about the gospel. You don't need to be perfect first — be consistent, but speak up. God chose you over a rock. 🔄 Notice the Change Look back and take inventory. What has God changed in you? What do you care about now that you didn't before? How do you see people differently? Give thanks for the transformation. Identify one person in your sphere of influence — a colleague, a neighbor, a family member — whom God has put on your heart. Pray for them daily this week, and look for one ordinary opportunity to show them the love of Christ. Not a grand gesture. Just salt and light. - Jesus' primary calling to each of us is to "be with Him." How would you describe your awareness of the presence of God in your life? What could make it better? - Do you sense you are being used by God to impact your sphere of influence? Who specifically has God put on your heart to intentionally reach out to?

chunks.heading: 🔍 Going Deeper
chunks.text: - Jesus avoided the crowds because they wanted His blessings more than His person. Is there an area where you pursue God's gifts more than God Himself? - The disciples were ordinary — fishermen, a tax collector, a political activist. Does your sense of "ordinariness" (or lack of qualifications) ever hold you back from following God's calling? How does this sermon speak to that? - Pastor Brett said: "The fruit of the Spirit — you don't get to pick. God is producing change in all nine areas." Which fruit feels most challenging for you right now? Which has God been growing in you lately? - "An extraordinary life is obtained by doing ordinary things for God." Can you think of someone whose faithfulness in small things has had an extraordinary impact on you? - Revelation 3:20 pictures Jesus wanting to dine with you — an intimate, relational invitation. How does this image change or deepen your understanding of your relationship with God?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Prompts
chunks.text: - Gratitude: Thank God for choosing you — ordinary, flawed, and all. Thank Him for the privilege of being His ambassador. - Awareness: Ask God to increase your awareness of His presence throughout your daily routine. Pray for eyes to see Him in the ordinary moments. - Transformation: Ask the Holy Spirit to continue the metamorphosis — to grow the fruit of the Spirit in the areas where you need it most. - Mission: Pray by name for the people God has placed in your life who don't yet know Him. Ask for ordinary opportunities to be salt and light.

sermons.title: The Way: The Temptation of Jesus | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "Lead us not into temptation."

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: Our struggles against temptation are far more sophisticated than simply avoiding "big" sins. Jesus was tempted in all ways and was without sin. His three temptations in the wilderness reveal deeper questions every human wrestles with: Will God meet my needs? Is God powerful in my circumstances? Will I worship God only when there are rewards? Defeating temptation comes not from deeper resolve, but from fixing our eyes on Jesus and knowing who we are in Him. Series Context: This is the second sermon in "The Way" — a Lent series exploring the life of Jesus as we make our way toward Easter. Jesus himself taught us to pray, "Lead us not into temptation" — and He can speak those words with authority because He endured temptation Himself.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Matthew 4:1-4 (NIV) "Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, 'If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.' Jesus answered, 'It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.'" 📖 Hebrews 4:15-16 (NIV) "For we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses, but we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin. Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence."

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - Jesus was fully God and fully man — The Council of Chalcedon (451 AD) affirmed the hypostatic union. He was hungry, depleted, vulnerable — yet without sin. This means He truly understands our struggles. - Temptation is more subtle than "big sins" — The enemy's real strategy targets deeper questions: Does God provide? Does God care? Is God enough? - Three temptations, three deeper questions — Stones to bread (Will God meet my needs?), temple jump (Is God powerful in my circumstances?), kingdoms for worship (Will you worship God only if there are rewards?). - Jesus countered every temptation with Scripture — He responded to lies with truth, modeling how God's word corrects erroneous thinking. - Know your weaknesses — Restraint bias makes us think we're stronger than we are. "Let anyone who thinks they stand take heed lest they fall." - Imagine the cost — Sin always brings death — death of trust, intimacy, relationships. Thinking through consequences cuts temptation short. - Know your identity — The enemy's first move against Jesus was to undermine His identity: "If you are the Son of God." The greatest defense is knowing who God says you are. 🙏 Lead Us Not The Lord's Prayer & temptation → 🏜️ 3 Temptations Matthew 4 — deeper questions → ⚔️ 3 Defenses Weakness, cost, identity → ✝️ Fix Eyes on Jesus Not avoidance — attraction

chunks.heading: 🙏 "Lead Us Not Into Temptation"
chunks.text: The followers of Jesus came to Him with a simple question: "Teach us to pray." In response, Jesus gave them the Lord's Prayer — and within it, a line that is as direct as it gets: "Lead us not into temptation." No interpretation needed. No original Greek to decode. The instruction is plain: pray that God would keep you away from temptation. Yet how often do we actually pray this? We tend to pray for provision, direction, answers — but rarely, "God, don't let me get close to temptation today." The Paradox: Jesus teaches us to pray against being led into temptation — yet He Himself was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted. This is not something for us to model. Jesus is unique. Our role is to pray: "Keep me from it." After fasting forty days and forty nights, Matthew records what may be the greatest understatement in the New Testament: "He was hungry." Every word of Scripture is there for a reason. This line isn't just about food — it reveals His state of vulnerability before the temptations came, and it confirms His full humanity.

chunks.heading: ⚖️ Fully God and Fully Man
chunks.text: Throughout church history, the nature of Jesus has been debated. Docetism said Jesus was fully God but not fully human. Arianism said He was fully man but not fully God. At the Council of Chalcedon in 451 AD, the church affirmed the hypostatic union: Jesus was fully God and fully man — both natures coexisting without diminishment. Why does this matter? Because fully man means He understands us. He was depleted, vulnerable, hungry. He empathizes — not just sympathizes — with our weakness. And fully God means His sacrifice on the cross is sufficient to cover the sins of the entire world. Without both, the gospel doesn't hold. 📖 Hebrews 4:15-16 (NIV) "For we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses, but we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin. Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence." Because Jesus has endured temptation, we can come boldly before God — not with shame, but with confidence that He understands.

chunks.heading: 🏜️ The Three Temptations
chunks.text: In Matthew 4, the enemy comes to Jesus at His most depleted — forty days of fasting in the wilderness. Each temptation targets not just a surface-level desire, but a profound question of the soul. 1 🍞 Stones to Bread "If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread." The real question: Will God meet my needs? Valid needs — not counterfeit, not unholy — but will He provide financially, emotionally, relationally? 2 🏛️ Throw Yourself Down "If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down. For it is written: He will command his angels concerning you." The real question: Is God powerful in my circumstances? Is He able and willing to enter into my situation, to see me, to care, and to respond? 3 👑 Bow Down for Kingdoms "All this I will give you, if you will bow down and worship me." The real question: Will you worship God only if there are rewards? Is your commitment contingent? Is there an asterisk by your faith?

chunks.heading: 🍞 Temptation 1: Will God Meet My Needs?
chunks.text: The enemy goes straight for Jesus' point of weakness — He's starving. "If you are the Son of God, meet a natural need in a supernatural way." This isn't about literal stone-to-bread power for us. It's the question bumbling about within your psyche every single day, whether you articulate it or not: Will God protect me? Will He provide? Will He answer my prayers? Jesus responds with Scripture: "Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God." He anchors His answer in truth that transcends the physical — there is something more sustaining than bread.

chunks.heading: 🏛️ Temptation 2: Is God Powerful in My Circumstances?
chunks.text: Having been defeated once, the enemy gets clever — he uses Scripture against Jesus, quoting God's own word: "He will command his angels concerning you." The devil says: Your God promised this. Test Him. Make Him prove it. Jesus rightly applies the full counsel of Scripture: "It is also written: Do not put the Lord your God to the test." The enemy took a verse out of context; Jesus put it back. The deeper question for us: Is God able and willing? When prayers go unanswered, when circumstances disappoint, the enemy whispers: "Your God doesn't care." But we can counter that whisper by recalling the ways God has intervened, answered, and provided in our past. He knows the number of hairs on your head. If a sparrow falls, He notices. If it's a concern of yours, it's a concern of His.

chunks.heading: 👑 Temptation 3: Will You Worship God Only If There Are Rewards?
chunks.text: The enemy introduces a transactional concept: If you do this, then you will get this. He shows Jesus all the kingdoms of the world and says: "Bow down and this is all yours." Jesus responds: "Away from me, Satan! For it is written: Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only." He counters the offer of physical blessing with the reminder that true blessing is spiritual — to worship and serve God alone. Is the quality of your life based on what is seen or what is unseen? We live in a material world. Hong Kong takes it to the next level. But purpose and meaning is found in recognizing that we are souls — there is an unseen side of life. — Pastor Brett 📖 2 Corinthians 4:18 (NIV) "So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." The gravitational pull of every life is toward the tangible, countable, material. Yet what is eternal is unseen — our connection with God, our identity as souls.

chunks.heading: ⚔️ How to Defeat Temptation
chunks.text: The temptation when talking about temptation is to think only about "big" sins. But the enemy has a plan for every stage, every age, every background — subtle strategies to distract you from devotion to God. 🔍 1. Know Your Weaknesses In a world that covers up weakness, you might not even know your own. Restraint bias says you think you have greater restraint than you actually do. Scripture says the opposite: "Let anyone who thinks they stand take heed lest they fall" (1 Corinthians 10:12). Err on the side of stepping far back from temptation rather than snuggling up next to it. 💰 2. Imagine the Cost If you thought about the actual cost of giving in, it would cut you short. Sin always brings death — death of trust, death of intimacy, death of relationships. "All at once he follows her... he does not know that it will cost him his life" (Proverbs 7:22-23). Sin never stays in a vacuum. 👤 3. Know Your Identity At Jesus' baptism (Matthew 3:17), the Father declared: "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased." Immediately after, the enemy's first move was to attack that identity: "If you are the Son of God..." Jesus didn't need to prove it — the Father already said it. How much of our lives is spent proving things to people when God has already declared who we are?

chunks.heading: The Skiing Story
chunks.text: Pastor Brett shared a personal illustration: at a party, three men were discussing skiing trips and fine wine — topics where he felt completely out of his depth. His temptation? To use words — sarcasm, passive-aggressive comments — to elevate himself at their expense. "I didn't go skiing. I was here doing ministry. But how are the Alps?" Nobody was trying to make him feel small. But insecurity found the weakness, and his instinct was to use his greatest strength (words) as a weapon. If you don't know your weakness, you'll walk right into the temptation — and then wonder, "How did I get here?"

chunks.heading: Church Hurt
chunks.text: Pastor Brett addressed "church hurt" directly: "If you stick around just a little bit, you're gonna be hurt — because somebody won't remember your name, or our worship will be too loud, or our aircon will be too low." The church is a group of forgiven sinners who try but still miss the mark. But here's the plea: take that hurt to God, not away from God. Don't let horizontal disappointment become a conclusion about the vertical. God doesn't hurt you. God doesn't disappoint. And His greatest affection for you is proven once and for all at the cross. 📖 John 3:16 (NIV) "For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life." If you ever question God's love, look at the cross. He proved it, displayed it, once and for all. That proof can carry you through every disappointment.

chunks.heading: ✝️ The Greatest Defense
chunks.text: The greatest thing you can do in the struggle against any temptation is not to develop deeper resolve, some kind of perseverance, some determination that "never again." The greatest thing is to fix your eyes on Jesus. It's not to avoid negative — not to say "don't sin, don't sin, don't sin" — but to love God, love God, love God. To understand how great His love is for you and to walk in that. It's not just to avoid temptation, but rather to be drawn into the beauty that is Jesus Christ. — Pastor Brett Yes, avoid temptations. But do so primarily by being swept up in how great God's love is for you. Worship Him alone. Serve Him alone. And watch how beautifully it affects your relationships with everyone around you.

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Matthew 4:1-4 (NIV) "Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, 'If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.' Jesus answered, 'It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.'" The first temptation: meeting a legitimate need through illegitimate means. Jesus responds with Deuteronomy 8:3 — there is sustenance deeper than bread. 📖 Matthew 4:5-7 (NIV) "Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. 'If you are the Son of God,' he said, 'throw yourself down. For it is written: He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.' Jesus answered him, 'It is also written: Do not put the Lord your God to the test.'" The second temptation: the enemy uses Scripture out of context. Jesus rightly applies the full counsel of God's word (Deuteronomy 6:16). 📖 Matthew 4:8-11 (NIV) "Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendor. 'All this I will give you,' he said, 'if you will bow down and worship me.' Jesus said to him, 'Away from me, Satan! For it is written: Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.'" The third temptation: a transactional offer — material blessing in exchange for worship. Jesus affirms that worship belongs to God alone (Deuteronomy 6:13). 📖 Hebrews 4:15-16 (NIV) "For we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses, but we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin. Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need." Jesus doesn't just sympathize — He empathizes. He has been there. This gives us confidence to approach God boldly. 📖 Matthew 6:13 (NIV) "And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one." From the Lord's Prayer — Jesus' direct instruction: pray that God keeps you away from temptation. How often do we actually pray this? 📖 Matthew 3:17 (NIV) "And a voice from heaven said, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.'" Spoken at Jesus' baptism — the event immediately before the temptation. The Father affirms Jesus' identity. The enemy's first move is to attack it: "If you are the Son of God..." 📖 John 3:16 (NIV) "For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life." The ultimate proof of God's love. Whenever we question whether He cares, the cross settles it once and for all. 📖 2 Corinthians 4:18 (NIV) "So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." Countering the third temptation's pull toward material reward. What defines us is not the tangible and countable, but the eternal and spiritual. 📖 Mark 8:36 (NIV) "What good is it for someone to gain the whole world, yet forfeit their soul?" The material and the spiritual often fight against one another. You can have things — but placing faith, hope, and significance in them is a counterfeit endeavor. 📖 1 Corinthians 10:12 (NIV) "So, if you think you are standing firm, be careful that you don't fall!" The antidote to restraint bias. If you think "it doesn't apply to me" or "I would never do that" — that's the first sign you don't know yourself well enough. 📖 Proverbs 7:22-23 (NIV) "All at once he followed her like an ox going to the slaughter... little knowing it will cost him his life." Sin always brings death — death of trust, intimacy, relationships. It may not be immediate, but the cost is always real.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Additional References
chunks.text: - Deuteronomy 8:3 — "Man shall not live on bread alone" — Jesus' first response to the tempter - Deuteronomy 6:16 — "Do not put the Lord your God to the test" — Jesus' second response - Deuteronomy 6:13 — "Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only" — Jesus' third response - Philippians 2:5-8 — God came in the form of man, humbled Himself (referenced regarding the hypostatic union) - Matthew 10:29-30 — He knows the hairs on your head and when a sparrow falls — God cares about the details

chunks.heading: 🎯 Defeating Temptation / 🎯 This Week's Challenge / 📝 From the Sermon
chunks.text: This sermon isn't just theology — it's a survival guide. The enemy has a plan for every stage of life, every age, every background. Here are three practical defenses, plus the ultimate strategy. 🔍 Know Your Weaknesses Stop hiding behind a veneer of strength. Be radically honest about where you're vulnerable. What triggers insecurity in you? What do you reach for when you feel small, tired, or threatened? Write them down. Confide in a trusted friend. The areas you refuse to acknowledge are the areas the enemy will exploit. 💰 Imagine the Cost Before you act, fast-forward the tape. Imagine the apology you'd have to make. The trust you'd break. The relationship you'd damage. Sin never stays in a vacuum — it always brings death in some form. Visualizing the cost is one of the most effective circuit breakers. 👤 Know Your Identity So much temptation is really about trying to create or prove our identity. But God has already spoken. You are His son, His daughter. Christ is enough. You don't need to prove anything to anyone. Let the Father's declaration settle it. ✝️ Fix Your Eyes on Jesus The greatest strategy isn't "don't sin, don't sin, don't sin" — it's "love God, love God, love God." Be so swept up in the beauty and love of Christ that temptation loses its grip. The vertical always transforms the horizontal. 📖 Arm Yourself with Scripture Jesus countered every temptation with "It is written." Have God's word at your disposal — not just memorized, but internalized. When the enemy whispers a lie, answer it with truth. Scripture corrects what is erroneous and anchors what is true. 🤝 Bring the Hurt to God When church hurts you — and it will — bring that hurt to God, not away from Him. Don't let horizontal disappointment become a verdict on the vertical. God doesn't disappoint. His love was proven at the cross. Identify one specific weakness — not a strength-disguised-as-a-weakness, but a real vulnerability. Write it down. Then pray the Lord's Prayer deliberately this week, pausing at "Lead us not into temptation." Ask God specifically to keep you from the situations where that weakness is exploited. And when you feel it pulling, counter it with one truth about who God says you are. - Jesus taught us to pray "Lead us not into temptation." How often do you actually pray this? What would change if you prayed it daily? - Pastor Brett shared his own temptation to use words as a weapon when feeling insecure. What is your equivalent — the strength that becomes your weakness when triggered?

chunks.heading: 🔍 Going Deeper
chunks.text: - The three temptations map to three deeper questions: Will God meet my needs? Is God powerful in my circumstances? Will I worship Him without rewards? Which of these questions resonates most with where you are right now? - "Restraint bias" means we overestimate our ability to resist temptation. Where in your life might you be overconfident about your self-control? What would "stepping far back" look like? - The enemy's first attack on Jesus was against His identity: "If you are the Son of God." Where in your life does the enemy most frequently challenge who God says you are? - Pastor Brett said the greatest defense against temptation is not "don't sin" but "love God." How does this reframe your approach to areas where you struggle? - Is there any hurt — church hurt or otherwise — that you've laid on God that actually belongs in the horizontal realm? How might bringing it to God (instead of away from God) change things? - "Sin always brings death — death of trust, death of intimacy." Can you think of a time when imagining the cost ahead of time stopped you from making a mistake?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Prompts
chunks.text: - The Lord's Prayer: Pray Matthew 6:9-13 slowly and deliberately. Pause at each line. Especially linger on "Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one." - Self-Awareness: Ask God to reveal your blind spots — the weaknesses you've covered up or the areas where you think you're stronger than you are. - Identity: Meditate on what God says about you: beloved, chosen, enough. Ask the Holy Spirit to make these truths louder than the enemy's "If you are..." whispers. - Healing: If you carry church hurt or disappointment with God, bring it honestly before Him. Ask Him to separate the horizontal from the vertical and to heal what needs healing. - Adoration: Spend time simply worshipping — not asking, not confessing — just telling God who He is and what He means to you. Let the beauty of Christ crowd out the pull of temptation.

sermons.title: The Way: Mercy, Not Sacrifice | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "I desire mercy, not sacrifice." — Hosea 6:6

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: In every society there are outcasts — people at the fringes, ignored, or despised. While most move away from them, Jesus moves towards them. Through the calling of Matthew the tax collector, Jesus reveals a heart that prioritizes mercy and compassion over religious duty. God declares: "I desire mercy, not sacrifice." Our calling is not just to bring gifts to the table, but to bring people to the table. Series Context: This is the third sermon in "The Way" — a Lent series exploring how we follow Jesus as we make our way toward Easter. Week 1 explored how Jesus called ordinary people; Week 2 examined His temptation in the wilderness. This week: how Jesus moves toward those the world rejects.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Matthew 9:9-13 (NIV) "As Jesus went on from there, he saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth. 'Follow me,' he told him, and Matthew got up and followed him. While Jesus was having dinner at Matthew's house, many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with him and his disciples. When the Pharisees saw this, they asked his disciples, 'Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?' On hearing this, Jesus said, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. But go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, not sacrifice. For I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.'"

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - Jesus moves toward those the world moves away from — When everyone excluded tax collectors and sinners, Jesus deliberately sought them out. Matthew was a social outcast; Jesus made him a disciple. - Jesus took the initiative — Counter-culturally, it was Jesus the rabbi who approached Matthew, not the other way around. Grace initiates. - You don't need to clean up before following Jesus — Matthew didn't spend a year reforming his life first. He followed Jesus, and transformation came by grace. - Jesus sees himself as a doctor, not a judge — The Pharisees operated as self-righteous judges. Jesus said: "I came for the sick." Good doctors run to the sick, not away from them. - Grace always comes first — In the original Greek of John 1:17, the word order matters: it's "grace and truth" — grace precedes truth. - Mercy (chesed) is active, covenantal love — The Hebrew word means loyalty, loving-kindness. It seeks out its object. God's heart is for us to go and find the lost. - "Who do you bring to your table?" — The practical shift: not just what you bring to the table with your gifts, but who you bring — colleagues, friends, neighbours who need Jesus. ❓ What Is Jesus' Way? Move toward the outcast → 🔍 Why Is It This Way? Doctor, not judge — grace first → 🚶 How Do We Follow? Who do you bring to the table?

chunks.heading: ❓ Question 1: What Is Jesus' Way? / 📍 The Setting: Capernaum
chunks.text: We are in the middle of a Lent sermon series called "The Way." Jesus said, "I am the way, the truth, and the life." Before Christians were called "Christians," they were called followers of the way. So we study a passage from Jesus' early ministry, asking three questions: What is Jesus' way? Why is it this way? And how do we follow the way? Jesus was in Capernaum — the headquarters of His ministry, located at the northern tip of the Sea of Galilee. Israel sat on a crucial international trade route — goods moving between Syria to the north and Egypt to the south passed through here. This made it a prime location for collecting taxes and tariffs.

chunks.heading: 🏦 The Tax Collector's Booth
chunks.text: As Jesus walked through Capernaum, He saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth — like a toll booth on a highway. Tax collectors charged tariffs on international trade, land purchases, and personal goods. They routinely inflated values to pocket the difference. And there was no appeals process — you paid again and again. Two critical facts made tax collectors universally despised: - They worked for the Roman Empire — Israel was not an independent state but an occupied territory. Rome auctioned off the right to collect taxes to the highest bidder. Matthew, as a tax collector, was viewed as a political traitor collaborating with the oppressor. - They were dishonest — They routinely cheated people by inflating the value of goods and land. Matthew was seen as morally corrupt. Tax collectors were social outcasts. Everyone hated them. No one wanted to be their friend.

chunks.heading: 🤝 Jesus Moves Toward Matthew
chunks.text: And yet — Jesus saw Matthew. He didn't walk past. He didn't move away. He moved toward him and said two words: "Follow me." And Matthew immediately got up, left everything behind, and followed. This was counter-cultural on multiple levels: - No reputable man would talk to a tax collector — yet Jesus sought him out - In Jesus' time, students sought rabbis, not the other way around — yet Jesus initiated - A student would prove himself for months before being accepted — yet Jesus called Matthew immediately

chunks.heading: 🎉 Matthew's Party / 🔍 Question 2: Why Is It This Way?
chunks.text: Matthew was so transformed by this encounter that he threw a party. And who came? "Many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with him and his disciples." Matthew's new life in Christ was so remarkable that he brought his entire network to the table to introduce them to Jesus. I want to believe in Jesus, but look at me — I'm in a mess. I want to clean up my act before I believe in Jesus. — A common sentiment Pastor Albert encounters in counseling Pastor Albert addressed this directly: Did Matthew spend a year cleaning up his act and then follow Jesus? Or did he follow Jesus first, and then by the grace of God, transformation came? The answer is clear. Follow Jesus first. He will transform your life. Jesus' Way: When the world moves away from people ignored by society, Jesus moves toward them. He moved toward the mess — spiritual mess, moral mess, social mess — because that's where a doctor is needed most. The Pharisees saw Jesus eating with tax collectors and sinners, and they asked His disciples: "Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?" A good question — it could have sparked a theological, social, or philosophical debate. But Jesus changed the entire paradigm.

chunks.heading: 🏥 Doctor, Not Judge
chunks.text: ⚖️ The Pharisees Saw themselves as self-righteous judges. They ran away from sinners to preserve their own holiness. vs 🩺 Jesus Saw himself as a doctor. He ran toward the sick to bring healing and life. Jesus responded calmly but authoritatively: "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick." Every good doctor runs to the sick, not away from them. If you've been to an emergency room, you know — doctors and nurses literally run toward the patient.

chunks.heading: 💝 Grace Always Comes First
chunks.text: 📖 John 1:17 (NIV) "For the law was given through Moses, but grace and truth came through Jesus Christ." In the original Greek, word order matters. It is "grace and truth" — not "truth and grace." In Jesus, grace always comes first. The law given through Moses was not bad — it was given by God. But look at the contrast: grace and truth came through Jesus. Our Savior is a doctor full of grace. That's why we have forgiveness, and that's why we can share it with others.

chunks.heading: 📜 "Go and Learn What This Means"
chunks.text: Jesus then told the Pharisees: "Go and learn what this means: 'I desire mercy, not sacrifice.'" The phrase "go and learn" was an idiom — it basically meant: You totally missed the point. You think you have a PhD in the Old Testament? Go home and study it again. Jesus was quoting Hosea 6:6, where God Himself spoke: "I desire mercy, not sacrifice." In Greek, the word for mercy can be translated as compassion. In the original Hebrew, the word is chesed — meaning loyalty, loving-kindness, covenantal love. It is a kind of active love that seeks out its object. Sacrifice = a vertical relationship between you and God (religious duty) Mercy / Compassion = a horizontal relationship between you and other people God desires both — but the horizontal matters more than we think.

chunks.heading: ⚖️ The More Important Matters
chunks.text: Later in Matthew 23:23, Jesus confronted the Pharisees again: they tithed even their herbs — mint, dill, and cumin — but neglected justice, mercy, and faithfulness. Jesus called these "the more important matters of the law." Notice the objects: What is the object of justice? People. What is the object of mercy? People. What is the object of faithfulness? People. In God's eyes, people have value. At the heart of the issue is actually the issue of the heart. If our heart is not in the right place, our acts become meaningless. — Pastor Albert The Pharisees were not wrong in obeying the law. They were wrong because their hearts were in the wrong place. They thought getting the outward actions right was enough. But God looks at the heart.

chunks.heading: ⛪ As a Church
chunks.text: Pastor Albert shared how Island ECC is already following this way: 🏙️ Hope of the City (Sham Shui Po) Church members go to Sham Shui Po to tutor kids, care for single moms, teach skills and English, run Alpha courses, help the unemployed find jobs, and assist families in applying for government aid. Moving toward people otherwise ignored by society. 💜 Marvel Ministry A ministry to special-needs children — some with ADHD, some autistic. Behind every special-needs child is a soul that Jesus died for. The society may question their value; the church affirms it. There is much joy but also many tears behind the scenes.

chunks.heading: 🍽️ As Individuals: Who Do You Bring to Your Table?
chunks.text: The common question in business and church is: "What do you bring to the table?" — focusing on your gifts, skills, and contributions. Pastor Albert changed the question from what to who: Who do you bring to your table? You are in touch with people that nobody else in this church is. God has uniquely placed you in your social circle. A colleague going through a difficult time. A friend who just had a miscarriage. A guy who lost his job. A family with financial needs. They are in your network. Pastor Albert shared examples: a friend who played golf brought the caddy to a local church. Another friend who frequented a restaurant brought the waiter. Jesus brought Matthew to the table. Matthew brought his friends to the table. Who can you bring? I am created to do something or to be something for which no one else is created. I have a place in God's counsels, in God's will, which no one else has. Whether I be rich or poor, despised or esteemed by man, God knows me and calls me by name. God has created me to do him some definite service. He has committed somewhere to me which he has not committed to another. I have my mission. — John Henry Newman You are uniquely placed by God — in your network, in your neighbourhood, in your workplace — because He has an assignment for you. Ask yourself: Who can I bring to my table so that I can introduce them to Jesus?

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Matthew 9:9 (NIV) "As Jesus went on from there, he saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth. 'Follow me,' he told him, and Matthew got up and followed him." The calling of Matthew — Jesus takes the initiative toward a social outcast, reversing the cultural norm where students sought rabbis. 📖 Matthew 9:10-11 (NIV) "While Jesus was having dinner at Matthew's house, many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with him and his disciples. When the Pharisees saw this, they asked his disciples, 'Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?'" Matthew's party — his transformation was so real that he immediately brought his entire network to Jesus. The Pharisees' question reveals their self-righteous posture. 📖 Matthew 9:12-13 (NIV) "On hearing this, Jesus said, 'It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. But go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, not sacrifice. For I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.'" The heart of the sermon. Jesus reframes the debate entirely: He is a doctor, not a judge. He quotes Hosea 6:6 — God desires mercy (chesed) over mere religious observance. 📖 Hosea 6:6 (NIV) "For I desire mercy, not sacrifice, and acknowledgment of God rather than burnt offerings." The Old Testament passage Jesus quoted. In Hebrew, "mercy" is chesed — covenantal love, loyal kindness, active love that seeks out its object. God values compassion toward people over mere religious ritual. 📖 John 1:17 (NIV) "For the law was given through Moses, but grace and truth came through Jesus Christ." In the original Greek, the word order is deliberate: grace comes before truth. In Jesus, grace always leads. The law was good, but Jesus brings something greater. 📖 Matthew 23:23 (NIV) "Woe to you, teachers of the law and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You give a tenth of your spices — mint, dill and cumin. But you have neglected the more important matters of the law — justice, mercy and faithfulness. You should have practiced the latter, without neglecting the former." Jesus affirms that religious duty is not wrong — but justice, mercy, and faithfulness are the "more important matters." The objects of all three are people. People have value in God's eyes.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Additional References / 📍 Capernaum: The Town of Jesus / 🌍 Israel's Geopolitical Position
chunks.text: - Matthew 4:1-11 — Referenced from previous sermon in the series (Week 2: The Temptation of Jesus) - John 14:6 — "I am the way, the truth, and the life" — the foundation of this series - 2 Corinthians 5:17 — "If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation" — referenced in the worship response - Matthew 11:28 — "Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" — referenced in the worship response Capernaum sat at the northern tip of the Sea of Galilee. While Jesus grew up in Nazareth ("Jesus of Nazareth"), Capernaum was the headquarters of His ministry — called "the town of Jesus." He performed many miracles, gave many teachings, and based much of His public ministry in this area. Israel occupied a critical position on the international trade route. Go north — you hit Lebanon and Syria. Go south — you reach the Sinai Peninsula and Egypt. This made Israel (and Capernaum especially) a prime location for trade — and for taxing that trade.

chunks.heading: 🛤️ The Via Maris: Highway Through Capernaum
chunks.text: The Via Maris (Latin for "Way of the Sea") was one of the ancient world's most important trade routes, dating from the early Bronze Age. It linked Egypt with the northern empires of Syria, Anatolia, and Mesopotamia. The route followed the Mediterranean coast before turning inland through the Jezreel Valley, passing along the western shore of the Sea of Galilee through Magdala and Capernaum, then crossing the Jordan River near Hazor before climbing northeast into Damascus. This explains why Capernaum — a seemingly modest fishing town — had a tax collector's booth. It sat directly on this major international highway. Goods flowing between Egypt and Mesopotamia passed through here, making it a strategic point for the Roman tax system. Matthew's booth was likely positioned to collect tariffs on this very traffic. Sea of Galilee & Capernaum Capernaum sat on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee, at a junction where the Via Maris trade route passed — making it a strategic location for tax collection and the headquarters of Jesus' ministry. Image: Tamuz via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0. Ancient Levantine Trade Routes, c. 1300 BCE The Via Maris (purple) ran from Egypt through Gaza, up the coast, inland through the Jezreel Valley and Capernaum to Damascus. The King's Highway (red) was a parallel route further east. Image: Briangotts via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0.

chunks.heading: 💰 The Roman Tax System
chunks.text: Israel was not an independent state — it was an occupied territory under the Roman Empire. Rome sent governors to rule and imposed taxes on the population. But they didn't collect taxes themselves. Instead, they auctioned off the right to collect taxes to local businesspeople who bid the highest price. 🏛️ Trade Tariffs Goods moving north to Syria or south to Egypt were taxed at checkpoints 🏠 Property Tax Purchased land? Taxed. Tax collectors inflated values to pocket the difference 👜 Personal Goods "Nice handbag! I charge you tax." Even personal items were subject to levies 🔄 Double Taxation "Already paid? Pay again." No appeals, no refunds — pay at every booth

chunks.heading: 🚫 Who Were the "Outcasts"?
chunks.text: The "sinners" and outcasts in Jesus' time fell into several overlapping categories: 💰 Tax Collectors Political traitors working for Rome; dishonest profiteers ⚖️ Criminals Murderers, thieves, and others who broke the law 🚷 Morally Dubious Prostitutes and those living immoral lifestyles 📜 Ceremonially Unclean Violated purity laws — wrong handwashing, handling dead bodies, bodily fluids 🏥 The Sick Mental illness, leprosy, skin diseases — driven out of town The Jewish society had strict rules governing sacrifices, tithing (not just money — flock, crops, everything), and ceremonial purity. It was very easy to become "unclean" — and once you were, you were excluded.

chunks.heading: 📚 The Hebrew Word: Chesed (חֶסֶד)
chunks.text: When Jesus quoted Hosea 6:6 — "I desire mercy, not sacrifice" — the word translated "mercy" in the Hebrew original is chesed. This is one of the richest words in the Old Testament: - Loyalty — faithful commitment that doesn't waver - Loving-kindness — tender compassion in action - Covenantal love — love rooted in relationship and promise - Active love — love that seeks out its object rather than waiting passively This is not just feeling sorry for someone. Chesed is love that moves — love that goes to find the lost, comfort the broken, and invite the excluded to the table.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Following the Way of Mercy
chunks.text: This sermon doesn't just teach us about Jesus — it gives us a pattern to follow. Jesus moved toward those the world moved away from. How can we do the same? 🚶 Move Toward the Mess When you see someone struggling — socially, emotionally, spiritually — resist the instinct to look away. Jesus didn't need to earn anything from Matthew. He simply moved toward him. Ask: Who in my world is being ignored? 🤲 Take the Initiative Jesus didn't wait for Matthew to approach Him. Grace initiates. Don't wait for someone to ask for help. Reach out to the colleague who's been quiet, the neighbour who seems overwhelmed, the friend who disappeared. Be the one who moves first. 🍽️ Bring People to Your Table Shift from "What do I bring to the table?" to "Who do I bring to my table?" God has uniquely placed you in your network. Who is a colleague, friend, or neighbour you could invite into community — and ultimately introduce to Jesus? 🩺 Be a Doctor, Not a Judge When you encounter people in moral, spiritual, or social trouble, check your posture. Are you approaching as a judge ("they should know better") or as a healer ("how can I help")? Grace leads; truth follows. Both matter, but the order matters too. ❤️ Check Your Heart "At the heart of the issue is the issue of the heart." The Pharisees' outward obedience was impeccable — they even tithed their herbs. But their hearts were wrong. Ask God to examine not just your actions but your motives. 🙌 Come as You Are If you're the one who feels like an outcast — messy, unworthy, needing to "clean up" first — hear this: Matthew didn't clean up before following Jesus. He followed first, and grace transformed him. Come to Jesus as you are.

chunks.heading: 🎯 This Week's Challenge / 📝 From the Sermon
chunks.text: Identify one person in your network who is going through a difficult time — a colleague, a neighbour, a friend. Someone you might normally pass by or feel unsure about approaching. This week, take the initiative: invite them for a meal, send a message, offer to listen. Move toward them. And pray about whether this is someone God is calling you to bring to your table — and eventually to introduce to Jesus. - Who are the people that you tend to move away from? And why? - Who can you bring to your table? Who are the people you can approach and show mercy and compassion?

chunks.heading: 🔍 Going Deeper
chunks.text: - Matthew was a social outcast — a traitor and a cheat — yet Jesus called him first. Is there someone in your life whom you've written off as "too far gone"? What would it look like to move toward them? - Many people feel they need to "clean up their act" before coming to Jesus. Have you ever felt that way? How does Matthew's story challenge that thinking? - Jesus distinguished between being a doctor and being a judge. In your daily interactions — at work, at church, in your family — which role do you tend to default to? Why? - "Grace and truth" — not "truth and grace." How does the order change your approach to people who are struggling or living in ways you disagree with? - The Pharisees' outward obedience was impressive — they tithed even their herbs. But Jesus said they neglected "the more important matters." Where in your life might outward religious activity be masking an inward heart problem? - Chesed means active, covenantal love that seeks out its object. How is this different from the way our culture typically thinks about love? How does it change what "loving your neighbour" looks like? - Pastor Albert asked: "Who can you bring to your table?" Think concretely — name one or two people. What's one step you can take this week?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Prompts
chunks.text: - For Eyes to See: Ask God to help you see the "Matthews" in your life — the people others overlook, avoid, or dismiss. Pray for the courage to move toward them. - For a Doctor's Heart: Pray that God would transform your posture from judge to healer. Ask the Holy Spirit to fill you with compassion (chesed) — active, seeking love. - For Your Network: Lift up the specific people God has placed in your life — colleagues, neighbours, friends. Ask God to show you who to invite to your table and how to share His love. - For Humility: Confess any areas where outward religious performance has become disconnected from the heart. Ask God to realign your priorities with His: justice, mercy, and faithfulness. - Come as You Are: If you feel like the outcast, bring that to God honestly. You don't need to clean up first. Jesus is already moving toward you. Receive His grace.

sermons.title: The Way: The Upside-Down Kingdom | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant." — Mark 10:43

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: The invitation to follow Jesus involves a complete overhaul of our thinking, values, and behaviours. It's counterintuitive and seems risky. Through the disciples' misguided ambition and Luke's Beatitudes, Pastor Brett reveals how Jesus redefines greatness — not through power, comfort, success, and recognition, but through servanthood, sacrifice, endurance, and faithfulness. This upside-down kingdom is not a burden; it is the path to freedom. Series Context: This is the fourth sermon in "The Way" — a Lent series tracing the life and teaching of Jesus as we prepare for Easter. Week 1: Jesus called ordinary people. Week 2: His temptation in the wilderness. Week 3: Mercy, not sacrifice. This week: the upside-down kingdom — how Jesus redefines greatness and calls us to a counter-cultural life.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Mark 10:42-45 (NIV) "Jesus called them together and said, 'You know that those who are regarded as rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their high officials exercise authority over them. Not so with you. Instead, whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant, and whoever wants to be first must be slave of all. For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.'" 📖 Luke 6:20-23 (NIV) "Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh. Blessed are you when people hate you, when they exclude you and insult you and reject your name as evil, because of the Son of Man."

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - The early church "turned the world upside down" — Paul and Silas were described as disruptive to the status quo. Following Jesus is not a moral upgrade; it's a complete life reversal. - Even the disciples misunderstood greatness — James and John asked for the top seats in the kingdom. The other ten were indignant — not because the request was wrong, but because they wanted those seats too. - Jesus redefines greatness as servanthood — "Whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant." It's an entirely different org chart. - A new covenant, a new command, a new movement — Jesus ushered in grace over law, love over transaction, and the church as a counter-cultural community. - The Beatitudes contrast two kingdoms — Woe to those who live for power, comfort, success, and recognition. Blessed are those who embrace weakness, sacrifice, grief, and rejection for Christ. - The danger of "now" — Three of the four "woes" emphasise the present: "already received," "well fed now," "laugh now." Building your life on what is temporary leads to regret. - Jesus is not just an example — He equips — If Jesus is only an example, His commands become a crushing burden.
