But He dwells in us, giving new capacity, new motivation, new strength. 🔄 Turning the World Upside Down Acts 17 — the early church label → 👑 Greatness Redefined Mark 10 — servant, not ruler → ⚖️ Woe vs Blessed Luke 6 — the Beatitudes → 🕊️ Freedom in Christ Power to live upside-down

chunks.heading: 🔄 Turning the World Upside Down
chunks.text: Before they were called Christians, followers of Jesus were known as people of the way — drawing from Jesus' claim: "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." That radical, exclusive claim provided the moniker for the early church. Paul and Silas arrived in Thessalonica and spent three Sabbaths reasoning with people in the synagogues about who Jesus was and why He came. Some immediately converted. Others were outraged — this was disruptive to their spiritual and political expectations. 📖 Acts 17:6 (ESV) "These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also." This label — "turning the world upside down" — was meant as an accusation. But it's an apt description of what Jesus desires for every one of us. What Jesus desires is not simply a moral upgrade. Not just some theological enlightenment. He wants to turn your life upside down — your loves, passions, desires, priorities, all driven by the world and the flesh, completely inverted. If you've been a Christian for some time, you should be able to look back and see an almost unrecognisable version of yourself.

chunks.heading: ⚡ Radical Statements from Jesus
chunks.text: 📖 John 12:25 (NIV) "Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life." A call to "hate your life" — not about hating your job or circumstances, but disowning the values this world sets in place for you to pursue. 📖 Luke 6:35 (NIV) "But love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back." So radical. "I don't even really pray for my enemies. You want me to love them? Give money to them and walk away from it?" Jesus goes to extreme measures to get our attention. 📖 Colossians 1:13 (NIV) "For he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves." There are only two kingdoms. Conversion to faith is a transfer from the world's way of thinking, valuing, acting, and prioritising to a completely new approach to life. This kind of faith is not a minor change. Not Judaism 2.0. Jesus came to fulfil the law and usher in an entirely new way of thinking. His words are radical, intimidating. They cause us to shrink back. "You want me to forgive 70 times seven? Just on and on? That's unnatural." Exactly. It's supernatural.

chunks.heading: 👑 James, John, and the Redefinition of Greatness / 😤 The Indignation of the Ten
chunks.text: Among the twelve disciples, there was an inner circle — Peter, James, and John — who got extra access to Jesus: hearing Him pray, watching Him live, observing His ministry up close. And yet, having seen all this, James and John came to Jesus with a remarkable request: 📖 Mark 10:35-37 (NIV) "Then James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came to him. 'Teacher,' they said, 'we want you to do for us whatever we ask.' 'What do you want me to do for you?' he asked. They replied, 'Let one of us sit at your right and the other at your left in your glory.'" They'd had a pre-meeting before the meeting. They wanted the vice-president positions in Jesus' kingdom — still thinking of it as a political power structure. It's easy to look back and think, "Are you kidding me?" But how do we treat Jesus? Most of our prayers are probably more about how He can meet our immediate needs than about intercession for others or worship for who He is. We often treat Him like a genie or a Santa Claus. James and John were just the ones with the courage to say it. Jesus responded with remarkable patience: "You don't even know what you're asking." He asked if they could drink the cup He would drink — metaphorically speaking of His suffering and death. They overestimated their commitment: "Oh yes, we can." Of course, they would later abandon Him. The other ten disciples overheard and became indignant. But notice: they weren't offended that James and John asked the wrong thing. They were offended that they tried to get a step above them. "You're leaving us out? You think you're better than us?" It reveals that none of the disciples truly understood Jesus' new kingdom.

chunks.heading: 🔄 "Not So with You"
chunks.text: 📖 Mark 10:42-45 (NIV) "Jesus called them together and said, 'You know that those who are regarded as rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their high officials exercise authority over them. Not so with you. Instead, whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant, and whoever wants to be first must be slave of all. For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.'" Three words that change everything: "Not so with you." Jesus redefines greatness from the top of the org chart to the bottom — from being served to serving. Servant and slave. That's not what they aspired to. They wanted to have servants and slaves. But Jesus says greatness is defined in an entirely different way. It is possible to be great in the world's eyes but not in God's. And it is possible to be great in God's eyes but not the world's.

chunks.heading: ✝️ A New Covenant
chunks.text: Jesus' death and resurrection ushered in a new covenant — a new connection with God. It would no longer be about fastidiously adhering to Old Testament laws. It would be about understanding grace: a concept that doesn't make sense in a worldly view. The world is transactional — you study hard, you get good grades; you work hard, you get a promotion. But grace is not transactional. You get what you don't deserve. You are extended mercy instead.

chunks.heading: ❤️ A New Command
chunks.text: 📖 Mark 12:30-31 (NIV) "Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength... Love your neighbour as yourself." A new capacity and desire to express love to God with every fibre of your being, and having sensed God's love for you, to love all people — above you, beside you, below you. Not just the lovable. Not just those who can love you back or do you a favour. All people. Everyone created in the image of God, with intrinsic value. God calls His church to be the practical expression of that love — emotionally, practically, everywhere, without limits.

chunks.heading: ⛪ A New Movement / ⚖️ The Beatitudes: Woe vs. Blessed
chunks.text: This new command ushered in a new movement: the church — the ecclesia, the "called-out ones." People extricated from the world, gathering together around a common calling, living in a counter-cultural way. For 2,000-plus years, big churches, small churches, cathedrals, home churches, many languages — it may look different, but the message is the same: We believe in Jesus and all that He said and claimed. Perhaps Jesus' most famous sermon is the Beatitudes — recorded in a longer version in Matthew 5 and a shorter version in Luke 6. (Jesus repeated His sermons — Pastor Brett took great heart from this.) In Luke's account, Jesus makes direct comparisons between a worldly life and this other kingdom, this upside-down success.

chunks.heading: ⚠️ The Woes: Warning Against Worldly Values
chunks.text: 📖 Luke 6:24-26 (NIV) "But woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort. Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep. Woe to you when everyone speaks well of you, for that is how their ancestors treated the false prophets." Jesus is not judging — He is pleading. "I have pity on you if you embrace simply the values of the world. Don't do it." He is preventing regret. And lest anyone in the room say "Oh, that's not me" — we are all rich. We sit in air conditioning. We're about to go eat lunch. We are in the top five per cent of global wealth. Jesus says: be careful. These are all normal things to pursue. He's not against you doing well. His warning is: if this is what you live for, you'll be disappointed. Note the emphasis on "now": "You have already received your comfort. You are well fed now. You who laugh now." There's an over-emphasis on the present, with no regard for God's master plan over all of eternity. Our 80-ish years are a tiny dot on the line of eternity — and we lose focus thinking this dot is all that matters. The Greek word for "laugh" (γελάω / gelaō): It carries the nuance of gloating — relishing in your own victory at the expense of others. Your political party wins and you rub it in. You win a competition and mock the loser. Not simple joy, but self-congratulatory triumph.

chunks.heading: ✨ The Blessings: The Upside-Down Kingdom
chunks.text: 📖 Luke 6:20-23 (NIV) "Looking at his disciples, he said: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh. Blessed are you when people hate you, when they exclude you and insult you and reject your name as evil, because of the Son of Man.'" All the things we work hard to avoid — poverty, hunger, grief, exclusion — and Jesus says there is blessing in them. The upside-down kingdom. Who wants grief? Insult? Exclusion from the party? Poverty? And yet Jesus says there's blessing in these things you work so hard to avoid. In the life of God's people, it will be seen, first of all, as a remarkable reversal of values. The people of God will prize what the world finds pitiable and suspect what the world finds desirable. This doesn't mean you pursue grief or poverty. You don't throw away your money to be poor. But to prize sacrifice, to value humility, to see the presence of God rushing in during suffering — that is the mark of the upside-down kingdom. To be "poor in spirit" (as Matthew will tell us) is to have a humble disposition toward God: "God, I bring you nothing. I have nothing to offer. And yet you love me still."

chunks.heading: ⚡ Where the Power Comes From
chunks.text: In both Matthew and Luke, Jesus was on a mountainside — where revolutionaries would hide to escape authorities. But He came down to the people. And Luke tells us: Luke 6:19: "The people all tried to touch him, because power was coming from him and healing them all." — People were touching His robe and being healed. A watershed moment where Jesus' public ministry took off. Power was emanating from Him, as if to say: from this point on, there is a new kingdom.

chunks.heading: 🤔 Is Jesus Only an Example?
chunks.text: Is Jesus an example to follow? Seems like an easy answer. But if He is only an example, He will be a burden to you — because you won't be able to do it. You won't forgive your enemies over and over. You won't love those who don't love you back. You won't absorb insult without being riled up. At some point you'll either check out ("This Christian life is unattainable") or fake it and feel like an imposter. Jesus is not only your example. It is through Christ dwelling in you — the one who transferred you from the kingdom of darkness to the kingdom of light — that you receive a new capacity, a new source of strength, a new grounding, a new motivation. He never asks you to do something He doesn't also equip you to do.

chunks.heading: 🕊️ Freedom, Not Burden
chunks.text: The invitation is to live according to the freedom God offers, with eternity in view. You may not get the respect you deserve in this life. That person may never apologise. But there's a God in heaven who sees every infraction, balances the scales, and meets out judgment — and you can leave room for Him. I got unfair treatment by God — I got heaven and I didn't deserve it. I like when it works in that direction. And so you can endure, you can persevere, you can be gracious, kind, generous, encouraging — even if it's not reciprocated. — Pastor Brett You can live above the comparison trap. You can live beyond the transactional world. You can come before God with open hands. It's an upside-down way of living, and it doesn't come natural — you have to re-correct yourself often. But there is freedom there.

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:6 (ESV) "These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also." The description given to Paul, Silas, and early Christians in Thessalonica — meant as an accusation, but an apt description of the faith. Jesus desires to turn your life upside down. 📖 John 14:6 (NIV) "Jesus answered, 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'" The radical, exclusive claim that gave the early church their name: "people of the way." The foundation of this entire sermon series. 📖 John 12:25 (NIV) "Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life." A call not to hate your circumstances, but to disown the value system the world has set for you. A separation from worldly priorities. 📖 Luke 6:35 (NIV) "But love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back. Then your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and wicked." One of Jesus' most radical commands — love, do good, and give even to those who oppose you. Counter-cultural in every era. 📖 Colossians 1:13 (NIV) "For he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves." Only two kingdoms exist. Conversion is a transfer from one to the other — from the world's way of thinking, valuing, and acting to an entirely new approach to life. 📖 Mark 10:35-37 (NIV) "Then James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came to him. 'Teacher,' they said, 'we want you to do for us whatever we ask.' 'What do you want me to do for you?' he asked. They replied, 'Let one of us sit at your right and the other at your left in your glory.'" The disciples' misguided ambition — still thinking of Jesus' kingdom in political, power-based terms. They wanted to be vice-presidents in an org chart. 📖 Mark 10:42-45 (NIV) "Jesus called them together and said, 'You know that those who are regarded as rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their high officials exercise authority over them. Not so with you. Instead, whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant, and whoever wants to be first must be slave of all. For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.'" The definitive statement on kingdom greatness. Three words change everything: "Not so with you." Greatness = servanthood. First = slave of all. 📖 Luke 6:24-26 (NIV) "But woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort. Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep. Woe to you when everyone speaks well of you, for that is how their ancestors treated the false prophets." The "woes" — not judgment but a pleading warning. If you build your life on power, comfort, success, and recognition, enjoy it while it lasts. It will fade like a mirage. 📖 Luke 6:20-23 (NIV) "Looking at his disciples, he said: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh. Blessed are you when people hate you, when they exclude you and insult you and reject your name as evil, because of the Son of Man.'" The blessings — the upside-down kingdom. All the things we work to avoid carry blessing in God's economy. Not that we pursue suffering, but that God meets us in it. 📖 Mark 12:30-31 (NIV) "'Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength.' The second is this: 'Love your neighbour as yourself.' There is no commandment greater than these." The new command — a new capacity to love God with every fibre of your being, and from that overflow, to love all people without limits.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Additional References / ⚖️ Kingdom Contrast: Two Ways of Living
chunks.text: - Colossians 1:16 — "For in him all things were created" — read during the worship introduction - Psalm 111:10 — "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" — referenced in the worship prayer - Matthew 5 — The longer version of the Beatitudes - Luke 6:19 — "Power was coming from him and healing them all" — referenced in the sermon's discussion of where the power comes from Pastor Brett broadened the terms Jesus used in the Beatitudes to reveal two fundamentally different approaches to life. These aren't just theological categories — they describe the daily choices every person makes about what to pursue and what to value.

chunks.heading: 📊 The World's Values vs. The Kingdom's Values
chunks.text: ⚠️ Woe — The World's Way ✨ Blessed — The Kingdom Way 💰 Rich → Power Money = influence = power. Climbing the org chart. Being in charge. 🤲 Poor → Weakness / Humility "Poor in spirit" — coming to God empty-handed: "I have nothing to offer, yet you love me." 🍽️ Well-Fed → Comfort Not worried about the next meal. Life is secure and predictable. 🔥 Hungry → Sacrifice Willing sacrifice — seeing value in giving up comfort for something greater. 😏 Laughing → Success / Gloating Relishing your wins. Gloating over those who lost. Self-congratulatory triumph. 😢 Weeping → Grief / Endurance God doesn't promise pain-free life. But He is present in suffering, and His presence proves His character. 👏 Spoken Well Of → Recognition Being noticed, applauded, awarded. Others acknowledge your accomplishments. 🚫 Hated / Excluded → Rejection for Christ When faith has a social cost — when others distance themselves because of your values.

chunks.heading: ⏳ The Danger of "Now"
chunks.text: In three of the four woes, Jesus emphasises the present tense: - "You have already received your comfort" - "You are well fed now" - "You who laugh now" The danger is an over-emphasis on the present — what you can see, touch, and count — with no regard for God's master plan over all of eternity. Our lives, however long, represent a tiny dot on the line of eternity. And yet we work and grind and amass, trying to prove to the world that we matter. The mirage analogy: Remember that thing you really wanted to buy? You thought about it, saved for it, finally got it. How long before the newness wore off? Worldly satisfaction is like a mirage — it appears to be water on the horizon, but the closer you get, the further it moves. You never find the satisfaction you thought you would.

chunks.heading: 🔄 The Beatitude Pairs
chunks.text: Each woe has a corresponding blessing — an alternative that Jesus offers: ⚠️ Woe "Woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort." ✨ Blessed "Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God." ❌ Power, influence, top of the org chart ✅ Humility before God — "I bring nothing" ⚠️ Woe "Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry." ✨ Blessed "Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied." ❌ Comfort, security, no sacrifice needed ✅ Willing sacrifice — giving up comfort for God's purposes ⚠️ Woe "Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep." ✨ Blessed "Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh." ❌ Gloating over success at others' expense ✅ Finding God present in grief — His presence proves His character ⚠️ Woe "Woe to you when everyone speaks well of you." ✨ Blessed "Blessed are you when people hate you, exclude you, insult you, and reject your name as evil." ❌ Recognition, applause, universal approval ✅ Faithfulness to Christ even when it costs you socially

chunks.heading: 🔑 The Key Distinction
chunks.text: Jesus is not against you doing well. He's not against being powerful, comfortable, successful, or having a good reputation. His warning is this: If this is what you live for, you'll be disappointed. If this is what you build your life around, you will eventually regret it. You climbed that ladder of success, only to find it was leaning against the wrong wall. — A common saying referenced by Pastor Brett The alternative? Live according to the freedom God offers, with eternity in view. This is not all there is. And in that perspective, you find the power to endure, forgive, be generous, and live above the comparison trap.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Living in the Upside-Down Kingdom
chunks.text: Jesus doesn't just describe a counter-cultural reality — He invites you into it. And He doesn't leave you to figure it out on your own. Through His Spirit dwelling in you, He provides the power to live this way. Here are practical steps for embracing the upside-down kingdom. 🔄 Audit Your Definition of Greatness How do you define success? Is it titles, income, recognition, followers? Jesus says greatness is serving others. This week, ask: Where am I climbing a ladder that's leaning against the wrong wall? 🤲 Come to God Empty-Handed To be "poor in spirit" is to approach God without a résumé. Not "God, here's what I bring to the partnership," but "God, I have nothing to offer. It is by your grace alone." Practise this posture in prayer this week. ⏳ Think Beyond "Now" The world screams: grab it now, enjoy it now, prove yourself now. Jesus says: think in terms of eternity. When you're tempted to chase the immediate, ask: Will this matter in light of eternity? 🕊️ Leave Room for God That person who hurt you may never apologise. Instead of demanding justice yourself, leave room for a God who sees every infraction and balances the scales. You can endure because you already received the ultimate unfair treatment — heaven, undeserved. 💪 Lean on Christ's Power, Not Your Own If following Jesus feels impossible, it's because it is — on your own. He's not just an example; He equips. Ask the Holy Spirit for the power to forgive, love, and serve in ways that go beyond your natural ability. 🪞 Identify Your "James and John" Moments Where do you treat Jesus like a genie — handing Him a wish list instead of worshipping Him for who He is? Shift your prayers from "here's what I need" to "here's who you are" — and watch your perspective change.

chunks.heading: 🎯 This Week's Challenge / 📝 From the Sermon
chunks.text: Choose one area where your life looks too similar to the world's values — power, comfort, success, or recognition. Identify one concrete step to live counter-culturally in that area this week. It might be serving someone below you on the org chart, sacrificing comfort for someone else's benefit, celebrating someone else's success instead of comparing, or standing firm in your faith even when it's socially costly. Write it down. Pray over it. And then do it — not in your own strength, but leaning on Christ's power in you. - What does "upside-down" living look like in your life? How do you embrace a life that is counter-cultural to the values of our world? - Where does your life look too similar to the world's values? What changes might God be asking you to adopt in your life?

chunks.heading: 🔍 Going Deeper
chunks.text: - James and John wanted the top seats in the kingdom, and the other ten were jealous. In what ways do you find yourself competing for position, recognition, or status — even within church or Christian community? - Jesus said "Not so with you." Where in your life do you need to hear those words? Where have you adopted the world's approach to power and authority instead of Jesus' model of servanthood? - Pastor Brett said the world is transactional — you get what you deserve. Grace is the opposite. Where do you struggle to receive grace (from God or others)? Where do you struggle to extend it? - Of the four categories — power, comfort, success, recognition — which one is your strongest pull? Which "woe" hit closest to home? Why? - Jesus warned against the danger of "now" — building your life on what's temporary. What are you investing in right now that has eternal significance? What are you investing in that doesn't? - If Jesus is only an example, His commands become a crushing burden. How have you experienced this tension? What does it look like to lean on His power instead of your own effort? - Pastor Brett described "poor in spirit" as coming to God with open, empty hands — versus "middle class in spirit," where you see it as a divine teamwork. Which posture do you tend toward? Why? - The quote said: "The people of God will prize what the world finds pitiable and suspect what the world finds desirable." What does the world find pitiable that you've begun to prize? What does the world desire that you're beginning to suspect?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Prompts
chunks.text: - For an Upside-Down Heart: Ask God to turn your values right-side up — to prize humility, sacrifice, endurance, and faithfulness over power, comfort, success, and recognition. - For Freedom from "Now": Pray for an eternal perspective. Ask God to loosen your grip on temporary things and fix your eyes on what lasts. - For Christ's Power: Confess the areas where you've been trying to follow Jesus in your own strength. Ask the Holy Spirit to fill you with His power to forgive, love, serve, and endure. - For a Servant's Heart: Pray for the grace to see greatness the way Jesus sees it — not through climbing up, but through bending down. Ask for opportunities to serve this week. - For Open Hands: Come to God empty-handed in prayer. No résumé, no bargaining, no list of accomplishments. Just: "God, I bring you nothing. And yet you love me still."

sermons.title: The Way: Triumphal Entry? | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "If you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes." — Luke 19:42

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: Palm Sunday commemorates Jesus' entry into Jerusalem — but it was far from the purely joyous celebration we imagine. As the crowds waved palm branches and sang Hosanna, Jesus wept over the city. Pastor Kevin lifts the veil on what was going through Jesus' mind: Jerusalem had willfully turned a blind eye to his teachings, and their rejection of the Prince of Peace would have devastating consequences. The solution is not mere knowledge but active obedience — specifically, dying to self. Series Context: This is the fifth and final sermon in "The Way" — a Lent series tracing the life and teaching of Jesus. Week 1: Ordinary People. Week 2: The Temptation of Jesus. Week 3: Mercy, Not Sacrifice. Week 4: The Upside-Down Kingdom. This week: the Triumphal Entry — Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's willful blindness and calls us to die to self.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Luke 19:41-42 (NIV) "As he approached Jerusalem and saw the city, he wept over it and said, 'If you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes.'" 📖 Luke 19:26-27 (NIV) "'He replied, "I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given, but as for the one who has nothing, even what they have will be taken away. But those enemies of mine who did not want me to be king over them — bring them here and kill them in front of me."'"

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - The Triumphal Entry wasn't triumphal for Jesus — While the crowds celebrated, Jesus wept. There was a massive disparity between how Jerusalem felt and how Jesus felt. - Jesus rode a donkey to announce a kingdom of peace — Not primarily to fulfil prophecy or demonstrate humility, but to declare that his reign would bring shalom — everything as it should be. - Jerusalem rejected the real offer — They wanted a political messiah who would overthrow Rome. They got the Prince of Peace offering a kingdom of shalom, and they didn't want it. - Both groups in Jerusalem rejected Jesus — The Pharisees denied him outright; the crowds celebrated him for the wrong reasons. Both amounted to rejection. - Willful blindness is our great tragedy — We intentionally suppress awareness of truth when the cost of change is too great. We do it with finances, relationships, health — and most devastatingly, with Jesus' teachings. - The solution is dying to self — Releasing the desire to have things your way. Not trying to become like Jesus through legalism, but surrendering your comfort, pride, and preferences. - Dying to self doesn't save you — faith does — Salvation is by grace through faith in Jesus Christ alone. But genuine faith is expressed through obedience, and obedience can be simplified to: die to self. 📜 Parable of the Ten Coins Luke 19:11-27 — the rejected king → 🫏 The Donkey & Peace Shalom, not spectacle → 😢 Jesus Weeps Luke 19:41-44 — grief over blindness → ✝️ Die to Self The expression of true faith

chunks.heading: 📜 The Foundation: The Parable of the Ten Coins / 🫏 Why a Donkey? The Kingdom of Peace
chunks.text: Before Jesus entered Jerusalem, he told a parable that frames everything that follows. A master goes away to be anointed king and gives each of his servants ten coins. When he returns, the first servant presents ten additional coins — "Well done." The second presents five — "Well done." But the final servant presents nothing. He hid the coins because, he says, "Master, I know that you are a hard man." His excuse? The standards were too high. He didn't even try. And Jesus' response is startling: 📖 Luke 19:26-27 (NIV) "He replied, 'I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given, but as for the one who has nothing, even what they have will be taken away. But those enemies of mine who did not want me to be king over them — bring them here and kill them in front of me.'" Probably the two most chilling verses in the New Testament. The message: there are people who do not want Jesus as their king — and their rejection is characterised by not doing what he told them to do. It's after these ominous words that Jesus turns his attention toward Jerusalem. Everything he's just taught — about rejected kingship, about willful disobedience, about severe consequences — forms the foundation of his grief as he enters the city. Traditionally, two reasons are offered for why Jesus rode a donkey into Jerusalem. Pastor Kevin examined both and found them insufficient as the primary explanation:

chunks.heading: ❌ Theory 1: Fulfilment of Prophecy / ❌ Theory 2: Demonstration of Humility
chunks.text: 📖 Zechariah 9:9 (NIV) "Rejoice greatly, Daughter Zion, shout, Daughter Jerusalem. See, your king comes to you, righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey." The full Zechariah 9 prophecy describes a Messiah who is lowly, afflicted, and must endure suffering. If the crowds understood this, the tone would have been far more somber. The gospel writers certainly knew this prophecy — that's why they give so much detail about obtaining the donkey. But the question is whether the people of Jerusalem knew. Bible commentator Michael Steed argues they did not: the joyful celebration is inconsistent with understanding a prophecy about a suffering, afflicted Messiah. Many kings of Israel rode donkeys — not as a sign of humility, but on momentous occasions. King David rode a donkey fleeing from Saul. King Absalom rode one into battle. Most tellingly, King Solomon rode a mule into Jerusalem on the day of his inauguration.

chunks.heading: ✅ The Real Message: Shalom
chunks.text: These kings rode donkeys because the donkey represents peace. Their reign would bring peace. And Jesus does the same — his primary message in riding the donkey is: the kingdom of peace is at hand. Shalom (שָׁלוֹם): Not merely the absence of conflict, but everything as it should be. A restoration of harmony, hope, and purpose. Relationships no longer characterised by selfishness and pride. Children getting to be children. Systems of government operating benevolently. Our relationship with God the Father finally restored. And Jesus knows that ultimately, this is not what Israel wants. They want a military messiah who will crush Rome. They want political salvation, not spiritual peace. I offered my roommate a beautiful, nutritious salad — mixed greens, walnuts, apples, blue cheese, avocado, the works. And he said, "How about a cheeseburger instead?" The people of Jerusalem kept longing for the cheeseburger version of the kingdom. And because of that, they rejected the true offer of a kingdom of peace. — Pastor Kevin

chunks.heading: 🎭 Two Groups, One Rejection
chunks.text: Jerusalem had two segments of people. Both rejected Jesus — just in different ways: 🎉 The Crowds 🚫 The Pharisees 🌿 Celebrated Jesus Laid cloaks on the road, waved palm branches, sang "Hosanna in the highest!" 🤫 Denied Jesus "Teacher, rebuke your disciples!" — demanded Jesus silence the celebration ⚔️ Wrong Reason Expected a military leader who would raise an army and defeat Rome 😨 Feared or Disbelieved Scared of Rome's repercussions or simply did not believe Jesus was the Messiah 💔 Would Turn on Him Within days, "Hosanna!" became "Crucify him!" when expectations weren't met 🏛️ Would Conspire Against Him Religious leaders actively plotted to arrest and execute Jesus One group denies him outright. The other celebrates him for the wrong reasons. Both reject him. And this leads to what Jesus says next — some of the most tragic words in Scripture: 📖 Luke 19:41-44 (NIV) "As he approached Jerusalem and saw the city, he wept over it and said, 'If you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes. The days will come upon you when your enemies will build an embankment against you and encircle you and hem you in on every side. They will dash you to the ground, you and the children within your walls. They will not leave one stone on another, because you did not recognize the time of God's coming to you.'" Jesus is not just directing this at the Pharisees — he's addressing all of Jerusalem. They had every chance to receive him. They willingly turned a blind eye. The Irony: "Jerusalem" in Hebrew — Yerushalayim (יְרוּשָׁלַיִם) — means "foundation of peace" or "city of peace." The city of peace rejected the Prince of Peace and his offer of a kingdom of peace.

chunks.heading: 🙈 Willful Blindness
chunks.text: Willful blindness is intentionally suppressing awareness of truth that demands costly change. — Pastor Kevin's working definition Jerusalem had access to Jesus' teachings — 15 to 20 parables just on the nature of the kingdom of God. They didn't miss it. They willingly turned a blind eye to the truth of Jesus. And we do the same thing. Pastor Kevin illustrated this with a relatable scenario: getting a WhatsApp from your doctor. The banner notification reads, "Kevin, I am really concerned about your..." — and you don't open it. Because if you do, the two blue check marks appear, and now you're accountable. So you pretend you never saw it.

chunks.heading: 💡 Where We Practise Willful Blindness
chunks.text: 💰 Money "You cannot serve both God and money." — Matthew 6:24 An absolute statement with no grey area. But we toss it aside: "That's just a hyper-spiritual statement." And we pursue money and justify it because it puts food on the plate. 😤 Anger & Conflict "If you are about to make an offering and have anger toward a brother — stop. Go resolve it first." — cf. Matthew 5:23-24 Before you even worship God, resolve your resentment. We suppress this truth every day: "If I resolved all my conflicts, I might never get to church." 🏦 Finances Ignoring the bank statement You know you've overspent. You know you should look. But you suppress awareness because the cost of change — cutting back — feels too high. 💔 Toxic Relationships Suppressing community concern Dysfunction obvious to everyone around you, but you retreat into isolation. "You don't see the good parts that I see." Willful blindness under the banner of love. 🍔 Health "I'll start the diet after this one last steak" You've been saying "one last time" for five years. You know the truth, but the cost of change keeps you blind. 📖 The Parable Servant "I knew your standards were too high." The last servant turned a blind eye to what the master said. His excuse: the expectations were impossible. Isn't that why we ignore Jesus' teachings too? The great tragedy is not that truth is hidden from us. The great tragedy is that we actively participate in our own blindness. Jerusalem had access to Jesus' teachings and the conviction of the Holy Spirit — and they chose not to perceive.

chunks.heading: ✝️ The Solution: Dying to Self
chunks.text: If the problem is willful blindness — suppressing truth because the cost of change is too great — then the solution is to obey the truth. But Pastor Kevin simplified this further: focus on dying to self. Our work isn't to try to become like Jesus. Our work is more difficult than that. It is more fruitful and freeing. Our work is to die to self. There is a canyon-sized gap between trying to become like Jesus and dying to self. One leads to legalism, bondage, and exhaustion. But dying to self leads to the freedom offered by Jesus. — Steve Cuss The nuance: we do want to become like Jesus. But when that's your sole focus, it leads to legalism and spiritual burnout. Much better to focus on releasing the desire to have things your way — surrendering comfort, pride, preference. When you die to self, Christlikeness becomes second nature. You naturally drift toward generosity, compassion, kindness, mercy, self-control.

chunks.heading: 🪢 The Rope Analogy: Grace, Faith, and Action
chunks.text: Even if you successfully practised dying to self every single day, that will not save you. Salvation is always by grace through faith in Jesus Christ alone: - Grace: The unmerited gift of God. You cannot work for it, achieve it, or warrant it. - Faith: The means of receiving that grace. Picture yourself hanging off a cliff. Someone throws a rope. Faith is reaching up and grasping that rope — not to pull yourself up, but trusting that the rope and the person holding it will save you. - In Jesus Christ alone: The exclusivity of the Christian message. Not faith in good works, morality, humanity, or technology. But faith is not just mental assent — looking at the rope and thinking, "Yeah, I believe it will save me" while doing nothing. Faith is commitment to a belief through action. 📖 James 2:17 (NIV) "In the same way, faith by itself, if it is not accompanied by action, is dead." The greatest expression of faith — of reaching up and grasping that rope — is obedience to all Christ has taught. And that obedience can be simplified to: die to self. Dying to self is the act of grasping the rope. Surrender your comfort so money has no hold on you. Surrender your pride so you can forgive easily. Surrender your preference so you can follow Jesus wherever he leads. It is commitment to a belief. It is the action that solidifies your faith.

chunks.heading: 🌿 Two Options This Palm Sunday
chunks.text: Palm Sunday gives us the opportunity to reflect on Jesus coming into our lives with the gospel of peace — everything as it should be. But we follow in the footsteps of Jerusalem: we give lip service to trusting him, we sing worship songs, but true faith is expressed in obedience. ❌ Option 1: Follow Jerusalem ✅ Option 2: Receive Him Fully 🗣️ Celebrate for our own agenda Lip service, wrong reasons, turning against him when things get hard 🤲 Receive in full obedience Dying to self — and at the end of that narrow path: life and peace everlasting Jesus surrendered himself for us. That was the gravity of his love. He gave himself up so that we might have life. We only die to self because he first did it for us. And what's on the other side of dying to self is wonderful life in abundance. — Pastor Kevin

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages / 🔍 Additional References / 🏛️ Historical Context
chunks.text: 📖 Luke 19:28-38 (NIV) — The Triumphal Entry "As he approached Bethphage and Bethany at the hill called the Mount of Olives, he sent two of his disciples, saying to them, 'Go to the village ahead of you, and as you enter it, you will find a colt tied there, which no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it here.' ... They brought it to Jesus, threw their cloaks on the colt and put Jesus on it. As he went along, people spread their cloaks on the road. When he came near the place where the road goes down the Mount of Olives, the whole crowd of disciples began joyfully to praise God in loud voices for all the miracles they had seen. 'Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!'" The core Palm Sunday narrative — the crowds are joyful and celebrating, but Jesus' own emotional state is about to reveal a radically different perspective. 📖 Luke 19:39-40 (NIV) "Some of the Pharisees in the crowd said to Jesus, 'Teacher, rebuke your disciples!' 'I tell you,' he replied, 'if they keep quiet, the stones will cry out.'" The Pharisees represent the second group in Jerusalem — those who outright deny Jesus, whether from fear of Rome or disbelief. 📖 Luke 19:41-44 (NIV) — Jesus Weeps Over Jerusalem "As he approached Jerusalem and saw the city, he wept over it and said, 'If you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes. The days will come upon you when your enemies will build an embankment against you and encircle you and hem you in on every side. They will dash you to the ground, you and the children within your walls. They will not leave one stone on another, because you did not recognize the time of God's coming to you.'" A devastating prophecy — literally fulfilled in AD 70 when Rome destroyed Jerusalem. Every detail matches: embankments, encirclement, razing the temple stone by stone. 📖 Luke 19:26-27 (NIV) — Parable of the Ten Coins "'He replied, "I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given, but as for the one who has nothing, even what they have will be taken away. But those enemies of mine who did not want me to be king over them — bring them here and kill them in front of me."'" The parable immediately preceding the entry — framing the entire event. The rejected king pronounces judgment on those who refused his authority. 📖 Zechariah 9:9 (NIV) "Rejoice greatly, Daughter Zion, shout, Daughter Jerusalem. See, your king comes to you, righteous and victorious, lowly and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey." The Old Testament prophecy behind the donkey. Zechariah 9 in full describes a Messiah who is lowly and afflicted, enduring suffering to achieve salvation — not the triumphant military leader the crowds expected. 📖 James 2:17 (NIV) "In the same way, faith by itself, if it is not accompanied by action, is dead." The capstone verse for Pastor Kevin's argument: faith is not passive mental assent but active commitment expressed through obedience — specifically, dying to self. 📖 Philippians 2:7-8 (NIV) "Rather, he made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross!" Read during the worship introduction — Jesus gave up his divine privileges and took the humble position of a slave, the ultimate model of dying to self. - Matthew 6:24 — "You cannot serve both God and money" — referenced as an example of teachings we suppress - Matthew 5:23-24 — Resolve conflict before offering worship — another teaching we practise willful blindness toward - Ephesians 2:8-9 — "By grace you have been saved, through faith" — the foundation of salvation by grace Pastor Kevin's sermon draws on rich historical background to illuminate the Triumphal Entry. Understanding the symbolism of the donkey, the scholarly debate over Zechariah, and the devastating fulfilment of Jesus' prophecy helps us feel the weight of this passage.

chunks.heading: 🫏 The Donkey in Israelite Royal Tradition
chunks.text: In the ancient Near East, a king riding a horse or chariot signified war; riding a donkey signified peace. Several Israelite kings rode donkeys on significant occasions: - King David — Rode a donkey while fleeing from Saul, during a vulnerable moment, not as a war posture - King Absalom — Rode a donkey (mule) into battle, a royal mount - King Solomon — Rode a mule into Jerusalem on the day of his inauguration (1 Kings 1:33-40), signalling his peaceful reign - King Jesus — Rode a donkey into Jerusalem, declaring: the kingdom of peace is at hand The donkey was not a sign of lowliness — it was a royal peace symbol. Jesus' choice was a deliberate proclamation.

chunks.heading: 📜 The Zechariah 9 Debate
chunks.text: Scholars are divided on whether the crowds understood the Zechariah 9:9 prophecy. Pastor Kevin cited Michael Steed, who wrote a full-length study on the fulfilment of Zechariah and argued that the crowds did not know the prophecy: - The full prophecy (Zechariah 9) describes a Messiah who is lowly and afflicted and must endure a path of opposition and suffering to achieve salvation - The joyful, celebratory response of the crowds is inconsistent with understanding a suffering-Messiah prophecy - If the crowds understood, there would have been a far more somber tone — recognition that this king rides toward suffering, not victory - The donkey detail was included by the gospel writers for us as readers to recognise the prophetic fulfilment Two possibilities: either the crowds misunderstood the paradox of the prophecy, or — more likely — they simply didn't know it.

chunks.heading: 🔥 The Fall of Jerusalem — AD 70
chunks.text: Jesus' prophecy in Luke 19:43-44 was fulfilled literally, only forty years after his death and resurrection. The destruction of Jerusalem by Rome remains one of the most devastating events in Jewish history. May AD 70 Rome begins its siege of Jerusalem under General Titus. Earthen embankments are constructed around the city — exactly as Jesus prophesied: "Your enemies will build an embankment against you." Siege Wall The Romans build a wall of earth around the city to prevent inhabitants from escaping — "encircle you and hem you in on every side." The goal: starvation. Outer Wall Falls — 15 Days The outer wall of Jerusalem falls within 15 days of the siege beginning. Inner Wall Falls — 5 Days Later The inner wall falls five days after the outer wall. This begins a period of unparalleled suffering for Jerusalem's inhabitants. 500 Crucifixions Per Day Roman soldiers crucified captured Jews at a rate of 500 per day during the siege. The total death toll: approximately 1.1 million Jews. Temple Dismantled Stone by Stone Roman soldiers dismantled every stone of the temple, believing gold was hidden between the cracks — "They will not leave one stone on another." Caesar's Decree Historian Josephus records that Caesar decreed the entire city and temple be completely razed to the ground — so that future generations would never know people had lived there. The precision of fulfilment: Embankments, encirclement, hemmed in on every side, dashed to the ground, not one stone on another — every detail of Jesus' prophecy was realised within a generation. This is not allegory; it is history.

chunks.heading: 📚 Parable Context: The Ten Minas
chunks.text: The Parable of the Ten Coins (also called the Parable of the Ten Minas) in Luke 19:11-27 was told specifically because "the people thought that the kingdom of God was going to appear at once" (Luke 19:11). Jesus needed to correct their expectation that his arrival in Jerusalem would immediately bring political liberation. The parable's master goes away to be appointed king — rejected by citizens who send a delegation saying, "We don't want this man to be our king." When he returns, those who invested are rewarded, and those who rejected his reign face judgment. The parallels to Jesus' own journey are unmistakable: departure (death), return (second coming), accountability, and judgment on those who refused him.

chunks.heading: 🎯 Living Without Willful Blindness
chunks.text: The call is clear: stop suppressing the truth that demands costly change. Start dying to self. This is not legalism — it's the freeing act of grasping the rope of faith. Here are practical ways to begin. 📱 Open the Message What truth have you been avoiding? Like the unread WhatsApp from the doctor, identify one area where you've been suppressing awareness. This week, "open the message." Face it. Let the blue check marks appear. 💰 Surrender Your Comfort "You cannot serve both God and money." Where is money — or the security it represents — competing with God for first place in your life? Dying to self means loosening your grip so money has no hold on you. 🤝 Resolve the Conflict Jesus said to resolve your issues before coming to worship. Is there a broken relationship you've been avoiding? Dying to self means surrendering your pride and taking the first step toward reconciliation. 🪢 Grasp the Rope Faith is not passive agreement — it's active commitment. Identify one teaching of Jesus you've been intellectually accepting but practically ignoring. This week, act on it. That's grasping the rope. 🕊️ Release Your Preferences Dying to self means not needing to be seen a certain way or treated a certain way. Love without condition, without expectation of being loved back. When you do, you naturally become generous, compassionate, kind. 🌿 Receive the Peace Offering Jesus rides in offering shalom — everything as it should be. Don't demand the "cheeseburger" version of the kingdom. Receive what he's actually offering: peace with God, peace with each other, peace in a broken world.

chunks.heading: 🎯 This Week's Challenge — Holy Week / 📝 From the Sermon
chunks.text: As we enter Holy Week leading to Good Friday and Easter Sunday, choose the "Option 2" path: receive Jesus fully, not just with lip service. Each day this week, identify one moment where you can die to self — surrender your comfort, pride, or preference. It might be forgiving someone who doesn't deserve it. It might be giving generously when it feels risky. It might be simply being honest with God about your willful blindness. Write it down each evening. By Easter Sunday, you'll have walked a small version of the road Jesus walked — one of surrender, sacrifice, and ultimately, resurrection life. - In what ways have you willfully ignored the teachings of Jesus? Which of those teachings have been especially challenging to live out, and what makes it hard? - What areas in your life do you need to practise "dying to self"? What small steps can you take tomorrow to begin living it out?

chunks.heading: 🔍 Going Deeper
chunks.text: - The crowds celebrated Jesus for the wrong reasons — expecting a political saviour. In what ways do you come to Jesus with your own agenda rather than receiving what he's actually offering? - Pastor Kevin defined willful blindness as "suppressing awareness of truth that demands costly change." Where in your life are you currently practising willful blindness? What makes the cost of change feel too high? - The last servant in the parable said, "I knew your standards were too high." Do you ever use the difficulty of Jesus' teachings as an excuse not to try? How does the Steve Cuss quote about dying to self (vs. trying to become like Jesus) change your perspective? - Jesus offered shalom — everything as it should be. But Jerusalem wanted political liberation instead. What "cheeseburger version" of the kingdom do you find yourself wanting? What would it look like to receive the "salad" — the true kingdom of peace? - Pastor Kevin said faith is not mental assent — it's grasping the rope. Where in your life is your faith more like looking at the rope than actually reaching for it? What would "grasping the rope" look like practically? - The city of peace (Yerushalayim) rejected the Prince of Peace. Is there an irony in your own life — where what you claim to value or pursue is contradicted by your actions? - Jesus wept over Jerusalem, not because they lacked access to truth, but because they chose not to see. How does it change your understanding of God to know that rejection of truth grieves him, not just angers him? - "What's on the other side of dying to self is wonderful life in abundance." Have you experienced this? Can you share a time when surrendering something led to unexpected freedom or blessing?

chunks.heading: 🙏 Prayer Prompts
chunks.text: - For Open Eyes: Lord, show me where I am practising willful blindness. Give me the courage to open the messages I've been avoiding — in your Word, in my relationships, in my own heart. - For Dying to Self: Jesus, you surrendered yourself for me. Help me to surrender my comfort, my pride, and my need to have things my way. Not to earn your love — but because you first loved me. - For Receiving Shalom: Father, I confess that I often want the kingdom on my terms. Help me receive the peace you're actually offering — peace with you, peace with others, everything as it should be. - For Holy Week: As we walk through this week toward the cross and the empty tomb, keep my heart soft and my eyes open. Let the gravity of what your Son endured humble me and fill me with gratitude. - For Active Faith: Lord, I don't want a faith that only looks at the rope. Give me the strength to reach up and grasp it — to commit to belief through action, to obey even when it's costly.

sermons.title: Easter Sunday: Proof of Life | Island ECC
sermons.subtitle: "He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." — Acts 17:31

chunks.heading: 📋 Sermon Overview
chunks.text: Central Theme: The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the single historical event that changed the trajectory of the entire Christian movement. But it's not just a doctrine to affirm — it's proof that God is personal and that he loves you. Pastor Brett traces the story from the fearful, disbelieving disciples to the Apostle Paul's address at Mars Hill in Athens, showing that the resurrection answers two fundamental questions: Is there a personal God? And does that make a difference in my life? Easter Context: This is Easter Sunday — Christianity's pinnacle day. Roughly two billion people worldwide, nearly thirty percent of the global population, celebrate the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Island ECC joins churches in every country — big and small, open and underground, formal and informal — united around one central idea: the tomb is empty.

chunks.heading: 📖 Key Passages
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:30-31 (NIV) "In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent. For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." 📖 John 20:19 (NIV) "On the evening of that first day of the week, when the disciples were together, with the doors locked for fear of the Jewish leaders, Jesus came and stood among them."

chunks.heading: 🌟 Main Takeaways / 📐 Sermon Structure
chunks.text: - Nobody expected the resurrection — Even though Jesus predicted it, every single disciple was surprised. They hid in fear, resumed old occupations, and dismissed eyewitness reports as nonsense. - The resurrection changed everything — Cowards became martyrs. The single fact of the risen Christ transformed a fearful huddle into a movement that would reshape human history. - Paul at Athens: filling in the gap — In Acts 17, Paul encounters a city full of idols, including an altar "to an unknown God." He presents the resurrection as the answer to humanity's spiritual searching. - God is a giver, not a taker — He doesn't live in temples built by human hands. He doesn't need our offerings. He gives everyone life and breath and everything else. - God initiated the seeking process — The longing for more, the suspicion that "this can't be it," the curiosity that brought you to church — that was a God thing. He stamped eternity on every human heart. - The resurrection is proof — It validated everything Jesus said and did. It demonstrated God's power and his love. It is the exclamation point on God's statement to all of humanity. - Three responses, then and now — Some sneered. Some were curious. Some believed. The same categories describe people in our world today. 😰 Disciples in Fear John 20:19 — doors locked → 🪦 Empty Tomb Luke 24 — he is risen → 🏛️ Paul at Mars Hill Acts 17 — the unknown God → ✝️ Proof of Life The resurrection changes you

chunks.heading: 😰 The Surprise Nobody Saw Coming
chunks.text: We celebrate today arguably the most famous week in the Christian calendar — Easter. We join with over two billion people throughout the world, roughly thirty percent of the global population, that would name the name of Jesus as their Lord and Saviour. But when we look back at the story, we discover something remarkable: the initial disciples were completely surprised by the resurrection. Jesus had actually predicted it. He prophesied that he would, like Jonah in the belly of the whale for three days, be in the tomb for three days and then out. They heard it — and yet they didn't believe it. C. S. Lewis calls this "chronological snobbery" — the tendency to look back in history and say, "Oh, I would have been smarter; I would have responded better." But evidently all of those disciples, without exception, were surprised.

chunks.heading: 🔒 The Fearful Huddle
chunks.text: 📖 John 20:19 (NIV) "On the evening of that first day of the week, when the disciples were together, with the doors locked for fear of the Jewish leaders, Jesus came and stood among them." This is not a bold faith. This is not a courageous group of men. This is not a burgeoning movement. These are cowards who believe it's over — that their three years following Jesus ended at the cross. Right after the crucifixion, the disciples gave up. They resumed their old occupations — they went back to being fishermen. They gathered in fear, believing they would be arrested for their association with Jesus.

chunks.heading: 🪦 The Empty Tomb
chunks.text: At least four women, probably more, went to the tomb bringing burial spices to anoint the body of Jesus. Their intent was to complete the burial rites. Instead, they are told by angels: 📖 Luke 24:6-8 (NIV) "He is not here; he has risen! Remember how he told you, while he was still with you in Galilee: 'The Son of Man must be delivered over to the hands of sinners, be crucified and on the third day be raised again.' Then they remembered his words." Only when the angels quoted Jesus' own prediction did the women remember. They had heard the words but hadn't internalised them. The women verify the empty tomb and rush back to the disciples, fully anticipating joy and excitement. But that's not what happens: 📖 Luke 24:11 (NIV) "But they did not believe the women, because their words seemed to them like nonsense." Even with actual eyewitness validation, the disciples dismiss the report. They were so locked in fear and grief that resurrection seemed impossible. The movement doesn't start off well. Nobody expected it. They thought his body would still be there. Nobody historically had ever predicted their death and resurrection and conquered death. So you can give them that — nobody before had done this.

chunks.heading: 🔄 The Resurrection Changes Everything
chunks.text: It would be this single fact — the resurrection — that would change the trajectory of the entire Jesus movement, what we know as Christianity. The disciples were huddled in fear, resuming their old vocations, counting it as a loss. And then: the resurrection. Suddenly they are galvanised, renewed. They go from cowering in fear to boldly preaching the gospel. The majority of those fearful disciples would go on to live and give their lives for what they believed in — most would be martyred for their faith. They were so convinced of it, they would give their lives for this truth. And the difference was the resurrection. That's what made a difference in their lives. — Pastor Brett So today we ask the question: the resurrection made a difference in their lives — what about yours and mine? So what? Does it make a difference in our lives? Is that even God's intent — for the resurrection to actually change you, to inform you, to give you hope, to give you purpose? Pastor Brett proposed that Easter practically answers two big questions — questions you probably don't ask out loud, but deep in your soul you actually do: - Is there a personal God? — A God that can be known, not just some distant deity. A God I can know personally, who knows me. - What does that mean for me personally? — How does it change my life, my perspective, my marriage, my family, my career? How does that reality ooze into every crevice of my daily life?

chunks.heading: 🏛️ Paul at Athens: The Unknown God
chunks.text: Fast-forward to about 50 AD — just under twenty years after the crucifixion. Most people alive at that time would have had either first-hand experience or at least second-hand information about this Jesus who lived, walked, performed miracles, and was risen from the dead. After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to more than 500 people — eating with them, talking with them, showing his pierced hands and side. The Apostle Paul — once the most anti-Christian man alive, a persecutor of the church who sought to find and kill Christians — had a face-to-face encounter with the risen Jesus, and his life was forever changed. He went on to write much of the New Testament and plant churches throughout the Mediterranean.

chunks.heading: 🏙️ A City Full of Idols
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:16 (NIV) "While Paul was waiting for [Silas and Timothy] in Athens, he was greatly distressed to see that the city was full of idols." Athens was the intellectual capital of the ancient world — home to the movers and shakers, the intelligentsia. And it was covered in shrines: to Athena, Poseidon, Hermes, even a temple to the seven Muses. Paul spoke to the people, asking about their beliefs, and presented his case about Jesus and the resurrection. The responses were mixed: 📖 Acts 17:18 (NIV) "A group of Epicurean and Stoic philosophers began to debate with him. Some of them asked, 'What is this babbler trying to say?' Others remarked, 'He seems to be advocating foreign gods.'" Paul's conviction and his powerful personal testimony earned him an invitation to address the Areopagus — Mars Hill — the centre of Athenian intellectual discourse.

chunks.heading: 🗿 Filling in the Gap
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:22-23 (NIV) "Paul then stood up in the meeting of the Areopagus and said: 'People of Athens! I see that in every way you are very religious. For as I walked around and looked carefully at your objects of worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. So you are ignorant of the very thing you worship — and this is what I am going to proclaim to you.'" Historians record that about a hundred years earlier, a plague hit the region. When their many gods didn't prevent it, the Athenians built an altar "to an unknown God" — just in case they'd missed one. Paul seizes this gap in their certainty. Paul's strategy is remarkable. He doesn't attack their beliefs — he reads the room and redirects their spiritual curiosity. Their elaborate religious life reveals that God has stamped eternity on the heart of every human being: all people in all places at all times have a longing that cries out, "There's got to be more. This can't be it."

chunks.heading: 🌅 The God Who Gives
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:24-25 (NIV) "The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples built by human hands. And he is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything. Rather, he himself gives everyone life and breath and everything else." Paul looks out at the temples surrounding him — built by human hands, maintained by human offerings — and declares that the true God is above all of it. He doesn't take; he gives. Paul graciously navigated a religious minefield. He could have been incredibly offensive, but instead he pointed out: "What you're doing is trying to find God because God — the God — put that yearning within you." 📖 Acts 17:26-27 (NIV) "From one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands. God did this so that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from any one of us." God started the seeking process. No one truly seeks after God on their own — God initiates it in everyone. Your curiosity, your invitation to church, your spiritual questions: all God things. God is not playing a game of hide and seek. He's not hiding, hoping nobody will find him. He's wide open — "Please find me." Here's a sunrise. Here's a sunset. Here's an ocean. Here's northern lights. I'm going to show you my creativity, and I'm going to point to the fact that you're really small, that this universe is really big, and it's going to give you a sense of awe that makes you go, "Is there more?" — Pastor Brett The beautiful promise of God is that he always answers the prayer, "God, show me who you are." He jumps on that prayer. He will bring people, invitations, awareness, books, experiences — he does all things to answer that prayer sincerely prayed. And when that prayer is answered, there's more to know. It's an inexhaustible place of discovery.

chunks.heading: ✝️ The Proof: Raised from the Dead
chunks.text: 📖 Acts 17:30-31 (NIV) "In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent. For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." Paul's climax: we've reached a pivotal moment in history. God has invaded history through the person of Jesus Christ. The resurrection is the proof — the exclamation point on God's statement to all of humanity. Paul didn't preach the parables, the Sermon on the Mount, or the miracles of feeding thousands. He went straight to the big one — the resurrection. That's what sets Jesus apart from all other faiths and belief systems. Other teachers spoke of love and forgiveness — but only Jesus was resurrected. Only Jesus predicted his death and resurrection and saw it become reality.

chunks.heading: Three Responses
chunks.text: 😏 Some Sneered They mocked. It was too unreal, too unprecedented. Whether genuine unbelief or a convenient excuse — "Faith is for emotionally weak people who need a crutch." 🤔 Some Were Curious "Tell me more." It's a lot to take in. It would mean a complete overhaul of my belief system. But what you say — I'm interested. 🙏 Some Believed Convinced by the evidence, transformed by the truth. Including Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus, and a woman named Damaris. These three categories still describe our world today. Some people think it's ridiculous that you're at church right now. Some of you might think it's ridiculous that you're at church right now. Some are on a journey — "I just want to learn a little more." Others are absolutely convinced.

chunks.heading: 💛 Does Easter Matter?
chunks.text: Does it matter that a resurrection actually happened two thousand years ago? Does it matter now? For Paul, the resurrection was incredibly relevant — it changed his whole life. For Christianity as a belief system, it makes or breaks the validity of the claims of Christ. But what about for you? The resurrection demonstrates the power of God. God sent his Son because he loved you and me, allowed him to die a torturous, unfair death on a cross, and raised him from the dead — to prove the love of God. If you're convinced that God loves you — which is what he wants you to be — it changes everything. The way you relate to people, the way you keep score or don't, the way you value yourself as a human being. It changes the things that are important to you. It changes everything slowly but certainly. — Pastor Brett The resurrection power — which is God shouting his love for you — will change every aspect of who you are for the better. The tomb is empty, which is good news for our empty souls, because he fills us up. 📖 Romans 8:1 (NIV) — Next Week's Preview "Therefore, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus." Pastor Brett closed by previewing the next series — a study of Romans 8, "the most important chapter of the whole Bible."

chunks.heading: ✝️ Key Scripture Passages
chunks.text: 📖 John 20:19 (NIV) — The Disciples in Fear "On the evening of that first day of the week, when the disciples were together, with the doors locked for fear of the Jewish leaders, Jesus came and stood among them and said, 'Peace be with you!'" The stark contrast between the disciples' fear and Jesus' greeting of peace. The people who had walked with Jesus for three years were hiding, convinced it was over. 📖 Luke 24:6-8 (NIV) — Then They Remembered "He is not here; he has risen! Remember how he told you, while he was still with you in Galilee: 'The Son of Man must be delivered over to the hands of sinners, be crucified and on the third day be raised again.' Then they remembered his words." The angels at the tomb had to remind the women of Jesus' own prediction. They had heard his words but hadn't internalised them — until faced with the empty tomb. 📖 Luke 24:11 (NIV) — Dismissed as Nonsense "But they did not believe the women, because their words seemed to them like nonsense." Even with eyewitness validation, the disciples dismissed the report. The idea of resurrection was too unprecedented to accept — despite Jesus' own prediction. 📖 Acts 17:16 (NIV) — Paul Distressed in Athens "While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he was greatly distressed to see that the city was full of idols." Athens — the intellectual capital of the ancient world — was covered in shrines and temples to Greek gods, revealing humanity's universal longing to connect with the divine. 📖 Acts 17:22-23 (NIV) — To an Unknown God "Paul then stood up in the meeting of the Areopagus and said: 'People of Athens! I see that in every way you are very religious. For as I walked around and looked carefully at your objects of worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. So you are ignorant of the very thing you worship — and this is what I am going to proclaim to you.'" Paul's masterful rhetorical strategy — he doesn't attack their religiosity but redirects it. Their "unknown God" altar reveals that they know their system has gaps. Paul fills in those gaps with Jesus. 📖 Acts 17:24-25 (NIV) — God Who Gives "The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples built by human hands. And he is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything. Rather, he himself gives everyone life and breath and everything else." A radical reorientation: God is not contained, managed, or manipulated. He doesn't need offerings. He is a giver, not a taker — he gives everyone life and breath and everything else. 📖 Acts 17:26-28 (NIV) — So They Would Seek Him "From one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands. God did this so that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from any one of us. 'For in him we live and move and have our being.'" God initiated the seeking process. Every spiritual impulse, every question about meaning, every sense of awe at creation — God started that pull on your heart. 📖 Acts 17:30-31 (NIV) — Proof by Resurrection "In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent. For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The climax of Paul's address: the resurrection is God's proof — his exclamation point to all of humanity. A hinge moment in history where God invaded through the person of Jesus Christ.

chunks.heading: 🔍 Additional References / 🏛️ Historical Evidence for the Resurrection
chunks.text: - Matthew 12:40 — Jesus' prediction using the Jonah analogy: "For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of a huge fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth." - 1 Corinthians 15:6 — After his resurrection, Jesus "appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at the same time, most of whom are still living." - Romans 1:20 — General revelation through nature: "For since the creation of the world God's invisible qualities — his eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen." - Acts 17:29 — "We should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone — an image made by human design and skill." - Acts 17:32-34 — The three responses: some sneered, some said "we want to hear you again," and some believed — including Dionysius and Damaris. - Romans 8:1 — Previewed as the launch of next week's series: "There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus." Pastor Brett emphasised that the Christian faith is historically grounded — we don't "check our brains at the door." The resurrection is not blind faith; it's a conclusion supported by reasoning, observation, and study. Here is the historical context and evidence that undergirds the Easter message.

chunks.heading: 📜 The Transformation of the Disciples
chunks.text: Perhaps the most compelling evidence for the resurrection is the radical, documented transformation of Jesus' followers: Friday — Crucifixion (AD 33) Jesus is crucified. All disciples scatter. Peter denies knowing Jesus three times. They retreat to a locked room in Jerusalem, terrified of the Jewish authorities. Sunday — Empty Tomb Women discover the empty tomb. Angels remind them of Jesus' prediction. They rush to tell the disciples — who dismiss their report as "nonsense" (Luke 24:11). 40 Days — Post-Resurrection Appearances Jesus appears to the disciples, to more than 500 people at one time, eats with them, shows his wounds. He appears in bodily form — not as a vision or ghost. Pentecost — Bold Proclamation The same men who were cowering behind locked doors now stand in public, boldly proclaiming the resurrection. Peter preaches to thousands. The church is born. AD 33–67 — Martyrdom The majority of the original disciples would be martyred for their faith. People die for what they believe to be true — but people do not die for what they know to be a lie.

chunks.heading: 🏛️ Paul's Conversion and Strategy at Athens
chunks.text: Paul (originally Saul of Tarsus) represents an "enemy witness" — someone whose testimony is particularly credible because he had every reason not to believe. He was a Pharisee, a zealous persecutor of the early church, who actively sought to arrest and execute Christians. His encounter with the risen Jesus on the Damascus Road (Acts 9) produced one of history's most dramatic conversions — from chief persecutor to chief proponent of Christianity.

chunks.heading: 🗺️ Athens in the First Century
chunks.text: When Paul arrived in Athens around AD 50, he encountered a city that was: - The intellectual capital of the ancient world — home to the philosophical traditions of Plato, Aristotle, Socrates, and the Stoic and Epicurean schools - Saturated with religious sites — temples to Athena (the Parthenon), Poseidon, Hermes, Hephaestus, and many others; a temple to the seven Muses (arts, music, architecture) - Home to the Areopagus (Mars Hill) — the council that evaluated new teachings and religious ideas; Paul was invited to present his case there - A city with an "Unknown God" altar — built approximately 100 years earlier during a plague, when existing gods failed to prevent it; the Athenians hedged their bets by honouring any deity they might have overlooked Paul's Rhetorical Strategy: Rather than condemning the Athenians' polytheism, Paul commended their spiritual curiosity ("I see that in every way you are very religious") and used their own "Unknown God" altar as a bridge to the gospel. He quoted their own poets ("For in him we live and move and have our being") before presenting the resurrection as the decisive proof.

chunks.heading: ⚖️ Why the Resurrection Demands Consideration
chunks.text: 🪦 The Empty Tomb The tomb was empty — a fact acknowledged even by opponents. If the body could have been produced, Christianity would have been stopped in its tracks. No one — Roman, Jewish, or otherwise — ever produced the body. 👁️ 500+ Eyewitnesses Paul records that Jesus appeared to more than 500 people at once (1 Corinthians 15:6), and notes that most were still alive — inviting verification. This is not hearsay; it's a testable claim. 🔄 Transformed Lives Fearful men became fearless martyrs. The pendulum swing — especially in Paul — was massive. Something extraordinary must have happened to produce such a radical change. 👩 Women as First Witnesses In first-century Jewish culture, women's testimony was not admissible in court. If the gospel writers were inventing the story, they would never have chosen women as the first witnesses — unless that's what actually happened. ⚡ Enemy Conversions Paul (a persecutor) and James (Jesus' sceptical brother) both became leaders of the early church. Their conversions are difficult to explain apart from a genuine encounter with the risen Christ. 📅 Rapid Emergence The resurrection claim emerged immediately — not decades or centuries later. Paul's letter (1 Corinthians 15) contains a creed that scholars date to within 3-5 years of the crucifixion. This is remarkably early testimony. We don't check our brains at the door when we believe in Christ. We in fact reason, we think, we observe, we study, and the conclusion that makes the most sense is that you are right — is that you are true. — Pastor Brett

chunks.heading: 🎯 The Resurrection Makes a Personal Difference
chunks.text: The resurrection is not merely a historical event to acknowledge — it is proof of God's love that is meant to transform how you live. Pastor Brett urged us to move beyond intellectual assent and let the reality of the risen Christ change everything. 🔍 Examine Your Response Of the three responses to the resurrection — sneering, curiosity, or belief — which describes you right now? If you're in the "curious" category, lean into that. Ask God to show you who he is. He promises to answer that prayer every time. 💛 Receive God's Love The resurrection is God shouting his love for you. This is not the love of a boyfriend or girlfriend — this is the love of a cosmic Creator whose very definition is love. Let that truth settle in: he could not possibly love you any more than he already does. 🙏 Pray the Brave Prayer If you're seeking, trying to make sense of it all, simply pray: "God, help me make sense of it. Show me who you are." God promises to answer that prayer — bringing people, invitations, awareness, and experiences to reveal himself to you. 🔄 Let It Change Everything If you're convinced God loves you, let that conviction transform the way you relate to people, the way you value yourself, the things you prioritise.
